wives and daughter . 

 an EVERY - DAY STORY . 

 

 BY 

 MRS . GASKELL . 
 CHAPTER I. 

 the dawn of a GALA DAY . 
 

 Illustrationo begin with the old rigmarole of childhood . in a country there be a shire , and in that shire there be a town , and in that town there be a house , and in that house there be a room , and in that room there be a bed , and in that bed there lie a little girl ; wide awake and long to get up , but not dare to do so for fear of the unseen power in the next room a certain Betty , whose slumber must not be disturb until six o'clock strike , when she waken of herself " as sure as clockwork , " and leave the household very little peace afterwards . it be a June morning , and early as it be , the room be full of sunny warmth and light . 

 on the drawer opposite to the little white dimity bed in which Molly Gibson lie , be a primitive kind of bonnet - stand on which be hang a bonnet , carefully cover over from any chance of dust with a large cotton handkerchief , of so heavy and serviceable a texture that if the thing underneath it have be a flimsy fabric of gauze and lace and flower , it would have be altogether ' scomfishe "   again to quote from Betty 's vocabulary . but the bonnet be make of solid straw , and its only trimming be a plain white ribbon put over the crown , and form the string . still , there be a neat little quilling inside , every plait of which molly know , for have she not make it herself the evening before , with infinite pain ? and be there not a little blue bow in this quilling , the very first bit of such finery Molly have ever have the prospect of wear ? 

 six o'clock now ! the pleasant , brisk ringing of the church bell tell that ; call every one to their daily work , as they have do for hundred of year . up jump molly , and run with her bare little foot across the room , and lift off the handkerchief and see once again the bonnet ; the pledge of the gay bright day to come . then to the window , and after some tugging she open the casement , and let in the sweet morning air . the dew be already off the flower in the garden below , but still rise from the long hay - grass in the meadow directly beyond . at one side lie the little town of Hollingford , into a street of which Mr. Gibson 's front door open ; and delicate column , and little puff of smoke be already begin to rise from many a cottage chimney where some housewife be already up , and prepare breakfast for the bread - winner of the family . 

 molly Gibson see all this , but all she think about it be , " oh ! it will be a fine day ! I be afraid it never , never would come ; or that , if it ever come , it would be a rainy day ! " five - and - forty year ago , child 's pleasure in a country town be very simple , and Molly have live for twelve long year without the occurrence of any event so great as that which be now impend . poor child ! it be true that she have lose her mother , which be a jar to the whole tenour of her life ; but that be hardly an event in the sense refer to ; and besides , she have be too young to be conscious of it at the time . the pleasure she be look forward to to - day be her first share in a kind of annual festival in Hollingford . 

 the little straggle town fade away into country on one side close to the entrance - lodge of a great park , where live my Lord and Lady Cumnor : " the earl " and " the countess , " as they be always call by the inhabitant of the town ; where a very pretty amount of feudal feeling still linger , and show itself in a number of simple way , droll enough to look back upon , but serious matter of importance at the time . it be before the passing of the Reform Bill , but a good deal of liberal talk take place occasionally between two or three of the more enlightened freeholder live in Hollingford ; and there be a great Tory family in the county who , from time to time , come forward and contest the election with the rival Whig family of Cumnor . one would have think that the above - mention liberal - talk inhabitant would have , at least , admit the possibility of their voting for the Hely - Harrison , and thus try to vindicate their independence . but no such thing . " the earl " be lord of the manor , and owner of much of the land on which Hollingford be build ; he and his household be feed , and doctor , and , to a certain measure , clothe by the good people of the town ; their father ' grandfather have always vote for the eld son of Cumnor Towers , and follow in the ancestral track , every man - jack in the place give his vote to the liege lord , totally irrespective of such chimera as political opinion . 

 this be no unusual instance of the influence of the great land - owner over humbler neighbour in those day before railway , and it be well for a place where the powerful family , who thus overshadow it , be of so respectable a character as the Cumnors . they expect to be submit to , and obey ; the simple worship of the townspeople be accept by the earl and countess as a right ; and they would have stand still in amazement , and with a horrid memory of the french sansculotte who be the bugbear of their youth , have any inhabitant of Hollingford venture to set his will or opinion in opposition to those of the earl . but , yield all that obeisance , they do a good deal for the town , and be generally condescend , and often thoughtful and kind in their treatment of their vassal . Lord Cumnor be a forbearing landlord ; put his steward a little on one side sometimes , and take the rein into his own hand now and then , much to the annoyance of the agent , who be , in fact , too rich and independent to care greatly for preserve a post where his decision might any day be overturn by my lord 's take a fancy to go " potter "   as the agent irreverently express it in the sanctuary of his own home , which , be interpret , mean that occasionally the earl ask his own question of his own tenant , and use his own eye and ear in the management of the small detail of his property . but his tenant like my lord all the well for this habit of his . Lord Cumnor have certainly a little time for gossip , which he contrive to combine with the failing of personal intervention between the old land - steward and the tenantry . but , then , the countess make up by her unapproachable dignity for this weakness of the earl 's . once a year she be condescend . she and the lady , her daughter , have set up a school ; not a school after the manner of school now - a - day , where far well intellectual teaching be give to the boy and girl of labourer and work - people than often fall to the lot of their better in worldly estate ; but a school of the kind we should call " industrial , " where girl be teach to sew beautifully , to be capital housemaid , and pretty fair cook , and , above all , to dress neatly in a kind of charity uniform devise by the lady of Cumnor Towers ; white cap , white tippet , check apron , blue gown , and ready curtsey , and " please , ma'ams , " be de rigueur . 

 now , as the countess be absent from the tower for a considerable part of the year , she be glad to enlist the sympathy of the Hollingford lady in this school , with a view to obtain their aid as visitor during the many month that she and her daughter be away . and the various unoccupied gentlewoman of the town respond to the call of their liege lady , and give she their service as require ; and along with it , a great deal of whispered and fussy admiration . " how good of the countess ! so like the dear countess always think of other ! " and so on ; while it be always suppose that no stranger have see Hollingford properly , unless they have be take to the countess 's school , and be duly impress by the neat little pupil , and the still neater needlework there to be inspect . in return , there be a day of honour set apart every summer , when with much gracious and stately hospitality , Lady Cumnor and her daughter receive all the school visitor at the Towers , the great family mansion stand in aristocratic seclusion in the centre of the large park , of which one of the lodge be close to the little town . the order of this annual festivity be this . about ten o'clock one of the Towers ' carriage roll through the lodge , and drive to different house , wherein dwelt a woman to be honour ; pick they up by one or two , till the loaded carriage drive back again through the ready portal , bowl along the smooth tree - shade road , and deposit its covey of smartly - dress lady on the great flight of step lead to the ponderous door of Cumnor Towers . back again to the town ; another pick up of womankind in their good clothe , and another return , and so on till the whole party be assemble either in the house or in the really beautiful garden . after the proper amount of exhibition on the one part , and admiration on the other , have be do , there be a collation for the visitor , and some more display and admiration of the treasure inside the house . towards four o'clock , coffee be bring round ; and this be a signal of the approach carriage that be to take they back to their own home ; whither they return with the happy consciousness of a well - spend day , but with some fatigue at the long - continue exertion of behave their good , and talk on stilt for so many hour . nor be Lady Cumnor and her daughter free from something of the same self - approbation , and something , too , of the same fatigue ; the fatigue that always follow on conscious effort to behave as will well please the society you be in . 

 for the first time in her life , Molly Gibson be to be include among the guest at the tower . she be much too young to be a visitor at the school , so it be not on that account that she be to go ; but it have so happen that one day when Lord Cumnor be on a " potter " expedition , he have meet Mr. Gibson , the doctor of the neighbourhood , come out of the farm - house my lord be enter ; and have some small question to ask the surgeon   Lord Cumnor seldom pass any one of his acquaintance without ask a question of some sort not always attend to the answer ; it be his mode of conversation , he accompany Mr. Gibson to the out - building , to a ring in the wall of which the surgeon 's horse be fasten . molly be there too , sit square and quiet on her rough little pony , wait for her father . her grave eye open large and wide at the close neighbourhood and evident advance of " the earl ; " for to her little imagination the grey - haired , red - face , somewhat clumsy man , be a cross between an arch - angel and a king . 

 " your daughter , eh , Gibson ? nice little girl , how old ? Pony want groom though , " pat it as he talk . " what be your name , my dear ? he be sadly behindhand with his rent , as I be say , but if he be really ill , I must see after sheepshank , who be a hardish man of business . what be his complaint ? you will come to our school - scrimmage on Thursday , little girl what' - your - name ? mind you send she , or bring she , Gibson ; and just give a word to your groom , for I be sure that pony be not   sing last year , now , be he ? do not   forget Thursday , little girl what' - your - name ? it be a promise between we , be it not ? " and off the earl trot , attract by the sight of the farmer 's eld son on the other side of the yard . 

 Mr. Gibson mount , and he and Molly ride off . they do not speak for some time . then she say , " may I go , papa ? " in rather an anxious little tone of voice . 

 " where , my dear ? " say he , waken up out of his own professional thought . 

 " to the tower on Thursday , you know . that gentleman "   she be shy of call he by his title , " ask I . " 

 " would you like it , my dear ? it have always seem to I rather a tiresome piece of gaiety rather a tiring day , I mean begin so early and the heat , and all that . " 

 " oh , papa ! " say Molly , reproachfully . 

 " you would like to go then , would you ? " 

 " yes ; if I may ! he ask I , you know . do not   you think I may ? he ask I twice over . " 

 " well ! we will see yes ! I think we can manage it , if you wish it so much , Molly . " 

 then they be silent again . by - and - by , Molly say , 

 " please , papa I do wish to go , but I do not   care about it . " 

 " that be rather a puzzle speech . but I suppose you mean you do not   care to go , if it will be any trouble to get you there . I can easily manage it , however , so you may consider it settle . you will want a white frock , remember ; you would well tell Betty you be go , and she will see after make you tidy . " 

 now , there be two or three thing to be do by Mr. Gibson , before he could feel quite comfortable about Molly 's go to the festival at the Towers , and each of they involve a little trouble on his part . but he be very willing to gratify his little girl ; so the next day he ride over to the Towers , ostensibly to visit some sick housemaid , but , in reality , to throw himself in my lady 's way , and get she to ratify Lord Cumnor 's invitation to Molly . he choose his time , with a little natural diplomacy ; which , indeed , he have often to exercise in his intercourse with the great family . he ride into the stable - yard about twelve o'clock , a little before luncheon - time , and yet after the worry of open the post - bag and discuss its content be over . after he have put up his horse , he go in by the back - way to the house ; the " House " on this side , the " tower " at the front . he see his patient , give his direction to the housekeeper , and then go out , with a rare wild - flower in his hand , to find one of the lady tranmere in the garden , where , accord to his hope and calculation , he come upon Lady Cumnor too , now talk to her daughter about the content of an open letter which she hold in her hand , now direct a gardener about certain bed - out plant . 

 " I be call to see nanny , and I take the opportunity of bring Lady Agnes the plant I be tell she about as grow on Cumnor Moss . " 

 " thank you , so much , Mr. Gibson . Mamma , look ! this be the Drosera rotundifolia I have be want so long . " 

 " ah ! yes ; very pretty I daresay , only I be no botanist . nanny be well , I hope ? we can not   have any one lay up next week , for the house will be quite full of people , and here be the Danbys wait to offer themselves as well . one come down for a fortnight of quiet , at Whitsuntide , and leave half one 's establishment in town , and as soon as people know of our be here , we get letter without end , long for a breath of country air , or say how lovely the tower must look in spring ; and I must own , Lord Cumnor be a great deal to blame for it all , for as soon as ever we be down here , he rid about to all the neighbour , and invite they to come over and spend a few day . " 

 " we shall go back to town on Friday the 18th , " say Lady Agnes , in a consolatory tone . 

 " ah , yes ! as soon as we have get over the school visitor ' affair . but it be a week to that happy day . " 

 " by the way ! " say Mr. Gibson , avail himself of the good opening thus present , " I meet my lord at the Cross - tree Farm yesterday , and he be kind enough to ask my little daughter , who be with I , to be one of the party here on Thursday ; it would give the lassie great pleasure , I believe . " he pause for Lady Cumnor to speak . 

 " oh , well ! if my lord ask she , I suppose she must come , but I wish he be not so amazingly hospitable ! not but what the little girl will be quite welcome ; only , you see , he meet a young Miss Browning the other day , of whose existence I have never hear . " 

 " she visit at the school , mamma , " say Lady Agnes . 

 " well , perhaps she do ; I never say she do not . I know there be one visitor of the name of Browning ; I never know there be two , but , of course , as soon as Lord Cumnor hear there be another , he must need ask she ; so the carriage will have to go backwards and forwards four time now to fetch they all . so your daughter can come quite easily , Mr. Gibson , and I shall be very glad to see she for your sake . she can sit bodkin with the Brownings , I suppose ? you will arrange it all with they ; and mind you get Nanny well up to her work next week . " 

 just as Mr. Gibson be go away , Lady Cumnor call after he , " oh ! by - the - by , Clare be here ; you remember Clare , do not   you ? she be a patient of your , long ago . " 

 " Clare , " he repeat , in a bewildered tone . 

 " do not   you recollect she ? Miss Clare , our old governess , " say Lady Agnes . " about twelve or fourteen year ago , before Lady Cuxhaven be marry . " 

 " oh , yes ! " say he . " Miss Clare , who have the scarlet fever here ; a very pretty delicate girl . but I think she be marry ! " 

 " yes ! " say Lady Cumnor . " she be a silly little thing , and do not know when she be well off ; we be all very fond of she , I be sure . she go and marry a poor curate , and become a stupid Mrs. Kirkpatrick ; but we always keep on call she ' Clare . ' and now he be dead , and leave she a widow , and she be stay here ; and we be rack our brain to find out some way of help she to a livelihood without part she from her child . she be somewhere about the ground , if you like to renew your acquaintance with she . " 

 " thank you , my lady . I be afraid I can not stop to - day . I have a long round to go ; I have   stay here too long as it be , I be afraid . " 

 long as his ride have be that day , he call on the Miss Brownings in the evening , to arrange about Molly 's accompany they to the tower . they be tall handsome woman , past their first youth , and incline to be extremely complaisant to the widow doctor . 

 " eh dear ! Mr. Gibson , but we shall be delighted to have she with we . you should never have think of ask we such a thing , " say Miss Browning the elder . 

 " I be sure I be hardly sleep at night for thinking of it , " say Miss Phobe . " you know I have   never be there before . Sister have many a time ; but somehow , though my name have be down on the visitor ' list these three year , the countess have never name I in her note ; and you know I could not push myself into notice , and go to such a grand place without be ask ; how could I ? " 

 " I tell Phobe last year , " say her sister , " that I be sure it be only inadvertence , as one may call it , on the part of the countess , and that her ladyship would be as hurt as any one when she do not   see Phobe among the school visitor ; but Phobe have get a delicate mind , you see , Mr. Gibson , and all I could say she would not   go , but stop here at home ; and it spoil all my pleasure all that day , I do assure you , to think of Phobe 's face , as I see it over the window - blind , as I ride away ; her eye be full of tear , if you will believe I . " 

 " I have a good cry after you be go , Dorothy , " say Miss Phobe ; " but for all that , I think I be right in stop away from where I be not ask . do not   you , Mr. Gibson ? " 

 " certainly , " say he . " and you see you be go this year ; and last year it rain . " 

 " yes ! I remember ! I set myself to tidy my drawer , to string myself up , as it be ; and I be so take up with what I be about that I be quite startled when I hear the rain beat against the window - pane . ' goodness I ! ' say I to myself , ' whatever will become of sister 's white satin shoe , if she have to walk about on soppy grass after such rain as this ? ' for , you see , I think a deal about she have a pair of smart shoe ; and this year she have go and get I a white satin pair just as smart as her , for a surprise . " 

 " molly will know she be to put on her good clothe , " say Miss Browning . " we could perhaps lend she a few bead , or artificial , if she want they . " 

 " molly must go in a clean white frock , " say Mr. Gibson , rather hastily ; for he do not admire the Miss Brownings ' taste in dress , and be unwilling to have his child deck up accord to their fancy ; he esteem his old servant Betty 's as the more correct , because the more simple . Miss Browning have just a shade of annoyance in her tone as she draw herself up , and say , " oh ! very well . it be quite right , I be sure . " but Miss Phobe say , " molly will look very nice in whatever she put on , that be certain . " 

 

 

 CHAPTER II . 

 a novice amongst the GREAT FOLK . 
 

 at ten o'clock on the eventful Thursday the tower ' carriage begin its work . molly be ready long before it make its first appearance , although it have be settle that she and the Miss Brownings be not to go until the last , or fourth , time of its coming . her face have be soap , scrub , and shine brilliantly clean ; her frill , her frock , her ribbon be all snow - white . she have on a black mode cloak that have be her mother 's ; it be trim round with rich lace , and look quaint and old - fashioned on the child . for the first time in her life she wear kid glove ; hitherto she have only have cotton one . her glove be far too large for the little dimpled finger , but as Betty have tell she they be to last she for year , it be all very well . she tremble many a time , and almost turn faint once with the long expectation of the morning . Betty might say what she like about a watch pot never boil ; molly never cease to watch the approach through the wind street , and after two hour the carriage come for she at last . she have to sit very forward to avoid crush the Miss Brownings ' new dress ; and yet not too forward , for fear of incommode fat Mrs. Goodenough and her niece , who occupy the front seat of the carriage ; so that altogether the fact of sit down at all be rather doubtful , and to add to her discomfort , Molly feel herself to be very conspicuously place in the centre of the carriage , a mark for all the observation of Hollingford . it be far too much of a gala day for the work of the little town to go forward with its usual regularity . maid - servant gaze out of upper window ; shopkeeper ' wife stand on the door - step ; cottager run out , with baby in their arm ; and little child , too young to know how to behave respectfully at the sight of an earl 's carriage , huzzae merrily as it bowl along . the woman at the lodge hold the gate open , and drop a low curtsey to the livery . and now they be in the Park ; and now they be in sight of the Towers , and silence fall upon the carriage - full of lady , only break by one faint remark from Mrs. Goodenough 's niece , a stranger to the town , as they draw up before the double semicircle flight of step which lead to the door of the mansion . 

 " they call that a perron , I believe , do not   they ? " she ask . but the only answer she obtain be a simultaneous " hush . " it be very awful , as Molly think , and she half wish herself at home again . but she lose all consciousness of herself by - and - by when the party stroll out into the beautiful ground , the like of which she have never even imagine . green velvet lawn , bath in sunshine , stretch away on every side into the finely woode park ; if there be division and ha - have between the soft sunny sweep of grass , and the dark gloom of the forest - tree beyond , Molly do not see they ; and the melting away of exquisite cultivation into the wilderness have an inexplicable charm to she . near the house there be wall and fence ; but they be cover with climbing rose , and rare honeysuckle and other creeper just burst into bloom . there be flower - bed , too , scarlet , crimson , blue , orange ; mass of blossom lie on the greensward . molly hold Miss Browning 's hand very tight as they loiter about in company with several other lady , and marshal by a daughter of the Towers , who seem half amuse at the voluble admiration shower down upon every possible thing and place . molly say nothing , as become her age and position , but every now and then she relieve her full heart by draw a deep breath , almost like a sigh . presently they come to the long glitter range of greenhouse and hothouse , and an attendant gardener be there to admit the party . molly do not care for this half so much as for the flower in the open air ; but Lady Agnes have a more scientific taste , she expatiate on the rarity of this plant , and the mode of cultivation require by that , till molly begin to feel very tired , and then very faint . she be too shy to speak for some time ; but at length , afraid of make a great sensation if she begin to cry , or if she fall against the stand of precious flower , she catch at Miss Browning 's hand , and gasp out 

 " may I go back , out into the garden ? I can not   breathe here ! " 

 " oh , yes , to be sure , love . I daresay it be hard understanding for you , love ; but it be very fine and instructive , and a deal of Latin in it too . " 

 she turn hastily round not to lose another word of Lady Agnes ' lecture on orchid , and Molly turn back and pass out of the heated atmosphere . she feel well in the fresh air ; and unobserve , and at liberty , go from one lovely spot to another , now in the open park , now in some shut - in flower - garden , where the song of the bird , and the drip of the central fountain , be the only sound , and the tree - top make an enclosing circle in the blue June sky ; she go along without more thought as to her whereabouts than a butterfly have , as it skim from flower to flower , till at length she grow very weary , and wish to return to the house , but do not know how , and feel afraid of encounter all the stranger who would be there , unprotecte by either of the Miss Brownings . the hot sun tell upon her head , and it begin to ache . she see a great wide - spread cedar - tree upon a burst of lawn towards which she be advance , and the black repose beneath its branch lure she thither . there be a rustic seat in the shadow , and weary Molly sate down there , and presently fall asleep . 

 she be startle from her slumber after a time , and jump to her foot . two lady be stand by she , talk about she . they be perfect stranger to she , and with a vague conviction that she have do something wrong , and also because she be wear - out with hunger , fatigue , and the morning 's excitement , she begin to cry . 

 " poor little woman ! she have lose herself ; she belong to some of the people from Hollingford , I have no doubt , " say the oldest - looking of the two lady ; she who appear to be about forty , though she do not really number more than thirty year . she be plain - feature , and have rather a severe expression on her face ; her dress be as rich as any morning dress could be ; her voice deep and unmodulated , what in a low rank of life would have be call gruff ; but that be not a word to apply to Lady Cuxhaven , the eld daughter of the earl and countess . the other lady look much young , but she be in fact some year the elder ; at first sight molly think she be the most beautiful person she have ever see , and she be certainly a very lovely woman . her voice , too , be soft and plaintive , as she reply to Lady Cuxhaven , 

 " poor little darling ! she be overcome by the heat , I have no doubt such a heavy straw bonnet , too . let I untie it for you , my dear . " 

 molly now find voice to say " I be Molly Gibson , please . I come here with Miss Brownings ; " for her great fear be that she should be take for an unauthorized intruder . 

 " Miss Brownings ? " say Lady Cuxhaven to her companion , as if inquiringly . 

 " I think they be the two tall large young woman that Lady Agnes be talk about . " 

 " oh , I daresay . I see she have a number of people in tow ; " then look again at Molly , she say , " have you have anything to eat , child , since you come ? you look a very white little thing ; or be it the heat ? " 

 " I have have nothing to eat , " say Molly , rather piteously ; for , indeed , before she fall asleep she have be very hungry . 

 the two lady speak to each other in a low voice ; then the elder say in a voice of authority , which , indeed , she have always use in speak to the other , " sit still here , my dear ; we be go to the house , and Clare shall bring you something to eat before you try to walk back ; it must be a quarter of a mile at least . " so they go away , and Molly sit upright , wait for the promise messenger . she do not know who Clare might be , and she do not care much for food now ; but she feel as if she could not walk without some help . at length she see the pretty lady come back , follow by a footman with a small tray . 

 " look how kind Lady Cuxhaven be , " say she who be call Clare . " she choose you out this little lunch herself ; and now you must try and eat it , and you will be quite right when you have   have some food , darle you need not stop , Edwards ; I will bring the tray back with I . " 

 there be some bread , and some cold chicken , and some jelly , and a glass of wine , and a bottle of sparkle water , and a bunch of grape . molly put out her tremble little hand for the water ; but she be too faint to hold it . Clare put it to her mouth , and she take a long draught and be refresh . but she could not eat ; she try , but she could not ; her headache be too bad . Clare look bewildered . " take some grape , they will be the good for you ; you must try and eat something , or I do not   know how I shall get you to the house . " 

 " my head ache so , " say Molly , lift her heavy eye wistfully . 

 " oh , dear , how tiresome ! " say Clare , still in her sweet gentle voice , not at all as if she be angry , only express an obvious truth . molly feel very guilty and very unhappy . Clare go on , with a shade of asperity in her tone : " you see , I do not   know what to do with you here if you do not   eat enough to enable you to walk home . and I have   be out for these three hour trapese about the ground till I be as tired as can be , and miss my lunch and all . " then , as if a new idea have strike she , she say , " you lie back in that seat for a few minute , and try to eat the bunch of grape , and I will wait for you , and just be eat a mouthful meanwhile . you be sure you do not   want this chicken ? " 

 molly do as she be bid , and leant back , pick languidly at the grape , and watch the good appetite with which the lady eat up the chicken and jelly , and drink the glass of wine . she be so pretty and so graceful in her deep mourning , that even her hurry in eating , as if she be afraid of some one come to surprise she in the act , do not keep she little observer from admire she in all she do . 

 " and now , darling , be you ready to go ? " say she , when she have eat up everything on the tray . " oh , come ; you have nearly finish your grape ; that be a good girl . now , if you will come with I to the side entrance , I will take you up to my own room , and you shall lie down on the bed for an hour or two ; and if you have a good nap your headache will be quite go . " 

 so they set off , Clare carry the empty tray , rather to Molly 's shame ; but the child have enough work to drag herself along , and be afraid of offer to do anything more . the ' side entrance " be a flight of step lead up from a private flower - garden into a private matted hall , or ante - room , out of which many door open , and in which be deposit the light garden - tool and the bow and arrow of the young lady of the house . Lady Cuxhaven must have see their approach , for she meet they in this hall as soon as they come in . 

 " how be she now ? " she ask ; then glance at the plate and glass , she add , " come , I think there can not   be much amiss ! you be a good old Clare , but you should have let one of the man fetch that tray in ; life in such weather as this be trouble enough of itself . " 

 molly could not help wish that her pretty companion would have tell Lady Cuxhaven that she herself have help to finish up the ample luncheon ; but no such idea seem to come into her mind . she only say , " poor dear ! she be not quite the thing yet ; have get a headache , she say . I be go to put she down on my bed , to see if she can get a little sleep . " 

 molly see Lady Cuxhaven say something in a half - laugh manner to " Clare , " as she pass she ; and the child could not keep from torment herself by fancy that the word speak sound wonderfully like " over - eat herself , I suspect . " however , she feel too poorly to worry herself long ; the little white bed in the cool and pretty room have too many attraction for her aching head . the muslin curtain flap softly from time to time in the scented air that come through the open window . Clare cover she up with a light shawl , and darken the room . as she be go away molly rouse herself to say , " please , ma'am , do not   let they go away without I . please ask somebody to waken I if I go to sleep . I be to go back with Miss Brownings . " 

 " do not   trouble yourself about it , dear ; I will take care , " say Clare , turn round at the door , and kiss her hand to little anxious Molly . and then she go away , and think no more about it . the carriage come round at half - past four , hurry a little by Lady Cumnor , who have suddenly become tired of the business of entertaining , and annoy at the repetition of indiscriminate admiration . 

 " why not have both carriage out , mamma , and get rid of they all at once ? " say Lady Cuxhaven . " this go by instalment be the most tiresome thing that could be imagine . Miss Browning have go in the chariot   or " chawyot , " as Lady Cumnor call it ; it rhyme to her daughter , Lady Hawyot or Harriet , as the name be spell in the peerage , and Miss Phobe have be speed along with several other guest , away in a great roomy family conveyance , of the kind which we should now call an " omnibus .  the housemaid come in to arrange the room . their talking arouse Molly , who sit up on the bed , and try to push back the hair from her hot forehead , and to remember where she be . she drop down on her foot by the side of the bed , to the astonishment of the woman , and say , " please , how soon be we go away ? " 

 " bless we and save we ! who would ha ' thought of any one be in the bed ? be you one of the Hollingford lady , my dear ? they be all go this hour or more ! "

 the child begin to cry , and the housemaid look at each other in some dismay and much sympathy . just then , they hear Mrs. Kirkpatrick 's step along the passage , approach . she be sing some little italian air in a low musical voice , come to her bedroom to dress for dinner . one housemaid say to the other , with a know look , " good leave it to she ; " and they pass on to their work in the other room . 

 Mrs. Kirkpatrick open the door , and stand aghast at the sight of Molly . 

 " why , I quite forget you ! " she say at length . " Nay , do not   cry ; you will make yourself not fit to be see . of course I must take the consequence of your over - sleep yourself , and if I can not   manage to get you back to Hollingford to - night , you shall sleep with I , and we will do our good to send you home to - morrow morning . "

I think it be a very fine thing for you to be stop in such a grand house as this ; many a little girl would like nothing well . " 

 during this speech she be arrange she toilette for dinner take off her black morning gown ; put on her dressing - gown ; shake her long soft auburn hair over her shoulder , and glance about the room in search of various article of her dress , a run flow of easy talk come babble out all the time .

 " yes : darle Cynthia ! Gibson ? , Miss Gibson , to Mrs. Dyson , in the nursery , and ask she to allow she to drink tea with the young lady there ; and to send she in with they to dessert .  Lord Cumnor catch the word and the tone .

 he imitate the deep voice of the fabulous bear , who ask this question of the little child in the story ; but Molly have never read the " Three Bears , " and fancy that his anger be real ; she tremble a little , and draw near to the kind lady who have beckon she as to a refuge .  she follow the lady out of the dining - room , almost hop that no one would see she . but that be impossible , and she immediately become the subject of conversation between the awful Lady Cumnor and her kind neighbour at dinner .

 go ! be there a question of it in Molly 's mind , as she stand up quiver , sparkling , almost cry out loud . she be bring to her sens , though , by Mrs. Kirkpatrick 's next word .

 yes ! she be there forty foot away a hundred mile away ! all that blank space have to be cross ; and then a speech to be make !

Mrs. Brown , may I ask you for a shawl , or a plaid , or a wrap of some kind to pin about she for a petticoat ? " 

 he do not mention that he have come home from a long round not half an hour before , a round from which he have return dinnerless and hungry ; but , on find that Molly have not come back from the tower , he have ride his tired horse round by Miss Brownings ' , and find they in self - reproachful , helpless dismay . he would not wait to listen to their tearful apology ; he gallop home , have a fresh horse and Molly 's pony saddle , and though Betty call after he with a riding - skirt for the child , when he be not ten yard from his own stable - door , he refuse to turn back for it , but go off , as Dick the stableman say , " mutter to himself awful . "
