Mr. Gibson be a favourite in all the tower ' household , as family doctor generally be ; bring hope of relief at time of anxiety and distress ; and Mrs. Brown , who be subject to gout , especially delighted in pet he whenever he would allow she . she even go out into the stable - yard to pin molly up in the shawl , as she sate upon the rough - coat pony , and hazard the somewhat safe conjecture , 

 " I daresay she will be happy at home , Mr. Gibson , " as they ride away . 

 once out into the park molly strike her pony , and urge he on as hard as he would go . Mr. Gibson call out at last : 

 " molly ! we be come to the rabbit - hole ; it be not safe to go at such a pace . stop . " and as she draw rein he ride up alongside of she . 

 " we be get into the shadow of the tree , and it be not safe riding fast here . " 

 " oh ! papa , I never be so glad in all my life . I feel like a light candle when they be put the extinguisher on it . " 

 " do you ? how d'ye know what the candle feel ? " 

 " oh , I do not   know , but I do . " and again , after a pause she say , " oh , I be so glad to be here ! it be so pleasant riding here in the open , free , fresh air , crush out such a good smell from the dewy grass . Papa ! be you there ? I can not   see you . " 

 he ride close up alongside of she : he be not sure but what she might be afraid of rid in the dark shadow , so he lay his hand upon her . 

 " oh ! I be so glad to feel you , " squeeze his hand hard . " Papa , I should like to get a chain like Ponto 's , just as long as your long round , and then I could fasten we two to each end of it , and when I want you I could pull , and if you do not   want to come , you could pull back again ; but I should know you know I want you , and we could never lose each other . " 

 " I be rather lose in that plan of your ; the detail , as you state they , be a little puzzling ; but if I make they out rightly , I be to go about the country , like the donkey on the common , with a clog fasten to my hind leg . " 

 " I do not   mind your call I a clog , if only we be fasten together . " 

 " but I do mind you call I a donkey , " he reply . 

 " I never do . at least I do not   mean to . but it be such a comfort to know that I may be as rude as I like . " 

 " be that what you have   learn from the grand company you have   be keep to - day ? I expect to find you so polite and ceremonious , that I read a few chapter of Sir Charles Grandison , in order to bring myself up to concert pitch . " 

 " oh , I do hope I shall never be a lord or a lady . " 

 " well , to comfort you , I will tell you this : I be sure you will never be a lord ; and I think the chance be a thousand to one against your ever be the other , in the sense in which you mean . " 

 " I should lose myself every time I have to fetch my bonnet , or else get tired of long passage and great staircase long before I could go out walk . " 

 " but you would have your lady's - maid , you know . " 

 " do you know , papa , I think lady' - maid be bad than lady . I should not mind be a housekeeper so much . " 

 " no ! the jam - cupboard and dessert would lie very conveniently to one 's hand , " reply her father , meditatively . " but Mrs. Brown tell I that the thought of the dinner often keep she from sleep ; there be that anxiety to be take into consideration . still , in every condition of life , there be heavy care and responsibility . " 

 " well ! I suppose so , " say Molly , gravely . " I know Betty say I wear her life out with the green stain I get in my frock from sit in the cherry - tree . " 

 " and Miss Browning say she have fret herself into a headache with think how they have leave you behind . I be afraid you will be as bad as a bill of fare to they to - night . how do it all happen , goosey ? " 

 " oh , I go by myself to see the garden ; they be so beautiful ! and I lose myself , and sit down to rest under a great tree ; and Lady Cuxhaven and that Mrs. Kirkpatrick come ; and Mrs. Kirkpatrick bring I some lunch , and then put I to sleep on her bed , and I think she would waken I in time , and she do not ; and so they would all go away ; and when they plan for I to stop till to - morrow , I do not   like say how very , very much I want to go home , but I keep think how you would wonder where I be . " 

 " then it be rather a dismal day of pleasure , goosey , eh ? " 

 " not in the morning . I shall never forget the morning in that garden . but I be never so unhappy in all my life , as I have be all this long afternoon . " 

 Mr. Gibson think it his duty to ride round by the Towers , and pay a visit of apology and thank to the family , before they leave for London . he find they all on the wing , and no one be sufficiently at liberty to listen to his grateful civility but Mrs. Kirkpatrick , who , although she be to accompany Lady Cuxhaven , and pay a visit to her former pupil , make leisure enough to receive Mr. Gibson , on behalf of the family ; and assure he of her faithful remembrance of his great professional attention to she in former day in the most win manner . 

 

 

 CHAPTER III . 

 MOLLY GIBSON 's childhood . 
 

 sixteen year before this time , all Hollingford have be disturb to its foundation by the intelligence that Mr. Hall , the skilful doctor , who have attend they all their day , be go to take a partner . it be no use reasoning to they on the subject ; so Mr. Browning the vicar , Mr. Sheepshanks   Lord Cumnor 's agent , and Mr. Hall himself , the masculine reasoner of the little society , leave off the attempt , feel that the Che sara sara would prove more silence to the murmur than many argument . Mr. Hall have tell his faithful patient that , even with the strong spectacle , his sight be not to be depend upon ; and they might have find out for themselves that his hearing be very defective , although , on this point , he obstinately adhere to his own opinion , and be frequently hear to regret the carelessness of people 's communication nowadays , " like write on blot - paper , all the word run into each other , " he would say . and more than once Mr. Hall have have attack of a suspicious nature , " rheumatism " he use to call they , but he prescribe for himself as if they have be gout which have prevent his immediate attention to imperative summons . but , blind and deaf , and rheumatic as he might be , he be still Mr. Hall the doctor who could heal all their ailment unless they die meanwhile and he have no right to speak of grow old , and take a partner . 

 he go very steadily to work all the same ; advertising in medical journal , read testimonial , sift character and qualification ; and just when the elderly maiden lady of Hollingford think that they have convince their contemporary that he be as young as ever , he startle they by bring his new partner , Mr. Gibson , to call upon they , and begin ' slyly , " as these lady say , to introduce he into practice . and " who be this Mr. Gibson ? " they ask , and echo might answer the question , if she like , for no one else do . no one ever in all his life know anything more of his antecedent than the Hollingford people might have find out the first day they see he : that he be tall , grave , rather handsome than otherwise ; thin enough to be call " a very genteel figure , " in those day , before muscular Christianity have come into vogue ; speak with a slight Scotch accent ; and , as one good lady observe , ' so very trite in his conversation , " by which she mean sarcastic . as to his birth , parentage , and education , the favourite conjecture of Hollingford society be , that he be the illegitimate son of a Scotch duke , by a Frenchwoman ; and the ground for this conjecture be these : he speak with a Scotch accent ; therefore , he must be Scotch . he have a very genteel appearance , an elegant figure , and be apt so his ill - wisher say to give himself air ; therefore , his father must have be some person of quality ; and , that grant , nothing be easy than to run this supposition up all the note of the scale of the peerage , baronet , baron , viscount , earl , marquis , duke . higher they dare not go , though one old lady , acquaint with english history , hazard the remark , that ' she believe that one or two of the Stuarts hem have not always be , ahem quite correct in their conduct ; and she fancy such ahem thing run in family . " but , in popular opinion , Mr. Gibson 's father always remain a duke ; nothing more . 

 then his mother must have be a Frenchwoman , because his hair be so black ; and he be so sallow ; and because he have be in Paris . all this might be true , or might not ; nobody ever know , or find out anything more about he than what Mr. Hall tell they , namely , that his professional qualification be as high as his moral character , and that both be far above the average , as Mr. Hall have take pain to ascertain before introduce he to his patient . the popularity of this world be as transient as its glory , as Mr. Hall find out before the first year of his partnership be over . he have plenty of leisure leave to he now to nurse his gout and cherish his eyesight . the young doctor have carry the day ; nearly every one send for Mr. Gibson . even at the great house even at the Towers , that great of all , where Mr. Hall have introduce his new partner with fear and trembling , with untold anxiety as to his behaviour , and the impression he might make on my lord the Earl , and my lady the Countess , Mr. Gibson be receive at the end of a twelvemonth with as much welcome respect for his professional skill as Mr. Hall himself have ever be . Nay and this be a little too much for even the kind old doctor 's good temper Mr. Gibson have even be invite once to dinner at the tower , to dine with the great Sir Astley , the head of the profession ! to be sure , Mr. Hall have be ask as well ; but he be lay up just then with his gout   since he have have a partner the rheumatism have be allow to develope itself , and he have not be able to go . poor Mr. Hall never quite get over this mortification ; after it he allow himself to become dim of sight and hard of hearing , and keep pretty closely to the house during the two winter that remain of his life . he send for an orphan grand - niece to keep he company in his old age ; he , the woman - contemn old bachelor , become thankful for the cheerful presence of the pretty , bonny Mary Pearson , who be good and sensible , and nothing more . she form a close friendship with the daughter of the vicar , Mr. Browning , and Mr. Gibson find time to become very intimate with all three . Hollingford speculate much on which young lady would become Mrs. Gibson , and be rather sorry when the talk about possibility , and the gossip about probability , with regard to the handsome young surgeon 's marriage , end in the most natural manner in the world , by his marry his predecessor 's niece . the two Miss Brownings show no sign of go into a consumption on the occasion , although their look and manner be carefully watch . on the contrary , they be rather boisterously merry at the wedding , and poor Mrs. Gibson it be that die of consumption , four or five year after her marriage three year after the death of her great - uncle , and when her only child , Molly , be just three year old . 

 Mr. Gibson do not speak much about the grief at the loss of his wife , which it be suppose that he feel . indeed , he avoid all demonstration of sympathy , and get up hastily and leave the room when Miss Phobe Browning first see he after his loss , and burst into an uncontrollable flood of tear , which threaten to end in hysteric . Miss Browning declare she never could forgive he for his hard - heartedness on that occasion ; but a fortnight afterwards she come to very high word with old Mrs. Goodenough , for gasp out her doubt whether Mr. Gibson be a man of deep feeling ; judge by the narrowness of his crape hat - band , which ought to have cover his hat , whereas there be at least three inch of beaver to be see . and , in spite of it all , Miss Browning and Miss Phobe consider themselves as Mr. Gibson 's most intimate friend , in right of their regard for his dead wife , and would fain have take a quasi - motherly interest in his little girl , have she not be guard by a watchful dragon in the shape of Betty , her nurse , who be jealous of any interference between she and her charge ; and especially resentful and disagreeable towards all those lady who , by suitable age , rank , or propinquity , she think capable of " cast sheep 's eye at master . " 

 several year before the opening of this story , Mr. Gibson 's position seem settle for life , both socially and professionally . he be a widower , and likely to remain so ; his domestic affection be centre on little Molly , but even to she , in their most private moment , he do not give way to much expression of his feeling ; his most caressing appellation for she be " Goosey , " and he take a pleasure in bewilder her infant mind with his badinage . he have rather a contempt for demonstrative people , arise from his medical insight into the consequence to health of uncontrolled feeling . he deceive himself into believe that still his reason be lord of all , because he have never fall into the habit of expression on any other than purely intellectual subject . molly , however , have her own intuition to guide she . though her papa laugh at she , quiz she , joke at she , in a way which the Miss Brownings call " really cruel " to each other when they be quite alone , Molly take her little grief and pleasure , and pour they into her papa 's ear , soon even than into Betty 's , that kind - hearted termagant . the child grow to understand her father well , and the two have the most delightful intercourse together half banter , half seriousness , but altogether confidential friendship . Mr. Gibson keep three servant ; Betty , a cook , and a girl who be suppose to be housemaid , but who be under both the eld two , and have a pretty life of it in consequence . three servant would not have be require if it have not be Mr. Gibson 's habit , as it have be Mr. Hall 's before he , to take two " pupil " as they be call in the genteel language of Hollingford , " apprentice " as they be in fact be bind by indenture , and pay a handsome premium to learn their business . they live in the house , and occupy an uncomfortable , ambiguous , or , as Miss Browning call it with some truth , " amphibious " position . they have their meal with Mr. Gibson and Molly , and be feel to be terribly in the way ; Mr. Gibson not be a man who could make conversation , and hat the duty of talk under restraint . yet something within he make he wince , as if his duty be not rightly perform , when , as the cloth be draw , the two awkward lad rise up with joyful alacrity , give he a nod , which be to be interpret as a bow , knock against each other in their endeavour to get out of the dining - room quickly ; and then might be hear dash along a passage which lead to the surgery , choke with half - suppress laughter . yet the annoyance he feel at this dull sense of imperfectly fulfil duty only make his sarcasm on their inefficiency , or stupidity , or ill manner , more bitter than before . 

 beyond direct professional instruction , he do not know what to do with the succession of pair of young man , whose mission seem to be , to be plague by their master consciously , and to plague he unconsciously . once or twice Mr. Gibson have decline take a fresh pupil , in the hope of shake himself free from the incubus , but his reputation as a clever surgeon have spread so rapidly that his fee which he have think prohibitory , be willingly pay , in order that the young man might make a start in life , with the prestige of have be a pupil of Gibson of Hollingford . but as molly grow to be a little girl instead of a child , when she be about eight year old , her father perceive the awkwardness of she have her breakfast and dinner so often alone with the pupil , without his uncertain presence . to do away with this evil , more than for the actual instruction she could give , he engage a respectable woman , the daughter of a shopkeeper in the town , who have leave a destitute family , to come every morning before breakfast , and to stay with Molly till he come home at night ; or , if he be detain , until the child 's bed - time . 

 " now , Miss Eyre , " say he , sum up his instruction the day before she enter upon her office , " remember this : you be to make good tea for the young man , and see that they have their meal comfortably , and you be five - and - thirty , I think you say ? try and make they talk , rationally , I be afraid be beyond your or anybody 's power ; but make they talk without stammer or giggle . do not   teach molly too much : she must sew , and read , and write , and do she sum ; but I want to keep she a child , and if I find more learning desirable for she , I will see about give it to her myself . after all , I be not sure that read or writing be necessary . many a good woman get married with only a cross instead of her name ; it be rather a diluting of mother - wit , to my fancy ; but , however , we must yield to the prejudice of society , Miss Eyre , and so you may teach the child to read . " 

 Miss Eyre listen in silence , perplex but determine to be obedient to the direction of the doctor , whose kindness she and her family have good cause to know . she make strong tea ; she help the young man liberally in Mr. Gibson 's absence , as well as in his presence , and she find the way to unloosen their tongue , whenever their master be away , by talk to they on trivial subject in her pleasant homely way . she teach Molly to read and write , but try honestly to keep she back in every other branch of education . it be only by fight and struggle hard , that bit by bit molly persuade her father to let she have french and draw lesson . he be always afraid of she become too much educate , though he need not have be alarm ; the master who visit such small country town as Hollingford forty year ago , be no such great proficient in their art . once a week she join a dancing class in the assembly - room at the principal inn in the town : the " George ; " and , be daunt by her father in every intellectual attempt , she read every book that come in her way , almost with as much delight as if it have be forbid . for his station in life , Mr. Gibson have an unusually good library ; the medical portion of it be inaccessible to Molly , be keep in the surgery , but every other book she have either read , or try to read . her summer place of study be that seat in the cherry - tree , where she get the green stain on her frock , that have already be mention as likely to wear Betty 's life out . in spite of this " hide worm I ' th ' bud , " Betty be to all appearance strong , alert , and flourish . she be the one crook in Miss Eyre 's lot , who be otherwise so happy in have meet with a suitable well - pay employment just when she need it most . but Betty , though agree in theory with her master when he tell she of the necessity of have a governess for his little daughter , be vehemently oppose to any division of her authority and influence over the child who have be her charge , her plague , and her delight ever since Mrs. Gibson 's death . she take up her position as censor of all Miss Eyre 's saying and doing from the very first , and do not for a moment condescend to conceal her disapprobation . in her heart she could not help respect the patience and painstaking of the good lady , for a " lady " Miss Eyre be in the good sense of the word , though in Hollingford she only take rank as a shopkeeper 's daughter . yet Betty buzz about she with the teasing pertinacity of a gnat , always ready to find fault , if not to bite . Miss Eyre 's only defence come from the quarter whence it might least have be expect from her pupil ; on whose fancied behalf , as an oppress little personage , Betty always base her attack . but very early in the day molly perceive their injustice , and soon afterwards she begin to respect Miss Eyre for her silent endurance of what evidently give she far more pain than Betty imagine . Mr. Gibson have be a friend in need to her family , so Miss Eyre restrain her complaint , soon than annoy he . and she have her reward . Betty would offer molly all sort of small temptation to neglect Miss Eyre 's wish ; molly steadily resist , and plod away at her task of sewing or her difficult sum . Betty make cumbrous joke at Miss Eyre 's expense ; molly look up with the utmost gravity , as if request the explanation of an unintelligible speech ; and there be nothing so quench to a wag as to be ask to translate his jest into plain matter - of - fact English , and to show wherein the point lie . occasionally Betty lose her temper entirely , and speak impertinently to Miss Eyre ; but when this have be do in Molly 's presence , the girl fly out into such a violent passion of word in defence of her silent trembling governess , that even Betty herself be daunt , though she choose to take the child 's anger as a good joke , and try to persuade Miss Eyre herself to join in her amusement . 

 " bless the child ! one ' ud think I be a hungry pussy - cat , and she a hen - sparrow , with her wing all flutter , and her little eye aflame , and her beak ready to peck I just because I happen to look near her nest . Nay , child ! if thou lik'st to be stifle in a nasty close room , learn thing as be of no earthly good when they be learn , instead o ' riding on Job Donkin 's hay - cart , it be thy look - out , not mine . she be a little vixen , be not   she ? " smile at Miss Eyre , as she finish her speech . but the poor governess see no humour in the affair ; the comparison of Molly to a hen - sparrow be lose upon she . she be sensitive and conscientious , and know , from home experience , the evil of an ungovernable temper . so she begin to reprove Molly for give way to her passion , and the child think it hard to be blame for what she consider she just anger against Betty . but , after all , these be the small grievance of a very happy childhood . 

 

 

 CHAPTER IV . 

 MR . GIBSON 's NEIGHBOURS . 
 

 illustrationolly grow up among these quiet people in calm monotony of life , without any great event than that which have be record the being leave behind at the tower until she be nearly seventeen . she have become a visitor at the school , but she have never go again to the annual festival at the great house ; it be easy to find some excuse for keep away , and the recollection of that day be not a pleasant one on the whole , though she often think how much she should like to see the garden again . 

 Lady Agnes be marry ; there be only Lady Harriet remain at home ; Lord Hollingford , the eld son , have lose his wife , and be a good deal more at the tower since he have become a widower . he be a tall ungainly man , consider to be as proud as his mother , the countess ; but , in fact , he be only shy , and slow at make commonplace speech . he do not know what to say to people whose daily habit and interest be not the same as his ; he would have be very thankful for a handbook of small - talk , and would have learn off his sentence with good - humour diligence . he often envy the fluency of his garrulous father , who delight in talk to everybody , and be perfectly unconscious of the incoherence of his conversation . but , owe to his constitutional reserve and shyness , Lord Hollingford be not a popular man although his kindness of heart be very great , his simplicity of character extreme , and his scientific acquirement considerable enough to entitle he to much reputation in the european republic of learn man . in this respect Hollingford be proud of he . the inhabitant know that the great , grave , clumsy heir to its fealty be highly esteemed for his wisdom ; and that he have make one or two discovery , though in what direction they be not quite sure . but it be safe to point he out to stranger visit the little town , as " that be Lord Hollingford the famous Lord Hollingford , you know ; you must have hear of he , he be so scientific . " if the stranger know his name , they also know his claim to fame ; if they do not , ten to one but they would make as if they do , and so conceal not only their own ignorance , but that of their companion , as to the exact nature of the source of his reputation . 

 he be leave a widower with two or three boy . they be at a public school ; so that their companionship could make the house in which he have pass his married life but little of a home to he , and he consequently spend much of his time at the Towers ; where his mother be proud of he , and his father very fond , but ever so little afraid of he . his friend be always welcome by Lord and Lady Cumnor ; the former , indeed , be in the habit of welcome everybody everywhere ; but it be a proof of Lady Cumnor 's real affection for her distinguished son , that she allow he to ask what she call " all sort of people " to the Towers . " all sort of people " mean really those who be distinguish for science and learning , without regard to rank : and it must be confess , without much regard to polished manner likewise . 

 Mr. Hall , Mr. Gibson 's predecessor , have always be receive with friendly condescension by my lady , who have find he establish as the family medical man , when first she come to the tower on her marriage ; but she never think of interfere with his custom of take his meal , if he need refreshment , in the housekeeper 's room , not with the housekeeper , bien entendu . the comfortable , clever , stout , and red - faced doctor would very much have prefer this , even if he have have the choice give he   which he never have   of take his ' snack , " as he call it , with my lord and my lady , in the grand dining - room . of course , if some great surgical gun   like Sir Astley   be bring down from London to bear on the family 's health , it be due to he , as well as to the local medical attendant , to ask Mr. Hall to dinner , in a formal and ceremonious manner , on which occasion Mr. Hall bury his chin in voluminous fold of white muslin , put on his black knee - breech , with bunch of ribbon at the side , his silk stocking and buckled shoe , and otherwise make himself excessively uncomfortable in his attire , and go forth in state in a post - chaise from the " George , " console himself in the private corner of his heart for the discomfort he be endure with the idea of how well it would sound the next day in the ear of the squire whom he be in the habit of attend : " yesterday at dinner the earl say , " or " the countess remark , " or " I be surprised to hear when I be din at the Towers yesterday . " but somehow thing have change since Mr. Gibson have become " the doctor " par excellence at Hollingford . Miss Brownings think that it be because he have such an elegant figure , and ' such a distinguished manner ; " Mrs. Goodenough , " because of his aristocratic connection " " the son of a Scotch duke , my dear , never mind on which side of the blanket . " but the fact be certain ; although he might frequently ask Mrs. Brown to give he something to eat in the housekeeper 's room he have no time for all the fuss and ceremony of luncheon with my lady he be always welcome to the grand circle of visitor in the house . he might lunch with a duke any day that he choose ; give that a duke be forthcoming at the tower . his accent be Scotch , not provincial . he have not an ounce of superfluous flesh on his bone ; and leanness go a great way to gentility . his complexion be sallow , and his hair black ; in those day , the decade after the conclusion of the great continental war , to be sallow and black - a - vise be of itself a distinction ; he be not jovial   as my lord remark with a sigh , but it be my lady who endorse the invitation , spar of his word , intelligent , and slightly sarcastic . therefore he be perfectly presentable . 

 his Scotch blood   for that he be of scottish descent there could be no manner of doubt   give he just the kind of thistly dignity which make every one feel that they must treat he with respect ; so on that head he be assure . the grandeur of be an invited guest to dinner at the Towers from time to time , give he but little pleasure for many year , but it be a form to be go through in the way of his profession , without any idea of social gratification . 

 but when Lord Hollingford return to make the tower his home , affair be alter . Mr. Gibson really hear and learn thing that interest he seriously , and that give fresh flavour to his reading . from time to time he meet the leader of the scientific world ; odd - looking , simple - hearted man , very much in earnest about their own particular subject , and not have much to say on any other . Mr. Gibson find himself capable of appreciate such person , and also perceive that they value his appreciation , as it be honestly and intelligently give . indeed , by - and - by , he begin to send contribution of his own to the more scientific of the medical journal , and thus partly in receive , partly in give out information and accurate thought , a new zest be add to his life . there be not much intercourse between Lord Hollingford and himself ; the one be too silent and shy , the other too busy , to seek each other 's society with the perseverance require to do away with the social distinction of rank that prevent their frequent meeting . but each be thoroughly pleased to come into contact with the other . each could rely on the other 's respect and sympathy with a security unknown to many who call themselves friend ; and this be a source of happiness to both ; to Mr. Gibson the most so , of course ; for his range of intelligent and cultivate society be the small . indeed , there be no one equal to himself among the man with whom he associate , and this he have feel as a depressing influence , although he never recognize the cause of his depression . there be Mr. Ashton , the vicar , who have succeed Mr. Browning , a thoroughly good and kind - hearted man , but one without an original thought in he ; whose habitual courtesy and indolent mind lead he to agree to every opinion , not palpably heterodox , and to utter platitude in the most gentlemanly manner . Mr. Gibson have once or twice amuse himself , by lead the vicar on in his agreeable admission of argument " as perfectly convincing , " and of statement as " curious but undoubted , " till he have plant the poor clergyman in a bog of heretical bewilderment . but then Mr. Ashton 's pain and suffering at suddenly find out into what a theological predicament he have be bring , his real self - reproach at his previous admission , be so great that Mr. Gibson lose all sense of fun , and hasten back to the thirty - nine Articles with all the good - will in life , as the only mean of soothe the vicar 's conscience . on any other subject , except that of orthodoxy , Mr. Gibson could lead he any length ; but then his ignorance on most of they prevent bland acquiescence from arrive at any result which could startle he . he have some private fortune , and be not marry , and live the life of an indolent and refined bachelor ; but though he himself be no very active visitor among his poor parishioner , he be always willing to relieve their want in the most liberal , and , consider his habit , occasionally in the most self - deny manner , whenever Mr. Gibson , or any one else , make they clearly know to he . " use my purse as freely as if it be your own , Gibson , " he be will not to say . " I be such a bad one at go about and make talk to poor folk I daresay I do not   do enough in that way but I be most willing to give you anything for any one you may consider in want . " 

 " thank you ; I come upon you pretty often , I believe , and make very little scruple about it ; but if you will allow I to suggest , it be , that you should not   try to make talk when you go into the cottage ; but just talk . " 

 " I do not   see the difference , " say the vicar , a little querulously ; " but I daresay there be a difference , and I have no doubt what you say be quite true . I should not   make talk , but talk ; and as both be equally difficult to I , you must let I purchase the privilege of silence by this ten - pound note . " 

 " thank you . it be not so satisfactory to I ; and , I should think , not to yourself . but probably the Joneses and Greens will prefer it . " 

 Mr. Ashton would look with plaintive inquiry into Mr. Gibson 's face after some such speech , as if ask if a sarcasm be intend . on the whole , they go on in the most amicable way ; only beyond the gregarious feeling common to most man , they have very little actual pleasure in each other 's society . perhaps the man of all other to whom Mr. Gibson take the most kindly at least , until Lord Hollingford come into the neighbourhood be a certain squire Hamley . he and his ancestor have be call squire as long back as local tradition extend . but there be many a great land - owner in the county , for Squire Hamley 's estate be not more than eight hundred acre or so . but his family have be in possession of it long before the Earls of Cumnor have be hear of ; before the Hely - Harrisons have buy Coldstone Park ; no one in Hollingford know the time when the Hamleys have not live at Hamley . " ever since the Heptarchy , " say the vicar . " Nay , " say Miss Browning , " I have hear that there be Hamleys of Hamley before the Romans . " the vicar be prepare a polite assent , when Mrs. Goodenough come in with a still more startling assertion . " I have always heerd , " say she , with all the slow authority of an old inhabitant , " that there be Hamleys of Hamley afore the time of the pagan . " Mr. Ashton could only bow , and say , " possibly , very possibly , madam . " but he say it in so courteous a manner that Mrs. Goodenough look round in a gratified way , as much as to say , " the Church confirm my word ; who now will dare dispute they ? " at any rate , the Hamleys be a very old family , if not aborigine . they have not increase their estate for century ; they have hold their own , if even with an effort , and have not sell a rood of it for the last hundred year or so . but they be not an adventurous race . they never trade , or speculate , or try agricultural improvement of any kind . they have no capital in any bank ; nor what perhaps would have be more in character , hoard of gold in any stocking . their mode of life be simple , and more like that of yeoman than squire . indeed Squire Hamley , by continue the primitive manner and custom of his forefather , the squire of the eighteenth century , do live more as a yeoman , when such a class exist , than as a squire of this generation . there be a dignity in this quiet conservatism that gain he an immense amount of respect both from high and low ; and he might have visit at every house in the county have he so choose . but he be very indifferent to the charm of society ; and perhaps this be owe to the fact that the squire , Roger Hamley , who at present live and reign at Hamley , have not receive so good an education as he ought to have do . his father , Squire Stephen , have be pluck at Oxford , and , with stubborn pride , he have refuse to go up again . Nay , more ! he have swear a great oath , as man do in those day , that none of his child to come should ever know either university by become a member of it . he have only one child , the present squire , and he be bring up accord to his father 's word ; he be send to a petty provincial school , where he see much that he hat , and then turn loose upon the estate as its heir . such a bringing up do not do he all the harm that might have be anticipate . he be imperfectly educate , and ignorant on many point ; but he be aware of his deficiency , and regret it in theory . he be awkward and ungainly in society , and so keep out of it as much as possible ; and he be obstinate , violent - temper , and dictatorial in his own immediate circle . on the other side , he be generous , and true as steel ; the very soul of honour , in fact . he have so much natural shrewdness , that his conversation be always worth listen to , although he be apt to start by assume entirely false premise , which he consider as incontrovertible as if they have be mathematically prove ; but , give the correctness of his premise , nobody could bring more natural wit and sense to bear upon the argument base upon they . 

 he have marry a delicate fine London lady ; it be one of those perplexing marriage of which one can not understand the reason . yet they be very happy , though possibly Mrs. Hamley would not have sink into the condition of a chronic invalid , if her husband have care a little more for her various taste , or allow she the companionship of those who do . after his marriage he be will not to say he have get all that be worth have out of the crowd of house they call London . it be a compliment to his wife which he repeat until the year of her death ; it charm she at first , it please she up to the last time of she hear it ; but , for all that , she use sometimes to wish that he would recognize the fact that there might still be something worth hear and see in the great city . but he never go there again , and though he do not prohibit she go , yet he show so little sympathy with she when she come back full of what she have do on her visit that she cease care to go . not but what he be kind and willing in give his consent , and in furnish she amply with money . " there , there , my little woman , take that ! dress yourself up as fine as any on them , and buy what you like , for the credit of Hamley of Hamley ; and go to the park and the play , and show off with the good on them . I shall be glad to see thee back again , I know ; but have thy fling while thou'rt about it . " then when she come back it be , " well , well , it have please thee , I suppose , so that be all right . but the very talk about it tire I , I know , and I can not   think how you have stand it all . come out and see how pretty the flower be look in the south garden . I have   make they sow all the seed you like ; and I go over to Hollingford nursery to buy the cutting of the plant you admire last year . a breath of fresh air will clear my brain after listen to all this talk about the whirl of London , which be like to have turn I giddy . " 

 Mrs. Hamley be a great reader , and have considerable literary taste . she be gentle and sentimental ; tender and good . she give up her visit to London ; she give up her sociable pleasure in the company of her fellow in education and position . her husband , owe to the deficiency of his early year , dislike associate with those to whom he ought to have be an equal ; he be too proud to mingle with his inferior . he love his wife all the more dearly for her sacrifice for he ; but , deprive of all her strong interest , she sink into ill - health ; nothing definite ; only she never be well . perhaps if she have have a daughter it would have be well for she : but her two child be boy , and their father , anxious to give they the advantage of which he himself have suffer the deprivation , send the lad very early to a preparatory school . they be to go on to Rugby and Cambridge ; the idea of Oxford be hereditarily distasteful in the Hamley family . Osborne , the eld so call after his mother 's maiden name be full of taste , and have some talent . his appearance have all the grace and refinement of his mother 's . he be sweet - tempered and affectionate , almost as demonstrative as a girl . he do well at school , carry away many prize ; and be , in a word , the pride and delight of both father and mother ; the confidential friend of the latter , in default of any other . Roger be two year young than Osborne ; clumsy and heavily build , like his father ; his face be square , and the expression grave , and rather immobile . he be good , but dull , his schoolmaster say . he win no prize , but bring home a favourable report of his conduct . when he caress his mother , she use laughingly to allude to the fable of the lap - dog and the donkey ; so thereafter he leave off all personal demonstration of affection . it be a great question as to whether he be to follow his brother to college after he leave Rugby . Mrs. Hamley think it would be rather a throwing away of money , as he be so little likely to distinguish himself in intellectual pursuit ; anything practical such as a civil engineer would be more the kind of life for he . she think that it would be too mortifying for he to go to the same college and university as his brother , who be sure to distinguish himself and , to be repeatedly pluck , to come away wooden - spoon at last . but his father persevere doggedly , as be his will not , in his intention of give both his son the same education ; they should both have the advantage of which he have be deprive . if Roger do not do well at Cambridge it would be his own fault . if his father do not send he thither , some day or other he might be regret the omission , as the Squire have do himself for many a year . so Roger follow his brother Osborne to Trinity , and Mrs. Hamley be again leave alone , after the year of indecision as to Roger 's destination , which have be bring on by her urgency . she have not be able for many year to walk beyond her garden ; the great part of her life be spend on a sofa , wheel to the window in summer , to the fireside in winter . the room which she inhabit be large and pleasant ; four tall window look out upon a lawn dot over with flower - bed , and melt away into a small wood , in the centre of which there be a pond , fill with water - lily . about this unseen pond in the deep shade Mrs. Hamley have write many a pretty four - verse poem since she lie on her sofa , alternately read and compose verse . she have a small table by her side on which there be the new work of poetry and fiction ; a pencil and blotting - book , with loose sheet of blank paper ; a vase of flower always of her husband 's gathering ; winter and summer , she have a sweet fresh nosegay every day . she maid bring she a draught of medicine every three hour , with a glass of clear water and a biscuit ; her husband come to she as often as his love for the open air and his labour out - of - door permit ; but the event of her day , when her boy be absent , be Mr. Gibson 's frequent professional visit . 

 he know there be real secret harm go on all this time that people speak of she as a merely fanciful invalid ; and that one or two accuse he of humour her fancy . but he only smile at such accusation . he feel that his visit be a real pleasure and lightening of her grow and indescribable discomfort ; he know that Squire Hamley would have be only too glad if he have come every day ; and he be conscious that by careful watching of her symptom he might mitigate her bodily pain . besides all these reason , he take great pleasure in the Squire 's society . Mr. Gibson enjoy the other 's unreasonableness ; his quaintness ; his strong conservatism in religion , politic , and moral . Mrs. Hamley try sometimes to apologize for , or to soften away , opinion which she fancy be offensive to the doctor , or contradiction which she think too abrupt ; but at such time her husband would lay his great hand almost caressingly on Mr. Gibson 's shoulder , and soothe his wife 's anxiety , by say , " let we alone , little woman . we understand each other , do not   we , doctor ? why , bless your life , he give I well than he get many a time ; only , you see , he sugar it over , and say a sharp thing , and pretend it be all civility and humility ; but I can tell when he be give I a pill . " 

 one of Mrs. Hamley 's often - express wish have be , that Molly might come and pay she a visit . Mr. Gibson always refuse this request of her , though he could hardly have give his reason for these refusal . he do not want to lose the companionship of his child , in fact ; but he put it to himself in quite a different way . he think her lesson and her regular course of employment would be interrupt . the life in Mrs. Hamley 's heated and scented room would not be good for the girl ; Osborne and Roger Hamley would be at home , and he do not wish Molly to be throw too exclusively upon they for young society ; or they would not be at home , and it would be rather dull and depressing for his girl to be all the day long with a nervous invalid . 

 but at length the day come when Mr. Gibson ride over , and volunteer a visit from Molly ; an offer which Mrs. Hamley receive with the " open arm of her heart , " as she express it ; and of which the duration be unspecified . 

 the cause for the change in Mr. Gibson 's wish just refer to be as follow : it have be mention that he take pupil , rather against his inclination , it be true ; but there they be , a Mr. Wynne and Mr. Coxe , " the young gentleman , " as they be call in the household ; " Mr. Gibson 's young gentleman , " as they be term in the town . Mr. Wynne be the elder , the more experienced one , who could occasionally take his master 's place , and who gain experience by visit the poor , and the " chronic case . " Mr. Gibson use to talk over his practice with Mr. Wynne , and try and elicit his opinion in the vain hope that , some day or another , Mr. Wynne might start an original thought . the young man be cautious and slow ; he would never do any harm by his rashness , but at the same time he would always be a little behind his day . still Mr. Gibson remember that he have have far bad " young gentleman " to deal with ; and be content with , if not thankful for , such an eld pupil as Mr. Wynne . Mr. Coxe be a boy of nineteen or so , with brilliant red hair , and a tolerably red face , of both of which he be very conscious and much ashamed . he be the son of an indian officer , an old acquaintance of Mr. Gibson 's . Major Coxe be at some unpronounceable station in the Punjaub , at the present time ; but the year before he have be in England , and have repeatedly express his great satisfaction at have place his only child as a pupil to his old friend , and have in fact almost charge Mr. Gibson with the guardianship as well as the instruction of his boy , give he many injunction which he think be special in this case ; but which Mr. Gibson with a touch of annoyance assure the major be always attend to in every case , with every pupil . but when the poor major venture to beg that his boy might be consider as one of the family , and that he might spend his evening in the drawing - room instead of the surgery , Mr. Gibson turn upon he with a direct refusal . 

 " he must live like the other . I can not   have the pestle and mortar carry into the drawing - room , and the place smell of aloe . " 

 " must my boy make pill himself , then ? " ask the major , ruefully . 

 " to be sure . the young apprentice always do . it be not hard work . he will have the comfort of think he will not   have to swallow they himself . and he will have the run of the pomfret cake , and the conserve of hip , and on Sundays he shall have a taste of tamarind to reward he for his weekly labour at pill - making . " 

 Major Coxe be not quite sure whether Mr. Gibson be not laugh at he in his sleeve ; but thing be so far arrange , and the real advantage be so great , that he think it be good to take no notice , but even to submit to the indignity of pill - making . he be console for all these rub by Mr. Gibson 's manner at last when the supreme moment of final parting arrive . the doctor do not say much ; but there be something of real sympathy in his manner that speak straight to the father 's heart , and an imply " you have trust I with your boy , and I have accept the trust in full , " in each of the few last word . 

 Mr. Gibson know his business and human nature too well to distinguish young Coxe by any overt mark of favouritism ; but he could not help show the lad occasionally that he regard he with especial interest as the son of a friend . besides this claim upon his regard , there be something about the young man himself that please Mr. Gibson . he be rash and impulsive , apt to speak , hit the nail on the head sometimes with unconscious cleverness , at other time make gross and startling blunder . Mr. Gibson use to tell he that his motto would always be " kill or cure , " and to this Mr. Coxe once make answer that he think it be the good motto a doctor could have ; for if he could not cure the patient , it be surely good to get he out of his misery quietly , and at once . Mr. Wynne look up in surprise , and observe that he should be afraid that such put out of misery might be look upon as homicide by some people . Mr. Gibson say in a dry tone , that for his part he should not mind the imputation of homicide , but that it would not do to make away with profitable patient in so speedy a manner ; and that he think that as long as they be willing and able to pay two - and - sixpence for the doctor 's visit , it be his duty to keep they alive ; of course , when they become pauper the case be different . Mr. Wynne ponder over this speech ; Mr. Coxe only laugh . at last Mr. Wynne say , 

 " but you go every morning , sir , before breakfast to see old Nancy Grant , and you have   order she this medicine , sir , which be about the most costly in Corbyn 's bill ? " 

 " have you not find out how difficult it be for man to live up to their precept ? you have   a great deal to learn yet , Mr. Wynne ! " say Mr. Gibson , leave the surgery as he speak . 

 " I never can make the governor out , " say Mr. Wynne , in a tone of utter despair . " what be you laugh at , Coxey ? " 

 " oh ! I be think how bl you be in have parent who have instill moral principle into your youthful bosom . you would go and be poison all the pauper off , if you have not   be tell that murder be a crime by your mother ; you would be think you be do as you be bid , and quote old Gibson 's word when you come to be try . ' please , my lord judge , they be not able to pay for my visit , and so I follow the rule of the profession as teach I by Mr. Gibson , the great surgeon at Hollingford , and poison the pauper . ' " 

 " I can not   bear that scoff way of his . " 

 " and I like it . if it be not   for the governor 's fun , and the tamarind , and something else that I know of , I would run off to India . I hate stifle room , and sick people , and the smell of drug , and the stink of pill on my hand ; faugh ! " 

 

 

 CHAPTER V. 

 CALF - LOVE . 
 

 one day , for some reason or other , Mr. Gibson come home unexpectedly . he be cross the hall , have come in by the garden - door the garden communicate with the stable - yard , where he have leave his horse when the kitchen door open , and the girl who be underle in the establishment , come quickly into the hall with a note in her hand , and make as if she be take it upstairs ; but on see her master she give a little start , and turn back as if to hide herself in the kitchen . if she have not make this movement , so conscious of guilt , Mr. Gibson , who be anything but suspicious , would never have take any notice of she . as it be , he step quickly forwards , open the kitchen door , and call out " Bethia " so sharply that she could not delay come forwards . 

 " give I that note , " he say . she hesitate a little . 

 " it be for Miss Molly , " she stammer out . 

 " give it to I ! " he repeat more quickly than before . she look as if she would cry ; but still she keep the note tight hold behind her back . 

 " he say as I be to give it into her own hand ; and I promise as I would , faithful . " 

 " Cook , go and find Miss Molly . tell she to come here at once . " 

 he fix Bethia with his eye . it be of no use try to escape : she might have throw it into the fire , but she have not presence of mind enough . she stand immovable , only her eye look any way rather than encounter her master 's steady gaze . " molly , my dear ! " 

 " Papa ! I do not know you be at home , " say innocent , wonder molly . 

 " Bethia , keep your word . here be Miss Molly ; give she the note . " 

 " indeed , miss , I could not   help it ! " 

 molly take the note , but before she could open it , her father say , " that be all , my dear ; you need not   read it . give it to I . tell those who send you , Bethia , that all letter for Miss Molly must pass through my hand . now be off with you , goosey , and go back to where you come from . "

 Master Coxe .

 Mr. Gibson smile a little sadly as he re - read his word . " Poor Jeanie , " he say aloud . and then he choose out an envelope , enclose the fervid love - letter , and the above prescription ; seal it with his own sharply - cut seal - ring , R. G. , in old english letter , and then pause over the address . 

 " he will not like Master Coxe outside ; no need to put he to unnecessary shame . " so the direction on the envelope be 

 Edward Coxe , Esq . 
 then Mr. Gibson apply himself to the professional business which have bring he home so opportunely and unexpectedly , and afterwards he go back through the garden to the stable ; and just as he have mount his horse , he say to the stable - man , " oh ! by the way , here be a letter for Mr. Coxe . do not   send it through the woman ; take it round yourself to the surgery - door , and do it at once . " 

 the slight smile upon his face , as he ride out of the gate , die away as soon as he find himself in the solitude of the lane . he slacken his speed , and begin to think . it be very awkward , he consider , to have a motherless girl grow up into womanhood in the same house with two young man , even if she only meet they at meal - time ; and all the intercourse they have with each other be merely the utterance of such word as , " may I help you to potato ? " or , as Mr. Wynne would persevere in say , " may I assist you to potato ? " a form of speech which grate daily more and more upon Mr. Gibson 's ear . yet Mr. Coxe , the offender in this affair which have just occur , have to remain for three year more as a pupil in Mr. Gibson 's family . he should be the very last of the race . still there be three year to be get over ; and if this stupid passionate calf - love of his last , what be to be do ? soon or later molly would become aware of it . the contingency of the affair be so excessively disagreeable to contemplate , that Mr. Gibson determine to dismiss the subject from his mind by a good strong effort . he put his horse to a gallop , and find that the violent shake over the lane pave as they be with round stone , which have be dislocate by the wear and tear of a hundred year be the very good thing for the spirit , if not for the bone . he make a long round that afternoon , and come back to his home imagining that the bad be over , and that Mr. Coxe would have take the hint convey in the prescription . all that would be need be to find a safe place for the unfortunate Bethia , who have display such a daring aptitude for intrigue . but Mr. Gibson reckon without his host . it be the habit of the young man to come in to tea with the family in the dining - room , to swallow two cup , munch their bread or toast , and then disappear . this night Mr. Gibson watch their countenance furtively from under his long eye - lash , while he try against his will not to keep up a dťgagť manner , and a brisk conversation on general subject . he see that Mr. Wynne be on the point of break out into laughter , and that red - haired , red - faced Mr. Coxe be red and fiercer than ever , while his whole aspect and way betray indignation and anger . 

 " he will have it , will he ? " think Mr. Gibson to himself ; and he gird up his loin for the battle . he do not follow molly and Miss Eyre into the drawing - room as he usually do . he remain where he be , pretend to read the newspaper , while Bethia , her face swell up with crying , and with an aggrieved and offended aspect , remove the tea - thing . not five minute after the room be clear , come the expected tap at the door . " may I speak to you , sir ? " say the invisible Mr. Coxe , from outside . 

 " to be sure . come in , Mr. Coxe . I be rather want to talk to you about that bill of Corbyn 's . pray sit down . "

 " insulting prescription ! I be surprised at such a word be apply to any prescription of mine though , to be sure , patient be sometimes offend at be tell the nature of their illness ; and , I daresay , they may take offence at the medicine which their case require . "

I receive you into my house as a member of my family you induce one of my servant corrupt she with a bribe , I have no doubt "

 " I would have say , most probably I will not be certain of my exact word in a suppositional case that you be a young fool , but not a dishonourable young fool , and I should have tell you not to let your thought run upon a calf - love until you have magnify it into a passion . and I daresay , to make up for the mortification I should have give you , I might have prescribe your join the Hollingford Cricket Club , and set you at liberty as often as I could on the Saturday afternoon . as it be , I must write to your father 's agent in London , and ask he to remove you out of my household , repay the premium , of course , which will enable you to start afresh in some other doctor 's surgery . "

 " well , I suppose he must represent the reed . you know the story of King Midas 's barber , who find out that his royal master have the ear of an ass beneath his hyacinthine curl . so the barber , in default of a Mr. Wynne , go to the reed that grow on the shore of a neighbouring lake , and whisper to they , ' King Midas have the ear of an ass .  then Mr. Gibson ring for his horse , and go out on the last visit of the day . he use to reckon that he ride the world around in the course of the year . there be not many surgeon in the county who have so wide a range of practice as he ; he go to lonely cottage on the border of great common ; to farm - house at the end of narrow country lane that lead to nowhere else , and be overshadow by the elm and beeche overhead . he attend all the gentry within a circle of fifteen mile round Hollingford ; and be the appoint doctor to the still great family who go up to London every February as the fashion then be and return to their acre in the early week of July . he be , of necessity , a great deal from home , and on this soft and pleasant summer evening he feel the absence as a great evil . he be startle at discover that his little one be grow fast into a woman , and already the passive object of some of the strong interest that affect a woman 's life ; and he her mother as well as her father so much away that he could not guard she as he would have wish . the end of his cogitation be that ride to Hamley the next morning , when he propose to allow his daughter to accept Mrs. Hamley 's last invitation an invitation that have be decline at the time .

 Mr. Gibson be very glad in his heart that the young man of the house be out of the way ; he do not want his little Molly to be pass from Scylla to Charybdis ; and , as he afterwards scoff at himself for thinking , he have get an idea that all young man be wolf in chase of his one ewe - lamb .

" 

 when the Squire hear from his wife of Mr. Gibson 's proposal , he be as much pleased as she at the prospect of their youthful visitor ; for he be a man of a hearty hospitality , when his pride do not interfere with its gratification ; and he be delighted to think of his sick wife 's have such an agreeable companion in her hour of loneliness . after a while he say , " it be as well the lad be at Cambridge ; we might have be have a love - affair if they have be at home . People be not   like what they be when we be young . all the love nowadays be just silly fancy , and sentimental romance , as far as I can see . "
