" presently he begin to hum the air out of the " Beggar 's Opera " 

 I wonder any man alive 
  should ever rear a daughter . 
 

 

 CHAPTER VI . 

 a visit to the hamleys . 
 

 of course the news of Miss Gibson 's approach departure have spread through the household before the one o'clock dinner - time come ; and Mr. Coxe 's dismal countenance be a source of much inward irritation to Mr. Gibson , who keep give the youth sharp glance of savage reproof for his melancholy face , and want of appetite ; which he trot out , with a good deal of sad ostentation ; all of which be lose upon Molly , who be too full of her own personal concern to have any thought or observation to spare from they , except once or twice when she think of the many day that must pass over before she should again sit down to dinner with her father . 

 when she name this to he after the meal be over , and they be sit together in the drawing - room , wait for the sound of the wheel of the Hamley carriage , he laugh , and say , 

 " I be come over to - morrow to see Mrs. Hamley ; and I daresay I shall dine at their lunch ; so you will not   have to wait long before you have   the treat of see the wild beast feed . " 

 then they hear the approach carriage . 

 " oh , papa , " say Molly , catch at his hand , " I do so wish I be not   go , now that the time be come . " 

 " nonsense ; do not   let we have any sentiment . have you get your key ? that be more to the purpose . " 

 yes ; she have get her key , and her purse ; and her little box be put up on the seat by the coachman ; and her father hand she in ; the door be shut , and she drive away in solitary grandeur , look back and kiss her hand to her father , who stand at the gate , in spite of his dislike of sentiment , as long as the carriage could be see . then he turn into the surgery , and find Mr. Coxe have have his watch too , and have , indeed , remain at the window gazing , moonstruck , at the empty road , up which the young lady have disappear . Mr. Gibson startle he from his reverie by a sharp , almost venomous , speech about some small neglect of duty a day or two before . that night Mr. Gibson insist on pass by the bedside of a poor girl whose parent be wear - out by many wakeful anxious night succeed to hard - work day . 

 molly cry a little , but check her tear as soon as she remember how annoyed her father would have be at the sight of they . it be very pleasant drive quickly along in the luxurious carriage , through the pretty green lane , with dog - rose and honeysuckle so plentiful and rathe in the hedge , that she once or twice be tempt to ask the coachman to stop till she have gather a nosegay . she begin to dread the end of her little journey of seven mile ; the only drawback to which be , that her silk be not a true clan - tartan , and a little uncertainty as to Miss Rose 's punctuality . at length they come to a village ; straggle cottage line the road , an old church stand on a kind of green , with the public - house close by it ; there be a great tree , with a bench all round the trunk , midway between the church gate and the little inn . the wooden stock be close to the gate . molly have long pass the limit of her ride , but she know this must be the village of Hamley , and that they must be very near to the hall . 

 they swing in at the gate of the park in a few minute , and drive up through meadow - grass , ripen for hay , it be no grand aristocratic deer - park this to the old red - brick hall ; not three hundred yard from the high - road . there have be no footman send with the carriage , but a respectable servant stand at the door , even before they draw up , ready to receive the expect visitor , and take she into the drawing - room where his mistress lie await she . 

 Mrs. Hamley rise from her sofa to give molly a gentle welcome ; she keep the girl 's hand in her after she have finish speak , look into her face , as if study it , and unconscious of the faint blush she call up on the otherwise colourless cheek . 

 " I think we shall be great friend , " say she , at length . " I like your face , and I be always guide by first impression . give I a kiss , my dear . " 

 it be far easy to be active than passive during this process of ' swear eternal friendship , " and molly willingly kiss the sweet pale face hold up to she . 

 " I mean to have go and fetch you myself ; but the heat oppress I , and I do not feel up to the exertion . I hope you have a pleasant drive ? " 

 " very , " say Molly , with shy conciseness . 

 " and now I will take you to your room ; I have have you put close to I ; I think you would like it well , even though it be a small room than the other . " 

 she rise languidly , and wrap her light shawl round her yet elegant figure , lead the way upstairs . Molly 's bedroom open out of Mrs. Hamley 's private sitting - room ; on the other side of which be her own bedroom . she show molly this easy mean of communication , and then , tell her visitor she would await she in the sitting - room , she close the door , and Molly be leave at leisure to make acquaintance with her surrounding . 

 first of all , she go to the window to see what be to be see . a flower - garden right below ; a meadow of ripe grass just beyond , change colour in long sweep , as the soft wind blow over it ; great old forest - tree a little on one side ; and , beyond they again , to be see only by stand very close to the side of the window - sill , or by put her head out , if the window be open , the silver shimmer of a mere , about a quarter of a mile off . on the opposite side to the tree and the mere , the look - out be bound by the old wall and high - peak roof of the extensive farm - building . the deliciousness of the early summer silence be only break by the song of the bird , and the near hum of bee . listen to these sound , which enhance the exquisite sense of stillness , and puzzle out object obscure by distance or shadow , molly forget herself , and be suddenly startle into a sense of the present by a sound of voice in the next room some servant or other speak to Mrs. Hamley . molly hurry to unpack her box , and arrange her few clothe in the pretty old - fashioned chest of drawer , which be to serve she as dressing - table as well . all the furniture in the room be as old - fashioned and as well - preserve as it could be . the chintz curtain be indian calico of the last century the colour almost wash out , but the stuff itself exquisitely clean . there be a little strip of bedside carpeting , but the wooden flooring , thus liberally display , be of finely - grain oak , so firmly join , plank to plank , that no grain of dust could make its way into the interstice . there be none of the luxury of modern day ; no writing - table , or sofa , or pier - glass . in one corner of the wall be a bracket , hold an indian jar fill with pot - pourri ; and that and the climbing honeysuckle outside the open window scent the room more exquisitely than any toilette perfume . molly lay out her white gown   of last year 's date and size   upon the bed , ready for the   to her new   operation of dress for dinner , and have arrange her hair and dress , and take out her company worst - work , she open the door softly , and see Mrs. Hamley lie on the sofa . 

 " shall we stay up here , my dear ? I think it be pleasanter than down below ; and then I shall not have to come upstairs again at dressing - time . " 

 " I shall like it very much , " reply molly . 

 " ah ! you have   get your sewing , like a good girl , " say Mrs. Hamley . " now , I do not   sew much . I live alone a great deal . you see , both my boy be at Cambridge , and the squire be out of door all day long so I have almost forget how to sew . I read a great deal . do you like read ? " 

 " it depend upon the kind of book , " say Molly . " I be afraid I do not   like ' steady reading , ' as papa call it . " 

 " but you like poetry ! " say Mrs. Hamley , almost interrupt molly . " I be sure you do , from your face . have you read this last poem of Mrs. Hemans ? shall I read it aloud to you ? " 

 so she begin . molly be not so much absorb in listening but that she could glance round the room . the character of the furniture be much the same as in her own . Old - fashion , of handsome material , and faultlessly clean , the age and the foreign appearance of it give an aspect of comfort and picturesqueness to the whole apartment . on the wall there hang some crayon sketch portrait . she think she could make out that one of they be a likeness of Mrs. Hamley , in her beautiful youth . and then she become interested in the poem , and drop her work , and listen in a manner that be after Mrs. Hamley 's own heart . when the reading of the poem be end , Mrs. Hamley reply to some of Molly 's word of admiration , by say : 

 " ah ! I think I must read you some of Osborne 's poetry some day ; under seal of secrecy , remember ; but I really fancy they be almost as good as Mrs. Hemans ' . " 

 to be nearly as good as Mrs. Hemans ' be say as much to the young lady of that day , as say that poetry be nearly as good as Tennyson 's would be in this . molly look up with eager interest . 

 " Mr. Osborne Hamley ? do your son write poetry ? " 

 " yes . I really think I may say he be a poet . he be a very brilliant , clever young man , and he quite hop to get a fellowship at Trinity . he say he be sure to be high up among the wrangler , and that he expect to get one of the Chancellor 's medal . that be his likeness the one hang against the wall behind you . " 

 molly turn round , and see one of the crayon sketch represent two boy , in the most youthful kind of jacket and trouser , and fall collar . the elder be sit down , read intently . the young be stand by he , and evidently try to call the attention of the reader off to some object out of door out of the window of the very room in which they be sit , as molly discover when she begin to recognize the article of furniture faintly indicate in the picture . 

 " I like their face ! " say Molly . " I suppose it be so long ago now , that I may speak of their likeness to you as if they be somebody else ; may not I ? " 

 " certainly , " say Mrs. Hamley , as soon as she understand what Molly mean . " tell I just what you think of they , my dear ; it will amuse I to compare your impression with what they really be . " 

 " oh ! but I do not mean to guess at their character . I could not do it ; and it would be impertinent , if I could . I can only speak about their face as I see they in the picture . " 

 " well ! tell I what you think of they ! " 

 " the eld the reading boy be very beautiful ; but I can not   quite make out his face yet , because his head be down , and I can not   see the eye . that be the Mr. Osborne Hamley who write poetry . " 

 " yes . he be not quite so handsome now ; but he be a beautiful boy . Roger be never to be compare with he . " 

 " no ; he be not handsome . and yet I like his face . I can see his eye . they be grave and solemn - looking ; but all the rest of his face be rather merry than otherwise . it look too steady and sober , too good a face , to go tempt his brother to leave his lesson . " 

 " ah ! but it be not a lesson . I remember the painter , Mr. Green , once see Osborne read some poetry , while Roger be try to persuade he to come out and have a ride in the hay - cart that be the ' motive ' of the picture , to speak artistically . Roger be not much of a reader ; at least , he do not   care for poetry , and book of romance , or sentiment . he be so fond of natural history ; and that take he , like the squire , a great deal out of door ; and when he be in , he be always read scientific book that bear upon his pursuit . he be a good , steady fellow , though , and give we great satisfaction , but he be not likely to have such a brilliant career as Osborne . " 

 molly try to find out in the picture the characteristic of the two boy , as they be now explain to she by their mother ; and in question and answer about the various drawing hang round the room the time pass away until the dressing - bell ring for the six o'clock dinner . 

 Molly be rather dismay by the offer of the maid whom Mrs. Hamley have send to assist she . " I be afraid they expect I to be very smart , " she keep think to herself . " if they do , they will be disappoint ; that be all . but I wish my plaid silk gown have be ready . " 

 she look at herself in the glass with some anxiety , for the first time in her life . she see a slight , lean figure , promise to be tall ; a complexion browner than cream - colour , although in a year or two it might have that tint ; plentiful curly black hair , tie up in a bunch behind with a rose - colour ribbon ; long , almond - shape , soft gray eye , shade both above and below by curl black eyelash . 

 " I do not   think I be pretty , " think molly , as she turn away from the glass ; " and yet I be not sure . " she would have be sure , if , instead of inspect herself with such solemnity , she have smile her own sweet merry smile , and call out the gleam of her tooth , and the charm of her dimple . 

 she find her way downstairs into the drawing - room in good time ; she could look about she , and learn how to feel at home in her new quarter . the room be forty - foot long or so , fit up with yellow satin at some distant period ; high spindle - legge chair and pembroke - table abound . the carpet be of the same date as the curtain , and be thread - bare in many place ; and in other be cover with drugget . stand of plant , great jar of flower , old indian china and cabinet give the room the pleasant aspect it certainly have . and to add to it , there be five high , long window on one side of the room , all open to the prettiest bit of flower - garden in the ground or what be consider as such brilliant - colour , geometrically - shape bed , converge to a sun - dial in the midst . the squire come in abruptly , and in his morning dress ; he stand at the door , as if surprised at the white - rob stranger in possession of his hearth . then , suddenly remember himself , but not before Molly have begin to feel very hot , he say 

 " why , God bless my soul , I would quite forget you ; you be Miss Gibson , Gibson 's daughter , be not   you ? come to pay we a visit ? I be sure I be very glad to see you , my dear . " 

 by this time , they have meet in the middle of the room , and he be shake Molly 's hand with vehement friendliness , intend to make up for his not know she at first . 

 " I must go and dress , though , " say he , look at his soil gaiter . " Madam like it . it be one of her fine London way , and she be break I into it at last . very good plan , though , and quite right to make oneself fit for lady ' society . do your father dress for dinner , Miss Gibson ? " he do not stay to wait for her answer , but hasten away to perform his toilette . 

 they din at a small table in a great large room . there be so few article of furniture in it , and the apartment itself be so vast , that molly long for the snugness of the home dining - room ; nay , it be to be fear that , before the stately dinner at Hamley Hall come to an end , she even regret the crowd chair and table , the hurry of eat , the quick unformal manner in which everybody seem to finish their meal as fast as possible , and to return to the work they have leave . she try to think that at six o'clock all the business of the day be end , and that people might linger if they choose . she measure the distance from the sideboard to the table with her eye , and make allowance for the man who have to carry thing backwards and forwards ; but , all the same , this dinner appear to she a wearisome business , prolong because the squire like it , for Mrs. Hamley seem tired out . she eat even less than Molly , and send for fan and smell - bottle to amuse herself with , until at length the table - cloth be clear away , and the dessert be put upon a mahogany table , polish like a look - glass . 

 the Squire have hitherto be too busy to talk , except about the immediate concern of the table , and one or two of the great break to the usual monotony of his day ; a monotony in which he delight , but which sometimes become oppressive to his wife . now , however , peel his orange , he turn to molly 

 " to - morrow you will have to do this for I , Miss Gibson . " 

 " Shall I ? I will do it to - day , if you like , sir . " 

 " no ; to - day I shall treat you as a visitor , with all proper ceremony . to - morrow I shall send you errand , and call you by your christian name . " 

 " I shall like that , " say Molly . 

 " I be want to call you something less formal than Miss Gibson , " say Mrs. Hamley . 

 " my name be Molly . it be an old - fashioned name , and I be christen Mary . but papa like Molly . " 

 " that be right . keep to the good old fashion , my dear . " 

 " well , I must say I think Mary be pretty than Molly , and quite as old a name , too , " say Mrs. Hamley . 

 " I think it be , " say Molly , lower her voice , and drop her eye , " because mamma be Mary , and I be call molly while she live . " 

 " ah , poor thing , " say the squire , not perceive his wife 's sign to change the subject , " I remember how sorry every one be when she die ; no one think she be delicate , she have such a fresh colour , till all at once she pop off , as one may say . " 

 " it must have be a terrible blow to your father , " say Mrs. Hamley , see that molly do not know what to answer . 

 " ay , ay . it come so sudden , so soon after they be marry . " 

 " I think it be nearly four year , " say Molly . 

 " and four year be soon be a short time to a couple who look to spend their lifetime together . every one think Gibson would have marry again . " 

 " hush , " say Mrs. Hamley , see in Molly 's eye and change of colour how completely this be a new idea to she . but the squire be not so easily stop . 

 " well I would perhaps well not have say it , but it be the truth ; they do . he be not likely to marry now , so one may say it out . why , your father be past forty , be not   he ? " 

 " forty - three . I do not   believe he ever think of marry again , " say Molly , recur to the idea , as one do to that of danger which have pass by , without one 's be aware of it . 

 " no ! I do not   believe he do , my dear . he look to I just like a man who would be constant to the memory of his wife . you must not mind what the squire say . " 

 " ah ! you would well go away , if you be go to teach Miss Gibson such treason as that against the master of the house . " 

 molly go into the drawing - room with Mrs. Hamley , but her thought do not change with the room . she could not help dwell on the danger which she fancy she have escape , and be astonish at her own stupidity at never have imagine such a possibility as her father 's second marriage . she feel that she be answer Mrs. Hamley 's remark in a very unsatisfactory manner . 

 " there be papa , with the squire ! " she suddenly exclaim . there they be come across the flower - garden from the stable - yard , her father switch his boot with his rid whip , in order to make they presentable in Mrs. Hamley 's drawing - room . he look so exactly like his usual self , his home - self , that the see he in the flesh be the most efficacious way of dispel the phantom fear of a second wedding , which be begin to harass his daughter 's mind ; and the pleasant conviction that he could not rest till he have come over to see how she be go on in her new home , steal into her heart , although he speak but little to she , and that little be all in a joking tone . after he have go away , the Squire undertake to teach her cribbage , and she be happy enough now to give he all her attention . he keep on prattling while they play ; sometimes in relation to the card ; at other tell she of small occurrence which he think might interest she . 

 " so you do not   know my boy , even by sight . I should have think you would have do , for they be fond enough of rid into Hollingford ; and I know Roger have often enough be to borrow book from your father . Roger be a scientific sort of a fellow . Osborne be clever , like his mother . I should not   wonder if he publish a book some day . you be not count right , Miss Gibson . why , I could cheat you as easily as possible . " and so on , till the butler come in with a solemn look , place a large prayer - book before his master , who huddle the card away in a hurry , as if catch in an incongruous employment ; and then the maid and man troop in to prayer the window be still open , and the sound of the solitary corncrake , and the owl hoot in the tree , mingle with the word speak . then to bed ; and so end the day . 

 molly look out of her chamber window lean on the sill , and snuff up the night odour of the honeysuckle . the soft velvet darkness hide everything that be at any distance from she ; although she be as conscious of their presence as if she have see they . 

 " I think I shall be very happy here , " be in Molly 's thought , as she turn away at length , and begin to prepare for bed . before long the squire 's word , relate to her father 's second marriage , come across she , and spoil the sweet peace of her final thought . " who could he have marry ? " she ask herself . " Miss Eyre ? Miss Browning ? Miss Phobe ? Miss Goodenough ? " one by one , each of these be reject for sufficient reason . yet the unsatisfied question rankle in her mind , and dart out of ambush to disturb her dream . 

 Mrs. Hamley do not come down to breakfast ; and Molly find out with a little dismay , that the squire and she be to have it by themselves . on this first morning he put aside his newspaper one an old establish Tory journal , with all the local and county news , which be the most interesting to he ; the other the Morning Chronicle , which he call his dose of bitter , and which call out many a strong expression and tolerably pungent oath . to - day , however , he be " on his manner , " as he afterwards explain to Molly ; and he plunge about , try to find ground for a conversation . he could talk of his wife and his son , his estate , and his mode of farming ; his tenant , and the mismanagement of the last county election . Molly 's interest be her father , Miss Eyre , her garden and pony ; in a fainter degree Miss Brownings , the Cumnor Charity School , and the new gown that be to come from Miss Rose 's ; into the midst of which the one great question , " who be it that people think it be possible papa might marry ? " keep pop up into her mouth , like a troublesome Jack - in - the - box . for the present , however , the lid be snap down upon the intruder as often as he show his head between her tooth . they be very polite to each other during the meal ; and it be not a little tiresome to both . when it be end the Squire withdraw into his study to read the untasted newspaper . it be the custom to call the room in which Squire Hamley keep his coat , boot , and gaiter , his different stick and favourite spud , his gun and fishing - rod , " the study . " there be a bureau in it , and a three - cornered arm - chair , but no book be visible . the great part of they be keep in a large , musty - smell room , in an unfrequented part of the house ; so unfrequented that the housemaid often neglect to open the window - shutter , which look into a part of the ground over - grow with the luxuriant growth of shrub . indeed , it be a tradition in the servant ' hall that , in the late squire 's time he who have be pluck at college the library window have be board up to avoid pay the window - tax . and when the " young gentleman " be at home the housemaid , without a single direction to that effect , be regular in her charge of this room ; open the window and light fire daily , and dust the handsomely - bind volume , which be really a very fair collection of the standard literature in the middle of the last century . all the book that have be purchase since that time be hold in small book - case between each two of the drawing - room window , and in Mrs. Hamley 's own sitting - room upstairs . those in the drawing - room be quite enough to employ molly ; indeed , she be so deep in one of Sir Walter Scott 's novel that she jump as if she have be shoot , when an hour or so after breakfast the squire come to the gravel - path outside one of the window , and call to ask she if she would like to come out of door and go about the garden and home - field with he . 

 " it must be a little dull for you , my girl , all by yourself , with nothing but book to look at , in the morning here ; but you see , madam have a fancy for be quiet in the morning : she tell your father about it , and so do I , but I feel sorry for you all the same , when I see you sit on the ground all alone in the drawing - room . " 

 molly have be in the very middle of the Bride of Lammermoor , and would gladly have stay in - door to finish it , but she feel the squire 's kindness all the same . they go in and out of old - fashioned greenhouse , over trim lawn , the Squire unlock the great walled kitchen - garden , and go about give direction to gardener ; and all the time molly follow he like a little dog , her mind quite full of " Ravenswood " and " Lucy Ashton . " presently , every place near the house have be inspect and regulate , and the squire be more at liberty to give his attention to his companion , as they pass through the little wood that separate the garden from the adjoining field . molly , too , pluck away her thought from the seventeenth century ; and , somehow or other , that one question , which have so haunt she before , come out of her lip before she be aware a literal impromptu , 

 " who do people think papa would marry ? that time long ago soon after mamma die ? " 

 she drop her voice very soft and low , as she speak the last word . the Squire turn round upon she , and look at her face , he know not why . it be very grave , a little pale , but her steady eye almost command some kind of answer . 

 " whew , " say he , whistle to gain time ; not that he have anything definite to say , for no one have ever have any reason to join Mr. Gibson 's name with any know lady : it be only a loose conjecture that have be hazard on the probability a young widower , with a little girl . 

 " I never hear of any one his name be never couple with any lady 's ' twa only in the nature of thing that he should marry again ; he may do it yet , for aught I know , and I do not   think it would be a bad move either . I tell he so , the last time but one he be here . " 

 " and what do he say ? " ask breathless Molly . 

 " oh : he only smile and say nothing . you should not   take up word so seriously , my dear . very likely he may never think of marry again , and if he do , it would be a very good thing both for he and for you ! " 

 molly mutter something , as if to herself , but the squire might have hear it if he have choose . as it be , he wisely turn the current of the conversation . 

 " look at that ! " he say , as they suddenly come upon the mere , or large pond . there be a small island in the middle of the glassy water , on which grow tall tree , dark Scotch fir in the centre , silvery shimmering willow close to the water 's edge . " we must get you punt over there , some of these day . I be not fond of use the boat at this time of the year , because the young bird be still in the nest among the reed and water - plant ; but we will go . there be coot and grebe . " 

 " oh , look , there be a swan ! " 

 " yes ; there be two pair of they here . and in those tree there be both a rookery and a heronry ; the heron ought to be here by now , for they be off to the sea in August , but I have   not see one yet . Stay ! be not   that one that fellow on a stone , with his long neck bend down , look into the water ? " 

 " yes ! I think so . I have never see a heron , only picture of they . " 

 " they and the rook be always at war , which do not   do for such near neighbour . if both heron leave the nest they be build , the rook come and tear it to piece ; and once Roger show I a long straggle fellow of a heron , with a flight of rook after he , with no friendly purpose in their mind , I will be bind . Roger know a deal of natural history , and find out queer thing sometimes . he would have be off a dozen time during this walk of ours , if he have be here : his eye be always wander about , and see twenty thing where I only see one . why ! I have   know he bolt into a copse because he see something fifteen yard off some plant , maybe , which he would tell I be very rare , though I should say I would see its marrow at every turn in the wood ; and , if we come upon such a thing as this , " touch a delicate film of a cobweb upon a leaf with his stick , as he speak , " why , he could tell you what insect or spider make it , and if it live in rotten fir - wood , or in a cranny of good sound timber , or deep down in the ground , or up in the sky , or anywhere . it be a pity they do not   take honour in Natural history at Cambridge . Roger would be safe enough if they do . " 

 " Mr. Osborne Hamley be very clever , be he not ? " molly ask , timidly . 

 " oh , yes . Osborne 's a bit of a genius . his mother look for great thing from Osborne . I be rather proud of he myself . he will get a Trinity fellowship , if they play he fair . as I be say at the magistrate ' meeting yesterday , ' I have   get a son who will make a noise at Cambridge , or I be very much mistaken . ' now , be not   it a queer quip of Nature , " continue the squire , turn his honest face towards Molly , as if he be go to impart a new idea to she , " that I , a Hamley of Hamley , straight in descent from nobody know where the Heptarchy , they say what be the date of the Heptarchy ? " 

 " I do not   know , " say Molly , startle at be thus appeal to . 

 " well ! it be some time before King Alfred , because he be the King of all England , you know ; but , as I be say , here be I , of as good and as old a descent as any man in England , and I doubt if a stranger , to look at I , would take I for a gentleman , with my red face , great hand and foot , and thick figure , fourteen stone , and never less than twelve even when I be a young man ; and there be Osborne , who take after his mother , who could not   tell her great - grandfather from Adam , bless she ; and Osborne have a girl 's delicate face , and a slight make , and hand and foot as small as a lady 's . he take after madam 's side , who , as I say , can not   tell who be her grandfather . now , Roger be like I , a Hamley of Hamley , and no one who see he in the street will ever think that red - brown , big - bone , clumsy chap be of gentle blood . yet all those cumnor people , you make such ado of in Hollingford , be mere muck of yesterday . I be talk to madam the other day about Osborne 's marry a daughter of Lord Hollingford 's that be to say , if he have a daughter he be only get boy , as it happen ; but I be not sure if I should consent to it . I really be not sure ; for you see Osborne will have have a first - rate education , and his family date from the Heptarchy , while I should be glad to know where the Cumnor folk be in the time of Queen Anne ? " he walk on , ponder the question of whether he could have give his consent to this impossible marriage ; and after some time , and when Molly have quite forget the subject to which he allude , he break out with " no ! I be sure I should have look high . so , perhaps , it be as well my Lord Hollingford have only boy . " 

 after a while , he thank molly for her companionship , with old - fashioned courtesy ; and tell she that he think , by this time , madam would be up and dress , and glad to have her young visitor with she . he point out the deep purple house , with its stone facing , as it be see at some distance between the tree , and watch she protectingly on her way along the field - path . 

 " that be a nice girl of Gibson 's , " quoth he to himself . " but what a tight hold the wench get of the notion of his marrying again ! one have need be on one 's guard as to what one say before she . to think of she never have thought of the chance of a stepmother . to be sure , a stepmother to a girl be a different thing to a second wife to a man ! " 

 

 

 CHAPTER VII . 

 FORESHADOWS of LOVE PERILS . 
 

 Illustrationf Squire Hamley have be unable to tell Molly who have ever be think of as her father 's second wife , fate be all this time prepare an answer of a pretty positive kind to her wonder curiosity . but fate be a cunning hussy , and build up her plan as imperceptibly as a bird build her nest ; and with much the same kind of unconsidered trifle . the first " trifle " of an event be the disturbance which Jenny   Mr. Gibson 's cook   choose to make at Bethia 's be dismiss . Bethia be a distant relation and protťgťe of Jenny 's , and she choose to say it be Mr. Coxe the tempter who ought to have " be send packing , " not Bethia the tempt , the victim . in this view there be quite enough plausibility to make Mr. Gibson feel that he have be rather unjust . he have , however , take care to provide Bethia with another situation , to the full as good as that which she hold in his family . Jenny , nevertheless , choose to give warning ; and though Mr. Gibson know full well from former experience that her warning be word , not deed , he hat the discomfort , the uncertainty , the entire disagreeableness of meet a woman at any time in his house , who wear a grievance and an injury upon her face as legibly as Jenny take care to do . 

 down into the middle of this small domestic trouble come another , and one of great consequence . Miss Eyre have go with her old mother , and her orphan nephew and niece , to the sea - side , during Molly 's absence , which be only intend at first to last for a fortnight . after about ten day of this time have elapse , Mr. Gibson receive a beautifully write , beautifully word , admirably fold , and most neatly seal letter from Miss Eyre . her eld nephew have fall ill of scarlet fever , and there be every probability that the young child would be attack by the same complaint . it be distress enough for poor Miss Eyre this additional expense , this anxiety the long detention from home which the illness involve . but she say not a word of any inconvenience to herself ; she only apologize with humble sincerity for her inability to return at the appoint time to her charge in Mr. Gibson 's family ; meekly adding , that perhaps it be as well , for Molly have never have the scarlet fever , and even if Miss Eyre have be able to leave the orphan child to return to her employment , it might not have be a safe or a prudent step . 

 " to be sure not , " say Mr. Gibson , tear the letter in two , and throw it into the hearth , where he soon see it burn to ash . " I wish I have a five - pound house and not a woman within ten mile of I . I might have some peace then . " apparently , he forget Mr. Coxe 's power of make mischief ; but indeed he might have trace that evil back to the unconscious Molly . the martyr - cook 's entrance to take away the breakfast thing , which she announce by a heavy sigh , rouse Mr. Gibson from thought to action . 

 " molly must stay a little long at Hamley , " he resolve . " they have   often ask for she , and now they will have enough of she , I think . but I can not   have she back here just yet ; and so the good I can do for she be to leave she where she be . Mrs. Hamley seem very fond of she , and the child be look happy , and strong in health . I will ride round by Hamley to - day at any rate , and see how the land lie . " 

 he find Mrs. Hamley lie on a sofa place under the shadow of the great cedar - tree on the lawn . molly be flit about she , garden away under her direction ; tie up the long sea - green stalk of bright bud carnation , snip off dead rose . 

 " oh ! here 's papa ! " she cry out , joyfully , as he ride up to the white pal which separate the trim lawn and trimmer flower - garden from the rough park - like ground in front of the house . 

 " come in come here through the drawing - room window , " say Mrs. Hamley , raise herself on her elbow . " we have   get a rose - tree to show you that molly have bud all by herself . we be both so proud of it . " 

 so Mr. Gibson ride round to the stable , leave his horse there , and make his way through the house to the open - air summer - parlour under the cedar - tree , where there be chair , table , book , and tangle work . somehow , he rather dislike ask for Molly to prolong her visit ; so he determine to swallow his bitter first , and then take the pleasure of the delicious day , the sweet repose , the murmurous , scented air . molly stand by he , her hand on his shoulder . he sate opposite to Mrs. Hamley . 

 " I have   come here to - day to ask a favour , " he begin . 

 " grant before you name it . be not I a bold woman ? " 

 he smile and bow , but go straight on with his speech . 

 " Miss Eyre , who have be molly 's governess , I suppose I must call she for many year , write to - day to say that one of the little nephew she take with she to Newport while molly be stay here , have catch the scarlet fever . " 

 " I guess your request . I make it before you do . I beg for dear little Molly to stay on here . of course Miss Eyre can not   come back to you ; and of course molly must stay here ! " 

 " thank you ; thank you very much . that be my request . " 

 Molly 's hand steal down to his , and nestle in that firm compact grasp . 

 " Papa ! Mrs. Hamley ! I know you will both understand I but may not   I go home ? I be very happy here ; but oh papa ! I think I should like to be at home with you well . " 

 an uncomfortable suspicion flash across his mind . he pull her round , and look straight and piercingly into her innocent face . her colour come at his unwonted scrutiny , but her sweet eye be fill with wonder , rather than with any feeling which he dread to find . for an instant he have doubt whether young red - head Mr. Coxe 's love might not have call out a response in his daughter 's breast ; but he be quite clear now . 

 " molly , you be rude to begin with . I do not   know how you be to make your peace with Mrs. Hamley , I be sure . and in the next place , do you think you be wise than I be ; or that I do not   want you at home , if all other thing be conformable ? stay where you be , and be thankful . " 

 molly know he well enough to be certain that the prolongation of her visit at Hamley be quite a decide affair in his mind ; and then she be smite with a sense of ingratitude . she leave her father , and go to Mrs. Hamley , and bend over she and kiss she ; but she do not speak . Mrs. Hamley take hold of her hand , and make room on the sofa for she .

 " I wish he be whip , " say Mr. Gibson . 

 " have give we such a capital reason , I shall keep Molly for a real long visitation . you must come over and see we very often . there be a room here for you always , you know ; and I do not   see why you should not start on your round from Hamley every morning , just as well as from Hollingford . " 

 " thank you . if you have not   be so kind to my little girl , I might be tempt to say something rude in answer to your last speech . " 

 " pray say it . you will not   be easy till you have give it out , I know . " 

 " Mrs. Hamley have find out from whom I get my rudeness , " say Molly , triumphantly . " it be an hereditary quality . " 

 " I be go to say that proposal of yours that I should sleep at Hamley be just like a woman 's idea all kindness , and no common sense . how in the world would my patient find I out , seven mile from my accustomed place ? they would be sure to send for some other doctor , and I should be ruin in a month . " 

 " could not   they send on here ? a messenger cost very little . " 

 " fancy old Goody Henbury struggle up to my surgery , groan at every step , and then be tell to just step on seven mile far ! or take the other end of society : I do not   think my Lady Cumnor 's smart groom would thank I for have to ride on to Hamley every time his mistress want I . " 

 " well , well , I submit . I be a woman . molly , thou art a woman ! go and order some strawberry and cream for this father of your . such humble office fall within the province of woman . strawberry and cream be all kindness and no common sense , for they will give he a horrid fit of indigestion . " 

 " please speak for yourself , Mrs. Hamley , " say Molly , merrily . " I eat oh , such a great basketful yesterday , and the squire go himself to the dairy and bring out a great bowl of cream , when he find I at my busy work . and I be as well as ever I be , to - day , and never have a touch of indigestion near I . " 

 " she be a good girl , " say her father , when she have dance out of hearing . the word be not quite an inquiry , he be so certain of his answer . there be a mixture of tenderness and trust in his eye , as he await the reply , which come in a moment .

I be so delighted to think she be not   to go away for a long time . the first thing I think of this morning when I waken up , be that she would soon have to return to you , unless I could persuade you into leave she with I a little long . and now she must stay oh , two month at least . " 

 it be quite true that the squire have become very fond of Molly . the charm of have a young girl dance and sing inarticulate ditty about the house and garden , be indescribable in its novelty to he . and then Molly be so willing and so wise ; ready both to talk and to listen at the right time . Mrs. Hamley be quite right in speak of her husband 's fondness for Molly . but either she herself choose a wrong time for tell he of the prolongation of the girl 's visit , or one of the fit of temper to which he be liable , but which he generally strove to check in the presence of his wife , be upon he ; at any rate , he receive the news in anything but a gracious frame of mind . 

 " stay long ! do Gibson ask for it ? " 

 " yes ! I do not   see what else be to become of she ; Miss Eyre away and all . it be a very awkward position for a motherless girl like she to be at the head of a household with two young man in it . " 

 " that be Gibson 's look - out ; he should have think of it before take pupil , or apprentice , or whatever he call they . " 

 " my dear squire ! why , I think you would be as glad as I be as I be to keep molly . I ask she to stay for an indefinite time ; two month at least . " 

 " and to be in the house with Osborne ! Roger , too , will be at home . " 

 by the cloud in the squire 's eye , Mrs. Hamley read his mind . 

 " oh , she be not at all the sort of girl young man of their age would take to . we like she because we see what she really be ; but lad of one or two and twenty want all the accessory of a young woman . " 

 " want what ? " growl the squire . 

 " such thing as become dress , style of manner . they would not at their age even see that she be pretty ; their idea of beauty would include colour . " 

 " I suppose all that be very clever ; but I do not   understand it . all I know be , that it be a very dangerous thing to shut two young man of one and three and twenty up in a country - house like this with a girl of seventeen choose what her gown may be like , or her hair , or her eye . and I tell you particularly I do not   want Osborne , or either of they , indeed , to be fall in love with she . I be very much annoyed . " 

 Mrs. Hamley 's face fall ; she become a little pale . 

 " shall we make arrangement for their stop away while she be here ; stay up at Cambridge , or read with some one ? go abroad for a month or two ? " 

 " no ; you have   be reckon this ever so long on their come home . I have   see the mark of the week on your almanack . I would soon speak to Gibson , and tell he he must take his daughter away , for it be not convenient to we "

 " and Roger be always far too full of his natural history and comparative anatomy , and mess of that sort , to be think of fall in love with Venus herself . he have not the sentiment and imagination of Osborne . " 

 " ah , you do not   know ; you never can be sure about a young man ! but with Roger it would not   so much signify . he would know he could not   marry for year to come . " 

 all that afternoon the Squire try to steer clear of Molly , to whom he feel himself to have be an inhospitable traitor . but she be so perfectly unconscious of his shyness of she , and so merry and sweet in her behaviour as a welcome guest , never distrust he for a moment , however gruff he might be , that by the next morning she have completely win he round , and they be quite on the old term again . at breakfast this very morning , a letter be pass from the squire to his wife , and back again , without a word as to its content ; but 

 " Fortunate ! " 

 " yes ! very ! " 

 Little do molly apply these expression to the piece of news Mrs. Hamley tell she in the course of the day ; namely , that her son Osborne have receive an invitation to stay with a friend in the neighbourhood of Cambridge , and perhaps to make a tour on the Continent with he subsequently ; and that , consequently , he would not accompany his brother when Roger come home . 

 molly be very sympathetic . 

 " oh , dear ! I be so sorry ! " 

 Mrs. Hamley be thankful her husband be not present , Molly speak the word so heartily . 

 " you have be think so long of his come home . I be afraid it be a great disappointment . " 

 Mrs. Hamley smile relieve . 

 " yes ! it be a disappointment certainly , but we must think of Osborne 's pleasure . and with his poetical mind , he will write we such delightful travel letter . poor fellow ! he must be go into the examination to - day ! both his father and I feel sure , though , that he will be a high wrangler . only I should like to have see he , my own dear boy . but it be good as it be . " 

 molly be a little puzzled by this speech , but soon put it out of her head . it be a disappointment to she , too , that she should not see this beautiful , brilliant young man , his mother 's hero . from time to time her maiden fancy have dwell upon what he would be like ; how the lovely boy of the picture in Mrs. Hamley 's dressing - room would have change in the ten year that have elapse since the likeness be take ; if he would read poetry aloud ; if he would even read his own poetry . however , in the never - end feminine business of the day , she soon forget her own disappointment ; it only come back to she on first waken the next morning , as a vague something that be not quite so pleasant as she have anticipate , and then be banish as a subject of regret . her day at Hamley be well fill up with the small duty that would have belong to a daughter of the house have there be one . she make breakfast for the lonely squire , and would willingly have carry up madam 's , but that daily piece of work belong to the squire , and be jealously guard by he . she read the small print of the newspaper aloud to he , city article , money and corn market include . she stroll about the garden with he , gather fresh flower , meanwhile , to deck the drawing - room against Mrs. Hamley should come down . she be her companion when she take her drive in the close carriage ; they read poetry and mild literature together in Mrs. Hamley 's sit - room upstairs . she be quite clever at cribbage now , and could beat the squire if she take pain . besides these thing , there be her own independent way of employ herself . she use to try to practise an hour daily on the old grand piano in the solitary drawing - room , because she have promise Miss Eyre she would do so . and she have find her way into the library , and use to undo the heavy bar of the shutter if the housemaid have forget this duty , and mount the ladder , sit on the step , for an hour at a time , deep in some book of the old english classic . the summer day be very short to this happy girl of seventeen . 

 

 

 CHAPTER VIII . 

 drift into DANGER . 
 

 on Thursday , the quiet country household be stir through all its fibre with the thought of Roger 's come home . Mrs. Hamley have not seem quite so well , or quite in such good spirit for two or three day before ; and the squire himself have appear to be put out without any visible cause . they have not choose to tell Molly that Osborne 's name have only appear very low down in the mathematical tripos . so all that their visitor know be that something be out of tune , and she hop that Roger 's come home would set it to right , for it be beyond the power of her small care and wile . 

 on Thursday , the housemaid apologize to she for some slight negligence in her bedroom , by say she have be busy scour Mr. Roger 's room . " not but what they be as clean as could be beforehand ; but mistress would always have the young gentleman 's room clean afresh before they come home . if it have be Mr. Osborne , the whole house would have have to be do ; but , to be sure , he be the eld son , so it be but likely . " Molly be amuse at this testimony to the right of heirship ; but somehow she herself have fall into the family manner of think that nothing be too great or too good for " the eld son . " in his father 's eye , Osborne be the representative of the ancient house of Hamley of Hamley , the future owner of the land which have be theirs for a thousand year . his mother clung to he because they two be cast in the same mould , both physically and mentally because he bear her maiden name . she have indoctrinate molly with her faith , and , in spite of her amusement at the housemaid 's speech , the girl visitor would have be as anxious as any one to show her feudal loyalty to the heir , if indeed it have be he that be come . after luncheon , Mrs. Hamley go to rest , in preparation for Roger 's return ; and Molly also retire to her own room , feel that it would be well for she to remain there until dinner - time , and so to leave the father and mother to receive their boy in privacy . she take a book of MS . poem with she ; they be all of Osborne Hamley 's composition ; and his mother have read some of they aloud to her young visitor more than once . molly have ask permission to copy one or two of those which be her great favourite ; and this quiet summer afternoon she take this copying for her employment , sit at the pleasant open window , and lose herself in dreamy out - look into the garden and wood , quiver in the noon - tide heat . the house be so still , in its silence it might have be the " moate grange ; " the booming buzz of the blue fly , in the great staircase window , seem the loud noise in - door . and there be scarcely a sound out - of - door but the humming of bee , in the flower - bed below the window . distant voice from the far - away field where they be make hay the scent of which come in sudden waft distinct from that of the nearer rose and honeysuckle these merry piping voice just make molly feel the depth of the present silence . she have leave off copying , her hand weary with the unusual exertion of so much writing , and she be lazily try to learn one or two of the poem off by heart . 

 I ask of the wind , but answer make it none , 
 save its accustomed sad and solitary moan 
 she keep say to herself , lose her sense of whatever mean the word have ever have , in the repetition which have become mechanical . suddenly there be the snap of a shutting gate ; wheel crackling on the dry gravel , horse ' foot on the drive ; a loud cheerful voice in the house , come up through the open window , the hall , the passage , the staircase , with unwonted fulness and roundness of tone . the entrance - hall downstair be pave with diamond of black and white marble ; the low wide staircase that go in short flight around the hall , till you could look down upon the marble floor from the top story of the house , be uncarpeted uncover . the squire be too proud of his beautifully - joined oaken floor to cover this stair - case up unnecessarily ; not to say a word of the usual state of want of ready money to expend upon the decoration of his house . so , through the undraperied hollow square of the hall and staircase every sound ascend clear and distinct ; and molly hear the squire 's glad " Hallo ! here he be , " and madam 's soft , more plaintive voice ; and then the loud , full , strange tone , which she know must be Roger 's . then there be an opening and shutting of door , and only a distant buzz of talk . molly begin again 

 I ask of the wind , but answer make it none . 
 and this time she have nearly finish learn the poem , when she hear Mrs. Hamley come hastily into her sitting - room that adjoin Molly 's bedroom , and burst out into an irrepressible half - hysterical fit of sobbing . molly be too young to have any complication of motif which should prevent she go at once to try and give what comfort she could . in an instant she be kneel at Mrs. Hamley 's foot , hold the poor lady 's hand , kiss they , murmur soft word ; which , all unmeaning as they be of aught but sympathy with the untold grief , do Mrs. Hamley good . she check herself , smile sadly at Molly through the midst of her thick - come sob . 

 " it be only Osborne , " say she , at last . " Roger have be tell we about he . " 

 " what about he ? " ask Molly , eagerly . 

 " I know on Monday ; we have a letter he say he have not do so well as we have hop as he have hop himself , poor fellow ! he say he have just pass , but be only low down among the junior optime , and not where he have expect , and have lead we to expect . but the squire have never be at college , and do not understand college term , and he have be ask Roger all about it , and Roger have be tell he , and it have make he so angry . but the squire hat college slang ; he have never be there , you know ; and he think poor Osborne be take it too lightly , and he have be ask Roger about it , and Roger " 

 there be a fresh fit of the sobbing cry . molly burst out , " I do not   think Mr. Roger should have tell ; he have no need to begin so soon about his brother 's failure . why , he have not   be in the house an hour ! " 

 " hush , hush , love ! " say Mrs. Hamley . " Roger be so good . you do not   understand . the squire would begin and ask question before Roger have taste food as soon as ever we have get into the dining - room . and all he say to I , at any rate be that Osborne be nervous , and that if he could only have go in for the Chancellor 's medal , he would have carry all before he . but Roger say that after fail like this , he be not very likely to get a fellowship , which the Squire have place his hope on . Osborne himself seem so sure of it , that the squire can not   understand it , and be seriously angry , and grow more so the more he talk about it . he have keep it in two or three day , and that never suit he . he be always well when he be angry about a thing at once , and do not   let it smoulder in his mind . poor , poor Osborne ! I do wish he have be come straight home , instead of go to these friend of his ; I think I could have comfort he . but now I be glad , for it will be well to let his father 's anger cool first . " 

 so talk out what be in her heart , Mrs. Hamley become more composed ; and at length she dismiss Molly to dress for dinner , with a kiss , say , 

 " you be a real blessing to mother , child ! you give one such pleasant sympathy , both in one 's gladness and in one 's sorrow ; in one 's pride   for I be so proud last week , so confident , and in one 's disappointment . and now your be a fourth at dinner will keep we off that sore subject ; there be time when a stranger in the household be a wonderful help . " 

 molly think over all that she have hear , as she be dress and put on the terrible , over - smart plaid gown in honour of the new arrival . her unconscious fealty to Osborne be not in the least shake by his have come to grief at Cambridge . only she be indignant with or without reason against Roger , who seem to have bring the reality of bad news as an offering of first - fruit on his return home .

 he be a tall powerfully - make young man , give the impression of strength more than elegance . his face be rather square , ruddy - colour   as his father have say , hair and eye brown the latter rather deep - set beneath his thick eyebrow ; and he have a trick of wrinkle up his eyelid when he want particularly to observe anything , which make his eye look even small still at such time . he have a large mouth , with excessively mobile lip ; and another trick of his be , that when he be amuse at anything , he resist the impulse to laugh , by a droll manner of twitch and pucker up his mouth , till at length the sense of humour have its way , and his feature relax , and he break into a broad sunny smile ; his beautiful tooth his only beautiful feature break out with a white gleam upon the red - brown countenance . these two trick of his of crumple up the eyelid , so as to concentrate the power of sight , which make he look stern and thoughtful ; and the odd twitching of the lip that be preliminary to a smile , which make he look intensely merry give the vary expression of his face a great range " from grave to gay , from lively to severe , " than be common with most man . to Molly , who be not finely discriminative in her glance at the stranger this first night , he simply appear " heavy - look , clumsy , " and " a person she be sure she should never get on with . " he certainly do not seem to care much what impression he make upon his mother 's visitor . he be at that age when young man admire a form beauty more than a face with any amount of future capability of loveliness , and when they be morbidly conscious of the difficulty of find subject of conversation in talk to girl in a state of feminine hobbledehoyhood . besides , his thought be full of other subject , which he do not intend to allow to ooze out in word , yet he want to prevent any of that heavy silence which he fear might be impend with an angry and displeased father , and a timorous and distressed mother . he only look upon molly as a badly - dress , and rather awkward girl , with black hair and an intelligent face , who might help he in the task he have set himself of keep up a bright general conversation during the rest of the evening ; might help he if she would , but she would not . she think he unfeele in his talkativeness ; his constant flow of word upon indifferent subject be a wonder and a repulsion to she . how could he go on so cheerfully while his mother sit there , scarcely eat anything , and do she good , with ill - success , to swallow down the tear that would keep rise to her eye ; when his father 's heavy brow be deeply clouded , and he evidently care nothing at first at least for all the chatter his son pour forth ? have Mr. Roger Hamley no sympathy in he ? she would show that she have some , at any rate . so she quite decline the part , which he have hop she would have take , of respondent , and possible questioner ; and his work become more and more like that of a man walk in a quagmire . once the Squire rouse himself to speak to the butler ; he feel the need of outward stimulus of a well vintage than usual . 

 " bring up a bottle of the Burgundy with the yellow seal . " 

 he speak low ; he have no spirit to speak in his usual voice . the butler answer in the same tone . molly sit near they , and silent herself , hear what they say . 

 " if you please , sir , there be not above six bottle of that seal leave ; and it be Mr. Osborne 's favourite wine . " 

 the Squire turn round with a growl in his voice . 

 " bring up a bottle of the Burgundy with the yellow seal , as I say . " 

 the butler go away wonder . " Mr. Osborne 's " like and dislike have be the law of the house in general until now . if he have like any particular food or drink , any seat or place , any special degree of warmth or coolness , his wish be to be attend to ; for he be the heir , and he be delicate , and he be the clever one of the family . all the out - of - door man would have say the same . Mr. Osborne wish a tree cut down , or keep stand , or have such - and - such a fancy about the game , or desire something unusual about the horse ; and they have all to attend to it as if it be law . but to - day the Burgundy with the yellow seal be to be bring ; and it be bring . molly testify with quiet vehemence of action ; she never take wine , so she need not have be afraid of the man be pour it into her glass ; but as an open mark of fealty to the absent Osborne , however little it might be understand , she place the palm of her small brown hand over the top of the glass , and hold it there , till the wine have go round , and Roger and his father be in full enjoyment of it . 

 after dinner , too , the gentleman linger long over their dessert , and Molly hear they laugh ; and then she see they loiter about in the twilight out - of - door ; Roger hatless , his hand in his pocket , lounge by his father 's side , who be now able to talk in his usual loud and cheerful way , forget Osborne .  and so in mute opposition on Molly 's side , in polite indifference , scarcely verge upon kindliness on his , Roger and she steer clear of each other . he have many occupation in which he need no companionship , even if she have be qualified to give it . the bad be , that she find he be in the habit of occupy the library , her favourite retreat , in the morning before Mrs. Hamley come down . she open the half - close door a day or two after his return home , and find he busy among book and paper , with which the large leather - cover table be strew ; and she softly withdraw before he could turn his head and see she , so as to distinguish she from one of the housemaid . he ride out every day , sometimes with his father about the outlying field , sometimes far away for a good gallop . molly would have enjoy accompany he on these occasion , for she be very fond of riding ; and there have be some talk of send for her habit and grey pony when first she come to Hamley ; only the squire , after some consideration , have say he so rarely do more than go slowly from one field to another , where his labourer be at work , that he fear she would find such slow work ten minute rid through heavy land , twenty minute sit still on horseback , listen to the direction he should have to give to his man rather dull . now , when if she have have her pony here she might have ride out with Roger , without give he any trouble she would have take care of that nobody seem to think of renew the proposal . 

 altogether it be pleasanter before he come home . 

 her father ride over pretty frequently ; sometimes there be long unaccountable absence , it be true ; when his daughter begin to fidget after he , and to wonder what have become of he . but when he make his appearance he have always good reason to give ; and the right she feel that she have to his familiar household tenderness ; the power she possess of fully understand the exact value of both his word and his silence , make these glimpse of intercourse with he inexpressibly charming . Latterly her burden have always be , " when may I come home , papa ? " it be not that she be unhappy , or uncomfortable ; she be passionately fond of Mrs. Hamley , she be a favourite of the Squire 's , and could not as yet fully understand why some people be so much afraid of he ; and as for Roger , if he do not add to her pleasure , he scarcely take away from it . but she want to be at home once more . the reason why she could not tell ; but this she know full well . Mr. Gibson reason with she till she be weary of be completely convinced that it be right and necessary for she to stay where she be . and then with an effort she stop the cry upon her tongue , for she see that its repetition harass her father . 

 during this absence of hers Mr. Gibson be drift into matrimony . he be partly aware of whither he be go ; and partly it be like the soft float movement of a dream . he be more passive than active in the affair ; though , if his reason have not fully approve of the step he be tend to if he have not believe that a second marriage be the very good way of cut the gordian knot of domestic difficulty , he could have make an effort without any great trouble , and extricate himself without pain from the mesh of circumstance . it happen in this manner : Lady Cumnor have marry her two eld daughter , find her labour as a chaperone to Lady Harriet , the young , considerably lighten by co - operation ; and , at length , she have leisure to be an invalid . she be , however , too energetic to allow herself this indulgence constantly ; only she permit herself to break down occasionally after a long course of dinner , late hour , and London atmosphere : and then , leave Lady Harriet with either Lady Cuxhaven or Lady Agnes Manners , she betake herself to the comparative quiet of the Towers , where she find occupation in do her benevolence , which be sadly neglect in the hurly - burly of London . this particular summer she have break down early than usual , and long for the repose of the country . she believe that her state of health , too , be more serious than previously ; but she do not say a word of this to her husband or daughter ; reserve her confidence for Mr. Gibson 's ear . she do not wish to take Lady Harriet away from the gaiety of town which she be thoroughly enjoy , by any complaint of her , which might , after all , be ill - found ; and yet she do not quite like be without a companion in the three week or a month that might intervene before her family would join she at the tower , especially as the annual festivity to the school visitor be impending ; and both the school and the visit of the lady connect with it , have rather lose the zest of novelty . 

 " Thursday the 19th , Harriet , " say Lady Cumnor , meditatively ; " what do you say to come down to the tower on the 18th , and help I over that long day . you could stay in the country till Monday , and have a few day ' rest and good air ; you would return a great deal fresher to the remainder of your gaiety . your father would bring you down , I know : indeed , he be come naturally . " 

 " oh , mamma ! " say Lady Harriet , the young daughter of the house the prettiest , the most indulge ; " I can not go ; there be the water - party up to Maidenhead on the 20th , I should be so sorry to miss it : and Mrs. Duncan 's ball , and Grisi 's concert ; please , do not   want I . besides , I should do no good . I can not   make provincial small - talk ; I be not up in the local politic of Hollingford . I should be make mischief , I know I should . " 

 " very well , my dear , " say Lady Cumnor , sigh , " I have forget the Maidenhead water - party , or I would not have ask you . " 

 " what a pity it be not   the Eton holiday , so that you could have have Hollingford 's boy to help you to do the honours , mamma . they be such affable little prig . it be the great fun to watch they last year at Sir Edward 's , do the honour of their grandfather 's house to much such a collection of humble admirer as you get together at the Towers . I shall never forget see Edgar gravely squire about an old lady in a portentous black bonnet , and give her information in the correct grammar possible . "

"

"

 " I can not   think what you mean , Harriet , " say Lady Cumnor , a good deal annoy at this speech of her clever , heedless , young daughter . 

 " oh dear , mamma , you do everything you could think of for we ; but you see you would ever so many other engrossing interest , and Mary hardly ever allow her love for her husband to interfere with her all - absorb care for the child . you give we the good of master in every department , and Clare to dragonize and keep we up to our preparation for they , as well as ever she could ; but then you know , or rather you do not   know , some of the master admire our very pretty governess , and there be a kind of respectable veil flirtation go on , which never come to anything , to be sure ; and then you be often so overwhelmed with your business as a great lady fashionable and benevolent , and all that sort of thing that you use to call Clare away from we at the most critical time of our lesson , to write your note , or add up your account , and the consequence be , that I be about the most ill - inform girl in London . only Mary be so capitally train by good awkward Miss Benson , that she be always full to overflow with accurate knowledge , and her glory be reflect upon I . "

"

 " I sincerely hope Miss Bowes be not tell her love - affair to Grace or Lily . why , Harriet , you could not have be old than Grace when Clare be marry ! " say Lady Cuxhaven , in maternal alarm .

 this last speech refer to a recent refusal of Lady Harriet 's , which have displease Lady Cumnor , and rather annoy my lord ; as they , the parent , could see no objection to the gentleman in question . Lady Cuxhaven do not want to have the subject bring up , so she hasten to say , 

 " do ask the poor little daughter to come with her mother to the tower ; why , she must be seventeen or more ; she would really be a companion to you , mamma , if her mother be unable to come . "

 " and Hollingford be busy see after his new laboratory at the Towers , and be constantly backwards and forwards . and Agnes want to go there for change of air , as soon as she be strong enough after her confinement . and even my own dear insatiable ' I ' will have have enough of gaiety in two or three week , if this hot weather last . "

 so the plan in its present shape be tell to Lord Cumnor , who highly approve of it ; as he always do of every project of his wife 's . "

 " so it be ; you be always right , my dear . Preston 's a clever , sharp fellow . " 

 " I do not   like he , " say my lady .

"
