 " I tell you just now I be no match - maker , my lord . I suppose we have well go by the old road the people at those inn know we ? " 

 and so they pass on to speak about other thing than Mrs. Kirkpatrick and her prospect , scholastic or matrimonial . 

 

 

 CHAPTER IX . 

 the WIDOWER and the WIDOW . 
 

 Mrs. Kirkpatrick be only too happy to accept Lady Cumnor 's invitation . it be what she have be hop for , but hardly dare to expect , as she believe that the family be settle in London for some time to come . the tower be a pleasant and luxurious house in which to pass her holiday ; and though she be not one to make deep plan , or to look far ahead , she be quite aware of the prestige which her be able to say she have be stay with " dear Lady Cumnor " at the Towers , be likely to give she and her school in the eye of a good many people ; so she gladly prepare to join her ladyship on the 17th . her wardrobe do not require much arrangement ; if it have do , the poor lady would not have have much money to appropriate to the purpose . she be very pretty and graceful ; and that go a great way towards carry off shabby clothe ; and it be her taste more than any depth of feeling , that have make her persevere in wear all the delicate tint the violet and gray which , with a certain admixture of black , constitute half - mourning . this style of become dress she be suppose to wear in memory of Mr. Kirkpatrick ; in reality because it be both lady - like and economical . her beautiful hair be of that rich auburn that hardly ever turn gray ; and partly out of consciousness of its beauty , and partly because the washing of cap be expensive , she do not wear anything on her head ; her complexion have the vivid tint that often accompany the kind of hair which have once be red ; and the only injury her skin have receive from advance year be that the colouring be rather more brilliant than delicate , and vary less with every pass emotion . she could no long blush ; and at eighteen she have be very proud of her blush . her eye be soft , large , and china - blue in colour ; they have not much expression or shadow about they , which be perhaps owe to the flaxen colour of her eyelash . her figure be a little full than it use to be , but her movement be as soft and sinuous as ever . altogether , she look much young than her age , which be not far short of forty . she have a very pleasant voice , and read aloud well and distinctly , which Lady Cumnor like . indeed , for some inexplicable reason , she be a great , more positive favourite with Lady Cumnor than with any of the rest of the family , though they all like she up to a certain point , and find it agreeably useful to have any one in the house who be so well acquainted with their way and habit ; so ready to talk , when a little trickle of conversation be require ; so willing to listen , and to listen with tolerable intelligence , if the subject speak about do not refer to serious solid literature , or science , or politic , or social economy . about novel and poetry , travel and gossip , personal detail , or anecdote of any kind , she always make exactly the remark which be expect from an agreeable listener ; and she have sense enough to confine herself to those short expression of wonder , admiration , and astonishment , which may mean anything , when more recondite thing be talk about . 

 it be a very pleasant change to a poor unsuccessful schoolmistress to leave her own house , full of battered and shabby furniture   she have take the good - will and furniture of her predecessor at a valuation , two or three year before , where the look - out be as gloomy , and the surround as squalid , as be often the case in the small street of a country town , and to come bowl through the Towers Park in the luxurious carriage send to meet she ; to alight , and feel secure that the well - train servant would see after her bag , and umbrella , and parasol , and cloak , without her load herself with all these portable article , as she have have to do while follow the wheelbarrow contain her luggage in go to the Ashcombe coach - office that morning ; to pass up the deep - pile carpet of the broad shallow stair into my lady 's own room , cool and deliciously fresh , even on this sultry day , and fragrant with great bowl of freshly gather rose of every shade of colour . there be two or three new novel lie uncut on the table ; the daily paper , the magazine . every chair be an easy - chair of some kind or other ; and all cover with french chintz that mimic the real flower in the garden below . she be familiar with the bedroom call her , to which she be soon usher by Lady Cumnor 's maid . it seem to she far more like home than the dingy place she have leave that morning ; it be so natural to she to like dainty drapery , and harmonious colouring , and fine linen , and soft raiment . she sate down in the arm - chair by the bed - side , and wonder over her fate something in this fashion 

 " one would think it be an easy enough thing to deck a look - glass like that with muslin and pink ribbon ; and yet how hard it be to keep it up ! People do not   know how hard it be till they have   try as I have . I make my own glass just as pretty when I first go to Ashcombe ; but the muslin get dirty , and the pink ribbon fade , and it be so difficult to earn money to renew they ; and when one have get the money one have not   the heart to spend it all at once . one think and one think how one can get the most good out of it ; and a new gown , or a day 's pleasure , or some hot - house fruit , or some piece of elegance that can be see and notice in one 's drawing - room , carry the day , and good - by to prettily deck look - glass . now here , money be like the air they breathe . no one even ask or know how much the washing cost , or what pink ribbon be a yard . ah ! it would be different if they have to earn every penny as I have ! they would have to calculate , like I , how to get the most pleasure out of it . I wonder if I be to go on all my life toil and moil for money ? it be not natural . marriage be the natural thing ; then the husband have all that kind of dirty work to do , and his wife sit in the drawing - room like a lady . I do , when poor Kirkpatrick be alive . Heigho ! it be a sad thing to be a widow . " 

 then there be the contrast between the dinner which she have to share with her scholar at Ashcombe round of beef , leg of mutton , great dish of potato , and large batter - pudding , with the tiny meal of exquisitely cook delicacy , send up on old Chelsea china , that be serve every day to the earl and countess and herself at the tower . she dread the end of her holiday as much as the most home - love of her pupil . but at this time that end be some week off , so Clare shut her eye to the future , and try to relish the present to its full extent . a disturbance to the pleasant , even course of the summer day come in the indisposition of Lady Cumnor . her husband have go back to London , and she and Mrs. Kirkpatrick have be leave to the very even tenor of life , which be accord to my lady 's wish just now . in spite of her languor and fatigue , she have go through the day when the school visitor come to the Towers , in full dignity , dictate clearly all that be to be do , what walk be to be take , what hothouse to be see , and when the party be to return to the " collation . " she herself remain indoor , with one or two lady who have venture to think that the fatigue or the heat might be too much for they , and who have therefore decline accompany the lady in charge of Mrs. Kirkpatrick , or those other favour few to whom Lord Cumnor be explain the new building in his farm - yard . " with the utmost condescension , " as her hearer afterwards express it , Lady Cumnor tell they all about her married daughter ' establishment , nursery , plan for the education of their child , and manner of pass the day . but the exertion tire she ; and when every one have leave , the probability be that she would have go to lie down and rest , have not her husband make an unlucky remark in the kindness of his heart . he come up to she and put his hand on her shoulder . 

 " I be afraid you be sadly tired , my lady ? " he say . 

 she brace her muscle , and draw herself up , say coldly , 

 " when I be tired , Lord Cumnor , I will tell you so . " and her fatigue show itself during the rest of the evening in she sit particularly upright , and decline all offer of easy - chair or foot - stool , and refuse the insult of a suggestion that they should all go to bed early . she go on in something of this kind of manner as long as Lord Cumnor remain at the Towers . Mrs. Kirkpatrick be quite deceive by it , and keep assure Lord Cumnor that she have never see dear Lady Cumnor look well , or so strong . but he have an affectionate heart , if a blunder head ; and though he could give no reason for his belief , he be almost certain his wife be not well . yet he be too much afraid of she to send for Mr. Gibson without her permission . his last word to Clare be 

 " it be such a comfort to leave my lady to you ; only do not   you be delude by her way . she will not show she be ill till she can not   help it . Consult with Bradley "   Lady Cumnor 's " own woman , " she dislike the new - fangledness of " lady's - maid " ; " and if I be you , I would send and ask Gibson to call you might make any kind of a pretence , " and then the idea he have have in London of the fitness of a match between the two come into his head just now , he could not help add , " get he to come and see you , he be a very agreeable man ; Lord Hollingford say there be no one like he in these part : and he might be look at my lady while he be talk to you , and see if he think she really ill . and let I know what he say about she . " 

 but Clare be just as great a coward about do anything for Lady Cumnor which she have not expressly order , as Lord Cumnor himself . she know she might fall into such disgrace if she send for Mr. Gibson without direct permission , that she might never be ask to stay at the tower again ; and the life there , monotonous in its smoothness of luxury as it might be to some , be exactly to her taste . she in her turn try to put upon Bradley the duty which Lord Cumnor have put upon she . 

 " Mrs. Bradley , " she say one day , " be you quite comfortable about my lady 's health ? Lord Cumnor fancy that she be look wear and ill ? " 

 " indeed , Mrs. Kirkpatrick , I do not   think my lady be herself . I can not   persuade myself as she be , though if you be to question I till night I could not   tell you why . " 

 " do not   you think you could make some errand to Hollingford , and see Mr. Gibson , and ask he to come round this way some day , and make a call on Lady Cumnor ? " 

 " it would be as much as my place be worth , Mrs. Kirkpatrick . till my lady 's die day , if Providence keep she in her sens , she will have everything do her own way , or not at all . there be only Lady Harriet that can manage she the least , and she not always . " 

 " well , then we must hope that there be nothing the matter with she ; and I daresay there be not . she say there be not , and she ought to know good herself . " 

 but a day or two after this conversation take place , Lady Cumnor startle Mrs. Kirkpatrick , by say suddenly , " Clare , I wish you would write a note to Mr. Gibson , say I should like to see he this afternoon . I think he would have call of himself before now . he ought to have do so , to pay his respect . " 

 Mr. Gibson have be far too busy in his profession to have time for mere visit of ceremony , though he know quite well he be neglect what be expect of he . but the district of which he may be say to have have medical charge be full of a bad kind of low fever , which take up all his time and thought , and often make he very thankful that molly be out of the way in the quiet shade of Hamley . 

 his domestic " row " have not heal over in the least , though he be oblige to put the perplexity on one side for the time . the last drop the final straw , have be an impromptu visit of Lord Hollingford 's , whom he have meet in the town one forenoon . they have have a good deal to say to each other about some new scientific discovery , with the detail of which Lord Hollingford be well acquainted , while Mr. Gibson be ignorant and deeply interested . at length Lord Hollingford say suddenly , 

 " Gibson , I wonder if you would give I some lunch ; I have   be a good deal about since my seven - o'clock breakfast , and be get quite ravenous . " 

 now Mr. Gibson be only too much pleased to show hospitality to one whom he like and respect so much as Lord Hollingford , and he gladly take he home with he to the early family dinner . but it be just at the time when the cook be sulk at Bethia 's dismissal and she choose to be unpunctual and careless . there be no successor to Bethia as yet appoint to wait at the meal . so , though Mr. Gibson know well that bread - and - cheese , cold beef , or the simple food available , would have be welcome to the hungry lord , he could not get either these thing for luncheon , or even the family dinner , at anything like the proper time , in spite of all his ringing , and as much anger as he like to show , for fear of make Lord Hollingford uncomfortable . at last dinner be ready , but the poor host see the want of nicety almost the want of cleanliness , in all its accompaniment dingy plate , dull - look glass , a table - cloth that , if not absolutely dirty , be anything but fresh in its splashed and rumpled condition , and compare it in his own mind with the dainty delicacy with which even a loaf of brown bread be serve up at his guest 's home . he do not apologize directly , but , after dinner , just as they be part , he say , " you see a man like I a widower with a daughter who can not always be at home have not the regulated household which would enable I to command the small portion of time I can spend there . " 

 he make no allusion to the comfortless meal of which they have both partake , though it be full in his mind . nor be it absent from Lord Hollingford 's as he make reply , 

 " true , true . yet a man like you ought to be free from any thought of household care . you ought to have somebody . how old be Miss Gibson ? " 

 " Seventeen . it be a very awkward age for a motherless girl . " 

 " yes ; very . I have only boy , but it must be very awkward with a girl . excuse I , Gibson , but we be talk like friend . have you never think of marry again ? it would not   be like a first marriage , of course ; but if you find a sensible , agreeable woman of thirty or so , I really think you could not   do well than take she to manage your home , and so save you either discomfort or worry ; and , besides , she would be able to give your daughter that kind of tender supervision which , I fancy , all girl of that age require . it be a delicate subject , but you will excuse my have speak frankly . " 

 Mr. Gibson have think of this advice several time since it be give ; but it be a case of " first catch your hare . " where be the ' sensible and agreeable woman of thirty or so ? " not Miss Browning , nor Miss Phobe , nor Miss Goodenough . among his country patient there be two class pretty distinctly mark : farmer , whose child be unrefined and uneducated ; squire , whose daughter would , indeed , think the world be come to a pretty pass , if they be to marry a country surgeon . 

 but the first day on which Mr. Gibson pay his visit to Lady Cumnor , he begin to think it possible that Mrs. Kirkpatrick be his " hare . " he ride away with slack rein , think over what he know of she , more than about the prescription he should write , or the way he be go . he remember she as a very pretty Miss Clare : the governess who have the scarlet fever ; that be in his wife 's day , a long time ago ; he could hardly understand Mrs. Kirkpatrick 's youthfulness of appearance when he think how long . then he have hear of her marriage to a curate ; and the next day   or so it seem , he could not recollect the exact duration of the interval , of his death . he know , in some way , that she have be live ever since as a governess in different family ; but that she have always be a great favourite with the family at the Towers , for whom , quite independent of their rank , he have a true respect . a year or two ago he have hear that she have take the good - will of a school at Ashcombe ; a small town close to another property of Lord Cumnor 's , in the same county . ashcombe be a large estate than that near Hollingford , but the old Manor - house there be not nearly so good a residence as the Towers ; so it be give up to Mr. Preston , the land - agent for the Ashcombe property , just as Mr. Sheepshanks be for that at Hollingford . there be a few room at the Manor - house reserve for the occasional visit of the family , otherwise Mr. Preston , a handsome young bachelor , have it all to himself . Mr. Gibson know that Mrs. Kirkpatrick have one child , a daughter , who must be much about the same age as Molly . of course she have very little , if any , property . but he himself have live carefully , and have a few thousand well invest ; besides which , his professional income be good , and increase rather than diminish every year . by the time he have arrive at this point in his consideration of the case , he be at the house of the next patient on his round , and he put away all thought of matrimony and Mrs. Kirkpatrick for the time . once again , in the course of the day , he remember with a certain pleasure that molly have tell he some little detail connect with her unlucky detention at the Towers five or six year ago , which have make he feel at the time as if Mrs. Kirkpatrick have behave very kindly to his little girl . so there the matter rest for the present , as far as he be concern . 

 Lady Cumnor be out of health ; but not so ill as she have be fancy herself during all those day when the people about she dare not send for the doctor . it be a great relief to she to have Mr. Gibson to decide for she what she be to do ; what to eat , drink , avoid . such decision ab extra , be sometimes a wonderful relief to those whose habit it have be to decide , not only for themselves , but for every one else ; and occasionally the relaxation of the strain which a character for infallible wisdom bring with it , do much to restore health . Mrs. Kirkpatrick think in her secret soul that she have never find it so easy to get on with Lady Cumnor ; and Bradley and she have never do sing the praise of Mr. Gibson , " who always manage my lady so beautifully . " 

 Reports be duly send up to my lord , but he and his daughter be strictly forbid to come down . Lady Cumnor wish to be weak and languid , and uncertain both in body and mind , without family observation . it be a condition so different to anything she have ever be in before , that she be unconsciously afraid of lose her prestige , if she be see in it . sometimes she herself write the daily bulletin ; at other time she bade Clare do it , but she would always see the letter . any answer she receive from her daughter she use to read herself , occasionally impart some of their content to " that good Clare . " but anybody might read my lord 's letter . there be no great fear of family secret ooze out in his sprawl line of affection . but once Mrs. Kirkpatrick come upon a sentence in a letter from Lord Cumnor , which she be read out loud to his wife , that catch her eye before she come to it , and if she could have skip it and keep it for private perusal , she would gladly have do so . my lady be too sharp for she , though . in her opinion " Clare be a good creature , but not clever , " the truth be that she be not always quick at resource , though tolerably unscrupulous in the use of they . 

 " read on . what be you stop for ? there be no bad news , be there , about Agnes ? give I the letter . " 

 Lady Cumnor read , half aloud , 

 " ' how be Clare and Gibson get on ? you despise my advice to help on that affair , but I really think a little match - making would be a very pleasant amusement now that you be shut up in the house ; and I can not conceive any marriage more suitable . ' "

" she wonder what Lady Cumnor think of it .  CHAPTER X. 

 A CRISIS . 
 

 Illustrationrs . Kirkpatrick have be read aloud till Lady Cumnor fall asleep , the book rest on her knee , just keep from fall by her hold . she be look out of the window , not see the tree in the park , nor the glimpse of the hill beyond , but think how pleasant it would be to have a husband once more ; some one who would work while she sate at her elegant ease in a prettily - furnish drawing - room ; and she be rapidly invest this imaginary breadwinner with the form and feature of the country surgeon , when there be a slight tap at the door , and almost before she could rise , the object of her thought come in . she feel herself blush , and she be not displeased at the consciousness . she advance to meet he , make a sign towards her sleeping ladyship .

 " he must believe that it be . whatever you fancy , give he a chance . he can not have much comfort , I should think , if he see you fret or pin , you who have be so much to he , as you say . the lady herself , too if Harriet 's stepmother have be a selfish woman , and be always clutch after the gratification of her own wish ; but she be not : she be as anxious for Harriet to be happy as Harriet be for her father and your father 's future wife may be another of the same kind , though such people be rare .  " it be right to hope for the good about everybody , and not to expect the bad . this sound like a truism , but it have comfort I before now , and some day you will find it useful . one have always to try to think more of other than of oneself , and it be good not to prejudge people on the bad side . my sermon be not   long , be they ? have they give you an appetite for lunch ? sermon always make I hungry , I know .  " marry again ! you do not   say so . " 

 " yes , he be ; and she take it very hardly , poor girl . mother , I think if you could send some one to she with a glass of wine , a cup of tea , or something of that sort she be very nearly faint "

 " I do not   know . I do not   ask , and she do not   tell I . "

 to their surprise , Molly come in , try hard to look as usual . she have bath her eye , and arrange her hair ; and be make a great struggle to keep from cry , and to bring her voice into order . she be unwilling to distress Mrs. Hamley by the sight of pain and suffering . she do not know that she be follow Roger 's injunction to think more of other than of herself but so she be .  CHAPTER XI . 

 make FRIENDSHIP . 
 

 Mr. Gibson believe that Cynthia Kirkpatrick be to return to England to be present at her mother 's wedding ; but Mrs. Kirkpatrick have no such intention . she be not what be commonly call a woman of determination ; but somehow what she dislike she avoid , and what she like she try to do , or to have . so although in the conversation , which she have already lead to , as to the when and the how she be to be marry , she have listen quietly to Mr. Gibson 's proposal that Molly and Cynthia should be the two bridesmaid , still she have feel how disagreeable it would be to she to have her young daughter flash out her beauty by the side of the faded bride , her mother ; and as the further arrangement for the wedding become more definite , she see further reason in her own mind for Cynthia 's remain quietly at her school at Boulogne . 

 Mrs. Kirkpatrick have go to bed that first night of her engagement to Mr. Gibson , fully anticipate a speedy marriage . she look to it as a release from the thraldom of keep school keep an unprofitable school , with barely pupil enough to pay for house rent and taxis , food , washing , and the requisite master . she see no reason for ever go back to Ashcombe , except to wind up her affair , and to pack up her clothe . she hop that Mr. Gibson 's ardour would be such that he would press on the marriage , and urge she never to resume her school drudgery , but to relinquish it now and for ever . she even make up a very pretty , very passionate speech for he in her own mind ; quite sufficiently strong to prevail upon she , and to overthrow the scruple which she feel she ought to have , at tell the parent of her pupil that she do not intend to resume school , and that they must find another place of education for their daughter , in the last week but one of the midsummer holiday . 

 it be rather like a douche of cold water on Mrs. Kirkpatrick 's plan , when the next morning at breakfast Lady Cumnor begin to decide upon the arrangement and duty of the two middle - aged lover . 

 " of course you can not   give up your school all at once , Clare . the wedding can not   be before Christmas , but that will do very well . we shall all be down at the tower ; and it will be a nice amusement for the child to go over to Ashcombe , and see you married . " 

 " I think I be afraid I do not   believe Mr. Gibson will like wait so long ; man be so impatient under these circumstance . " 

 " oh , nonsense ! Lord Cumnor have recommend you to his tenant , and I be sure he would not   like they to be put to any inconvenience . Mr. Gibson will see that in a moment . he be a man of sense , or else he would not   be our family doctor . now , what be you go to do about your little girl ? have you fix yet ? " 

 " no . yesterday there seem so little time , and when one be agitate it be so difficult to think of anything . Cynthia be nearly eighteen , old enough to go out as a governess , if he wish it , but I do not   think he will . he be so generous and kind . " 

 " well ! I must give you time to settle some of your affair to - day . do not   waste it in sentiment , you be too old for that . come to a clear understanding with each other ; it will be for your happiness in the long run . " 

 so they do come to a clear understanding about one or two thing . to Mrs. Kirkpatrick 's dismay , she find that Mr. Gibson have no more idea than Lady Cumnor of her break faith with the parent of her pupil . though he really be at a serious loss as to what be to become of Molly till she could be under the protection of his new wife at her own home , and though his domestic worry tease he more and more every day , he be too honourable to think of persuade Mrs. Kirkpatrick to give up school a week soon than be right for his sake . he do not even perceive how easy the task of persuasion would be ; with all her win wile she could scarcely lead he to feel impatience for the wedding to take place at Michaelmas . 

 " I can hardly tell you what a comfort and relief it will be to I , Hyacinth , when you be once my wife the mistress of my home poor little Molly 's mother and protector ; but I would not   interfere with your previous engagement for the world . it would not   be right . " 

 " thank you , my own love . how good you be ! so many man would think only of their own wish and interest ! I be sure the parent of my dear pupil will admire you will be quite surprised at your consideration for their interest . " 

 " do not   tell they , then . I hate be admire . why should not   you say it be your wish to keep on your school till they have   have time to look out for another ? " 

 " because it be not , " say she , dare all . " I long to be make you happy ; I want to make your home a place of rest and comfort to you ; and I do so wish to cherish your sweet Molly , as I hope to do , when I come to be her mother . I can not   take virtue to myself which do not   belong to I . if I have to speak for myself , I shall say , ' good people , find a school for your daughter by Michaelmas , for after that time I must go and make the happiness of other . ' I can not   bear to think of your long ride in November come home wet at night with no one to take care of you . oh ! if you leave it to I , I shall advise the parent to take their daughter away from the care of one whose heart will be absent . though I could not   consent to any time before Michaelmas that would not   be fair or right , and I be sure you would not   urge I you be too good . "

 this plan do not quite suit Mrs. Kirkpatrick : but she think it well not to oppose it , until she have a presentable excuse to give , and perhaps also some reason would naturally arise out of future circumstance ; so at this time she only smile , and softly press the hand she hold in her .

Mrs. Kirkpatrick be rather weary of girl as a class . all the trial of her life be connect with girl in some way . she be very young when she first become a governess , and have be worst in her struggle with her pupil , in the first place she ever go to . her elegance of appearance and manner , and her accomplishment , more than her character and acquirement , have render it easy for she than for most to obtain good ' situation ; " and she have be absolutely pet in some ; but still she be constantly encounter naughty or stubborn , or over - conscientious , or severe - judging , or curious and observant girl . and again , before Cynthia be bear , she have long for a boy , think it possible that if some three or four intervene relation die , he might come to be a baronet ; and instead of a son , lo and behold it be a daughter ! nevertheless , with all her dislike to girl in the abstract as " the plague of her life "   and her aversion be not diminish by the fact of she have keep a school for " young lady " at Ashcombe , she really mean to be as kind as she could be to her new step - daughter , whom she remember principally as a black - haired , sleepy child , in whose eye she have read admiration of herself . " " anything but that horrid plaid silk " be the thought in Mrs. Hamley 's mind ; and , thank to she , molly set off for the tower , look a little quaint , it be true , but thoroughly lady - like , if she be old - fashioned . her father be to meet she there ; but he have be detain , and she have to face Mrs. Kirkpatrick by herself , the recollection of her last day of misery at the tower fresh in her mind as if it have be yesterday .  Molly 's face clear a little ; of course she do know . she have not watch and love he so long without believe that she understand he well than any one else : though how he have come to like Mrs. Kirkpatrick enough to wish to marry she , be an unsolved problem that she unconsciously put aside as inexplicable . I want to do everything in my power with a view to his approval . "

" please ! I should so like to know something about Cynthia your daughter . "

 molly follow Mrs. Kirkpatrick into the morning - room , where Lady Cumnor be sit a little annoyed , because , have complete her toilette early than usual , Clare have not be aware by instinct of the fact , and so have not bring molly Gibson for inspection a quarter of an hour before . every small occurrence be an event in the day of a convalescent invalid , and a little while ago molly would have meet with patronize appreciation , where now she have to encounter criticism . of Lady Cumnor 's character as an individual she know nothing ; she only know she be go to see and be see by a live countess ; nay , more , by " the countess " of Hollingford .

 Molly very heartily hop that she might never be know any well by the stern - look lady who sate so upright in the easy chair , prepare for lounging , and which therefore give all the more effect to the stiff attitude . " a sudden thought strike she , " you and she must become well acquaint you know nothing of each other at present ; you be not to be marry till Christmas , and what could be well than that she should go back with you to Ashcombe ! she would be with you constantly , and have the advantage of the companionship of your young people , which would be a good thing for an only child ! it be a capital plan ; I be very glad I think of it ! "

" I will try hard to love you , and to do all I can to make you happy ; but you must not take I away from papa just this very last bit of time that I shall have he . "

I do not   know what he might do , or how troublesome he might be both to Molly and you . but he have cool down now . absence have have a very tranquillizing effect , and I think molly may be in the same town with he , without any consequence beyond a few sigh every time she be bring to his mind by meet she . and I have   get another favour to ask of you , so you see it would never do for I to argue with you , Miss Browning , when I ought to be a humble suppliant . something must be do to the house to make it all ready for the future Mrs. Gibson . it want paint and paper shamefully , and I should think some new furniture , but I be sure I do not   know what . would you be so very kind as to look over the place , and see how far a hundred pound will go ? the dining - room wall must be paint ; we will keep the drawing - room paper for her choice , and I have   a little spare money for that room for she to lay out ; but all the rest of the house I will leave to you , if you will only be kind enough to help an old friend . " 

 this be a commission which exactly gratify Miss Browning 's love of power . the disposal of money involve patronage of trade people , such as she have exercise in her father 's lifetime , but have have very little chance of show since his death . her usual good - humour be quite restore by this proof of confidence in her taste and economy , while Miss Phobe 's imagination dwelt rather on the pleasure of a visit from Molly . 

 

 

 CHAPTER XIII . 

 MOLLY GIBSON 's new friend . 
 

 Time be speed on ; it be now the middle of August , if anything be to be do to the house , it must be do at once . indeed , in several way Mr. Gibson 's arrangement with Miss Browning have not be make too soon . the squire have hear that Osborne might probably return home for a few day before go abroad ; and , though the grow intimacy between Roger and Molly do not alarm he in the least , yet he be possess by a very hearty panic lest the heir might take a fancy to the surgeon 's daughter ; and he be in such a fidget for she to leave the house before Osborne come home , that his wife live in constant terror lest he should make it too obvious to their visitor . 

 every young girl of seventeen or so , who be at all thoughtful , be very apt to make a Pope out of the first person who present to she a new or large system of duty than that by which she have be unconsciously guide hitherto . such a Pope be Roger to Molly ; she look to his opinion , to his authority on almost every subject , yet he have only say one or two thing in a terse manner which give they the force of precept stable guide to her conduct and have show the natural superiority in wisdom and knowledge which be sure to exist between a highly educate young man of no common intelligence , and an ignorant girl of seventeen , who yet be well capable of appreciation . still , although they be draw together in this very pleasant relationship , each be imagine some one very different for the future owner of their whole heart their high and completest love . Roger look to find a grand woman , his equal , and his empress ; beautiful in person , serene in wisdom , ready for counsel , as be Egeria . Molly 's little waver maiden fancy dwelt on the unseen Osborne , who be now a troubadour , and now a knight , such as he write about in one of his own poem ; some one like Osborne , perhaps , rather than Osborne himself , for she shrink from give a personal form and name to the hero that be to be . the squire be not unwise in wish she well out of the house before Osborne come home , if he be consider her peace of mind . yet , when she go away from the hall he miss she constantly ; it have be so pleasant to have she there fulfil all the pretty office of a daughter ; cheer the meal , so often tete - a - tete betwixt he and Roger , with her innocent wise question , her lively interest in their talk , her merry reply to his banter . 

 and Roger miss she too . sometimes her remark have probe into his mind , and excite he to the deep thought in which he delight ; at other time he have feel himself of real help to she in her hour of need , and in make she take an interest in book , which treat of high thing than the continual fiction and poetry which she have hitherto read . he feel something like an affectionate tutor suddenly deprive of his most promising pupil ; he wonder how she would go on without he ; whether she would be puzzle and dishearten by the book he have lend she to read ; how she and her stepmother would get along together ? she occupy his thought a good deal those first few day after she leave the hall . Mrs. Hamley regret she more , and long than do the other two . she have give she the place of a daughter in her heart ; and now she miss the sweet feminine companionship , the playful caress , the never - cease attention ; the very need of sympathy in her sorrow , that Molly have show so openly from time to time ; all these thing have extremely endear she to the tender - hearted Mrs. Hamley . 

 molly , too , feel the change of atmosphere keenly ; and she blame herself for so feeling even more keenly still . but she could not help have a sense of refinement , which have make she appreciate the whole manner of be at the Hall . by her dear old friend the Miss Brownings she be pet and caress so much that she become ashamed of notice the coarser and loud tone in which they speak , the provincialism of their pronunciation , the absence of interest in thing , and their greediness of detail about person . they ask her question which she be puzzle enough to answer about her future stepmother ; her loyalty to her father forbid she to reply fully and truthfully . she be always glad when they begin to make inquiry as to every possible affair at the Hall . she have be so happy there ; she have like they all , down to the very dog , so thoroughly , that it be easy work reply : she do not mind tell they everything , even to the style of Mrs. Hamley 's invalid dress ; nor what wine the squire drink at dinner . indeed , talk about these thing help she to recall the happy time in her life . but one evening , as they be all sit together after tea in the little upstairs drawing - room , look into the High Street Molly discourse away on the various pleasure of Hamley Hall , and just then tell of all Roger 's wisdom in natural science , and some of the curiosity he have show she , she be suddenly pull up by this little speech , 

 " you seem to have see a great deal of Mr. Roger , Molly ! " say Miss Browning , in a way intend to convey a great deal of meaning to her sister and none at all to Molly . but 

 the man recover of the bite ; 
 the dog it be that die . 
 Molly be perfectly aware of Miss Browning 's emphatic tone , though at first she be perplex as to its cause ; while Miss Phobe be just then too much absorb in knit the heel of her stocking to be fully alive to her sister 's nod and wink .

I will not   speak another word about Mr. Roger or Hamley at all , if it put such silly notion into your head . "

"

" Cromer , the upholsterer , want to persuade I to have a sofa and a writing - table . these man will say anything be the fashion , if they want to sell an article .  Mr. Gibson and Molly drive over to Ashcombe , the night before the wedding , in the one yellow post - chaise that Hollingford possess . they be to be Mr. Preston 's , or , rather , my lord 's guest at the Manor - house . the Manor - house come up to its name , and delight Molly at first sight . it be build of stone , have many gable and mullione window , and be cover over with virginian creeper and late - blow rose . molly do not know Mr. Preston , who stand in the doorway to greet her father . she take stand with he as a young lady at once , and it be the first time she have meet with the kind of behaviour half complimentary , half flirt which some man think it necessary to assume with every woman under five - and - twenty .  CHAPTER XIV . 

 MOLLY find HERSELF PATRONIZED . 
 

 the wedding go off much as such affair do . Lord Cumnor and Lady Harriet drive over from the tower , so the hour for the ceremony be as late as possible . Lord Cumnor come in order to officiate as the bride 's father , and be in more open glee than either bride or bridegroom , or any one else . Lady Harriet come as a sort of amateur bridesmaid , to ' share Molly 's duty , " as she call it . they go from the Manor - house in two carriage to the church in the park , Mr. Preston and Mr. Gibson in one , and Molly , to her dismay , shut up with Lord Cumnor and Lady Harriet in the other . Lady Harriet 's gown of white muslin have see one or two garden - party , and be not in the fresh order ; it have be rather a freak of the young lady 's at the last moment . she be very merry , and very much inclined to talk to Molly , by way of find out what sort of a little personage Clare be to have for her future daughter . she begin : 

 " we must not   crush this pretty muslin dress of your . put it over papa 's knee ; he do not   mind it in the least . " 

 " what , my dear , a white dress ! no , to be sure not . I rather like it . besides , go to a wedding , who mind anything ? it would be different if we be go to a funeral . " 

 molly conscientiously strove to find out the meaning of this speech ; but before she have do so , Lady Harriet speak again , go to the point , as she always pique herself on do : 

 " I daresay it be something of a trial to you , this second marriage of your father 's ; but you will find Clare the most amiable of woman . she always let I have my own way , and I have   no doubt she will let you have your . " 

 " I mean to try and like she , " say Molly , in a low voice , strive hard to keep down the tear that would keep rise to her eye this morning . " I have   see very little of she yet . " 

 " why , it be the very good thing for you that could have happen , my dear , " say Lord Cumnor . " you be grow up into a young lady and a very pretty young lady , too , if you will allow an old man to say so and who so proper as your father 's wife to bring you out , and show you off , and take you to ball , and that kind of thing ? I always say this match that be go to come off to - day be the most suitable thing I ever know ; and it be even a well thing for you than for the people themselves . " 

 " poor child ! " say Lady Harriet , who have catch a sight of Molly 's troubled face , " the thought of ball be too much for she just now ; but you will like have Cynthia Kirkpatrick for a companion , shall not   you , dear ? " 

 " very much , " say Molly , cheer up a little . " do you know she ? " 

 " oh , I have   see she over and over again when she be a little girl , and once or twice since . she be the prettiest creature that you ever see ; and with eye that mean mischief , if I be not mistaken . but Clare keep her spirit under pretty well when she be stay with we , afraid of she be troublesome , I fancy . " 

 before molly could shape she next question , they be at the church ; and she and Lady Harriet go into a pew near the door to wait for the bride , in whose train they be to proceed to the altar . the earl drive on alone to fetch she from her own house , not a quarter of a mile distant . it be pleasant to she to be lead to the hymeneal altar by a belted earl , and pleasant to have his daughter as a volunteer bridesmaid . Mrs. Kirkpatrick in this flush of small gratification , and on the brink of matrimony with a man whom she like , and who would be bind to support she without any exertion of her own , look beamingly happy and handsome . a little cloud come over her face at the sight of Mr. Preston , the sweet perpetuity of her smile be rather disturb as he follow in Mr. Gibson 's wake . but his face never change ; he bow to she gravely , and then seem absorb in the service . ten minute , and all be over . the bride and bridegroom be drive together to the Manor - house , Mr. Preston be walk thither by a short cut , and Molly be again in the carriage with my lord , rub his hand and chuckle , and Lady Harriet , try to be kind and consolatory , when her silence would have be the good comfort . 

 molly find out , to her dismay , that the plan be for she to return with Lord Cumnor and Lady Harriet when they go back to the tower in the evening . in the meantime Lord Cumnor have business to do with Mr. Preston , and after the happy couple have drive off on their week 's holiday tour , she be to be leave alone with the formidable Lady Harriet . when they be by themselves after all the other have be thus dispose of , Lady Harriet sate still over the drawing - room fire , hold a screen between it and her face , but gaze intently at Molly for a minute or two . molly be fully conscious of this prolonged look , and be try to get up her courage to return the stare , when Lady Harriet suddenly say , 

 " I like you ; you be a little wild creature , and I want to tame you . come here , and sit on this stool by I . what be your name ? or what do they call you ? as north - country people would express it . " 

 " Molly Gibson . my real name be Mary . " 

 " molly be a nice , soft - sound name . People in the last century be not   afraid of homely name ; now we be all so smart and fine : no more ' Lady Bettys ' now . I almost wonder they have not   re - christen all the worsted and knitting - cotton that bear her name . Fancy Lady Constantia 's cotton , or Lady Anna - Maria 's worst . " 

 " I do not   know there be a Lady Betty 's cotton , " say Molly . 

 " that prove you do not   do fancy - work ! you will find Clare will set you to it , though . she use to set I at piece after piece : knight kneel to lady ; impossible flower . but I must do she the justice to add that when I get tired of they she finish they herself . I wonder how you will get on together ? " 

 " so do I ! " sigh out molly , under her breath . 

 " I use to think I manage she , till one day an uncomfortable suspicion arise that all the time she have be manage I . still it be easy work to let oneself be manage ; at any rate till one waken up to the consciousness of the process , and then it may become amusing , if one take it in that light . "

 " now I , " say Lady Harriet , " be too lazy to avoid trap ; and I rather like to remark the cleverness with which they be set . but then , of course , I know that if I choose to exert myself , I can break through the withe of green flax with which they try to bind I . now , perhaps , you will not   be able . " 

 " I do not   quite understand what you mean , " say Molly .

' you will get on capitally with she , I make no doubt . how you will get on with her daughter be another affair ; but I daresay very well . now we will ring for tea ; for I suppose that heavy breakfast be to stand for our lunch . " 

 Mr. Preston come into the room just at this time , and Molly be a little surprised at Lady Harriet 's cool manner of dismiss he , remember as she do how Mr. Preston have imply his intimacy with her ladyship the evening before at dinner - time .

I can talk to one of my father 's labourer with pleasure , while with a man like that underbred fop I be all over thorn and nettle . what be it the irish call that style of creature ? they have   some capital word for it , I know . what be it ? "

I will lend they to you while you be all alone . "

 molly sate quite silent for a minute or two ; then she muster up courage to speak out what be in her mind .

 " well , go on I like to hear you . " 

 still silence .

 molly hold her peace for two or three moment ; then she lift her beautiful , honest eye to Lady Harriet 's face , and say ,

" 

 it be a very pleasant little tea in the fading September twilight .

 molly see Roger 's eye twinkle at the misapplication of the word ; but though he have a strong sense of humour , it never appear to diminish his respect for the people who amuse he .

 CHAPTER XV . 

 the NEW MAMMA . 
 

 Illustrationn Tuesday afternoon Molly return home to the home which be already strange , and what Warwickshire people would call " unked , " to she . new paint , new paper , new colour ; grim servant dress in their good , and object to every change from their master 's marriage to the new oilcloth in the hall , " which trip them up , and throw them down , and be cold to the foot , and smell just abominable . " all these complaint molly have to listen to , and it be not a cheerful preparation for the reception which she already feel to be so formidable . 

 the sound of their carriage - wheel be hear at last , and Molly go to the front door to meet they . her father get out first , and take her hand and hold it while he help his bride to alight . then he kiss she fondly , and pass she on to his wife ; but her veil be so securely   and becomingly   fasten down , that it be some time before Mrs. Gibson could get her lip clear to greet her new daughter . then there be luggage to be see about ; and both the traveller be occupy in this , while molly stand by tremble with excitement , unable to help , and only conscious of Betty 's rather cross look , as heavy box after heavy box jam up the passage . 

 " molly , my dear , show your mamma to her room ! " 

 Mr. Gibson have hesitate , because the question of the name by which Molly be to call her new relation have never occur to he before . the colour flash into Molly 's face . be she to call she " mamma ? " the name long appropriate in her mind to some one else to her own dead mother . the rebellious heart rise against it , but she say nothing . she lead the way upstairs , Mrs. Gibson turn round , from time to time , with some fresh direction as to which bag or trunk she need most . she hardly speak to Molly till they be both in the newly - furnish bedroom , where a small fire have be light by Molly 's order . 

 " now , my love , we can embrace each other in peace . o dear , how tired I be ! "   after the embrace have be accomplish . " my spirit be so easily affect with fatigue ; but your dear papa have be kindness itself . dear ! what an old - fashioned bed ! and what a but it do not   signify . by - and - by we will renovate the house will not   we , my dear ? and you will be my little maid to - night , and help I to arrange a few thing , for I be just wear out with the day 's journey . " 

 " I have   order a sort of tea - dinner to be ready for you , " say Molly . " shall I go and tell they to send it in ? " 

 " I be not sure if I can go down again to - night . it would be very comfortable to have a little table bring in here , and sit in my dressing - gown by this cheerful fire . but , to be sure , there be your dear papa ? I really do not   think he would eat anything if I be not there . one must not think about oneself , you know . yes , I will come down in a quarter of an hour . " 

 but Mr. Gibson have find a note await he , with an immediate summon to an old patient , dangerously ill ; and , snatch a mouthful of food while his horse be be saddle , he have to resume at once his old habit of attention to his profession above everything . 

 as soon as Mrs. Gibson find that he be not likely to miss her presence he have eat a very tolerable lunch of bread and cold meat in solitude , so her fear about his appetite in her absence be not well found she desire to have her meal upstairs in her own room ; and poor Molly , not dare to tell the servant of this whim , have to carry up first a table , which , however small , be too heavy for she ; and afterwards all the choice portion of the meal , which she have take great pain to arrange on the table , as she have see such thing do at Hamley , intermix with fruit and flower that have that morning be send in from various great house where Mr. Gibson be respect and value . how pretty molly have think her handiwork an hour or two before ! how dreary it seem as , at last release from Mrs. Gibson 's conversation , she sate down in solitude to cold tea and the drumstick of the chicken ! no one to look at her preparation , and admire her deft - handedness and taste ! she have think that her father would be gratify by it , and then he have never see it . she have mean her care as an offering of good - will to her stepmother , who even now be ring her bell to have the tray take away , and Miss Gibson summon to her bedroom . 

 molly hastily finish her meal , and go upstairs again . 

 " I feel so lonely , darling , in this strange house ; do come and be with I , and help I to unpack . I think your dear papa might have put off his visit to Mr. Craven Smith for just this one evening . " 

 " Mr. Craven Smith could not   put off his dying , " say Molly , bluntly . 

 " you droll girl ! " say Mrs. Gibson , with a faint laugh . " but if this Mr. Smith be die , as you say , what be the use of your father 's go off to he in such a hurry ? do he expect any legacy , or anything of that kind ? " 

 molly bite her lip to prevent herself from say something disagreeable . she only answer , 

 " I do not   quite know that he be die . the man say so ; and papa can sometimes do something to make the last struggle easy . at any rate , it be always a comfort to the family to have he . " 

 " what dreary knowledge of death you have learn for a girl of your age ! really , if I have hear all these detail of your father 's profession , I doubt if I could have bring myself to have he ! " 

 " he do not   make the illness or the death ; he do his good against they . I call it a very fine thing to think of what he do or try to do . and you will think so , too , when you see how he be watch for , and how people welcome he ! " 

 " well , do not   let we talk any more of such gloomy thing , to - night ! I think I shall go to bed at once , I be so tired , if you will only sit by I till I get sleepy , darling . if you will talk to I , the sound of your voice will soon send I off . " 

 molly get a book , and read her stepmother to sleep , prefer that to the hard task of keep up a continual murmur of speech . 

 then she steal down and go into the dining - room , where the fire be go out ; purposely neglect by the servant , to mark their displeasure at their new mistress 's have have her tea in her own room . molly manage to light it , however , before her father come home , and collect and re - arrange some comfortable food for he . then she kneel down again on the hearth - rug , gaze into the fire in a dreamy reverie , which have enough of sadness about it to cause the tear to drop unnoticed from her eye . but she jump up , and shake herself into brightness at the sound of her father 's step . 

 " how be Mr. Craven Smith ? " say she . 

 " dead . he just recognize I . he be one of my first patient on come to Hollingford . " 

 Mr. Gibson sate down in the arm - chair make ready for he , and warm his hand at the fire , seem neither to need food nor talk , as he go over a train of recollection . then he rouse himself from his sadness , and look round the room , he say briskly enough , 

 " and where be the new mamma ? " 

 " she be tired , and go to bed early . oh , papa ! must I call she ' mamma ? ' " 

 " I should like it , " reply he , with a slight contraction of the brow . 

 molly be silent . she put a cup of tea near he ; he stir it , and sip it , and then he recur to the subject . 

 " why should not   you call she ' mamma ? ' I be sure she mean to do the duty of a mother to you . we all may make mistake , and her way may not be quite all at once our way ; but at any rate let we start with a family bond between we . " 

 what would Roger say be right ? that be the question that rise to Molly 's mind . she have always speak of her father 's new wife as Mrs. Gibson , and have once burst out at Miss Brownings with a protestation that she never would call she " mamma . " she do not feel draw to her new relation by their intercourse that evening . she keep silence , though she know her father be expect an answer . at last he give up his expectation , and turn to another subject ; tell about their journey , question she as to the Hamleys , the Brownings , Lady Harriet , and the afternoon they have pass together at the Manor - house . but there be a certain hardness and constraint in his manner , and in hers a heaviness and absence of mind . all at once she say , 

 " Papa , I will call she ' mamma ! ' " 

 he take her hand , and grasp it tight ; but for an instant or two he do not speak . then he say , 

 " you will not   be sorry for it , Molly , when you come to lie as poor Craven Smith do to - night . " 

 for some time the murmur and grumbling of the two eld servant be confine to Molly 's ear , then they spread to her father 's , who , to Molly 's dismay , make summary work with they . 

 " you do not   like Mrs. Gibson 's ring her bell so often , do not   you ? you have   be spoil , I be afraid ; but if you do not   conform to my wife 's desire , you have the remedy in your own hand , you know . " 

 what servant ever resist the temptation to give warning after such a speech as that ? Betty tell Molly she be go to leave , in as indifferent a manner as she could possibly assume towards the girl whom she have tend and be about for the last sixteen year . molly have hitherto consider her former nurse as a fixture in the house ; she would almost as soon have think of her father 's propose to sever the relationship between they ; and here be Betty coolly talk over whether her next place should be in town or country . but a great deal of this be assume hardness . in a week or two Betty be in flood of tear at the prospect of leave her nursling , and would fain have stay and answer all the bell in the house once every quarter of an hour . even Mr. Gibson 's masculine heart be touch by the sorrow of the old servant , which make itself obvious to he every time he come across she by her broken voice and her swollen eye . 

 one day he say to Molly , " I wish you would ask your mamma if Betty might not stay , if she make a proper apology , and all that sort of thing . " 

 " I do not   much think it will be of any use , " say Molly , in a mournful voice . " I know she be write , or have write , about some under - housemaid at the Towers . " 

 " well ! all I want be peace and a decent quantity of cheerfulness when I come home . I see enough of tear at other people 's house . after all , Betty have be with we sixteen year a sort of service of the antique world . but the woman may be happy elsewhere . do as you like about ask mamma ; only if she agree , I shall be quite willing . " 

 so molly try her hand at make a request to that effect to Mrs. Gibson . her instinct tell she she would be unsuccessful ; but surely favour be never refuse in so soft a tone . 

 " my dear girl , I should never have think of send an old servant away , one who have have the charge of you from your birth , or nearly so . I could not have have the heart to do it . she might have stay for ever for I , if she have only attend to all my wish ; and I be not unreasonable , be I ? but , you see , she complain ; and when your dear papa speak to she , she give warning ; and it be quite against my principle ever to take an apology from a servant who have give warning . " 

 " she be so sorry , " plead molly ; ' she say she will do anything you wish , and attend to all your order , if she may only stay . " 

 " but , sweet one , you seem to forget that I can not go against my principle , however much I may be sorry for Betty . she should not have give way to ill - temper . as I say before , although I never like she , and consider she a most inefficient servant , thoroughly spoil by have have no mistress for so long , I should have bear with she at least , I think I should as long as I could . now I have all but engaged Maria , who be under - housemaid at the tower , so do not   let I hear any more of Betty 's sorrow , or anybody else 's sorrow , for I be sure , what with your dear papa 's sad story and other thing , I be get quite low . " 

 molly be silent for a moment or two . 

 " have you quite engaged Maria ? " ask she . 

 " no I say ' all but engage . ' sometimes one would think you do not hear thing , dear Molly ! " reply Mrs. Gibson , petulantly . " Maria be live in a place where they do not   give she as much wage as she deserve . perhaps they can not   afford it , poor thing ! " 

 " Poor Betty ! " say Molly , softly . 

 " poor old soul ! I hope she will profit by the lesson , I be sure , " sigh out Mrs. Gibson ; " but it be a pity we have not   Maria before the county family begin to call . " 

 Mrs. Gibson have be highly gratified by the circumstance of so many call " from county family . " her husband be much respect ; and many lady from various hall , court , and house , who have profit by his service towards themselves and their family , think it right to pay his new wife the attention of a call when they drive into Hollingford to shop . the state of expectation into which these call throw Mrs. Gibson rather diminish Mr. Gibson 's domestic comfort . it be awkward to be carry hot , savoury - smell dish from the kitchen to the dining - room at the very time when high - bear lady , with nose of aristocratic refinement , might be call . still more awkward be the accident which happen in consequence of clumsy Betty 's haste to open the front door to a lofty footman 's ran - tan , which cause she to set down the basket contain the dirty plate right in his mistress 's way , as she step gingerly through the comparative darkness of the hall ; and then the young man , leave the dining - room quietly enough , but burst with long - repressed giggle , or no long restrain their tendency to practical joking , no matter who might be in the passage when they make their exit . the remedy propose by Mrs. Gibson for all these distressing grievance be a late dinner . the luncheon for the young man , as she observe to her husband , might be send into the surgery . a few elegant cold trifle for herself and Molly would not scent the house , and she would always take care to have some little dainty ready for he . he accede , but unwillingly , for it be an innovation on the habit of a lifetime , and he feel as if he should never be able to arrange his round aright with this new - fangle notion of a six o'clock dinner . 

 " do not   get any dainty for I , my dear ; bread - and - cheese be the chief of my diet , like it be that of the old woman 's . " 

 " I know nothing of your old woman , " reply his wife ; " but really I can not allow cheese to come beyond the kitchen . " 

 " then I will eat it there , " say he . " it be close to the stable - yard , and if I come in in a hurry I can get it in a moment . " 

 " really , Mr. Gibson , it be astonishing to compare your appearance and manner with your taste . you look such a gentleman , as dear Lady Cumnor use to say . " 

 then the cook leave ; also an old servant , though not so old a one as Betty . the cook do not like the trouble of late dinner ; and , be a Methodist , she object on religious ground to try any of Mrs. Gibson 's new receipt for french dish . it be not scriptural , she say . there be a deal of mention of food in the Bible ; but it be of sheep ready dress , which mean mutton , and of wine , and of bread - and - milk , and fig and raisin , of fat calf , a good well - brown fillet of veal , and such like ; but it have always go against her conscience to cook swine - flesh and make raise pork - pie , and now if she be to be set to cook heathen dish after the fashion of the Papists , she would soon give it all up together . so the cook follow in Betty 's track , and Mr. Gibson have to satisfy his healthy english appetite on badly - make omelette , rissole , vol - au - vent , croquet , and timbale ; never be exactly sure what he be eat . 

 he have make up his mind before his marriage to yield in trifle , and be firm in great thing . but the difference of opinion about trifle arise every day , and be perhaps more annoying than if they have relate to thing of more consequence . molly know her father 's look as well as she know her alphabet ; his wife do not ; and be an unperceptive person , except when her own interest be dependent upon another person 's humour , never find out how he be worry by all the small daily concession which he make to her will or her whim . he never allow himself to put any regret into shape , even in his own mind ; he repeatedly remind himself of his wife 's good quality , and comfort himself by think they should work together well as time roll on ; but he be very angry at a bachelor great - uncle of Mr. Coxe 's , who , after take no notice of his red - headed nephew for year , suddenly send for he , after the old man have partially recover from a serious attack of illness , and appoint he his heir , on condition that his great - nephew remain with he during the rest of his life . this have happen almost directly after Mr. and Mrs. Gibson 's return from their wedding journey , and once or twice since that time Mr. Gibson have find himself wonder why the deuce old Benson could not have make up his mind soon , and so have rid his house of the unwelcome presence of the young lover . to do Mr. Coxe justice , in the very last conversation he have as a pupil with Mr. Gibson he say , with hesitate awkwardness , that perhaps the new circumstance in which he should be place might make some difference with regard to Mr. Gibson 's opinion on 

 " not at all , " say Mr. Gibson , quickly . " you be both of you too young to know your own mind ; and if my daughter be silly enough to be in love , she should never have to calculate her happiness on the chance of an old man 's death . I dare say he will disinherit you after all . he may do , and then you would be bad off than ever . no ! go away , and forget all this nonsense ; and when you have   do , come back and see we ! " 

 so Mr. Coxe go away , with an oath of unalterable faithfulness in his heart ; and Mr. Gibson have unwillingly to fulfil an old promise make to a gentleman farmer in the neighbourhood a year or two before , and to take the second son of Mr. Browne in young Coxe 's place . he be to be the last of the race of pupil , and as he be rather more than a year young than Molly , Mr. Gibson trust that there would be no repetition of the Coxe romance . 

 

 

 CHAPTER XVI . 

 the BRIDE AT HOME . 
 

 among the " county people "   as Mrs. Gibson term they   who call upon she as a bride , be the two young Mr. Hamleys . the Squire , their father , have do his congratulation , as far as he ever intend to do they , to Mr. Gibson himself when he come to the hall ; but Mrs. Hamley , unable to go and pay visit herself , anxious to show attention to her kind doctor 's new wife , and with perhaps a little sympathetic curiosity as to how molly and her stepmother get on together , make her son ride over to Hollingford with her card and apology . they come into the newly - furnish drawing - room , look bright and fresh from their ride : osborne first , as usual , perfectly dress for the occasion , and with the sort of fine manner which sate so well upon he ; Roger , look like a strong - build , cheerful , intelligent country farmer , follow in his brother 's train . Mrs. Gibson be dress for receive caller , and make the effect she always intend to produce , of a very pretty woman , no long in first youth , but with such soft manner and such a caressing voice , that people forgot to wonder what her real age might be . molly be well dress than formerly ; her stepmother see after that . she dislike anything old or shabby , or out of taste about she ; it hurt her eye ; and she have already fidget molly into a new amount of care about the manner in which she put on her clothe , arrange her hair , and be gloved and shod . Mrs. Gibson have try to put she through a course of rosemary wash and cream in order to improve her tan complexion ; but about that Molly be either forgetful or rebellious , and Mrs. Gibson could not well come up to the girl 's bedroom every night and see that she daub her face and neck over with the cosmetic so carefully provide for she . still her appearance be extremely improve , even to Osborne 's critical eye . Roger seek rather to discover in her look and expression whether she be happy or not ; his mother have especially charge he to note all these sign . 

 Osborne and Mrs. Gibson make themselves agreeable to each other accord to the approved fashion when a young man call on a middle - aged bride . they talk of the " Shakspeare and musical glass " of the day , each vie with the other in their knowledge of London topic . molly hear fragment of their conversation in the pause of silence between Roger and herself . her hero be come out in quite a new character ; no long literary or poetical , or romantic , or critical , he be now full of the last new play , the singer at the opera . he have the advantage over Mrs. Gibson , who , in fact , only speak of these thing from hearsay , from listen to the talk at the tower , while Osborne have run up from Cambridge two or three time to hear this , or to see that wonder of the season . but she have the advantage over he in great boldness of invention to eke out her fact ; and besides she have more skill in the choice and arrangement of her word , so as to make it appear as if the opinion that be in reality quotation , be form by herself from actual experience or personal observation ; such as , in speak of the mannerism of a famous italian singer , she would ask ,

 " do you observe her constant trick of heave her shoulder and clasp her hand together before she take a high note ? Osborne be not the same Osborne he be when with his mother at the Hall . 

 Roger see molly glance at his brother . 

 " you think my brother look ill ? " say he , lower his voice .

 " your mother will feel your both go away , will not   she ? but of course young man will always have to live away from home . "

 as soon as they have leave , Mrs. Gibson begin her usual comment on the depart visitor . 

 " I do like that Osborne Hamley ! what a nice fellow he be ! somehow , I always do like eld son . he will have the estate , will not   he ?  molly be spiteful enough to have great pleasure in say ,

 a short silence ensue . then Mrs. Gibson say ,

I should like to have he feel at home in this house . it would be something cheerful for he after the dulness and solitude of Hamley Hall . for the old people do not   visit much , I believe ? "

 Mrs. Gibson pause for a moment before she reply : 

 " it be very much to your credit that you should have such feeling , I be sure . but do not   you think sentiment may be carry too far ? why , we should have no new furniture at all , and should have to put up with worm - eat horror . besides , my dear , Hollingford will seem very dull to Cynthia , after pretty , gay France , and I want to make the first impression attractive . I have   a notion I can settle she down near here ; and I want she to come in a good temper ; for , between ourselves , my dear , she be a little , leetle wilful . you need not mention this to your papa . "

 " no , indeed ! I could not   agree to that . only think what would be say of I by everybody ; pet my own child and neglect my husband 's ! I could not   bear it . " 

 " no one need know . " 

 " in such a tittle - tattle place as Hollingford ! really , Molly , you be either very stupid or very obstinate , or else you do not   care what hard thing may be say about I : and all for a selfish fancy of your own ! no ! I owe myself the justice of act in this matter as I please . every one shall know I be not a common stepmother . every penny I spend on Cynthia I shall spend on you too ; so it be no use talk any more about it . " 

 so Molly 's little white dimity bed , her old - fashioned chest of drawer , and her other cherished relic of her mother 's maiden - day , be consign to the lumber - room ; and after a while , when Cynthia and her great french box have come home , the old furniture that have fill up the space require for the fresh importation of trunk , disappear likewise into the same room . 

 all this time the family at the tower have be absent ; Lady Cumnor have be order to Bath for the early part of the winter , and her family be with she there . on dull rainy day , Mrs. Gibson use to bethink she of miss " the Cumnors , " for so she have take to call they since her position have become more independent of theirs . it mark a distinction between her intimacy in the family , and the reverential manner in which the townspeople be accustom to speak of " the earl and the countess . " both Lady Cumnor and Lady Harriet write to their " dear Clare " from time to time . the former have generally some commission that she wish to have execute at the tower , or in the town ; and no one could do they so well as Clare , who be acquaint with all the taste and way of the countess . these commission be the cause of various bill for fly and car from the George Inn . Mr. Gibson point out this consequence to his wife ; but she , in return , bid he remark that a present of game be pretty sure to follow upon the satisfactory execution of Lady Cumnor 's wish . somehow , Mr. Gibson do not quite like this consequence either ; but he be silent about it , at any rate . Lady Harriet 's letter be short and amusing . she have that sort of regard for her old governess which prompt she to write from time to time , and to feel glad when the half - voluntary task be accomplish . so there be no real outpouring of confidence , but enough news of the family and gossip of the place she be in , as she think would make Clare feel that she be not forget by her former pupil , intermix with moderate but sincere expression of regard . how those letter be quote and refer to by Mrs. Gibson in her conversation with the Hollingford lady ! she have find out their effect at Ashcombe ; and it be not less at Hollingford . but she be rather perplexed at kindly message to Molly , and at inquiry as to how the Miss Brownings like the tea she have send ; and Molly have first to explain , and then to narrate at full length , all the occurrence of the afternoon at Ashcombe Manor - house , and Lady Harriet 's subsequent call upon she at Miss Brownings ' . 

 " what nonsense ! " say Mrs. Gibson , with some annoyance . " Lady Harriet only go to see you out of a desire of amusement . she would only make fun of Miss Brownings , and those two will be quote she and talk about she , just as if she be their intimate friend . " 

 " I do not   think she do make fun of they . she really seem as if she have be very kind . " 

 " and you suppose you know her way well than I do who have know she these fifteen year ? I tell you she turn every one into ridicule who do not belong to her set . why , she use always to speak of Miss Brownings as ' Pecksy and Flapsy . ' " 

 " she promise I she would not , " say Molly drive to bay . 

 " promise you ! Lady Harriet ? what do you mean ? " 

 " only she speak of they as Pecksy and Flapsy and when she talk of come to call on I at their house , I ask she not to come if she be go to to make fun of they . " 

 " upon my word ! with all my long acquaintance with Lady Harriet , I should never have venture on such impertinence . " 

 " I do not   mean it as impertinence , " say Molly sturdily . " and I do not   think Lady Harriet take it as such . " 

 " you can not   know anything about it . she can put on any kind of manner . " 

 just then Squire Hamley come in . it be his first call ; and Mrs. Gibson give he a graceful welcome , and be quite ready to accept his apology for its tardiness , and to assure he that she quite understand the pressure of business on every land - owner who farm his own estate . but no such apology be make . he shake her hand heartily , as a mark of congratulation on her good fortune in have secure such a prize as his friend Gibson , but say nothing about his long neglect of duty . molly , who by this time know the few strong expression of his countenance well , be sure that something be the matter , and that he be very much disturbed . he hardly attend to Mrs. Gibson 's fluent opening of conversation , for she have already determine to make a favourable impression on the father of the handsome young man who be heir to an estate , besides his own personal agreeableness ; but he turn to Molly and , address she , say almost in a low voice , as if he be make a confidence to she that he do not intend Mrs. Gibson to hear , 

 " molly , we be all wrong at home ! Osborne have lose the fellowship at Trinity he go back to try for . then he have go and fail miserably in his degree , after all that he say , and that his mother say ; and I , like a fool , go and boast about my clever son . I can not   understand it . "

"

" I be sure dear papa quite forget that you be to go out with I to - night , to visit people , " continue she , address herself to the squire , " with whom I be quite unacquainted and it be very uncertain if Mr. Gibson can return in time to accompany I so , you see , I can not allow Molly to go with you . " 

 " I should not   have think it would have signify . bride be always bride , I suppose ; and it be their part to be timid ; but I should not   have think it in this case . and my wife set her heart on thing , as sick people do . well , Molly "   in a loud tone , for these foregoing sentence be speak sotto voce , " we must put it off till to - morrow : and it be our loss , not your , " he continue , as he see the reluctance with which she slowly return to her place . " you will be as gay as can be to - night , I daresay " 

 " no , I shall not , " break in Molly . " I never want to go , and now I shall want it less than ever . "
