" 

 " let she alone ! let she alone ! " quoth he . " I see what she mean . she would rather come and be in my wife 's sick - room than go out for this visit to - night . be there no way of get she off ? " 

 " none whatever , " say Mrs. Gibson . " an engagement be an engagement with I ; and I consider that she be not only engage to Mrs. Cockerell , but to I bind to accompany I , in my husband 's absence . " 

 the Squire be put out ; and when he be put out he have a trick of place his hand on his knee and whistle softly to himself . molly know this phase of his displeasure , and only hop he would confine himself to this wordless expression of annoyance . it be pretty hard work for she to keep the tear out of her eye ; and she endeavour to think of something else , rather than dwell on regret and annoyance . she hear Mrs. Gibson talk on in a sweet monotone , and wish to attend to what she be say , but the Squire 's visible annoyance strike sharper on her mind . at length , after a pause of silence , he start up , and say , 

 " well ! it be no use . poor madam ; she will not   like it . she will be disappoint ! but it be but for one evening ! but for one evening ! she may come to - morrow , may not   she ? or will the dissipation of such an evening as she describe , be too much for she ? " 

 there be a touch of savage irony in his manner which frighten Mrs. Gibson into good behaviour . 

 " she shall be ready at any time you name . I be so sorry : my foolish shyness be in fault , I believe ; but still you must acknowledge that an engagement be an engagement . " 

 " do I ever say an engagement be an elephant , madam ? however , there be no use say any more about it , or I shall forget my manner . I be an old tyrant , and she lie there in bed , poor girl have always give I my own way . so you will excuse I , Mrs. Gibson , will not   you ; and let molly come along with I at ten to - morrow morning ? " 

 " certainly , " say Mrs. Gibson , smile . but when his back be turn , she say to Molly ,

I do not   call he a squire ; I call he a boor , or a yeoman at well . you must not go on accept or reject invitation as if you be an independent young lady , Molly . pay I the respect of a reference to my wish another time , if you please , my dear ! " 

 " Papa have say I might go , " say Molly , choke a little . 

 " as I be now your mamma , your reference must be to I , for the future . but as you be to go you may as well look well dressed . I will lend you my new shawl for this visit , if you like it , and my set of green ribbon . I be always indulgent when proper respect be pay to I . and in such a house as Hamley Hall , no one can tell who may be come and go , even if there be sickness in the family . " 

 " thank you . but I do not   want the shawl and the ribbon , please : there will be nobody there except the family . there never be , I think ; and now that she be so ill " Molly be on the point of cry at the thought of her friend lie ill and lonely , and look for her arrival . moreover , she be sadly afraid lest the Squire have go off with the idea that she do not want to come that she prefer that stupid , stupid party at the Cockerells ' . Mrs. Gibson , too , be sorry ; she have an uncomfortable consciousness of have give way to temper before a stranger , and a stranger , too , whose good opinion she have mean to cultivate ; and she be also annoy at Molly 's tearful face . 

 " what can I do for you , to bring you back into good temper ? " she say . " first , you insist upon your know Lady Harriet well than I do I , who have know she for eighteen or nineteen year at least . then you jump at invitation without ever consult I , or think of how awkward it would be for I to go stump into a drawing - room all by myself ; follow my new name , too , which always make I feel uncomfortable , it be such a sad come - down after Kirkpatrick ! and then , when I offer you some of the prettiest thing I have get , you say it do not signify how you be dress . what can I do to please you , molly ? I , who delight in nothing more than peace in a family , to see you sit there with despair upon your face ? " 

 molly could stand it no long ; she go upstairs to her own room her own smart new room , which hardly yet seem a familiar place ; and begin to cry so heartily and for so long a time , that she stop at length for very weariness . she think of Mrs. Hamley weary for she ; of the old Hall whose very quietness might become oppressive to an ail person ; of the trust the Squire have have in she that she would come off directly with he . and all this oppress she much more than the querulousness of her stepmother 's word . 

 

 

 CHAPTER XVII . 

 TROUBLE at HAMLEY HALL . 
 

 if molly think that peace dwelt perpetually at Hamley Hall she be sorely mistaken . something be out of tune in the whole establishment ; and , for a very unusual thing , the common irritation seem to have produce a common bond . all the servant be old in their place , and be tell by some one of the family , or gather , from the unheeded conversation carry on before they , everything that affect master or mistress or either of the young gentleman . any one of they could have tell Molly that the grievance which lie at the root of everything , be the amount of the bill run up by Osborne at Cambridge , and which , now that all chance of his obtain a fellowship be over , come pour down upon the squire . but molly , confident of be tell by Mrs. Hamley herself anything which she wish she to hear , encourage no confidence from any one else . 

 she be strike with the change in " madam 's " look as soon as she catch sight of she in the darken room , lie on the sofa in her dressing - room , all dress in white , which almost rival the white wanness of her face . the squire usher Molly in with ,

" and Molly have scarcely imagine that he have so much variety in the tone of his voice the beginning of the sentence be speak in a loud congratulatory manner , while the last word be scarcely audible . he have see the death - like pallor on his wife 's face ; not a new sight , and one which have be present to he gradually enough , but which be now always give he a fresh shock . it be a lovely tranquil winter 's day ; every branch and every twig on the tree and shrub be glitter with drop of the sun - melt hoar - frost ; a robin be perch on a holly - bush , pip cheerily ; but the blind be down , and out of Mrs. Hamley 's window nothing of all this be to be see . there be even a large screen place between she and the wood - fire , to keep off that cheerful blaze . Mrs. Hamley stretch out one hand to Molly , and hold hers firm ; with the other she shade her eye .

" but never fear , my dear one ; here be the doctor 's daughter , nearly as good as the doctor himself . have you have your medicine ? your beef - tea ? " he continue , go about on heavy tiptoe and peep into every empty cup and glass . then he return to the sofa ; look at she for a minute or two , and then softly kiss she , and tell Molly he would leave she in charge . 

 as if Mrs. Hamley be afraid of Molly 's remark or question , she begin in her turn a hasty system of interrogatory . 

 " now , dear child , tell I all ; it be no breach of confidence , for I shall not   mention it again , and I shall not   be here long . how do it all go on the new mother , the good resolution ? let I help you if I can . I think with a girl I could have be of use a mother do not know boy . but tell I anything you like and will ; do not   be afraid of detail . " 

 even with Molly 's small experience of illness she see how much of restless fever there be in this speech ; and instinct , or some such gift , prompt she to tell a long story of many thing the wedding - day , her visit to Miss Brownings ' , the new furniture , Lady Harriet ,   c. , all in an easy flow of talk which be very soothing to Mrs. Hamley , inasmuch as it give she something to think about beyond her own immediate sorrow . but Molly do not speak of her own grievance , nor of the new domestic relationship . Mrs. Hamley notice this . 

 " and you and Mrs. Gibson get on happily together ? "

 " I do not   like what the Squire tell I last night . he be very angry . " 

 that sore have not yet heal over ; but molly resolutely keep silence , beat her brain to think of some other subject of conversation .

 she make molly sit down near she on a little stool , and , hold her hand , and look into her eye to catch her speak sympathy from their expression quick than she could from her word , she say ,

"

 what with his anger against his son , and his anxiety about his wife ; the difficulty of raise the money immediately require , and his irritation at the scarce - conceal inquiry make by stranger as to the value of his property , the poor squire be in a sad state . he be angry and impatient with every one who come near he ; and then be depressed at his own violent temper and unjust word . the old servant , who , perhaps , cheat he in many small thing , be beautifully patient under his upbraiding . they could understand burst of passion , and know the cause of his variable mood as well as he do himself . the butler , who be accustomed to argue with his master about every fresh direction as to his work , now nudge Molly at dinner - time to make her eat of some dish which she have just be decline , and explain his conduct afterwards as follow : 

 " you see , miss , I and cook have plan a dinner as would tempt master to eat ; but when you say , ' no , thank you , ' when I hand you anything , master never so much as look at it . but if you take a thing , and eat with a relish , why first he wait , and then he look , and by - and - by he smell ; and then he find out as he be hungry , and fall to eat as natural as a kitten take to mewing . that be the reason , miss , as I give you a nudge and a wink , which no one know well nor I be not manner . " 

 but the Squire 's bluff florid face be pinch with anxiety , and wear with the effort of be deaf to the footstep of fate as he say these word which belie his fear .

" of course we must all die ; and she must too . but the clever doctor in England shall not   go about coolly mete out the life of such as she . I daresay I shall die first .  Dear Gibson ,

 your ever , 

 R. Hamley . 

 P.S. molly be a treasure . God help I !

 CHAPTER XVIII . 

 MR . OSBORNE 'S SECRET . 
 

 Illustrationsborne and Roger come to the Hall ; Molly find Roger establish there when she return after this absence at home . she gather that Osborne be come ; but very little be say about he in any way . the Squire scarcely ever leave his wife 's room ; he sit by she , watch she , and now and then moan to himself . she be so much under the influence of opiate that she do not often rouse up ; but when she do , she almost invariably ask for Molly . on these rare occasion , she would ask after Osborne where he be , if he have be tell , and if he be come ? in her weakened and confused state of intellect she seem to have retain two strong impression one , of the sympathy with which Molly have receive her confidence about Osborne ; the other , of the anger which her husband entertain against he . before the squire she never mention Osborne 's name ; nor do she seem at her ease in speak about he to Roger ; while , when she be alone with Molly , she hardly speak of any one else . she must have have some sort of wandering idea that Roger blame his brother , while she remember Molly 's eager defence , which she have think hopelessly improbable at the time . at any rate , she make molly her confidante about her first - bear . she send she to ask Roger how soon he would come , for she seem to know perfectly well that he be come .

 but it be several day before molly could ask Roger any question ; and meanwhile Mrs. Hamley 's state have materially alter . at length molly come upon Roger sit in the library , his head bury in his hand . he do not hear her footstep till she be close beside he . then he lift up his face , red , and stain with tear , his hair all ruffle up and in disorder .

 he put his head again between his hand , and do not answer she for some time .

 " I can not   tell you . I do not   exactly know . I believe he be abroad , but I be not sure . "

 " yes ; I see . have you ever notice that she sometimes call you ' fanny ? ' it be the name of a little sister of our who die . I think she often take you for she . it be partly that , and partly that at such a time as this one can not   stand on formality , that make I call you molly . I hope you do not   mind it ? "

I believe he be in Belgium , and that he go there about a fortnight ago , partly to avoid his creditor . you know my father have refuse to pay his debt ? "

"

"

 so molly hang about the house , try to do all she could out of the sick - room , for the comfort of those in it . they only come out for meal , or for necessary business , and find little time for talk to she , so her life be solitary enough , wait for the call that never come . the evening of the day on which she have have the above conversation with Roger , Osborne arrive . he come straight into the drawing - room , where Molly be seat on the rug , read by firelight , as she do not like to ring for candle merely for her own use .  CHAPTER XIX . 

 CYNTHIA 's ARRIVAL . 
 

 Molly 's father be not at home when she return ; and there be no one to give she a welcome . Mrs. Gibson be out pay call , the servant tell Molly . she go upstairs to her own room , mean to unpack and arrange her borrow book . rather to her surprise she see the chamber , correspond to her own , be dust ; water and towel too be be carry in . 

 " be any one come ? " she ask of the housemaid . 

 " Missus 's daughter from France . Miss Kirkpatrick be come to - morrow . " 

 be Cynthia come at last ? oh , what a pleasure it would be to have a companion , a girl , a sister of her own age ! Molly 's depressed spirit spring up again with bright elasticity . she long for Mrs. Gibson 's return , to ask she all about it : it must be very sudden , for Mr. Gibson have say nothing of it at the Hall the day before . no quiet reading now ; the book be hardly put away with Molly 's usual neatness . she go down into the drawing - room , and could not settle to anything . at last Mrs. Gibson come home , tired out with her walk and her heavy velvet cloak . until that be take off , and she have rest herself for a few minute , she seem quite unable to attend to Molly 's question . 

 " oh , yes ! Cynthia be come home to - morrow , by the ' Umpire , ' which pass through at ten o'clock . what an oppressive day it be for the time of the year ! I really be almost ready to faint . Cynthia hear of some opportunity , I believe , and be only too glad to leave school a fortnight early than we plan . she never give I the chance of write to say I do , or do not , like she come so much before the time ; and I shall have to pay for she just the same as if she have stop . and I mean to have ask she to bring I a french bonnet ; and then you could have have one make after mine . but I be very glad she be come , poor dear . " 

 " be anything the matter with she ? " ask Molly . 

 " oh , no ! why should there be ? " 

 " you call she ' poor dear , ' and it make I afraid lest she might be ill . " 

 " oh , no ! it be only a way I get into , when Mr. Kirkpatrick die . a fatherless girl you know one always do call they ' poor dear . ' oh , no ! Cynthia never be ill . she be as strong as a horse . she never would have feel to - day as I have do . could you get I a glass of wine and a biscuit , my dear ? I be really quite faint . " 

 Mr. Gibson be much more excited about Cynthia 's arrival than her own mother be . he anticipate she come as a great pleasure to Molly , on whom , in spite of his recent marriage and his new wife , his interest principally centre . he even find time to run upstairs and see the bedroom of the two girl ; for the furniture of which he have pay a pretty round sum . 

 " well , I suppose young lady like their bedroom deck out in this way ! it be very pretty certainly , but " 

 " I like my own old room well , papa ; but perhaps Cynthia be accustom to such decking up . " 

 " perhaps ; at any rate , she will see we have   try to make it pretty . your be like her . that be right . it might have hurt she , if hers have be smart than your . now , good - night in your fine flimsy bed . " 

 molly be up betime almost before it be light arrange her pretty hamley flower in Cynthia 's room . she could hardly eat her breakfast that morning . she run upstairs and put on her thing , think that Mrs. Gibson be quite sure to go down to the " George Inn , " where the " Umpire " stop , to meet her daughter after a two year ' absence . but , to her surprise , Mrs. Gibson have arrange herself at her great worst - work frame , just as usual ; and she , in her turn , be astonish at Molly 's bonnet and cloak . 

 " where be you go so early , child ? the fog have not   clear away yet . " 

 " I think you would go and meet Cynthia ; and I want to go with you . " 

 " she will be here in half an hour ; and dear papa have tell the gardener to take the wheelbarrow down for her luggage . I be not sure if he be not go himself . " 

 " then be not you go ? " ask Molly , with a good deal of disappointment . 

 " no , certainly not . she will be here almost directly . and , besides , I do not   like to expose my feeling to every passer - by in High Street . you forget I have not see she for two year , and I hate scene in the market - place . " 

 she settle herself to her work again ; and molly , after some consideration , give up her own going , and employ herself in look out of the downstairs window which command the approach from the town . 

 " here she be here she be ! " she cry out at last . her father be walk by the side of a tall young lady ; William the gardener be wheel along a great cargo of baggage . molly fly to the front - door , and have it wide open to admit the new - comer some time before she arrive . 

 " well ! here she be . molly , this be Cynthia . Cynthia , Molly . you be to be sister , you know . " 

 molly see the beautiful , tall , sway figure , against the light of the open door , but could not see any of the feature that be , for the moment , in shadow . a sudden gush of shyness have come over she just at the instant , and quench the embrace she would have give a moment before . but Cynthia take she in her arm , and kiss she on both cheek . 

 " here 's mamma , " she say , look beyond Molly on to the stair where Mrs. Gibson stand , wrap up in a shawl , and shiver in the cold . she run past Molly and Mr. Gibson , who rather avert their eye from this first greeting between mother and child . 

 Mrs. Gibson say 

 " why , how you be grow , darling ! you look quite a woman . " 

 " and so I be , " say Cynthia . " I be before I go away ; I have   hardly grow since , except , it be always to be hop , in wisdom . " 

 " yes ! that we will hope , " say Mrs. Gibson , in rather a meaning way . indeed there be evidently hide allusion in their seem commonplace speech . when they all come into the full light and repose of the drawing - room , Molly be absorb in the contemplation of Cynthia 's beauty . perhaps her feature be not regular ; but the change in her expressive countenance give one no time to think of that . her smile be perfect ; her pout charming ; the play of the face be in the mouth . her eye be beautifully shape , but their expression hardly seem to vary . in colour she be not unlike her mother ; only she have not so much of the red - haired tint in her complexion ; and her long - shape , serious grey eye be fringe with dark lash , instead of her mother 's insipid flaxen one . molly fall in love with she , so to speak , on the instant . she sate there warm her foot and hand , as much at her ease as if she have be there all her life ; not particularly attend to her mother who , all the time , be study either her or her dress measure Molly and Mr. Gibson with grave observant look , as if guess how she should like they . 

 " there be hot breakfast ready for you in the dining - room , when you be ready for it , " say Mr. Gibson . " I be sure you must want it after your night journey . " he look round at his wife , at Cynthia 's mother , but she do not seem inclined to leave the warm room again . 

 " molly will take you to your room , darling , " say she ; " it be near her , and she have get her thing to take off . I will come down and sit in the dining - room while you be have your breakfast , but I really be afraid of the cold now . " 

 Cynthia rise and follow Molly upstairs . 

 " I be so sorry there be not   a fire for you , " say Molly , " but I suppose it be not   order ; and , of course , I do not   give any order . here be some hot water , though . " 

 " stop a minute , " say Cynthia , get hold of both Molly 's hand , and look steadily into her face , but in such a manner that she do not dislike the inspection . 

 " I think I shall like you . I be so glad ! I be afraid I should not . we be all in a very awkward position together , be not   we ? I like your father 's look , though . "

 Molly could not help smile at the way this be say . Cynthia reply to her smile . 

 " ah , you may laugh . but I do not   know that I be easy to get on with ; mamma and I do not   suit when we be last together . but perhaps we be each of we wise now . now , please leave I for a quarter of an hour . I do not   want anything more . " 

 molly go into her own room , wait to show Cynthia down to the dining - room . not that , in the moderate - sized house , there be any difficulty in find the way . a very little trouble in conjecture would enable a stranger to discover any room . but Cynthia have so captivate molly , that she want to devote herself to the new - comer 's service . ever since she have hear of the probability of she have a sister   she call she a sister , but whether it be a Scotch sister , or a sister a la mode de Brťtagne , would have puzzle most people   molly have allow her fancy to dwell much on the idea of Cynthia 's come ; and in the short time since they have meet , Cynthia 's unconscious power of fascination have be exercise upon she . some people have this power . of course , its effect be only manifest in the susceptible . a school - girl may be find in every school who attract and influence all the other , not by her virtue , nor her beauty , nor her sweetness , nor her cleverness , but by something that can neither be describe nor reason upon . it be the something allude to in the old line : 

 love I not for comely grace , 
 for my pleasing eye and face ; 
 no , nor for my constant heart , 
 for these may change , and turn to ill , 
 and thus true love may sever . 
 but love I on , and know not why , 
 so hast thou the same reason still 
 to dote upon I ever . 
 a woman will have this charm , not only over man but over her own sex ; it can not be define , or rather it be so delicate a mixture of many gift and quality that it be impossible to decide on the proportion of each . perhaps it be incompatible with very high principle ; as its essence seem to consist in the most exquisite power of adaptation to vary people and still more various mood ; " be all thing to all man . " at any rate , molly might soon have be aware that Cynthia be not remarkable for unflinche morality ; but the glamour throw over she would have prevent molly from any attempt at penetrate into and judge her companion 's character , even have such process be the least in accordance with her own disposition .

 Day after day the course of these small frivolity be break in upon by the news Mr. Gibson bring of Mrs. Hamley 's near approach to death . molly very often sit by Cynthia , and surround by ribbon , and wire , and net hear the bulletin like the toll of a funeral bell at a marriage feast . her father sympathize with she . it be the loss of a dear friend to he too ; but he be so accustomed to death , that it seem to he but as it be , the natural end of all thing human . to Molly , the death of some one she have know so well and love so much , be a sad and gloomy phenomenon . she loathe the small vanity with which she be surround , and would wander out into the frosty garden , and pace the walk , which be both shelter and conceal by evergreen . 

 at length and yet it be not so long , not a fortnight since molly have leave the Hall the end come . Mrs. Hamley have sink out of life as gradually as she have sink out of consciousness and her place in this world . the quiet wave close over she , and her place know she no more .

"

 only the action of the just

 CHAPTER XX . 

 MRS . GIBSON 's visitors . 
 

 one day , to Molly 's infinite surprise , Mr. Preston be announce as a caller . "

 CHAPTER XXI . 

 the HALF - sister . 
 

 Illustrationt appear as if Mrs. Gibson 's prediction be likely to be verify ; for Osborne Hamley find his way to her drawing - room pretty frequently . to be sure , sometimes prophet can help on the fulfilment of their own prophecy ; and Mrs. Gibson be not passive .

 CHAPTER XXII . 

 the OLD SQUIRE 's trouble . 
 

 Affairs be go on bad at the Hall than Roger have like to tell . moreover , very much of the discomfort there arise from " mere manner , " as people express it , which be always indescribable and indefinable . quiet and passive as Mrs. Hamley have always be in appearance , she be the rule spirit of the house as long as she live . the direction to the servant , down to the most minute particular , come from her sitting - room , or from the sofa on which she lay . her child always know where to find she ; and to find she , be to find love and sympathy . her husband , who be often restless and angry from one cause or another , always come to she to be smooth down and put right . he be conscious of her pleasant influence over he , and become at peace with himself when in her presence ; just as a child be at ease when with some one who be both firm and gentle . but the keystone of the family arch be go , and the stone of which it be compose begin to fall apart . it be always sad when a sorrow of this kind seem to injure the character of the mourning survivor . yet , perhaps , this injury may be only temporary or superficial ; the judgment so constantly pass upon the way in which people bear the loss of those whom they have deeply love , appear to be even more cruel , and wrongly mete out , than human judgment generally be . to careless observer , for instance , it would seem as though the squire be render more capricious and exact , more passionate and authoritative , by his wife 's death . the truth be , that it occur at a time when many thing come to harass he , and some to bitterly disappoint he ; and she be no long there to whom he use to carry his sore heart for the gentle balm of her sweet word . so the sore heart ache and smart intensely ; and often , when he see how his violent conduct affect other , he could have cry out for their pity , instead of their anger and resentment : " have mercy upon I , for I be very miserable . " how often have such dumb thought go up from the heart of those who have take hold of their sorrow by the wrong end , as prayer against sin ! and when the Squire see that his servant be learn to dread he , and his first - bear to avoid he , he do not blame they . he know he be become a domestic tyrant ; it seem as if all circumstance conspire against he , and as if he be too weak to struggle with they ; else , why do everything in door and out of door go so wrong just now , when all he could have do , have thing be prosperous , be to have submit , in very imperfect patience , to the loss of his wife ? but just when he need ready money to pacify Osborne 's creditor , the harvest have turn out remarkably plentiful , and the price of corn have sink down to a level it have not touch for year . the Squire have insure his life at the time of his marriage for a pretty large sum . it be to be a provision for his wife , if she survive he , and for their young child . Roger be the only representative of these interest now ; but the squire be unwilling to lose the insurance by cease to pay the annual sum . he would not , if he could , have sell any part of the estate which he inherit from his father ; and , besides , it be strictly entail . he have sometimes think how wise a step it would have be could he have sell a portion of it , and with the purchase - money have drain and reclaim the remainder ; and at length , learn from some neighbour that Government would make certain advance for drainage ,   c. , at a very low rate of interest , on condition that the work be do , and the money repay , within a give time , his wife have urge he to take advantage of the proffered loan . but now that she be no long there to encourage he , and take an interest in the progress of the work , he grow indifferent to it himself , and care no more to go out on his stout roan cob , and sit square on his seat , watch the labourer on the marshy land all overgrown with rush ; speak to they from time to time in their own strong nervous country dialect : but the interest to Government have to be pay all the same , whether the man work well or ill . then the roof of the Hall let in the melted snow - water this winter ; and , on examination , it turn out that a new roof be absolutely require . the man who have come about the advance make to Osborne by the London money - lender , have speak disparagingly of the timber on the estate " very fine tree sound , perhaps , too , fifty year ago , but go to rot now ; have want lop and clearing . be there no wood - ranger or forester ? they be nothing like the value young Mr. Hamley have represent they to be . " the remark have come round to the squire 's ear . he love the tree he have play under as a boy as if they be live creature ; that be on the romantic side of his nature . merely look at they as represent so many pound sterling , he have esteem they highly , and have have , until now , no opinion of another by which to correct his own judgment . so these word of the valuer cut he sharp , although he affect to disbelieve they , and try to persuade himself that he do so . but , after all , these care and disappointment do not touch the root of his deep resentment against Osborne . there be nothing like wounded affection for give poignancy to anger . and the squire believe that Osborne and his adviser have be make calculation , base upon his own death . he hat the idea so much it make he so miserable that he would not face it , and define it , and meet it with full inquiry and investigation . he choose rather to cherish the morbid fancy that he be useless in this world bear under an unlucky star that all thing go badly under his management . but he do not become humble in consequence . he put his misfortune down to the score of fate not to his own ; and he imagine that Osborne see his failure , and that his first - bear grudge he his natural term of life . all these fancy would have be set to right could he have talk they over with his wife ; or even have he be accustom to mingle much in the society of those whom he esteem his equal ; but , as have be state , he be inferior in education to those who should have be his mate ; and perhaps the jealousy and mauvaise honte that this inferiority have call out long ago , extend itself in some measure to the feeling he entertain towards his son less to Roger than to Osborne , though the former be turn out by far the most distinguished man . but Roger be practical ; interested in all out - of - door thing , and he enjoy the detail , homely enough , which his father sometimes give he of the every - day occurrence which the latter have notice in the wood and the field . Osborne , on the contrary , be what be commonly call " fine ; " delicate almost to effeminacy in dress and in manner ; careful in small observance . all this his father have be rather proud of in the day when he look forward to a brilliant career at Cambridge for his son ; he have at that time regard Osborne 's fastidiousness and elegance as another step - stone to the high and prosperous marriage which be to restore the ancient fortune of the Hamley family . but now that Osborne have barely obtain his degree ; that all the boasting of his father have prove vain ; that the fastidiousness have lead to unexpected expense   to attribute the most innocent cause to Osborne 's debt , the poor young man 's way and manner become a subject of irritation to his father .  take one instance from among many similar scene of the state of feeling between he and his eld son , which , if it could not be call active discord , show at least passive estrangement . 

 it take place on an evening in the March succeed Mrs. Hamley 's death . Roger be at Cambridge .  " I believe , sir , that Thomas " 

 " do not   talk to I of Thomas . send dinner in directly . " 

 about five minute elapse , spend by the hungry Squire in all sort of impatient way attack Thomas , who come in to look after the fire ; knock the log about , scatter out spark , but considerably lessen the chance of warmth ; touch up the candle , which appear to he to give a light unusually insufficient for the large cold room . while he be do this , Osborne come in dressed in full evening dress . he always move slowly ; and this , to begin with , irritate the squire . then an uncomfortable consciousness of a black coat , drab trouser , check cotton cravat , and splash boot , force itself upon he as he see Osborne 's point - device costume . he choose to consider it affectation and finery in Osborne , and be on the point of burst out with some remark , when the butler , who have watch Osborne downstairs before make the announcement , come in to say dinner be ready .

 now , impugn that old steady , turnip - shape watch of the Squire 's be one of the insult which , as it could not reasonably be resent , be not to be forgive . that watch have be give he by his father when watch be watch long ago . it have give the law to house - clock , stable - clock , kitchen - clock nay , even to Hamley Church clock in its day ; and be it now , in its respectable old age , to be look down upon by a little whipper - snapper of a french watch which could go into a man 's waistcoat pocket , instead of have to be extricate , with due effort , like a respectable watch of size and position , from a fob in the waistband ? no ! not if the whipper - snapper be back by all the Horse Guards that ever be , with the Life Guards to boot . poor Osborne might have know well than to cast this slur on his father 's flesh and blood ; for so dear do he hold his watch !

 CHAPTER XXIII . 

 OSBORNE HAMLEY review his position . 
 

 Osborne have his solitary cup of coffee in the drawing - room . he be very unhappy too , after his fashion . he stand on the hearth - rug pondering over his situation . he be not exactly aware how hardly his father be press for ready - money ; the squire have never speak to he on the subject without be angry ; and many of his loose contradictory statement all of which , however contradictory they might appear , have their basis in truth be set down by his son to the exaggeration of passion . but it be uncomfortable enough to a young man of Osborne 's age to feel himself continually hamper for want of a five - pound note . the principal supply for the liberal almost luxurious table at the Hall , come off the estate ; so that there be no appearance of poverty as far as the household go ; and as long as Osborne be content at home , he have everything he could wish for ; but he have a wife elsewhere he want to see she continually and that necessitate journey . she , poor thing ! have to be support where be the money for the journey and for Aimťe 's modest want to come from ? that be the puzzle in Osborne 's mind just now . while he have be at college his allowance heir of the Hamleys have be three hundred , while Roger have to be content with a hundred less . the payment of these annual sum have give the squire a good deal of trouble ; but he think of it as a merely temporary inconvenience ; perhaps unreasonably think so . Osborne be to do great thing ; take high honour , get a fellowship , marry a long - descend heiress , live in some of the many uninhabited room at the Hall , and help the squire in the management of the estate that would some time be his . Roger be to be a clergyman ; steady , slow Roger be just fit for that , and when he decline enter the Church , prefer a life of more activity and adventure , Roger be to be anything ; he be useful and practical , and fit for all the employment from which Osborne be shut out by his fastidiousness , and his   pseudo   genius ; so it be well he be an eld son , for he would never have do to struggle through the world ; and as for his settle down to a profession , it would be like cut block with a razor ! and now here be Osborne , live at home , but long to be elsewhere ; his allowance stop in reality ; indeed , the punctual payment of it during the last year or two have be owe to his mother 's exertion ; but nothing have be say about its present cessation by either father or son ; money matter be too sore a subject between they . every now and then the Squire throw he a ten - pound note or so ; but the sort of suppress growl with which it be give , and the entire uncertainty as to when he might receive such gift , render any calculation base upon their receipt exceedingly vague and uncertain . 

 " what in the world can I do to secure an income ? " think Osborne , as he stand on the hearth - rug , his back to a blaze fire , his cup of coffee send up in the rare old china that have belong to the Hall for generation ; his dress finish , as dress of Osborne 's could hardly fail to be . one could hardly have think that this elegant young man , stand there in the midst of comfort that verge on luxury , should have be turn over that one great problem in his mind ; but so it be . " what can I do to be sure of a present income ? thing can not go on as they be . I should need support for two or three year , even if I enter myself at the Temple , or Lincoln 's Inn . it would be impossible to live on my pay in the army ; besides , I should hate that profession . in fact , there be evil attend all profession I could not   bring myself to become a member of any I have   ever hear of . perhaps I be more fitted to take ' order ' than anything else ; but to be compel to write weekly sermon whether one have anything to say or not , and , probably , doom only to associate with people below one in refinement and education ! yet poor Aimťe must have money . I can not   bear to compare our dinner here , overload with joint and game and sweet , as Morgan will persist in send they up , with Aimťe 's two little mutton - chop . yet what would my father say if he know I would marry a Frenchwoman ? in his present mood he would disinherit I , if that be possible ; and he would speak about she in a way I could not   stand . a roman Catholic , too ! well , I do not   repent it . I would do it again . only if my mother have be in good health if she could have hear my story , and know Aimťe ! as it be I must keep it secret ; but where to get money ? where to get money ? " 

 then he bethink he of his poem would they sell , and bring he in money ? in spite of Milton , he think they might ; and he go to fetch his MSS . out of his room . he sate down near the fire , try to study they with a critical eye , to represent public opinion as far as he could . he have change his style since the Mrs. Hemans ' day . he be essentially imitative in his poetic faculty ; and of late he have follow the lead of a popular writer of sonnet . he turn his poem over : they be almost equivalent to an autobiographical passage in his life . arrange they in their order , they come as follow : 

 " to Aimťe , walk with a Little child . " 

 " to Aimťe , sing at her work . " 

 " to Aimťe , turn away from I while I tell my love . " 

 " Aimťe 's Confession . " 

 " Aimťe in despair . " 

 " the Foreign Land in which my Aimťe dwell . " 

 " the Wedding Ring . " 

 " the Wife . " 

 when he come to this last sonnet he put down his bundle of paper and begin to think . " the wife . " yes , and a french wife ; and a roman catholic wife and a wife who might be say to have be in service ! and his father 's hatred of the French , both collectively and individually collectively , as tumultuous brutal ruffian , who murder their king , and commit all kind of bloody atrocity individually , as represent by " Boney , " and the various caricature of " Johnny Crapaud " that have be in full circulation about five - and - twenty year before this time , when the squire have be young and capable of receive impression . as for the form of religion in which Mrs. Osborne Hamley have be bring up , it be enough to say that catholic emancipation have begin to be talk about by some politician , and that the sullen roar of the majority of Englishmen , at the bare idea of it , be surge in the distance with ominous threatening ; the very mention of such a measure before the squire be , as Osborne well know , like shake a red flag before a bull . 

 and then he consider that if Aimťe have have the unspeakable , the incomparable blessing of be bear of english parent , in the very heart of England Warwickshire , for instance and have never hear of priest , or mass , or confession , or the Pope , or Guy Fawkes , but have be bear , baptize , and breed in the Church of England , without have ever see the outside of a dissent meeting - house , or a papist chapel even with all these advantage , her have be a   what be the equivalent for " bonne " in English ? ' nursery - governess ' be a term hardly invent   nursery - maid , with wage pay down once a quarter , liable to be dismiss at a month 's warning , and have her tea and sugar dole out to she , would be a shock to his father 's old ancestral pride that he would hardly ever get over . 

 " if he see she ! " think Osborne . " if he could but see she ! " but if the squire be to see Aimťe , he would also hear she speak her pretty broken English precious to her husband , as it be in it that she have confess brokenly with her english tongue , that she love he soundly with her french heart and Squire Hamley pique himself on be a good hater of the French . " she would make such a love , sweet , docile little daughter to my father she would go as near as any one could towards fill up the blank void in this house , if he would but have she ; but he will not ; he never would ; and he sha'n't   have the opportunity of scout she . yet if I call she ' Lucy ' in these sonnet ; and if they make a great effect be praise in Blackwood and the Quarterly and all the world be agog to find out the author ; and I tell he my secret I could if I be successful I think then he would ask who Lucy be , and I could tell he all then . if how I hate ' if . ' ' if I no ifs . ' my life have be base on ' when s ; ' and first they have turn to ' if , ' and then they have vanish away . it be ' when Osborne get honours , ' and then ' if Osborne , ' and then a failure altogether . I say to Aimťe , ' when my mother see you , ' and now it be ' if my father see she , ' with a very faint prospect of its ever come to pass . " so he let the evening hour flow on and disappear in revery like these ; wind up with a sudden determination to try the fate of his poem with a publisher , with the direct expectation of get money for they , and an ulterior fancy that , if successful , they might work wonder with his father . 

 when Roger come home Osborne do not let a day pass before tell his brother of his plan . he never do conceal anything long from Roger ; the feminine part of his character make he always desirous of a confidant , and as sweet sympathy as he could extract . but Roger 's opinion have no effect on Osborne 's action ; and Roger know this full well . so when Osborne begin with " I want your advice on a plan I have get in my head , " Roger reply : " some one tell I that the Duke of Wellington 's maxim be never to give advice unless he could enforce its be carry into effect ; now I can not   do that ; and you know , old boy , you do not   follow out my advice when you have   get it . " 

 " not always , I know . not when it do not   agree with my own opinion . you be think about this concealment of my marriage ; but you be not up in all the circumstance . you know how fully I mean to have do it , if there have not   be that row about my debt ; and then my mother 's illness and death . and now you have   no conception how my father be change how irritable he have become ! wait till you have   be at home a week ! Robinson , Morgan it be the same with they all ; but bad of all with I . " 

 " poor fellow ! " say Roger ; " I think he look terribly change : shrunken , and his ruddiness of complexion alter . "

I be desperately hard up for money , and so I have   be collect my poem weed they well , you know go over they quite critically , in fact ; and I want to know if you think Deighton would publish they . you have   a name in Cambridge , you know ; and I daresay he would look at they if you offer they to he . "

 " I do not   expect much . I be a new man , and must make my name . I should be content with a hundred . if I have a hundred pound I would set myself to do something .  " Australia ! why , Osborne , what could you do there ? and leave my father ! I hope you will never get your hundred pound , if that be the use you be to make of it ! why , you would break the squire 's heart . "

"

Father and son sate together , and exchange all the necessary speech connect with the occasion civilly enough ; but it be a relief to they when their intercourse be over , and they separate the father to brood over his sorrow and his disappointment , which be real and deep enough , and the injury he have receive from his boy , which be exaggerate in his mind by his ignorance of the actual step Osborne have take to raise money . if the money - lender have calculate the chance of his father 's life or death in make their bargain , Osborne himself have think only of how soon and how easily he could get the money requisite for clear he from all imperious claim at Cambridge , and for enable he to follow Aimťe to her home in Alsace , and for the subsequent marriage . as yet , Roger have never see his brother 's wife ; indeed , he have only be take into Osborne 's full confidence after all be decide in which his advice could have be useful . and now , in the enforced separation , Osborne 's whole thought , both the poetical and practical side of his mind , run upon the little wife who be pass her lonely day in farmhouse lodging , wonder when her bridegroom husband would come to she next . with such an engrossing subject , it be , perhaps , no wonder that he unconsciously neglect his father ; but it be none the less sad at the time , and to be regret in its consequence . 

 " I may come in and have a pipe with you , sir , may not   I ? " say Roger , that first evening , push gently against the study - door , which his father hold only half open . 

 " you will not like it , " say the squire , still hold the door against he , but speak in a relent tone . " the tobacco I use be not   what young man like . well go and have a cigar with Osborne . " 

 " no . I want to sit with you , and I can stand pretty strong tobacco . " 

 Roger push in , the resistance slowly give way before he . 

 " it will make your clothe smell . you will have to borrow Osborne 's scent to sweeten yourself , " say the Squire , grimly , at the same time push a short smart amber - mouthed pipe to his son . 

 " no ; I will have a churchwarden . why , father , do you think I be a baby to put up with a doll 's head like this ? " look at the carving upon it .

" and then for some time they smoke in silence . but the voluntary companionship of his son be very soothing to the squire , though not a word might be say .

" 

 " he be a good deal trouble about this money , and the anxiety he have give you , " say Roger , rather take his brother 's feeling for grant .

" 

 Roger laugh a little

" I be sure I do not . if you be a clever fellow like osborne yonder , you would be all for care for book and writing , and you would perhaps find it as dull as he do to keep company with a bumpkin - squire Jones like I . yet , I daresay , they think a deal of you at Cambridge , " say he , after a pause , ' since you have   get this fine wranglership ; I would nearly forget that the news come at such a miserable time . " 

 " well , yes ! they be always proud of the senior wrangler of the year up at Cambridge . next year I must abdicate . " 

 the Squire sit and gaze into the ember , still hold his useless pipe - stem . at last he say , in a low voice , as if scarcely aware he have get a listener , " I use to write to she when she be away in London , and tell she the home news . but no letter will reach she now ! nothing reach she ! " 

 Roger start up . 

 " where be the tobacco - box , father ? let I fill you another pipe ! " and when he have do so , he stoop over his father and stroke his cheek . the squire shake his head . 

 " you have   only just come home , lad . you do not   know I , as I be now - a - day ! ask Robinson I will not   have you ask Osborne , he ought to keep it to himself but any of the servant will tell you I be not like the same man for get into passion with they . I use to be reckon a good master , but that be past now ! Osborne be once a little boy , and she be once alive and I be once a good master a good master yes ! it be all past now . " 

 he take up his pipe , and begin to smoke afresh , and Roger , after a silence of some minute , begin a long story about some Cambridge man 's misadventure on the hunting - field , tell it with such humour that the squire be beguile into hearty laughing . when they rise to go to bed his father say to Roger , 

 " well , we have   have a pleasant evening at least , I have . but perhaps you have not ; for I be but poor company now , I know . "

 CHAPTER XXIV . 

 MRS . GIBSON be little dinner . 
 

 Illustrationll this have take place before Roger 's first meeting with Molly and Cynthia at Miss Brownings ' ; and the little dinner on the Friday at Mr. Gibson 's , which follow in due sequence .

"

 she put in the final reservation in all simplicity of heart ; and in all simplicity do he understand it . he come ever so little near , and drop his voice a little .

 CHAPTER XXV . 

 hollingford in a BUSTLE . 
 

 all Hollingford feel as if there be a great deal to be do before Easter this year . there be Easter proper , which always require new clothing of some kind , for fear of certain consequence from little bird , who be suppose to resent the impiety of those that do not wear some new article of dress on Easter - day . and most lady consider it wiser that the little bird should see the new article for themselves , and not have to take it upon trust , as they would have to do if it be merely a pocket - handkerchief , or a petticoat , or any article of under - clothing . so piety demand a new bonnet , or a new gown ; and be barely satisfied with an Easter pair of glove . Miss Rose be generally very busy just before Easter in Hollingford . then this year there be the charity ball . Ashcombe , Hollingford , and Coreham be three neighbouring town , of about the same number of population , lie at the three equidistant corner of a triangle . in imitation of great city with their festival , these three town have agree to have an annual ball for the benefit of the county hospital to be hold in turn at each place ; and Hollingford be to be the place this year . 

 it be a fine time for hospitality , and every house of any pretension be as full as it could hold , and fly be engage long month before . 

 if Mrs. Gibson could have ask Osborne , or in default , Roger Hamley to go to the ball with they and to sleep at their house , or if , indeed , she could have pick up any stray scion of a " county family " to whom such an offer would have be a convenience , she would have restore her own dressing - room to its former use as the spare - room , with pleasure . but she do not think it be worth she while to put herself out for any of the humdrum and ill - dress woman who have be her former acquaintance at Ashcombe . for Mr. Preston it might have be worth while to give up her room , consider he in the light of a handsome and prosperous young man , and a good dancer besides . but there be more light in which he be to be view . Mr. Gibson , who really want to return the hospitality show to he by Mr. Preston at the time of his marriage , have yet an instinctive distaste to the man , which no wish of free himself from obligation , nor even the more worthy feeling of hospitality , could overcome . Mrs. Gibson have some old grudge of her own against he , but she be not one to retain angry feeling , or be very active in her retaliation ; she be afraid of Mr. Preston , and admire he at the same time . it be awkward too so she say to go into a ball - room without any gentleman at all , and Mr. Gibson be so uncertain ! on the whole partly for this last - give reason , and partly because conciliation be the good policy , Mrs. Gibson be slightly in favour of invite Mr. Preston to be their guest . but as soon as Cynthia hear the question discuss or rather , as soon as she hear it discuss in Mr. Gibson 's absence , she say that if Mr. Preston come to be their visitor on the occasion , she for one would not go to the ball at all . she do not speak with vehemence or in anger ; but with such quiet resolution that molly look up in surprise . she see that Cynthia be keep her eye fix on her work , and that she have no intention of meet any one 's gaze , or give any further explanation . Mrs. Gibson , too , look perplexed , and once or twice seem on the point of ask some question ; but she be not angry as Molly have fully expect . she watch Cynthia furtively and in silence for a minute or two , and then say that , after all , she could not conveniently give up her dressing - room ; and , altogether , they have well say no more about it . so no stranger be invite to stay at Mr. Gibson 's at the time of the ball ; but Mrs. Gibson openly speak of her regret at the unavoidable inhospitality , and hop that they might be able to build an addition to their house before the next triennial Hollingford ball . 

 another cause of unusual bustle at Hollingford this Easter be the expected return of the family to the tower , after their unusually long absence . Mr. Sheepshanks might be see trot up and down on his stout old cob , speak to attentive mason , plasterer , and glazier about put everything on the outside at least about the cottage belong to " my lord , " in perfect repair . Lord Cumnor own the great part of the town ; and those who live under other landlord , or in house of their own , be stir up by the dread of contrast to do up their dwelling . so the ladder of whitewasher and painter be sadly in the way of the lady trip daintily along to make their purchase , and hold their gown up in a bunch behind , after a fashion quite go out in these day . the housekeeper and steward from the tower might also be see come in to give order at the various shop ; and stop here and there at those keep by favourite , to avail themselves of the eagerly - tender refreshment . 

 Lady Harriet come to call on her old governess the day after the arrival of the family at the tower . molly and Cynthia be out walk when she come do some errand for Mrs. Gibson , who have a secret idea that Lady Harriet would call at the particular time she do , and have a not uncommon wish to talk to her ladyship without the corrective presence of any member of her own family .

 CHAPTER XXVI . 

 a CHARITY ball . 
 

 at the present time there be few people at a public ball besides the dancer and their chaperone , or relation in some degree interested in they . but in the day when molly and Cynthia be young before railroad be , and before their consequence , the excursion - train , which take every one up to London now - a - day , there to see their fill of gay crowd and fine dress to go to an annual charity - ball , even though all thought of dancing have pass by year ago , and without any of the responsibility of a chaperone , be a very allowable and favourite piece of dissipation to all the kindly old maid who throng the country town of England . they air their old lace and their good dress ; they see the aristocratic magnate of the country side ; they gossippe with their coeval , and speculate on the romance of the young around they in a curious yet friendly spirit . the Miss Brownings would have think themselves sadly defraud of the gay event of the year , if anything have prevent their attend the charity ball , and Miss Browning would have be indignant , Miss Phobe aggrieve , have they not be ask to Ashcombe and Coreham , by friend at each place , who have , like they , go through the dancing - stage of life some five - and - twenty year before , but who like still to haunt the scene of their former enjoyment , and see a young generation dance on " regardless of their doom . " they have come in one of the two sedan - chair that yet linger in use at Hollingford ; such a night as this bring a regular harvest of gain to the two old man who , in what be call the " town 's livery , " trot backwards and forwards with their many load of lady and finery . there be some postchaise , and some " fly , " but after mature deliberation Miss Browning have decide to keep to the more comfortable custom of the sedan - chair ; " which , " as she say to Miss Piper , one of her visitor , " come into the parlour , and get full of the warm air , and nip you up , and carry you tight and cosy into another warm room , where you could walk out without have to show your leg by go up step , or down step . " of course only one could go at a time ; but here again a little of Miss Browning 's good management arrange everything so very nicely , as Miss Hornblower   their other visitor   remark . she go first , and remain in the warm cloak - room until her hostess follow ; and then the two lady go arm - in - arm into the ball - room , find out convenient seat whence they could watch the arrival and speak to their pass friend , until Miss Phobe and Miss Piper enter , and come to take possession of the seat reserve for they by Miss Browning 's care . these two young lady come in , also arm - in - arm , but with a certain timid flurry in look and movement very different from the compose dignity of their senior   by two or three year . when all four be once more assemble together , they take breath , and begin to converse . 

 " upon my word , I really do think this be a well room than our Ashcombe Court - house ! "

" 

 probably Mr. Roscoe would have feel more aggrieved by this speech than he really be , if his attention have not be call off just then by the entrance of the very Mr. Gibson who be be speak of . almost before Miss Browning have end her severe and depreciatory remark , he have ask his friend Miss Hornblower ,

 when Miss Hornblower return from her greeting to her former acquaintance , Mrs. Gibson , and have accomplish the introduction which Mr. Roscoe have request , she begin her little confidence to Miss Browning . 

 " well , to be sure ! how condescend we be !  so it be , for this be to be a very good ball , people say ; and a large party from the Towers be come , and a duchess in diamond among the number . every great house in the district be expect to be full of guest on these occasion ; but at this early hour , the townspeople have the floor almost entirely to themselves ; the county magnate come drop in later ; and chief among they all be the lord - lieutenant from the tower . but to - night they be unusually late , and the aristocratic ozone be absent from the atmosphere , there be a flatness about the dancing of all those who consider themselves above the plebeian rank of the tradespeople . they , however , enjoy themselves thoroughly , and sprang and bound till their eye sparkle and their cheek glow with exercise and excitement . some of the more prudent parent , mindful of the next day 's duty , begin to consider at what hour they ought to go home ; but with all there be an expressed or unexpressed curiosity to see the duchess and her diamond ; for the Menteith diamond be famous in high circle than that now assemble ; and their fame have trickle down to it through the medium of ladies'-maid and housekeeper .  " if Miss Gibson find any difficulty in refuse a partner , she have only to apply to Miss Kirkpatrick for instruction . " 

 Cynthia lift up her beautiful eye , and , fix they on Mr. Preston 's face , say , very quietly , as if only state a matter of fact ,

 and to the rest of this little conversation , Cynthia appear to lend no ear ; and she be almost directly claim by her next partner .  " Miss Kirkpatrick have not do I the honour of wear the bouquet I send she , I see . she receive it , I suppose , and my note ? "

 Mrs. Gibson be just the person to come to the rescue with her honey word on such an occasion as the present . she evidently be rather afraid of Mr. Preston , and wish to keep at peace with he .

" say Mr. Preston , with a certain accent of pleasure in his voice , as though he be glad she set so little store by the nosegay ; and he walk off to stand behind Cynthia in the quadrille that be be dance ; and molly see he make her reply to he against her will , Molly be sure . but , somehow , his face and manner imply power over she . she look grave , deaf , indifferent , indignant , defiant ; but , after a half - whisper speech to Cynthia , at the conclusion of the dance , she evidently throw he an impatient consent to what he be ask , for he walk off with a disagreeable smile of satisfaction on his handsome face . 

 all this time the murmur be spread at the lateness of the party from the tower , and person after person come up to Mrs. Gibson as if she be the accredited authority as to the earl and countess 's plan . in one sense this be flattering ; but then the acknowledgment of common ignorance and wonder reduce she to the level of the inquirer . I take it from my mother , who be such a one against waste as you never see now - a - day . she be a manager , if ever there be a one ; and bring up nine child on less than any one else could do , I will be bind . why ! she would not   let we be extravagant not even in the matter of cold . whenever any on we have get a pretty bad cold , she take the opportunity and cut our hair ; for she say , say she , it be of no use have two cold when one would do and cut of our hair be sure to give we a cold . but , for all that , I wish the duchess would come . "

" 

 every one of any pretension to gentility be painfully affect by the absence of the family from the tower ; the very fiddler seem unwilling to begin play a dance that might be interrupt by the entrance of the great folk . Miss Phobe Browning have apologize for they Miss Browning have blame they with calm dignity ; it be only the butcher and baker and candlestick - maker who rather enjoy the absence of restraint , and be happy and hilarious . 

 at last , there be a rumble , and a rushing , and a whispering , and the music stop ; so the dancer be oblige to do so too ; and in come Lord Cumnor in his state dress , with a fat , middle - aged woman on his arm ; she be dress almost like a girl in a sprig muslin , with natural flower in her hair , but not a vestige of a jewel or a diamond . yet it must be the duchess ; but what be a duchess without diamond ? and in a dress which farmer Hudson 's daughter might have wear ! be it the duchess ? could it be the duchess ? the little crowd of inquirer around Mrs. Gibson thicken , to hear she confirm their disappointing surmise . after the duchess come Lady Cumnor , look like Lady Macbeth in black velvet a cloud upon her brow , make more conspicuous by the line of age rapidly gather on her handsome face ; and Lady Harriet , and other lady , amongst whom there be one dress so like the duchess as to suggest the idea of a sister rather than a daughter , as far as dress go . there be Lord Hollingford , plain in face , awkward in person , gentlemanly in manner ; and half - a - dozen young man , Lord Albert Monson , Captain James , and other of their age and standing , who come in look anything if not critical . this long - expect party sweep up to the seat reserve for they at the head of the room , apparently regardless of the interruption they cause ; for the dancer stand aside , and almost disperse back to their seat , and when " money - musk " strike up again , not half the former set of people stand up to finish the dance . 

 Lady Harriet , who be rather different to Miss Piper , and no more minded cross the room alone than if the looker - on be so many cabbage , spy the Gibson party pretty quickly out , and come across to they . 

 " here we be at last . how d'ye do , dear ? why , little one "   to Molly , " how nice you be look ! be not   we shamefully late ? "

 " it be not   that ; it be that ill - mannered woman , who go to her own room after we come out from dinner , and she and Lady Alice stay there invisible , till we think they be put on some splendid attire as they ought to have do and at half - past ten , when mamma send up to they to say the carriage be at the door , the duchess send down for some beef - tea , and at last appear a l'enfant as you see she .  " deary I , your ladyship ! I hope I have   give no offence ! but , you see that be to say , your ladyship see , that it be late hour for such folk as I , and I only stay out of my bed to see the duchess , and I think she would come in diamond and a coronet ; and it put one out at my age , to be disappoint in the only chance I be like to have of so fine a sight . "

 she say this so sweetly that Mrs. Goodenough relax into a smile , and her crabbedness into a compliment .

Clare ! will you allow I to introduce my brother to Miss Gibson ? he hop to engage she for this dance . Lord Hollingford , Miss Gibson ! " 

 Poor Lord Hollingford ! there be nothing for it but for he to follow his sister 's very explicit lead , and Molly and he walk off to their place , each heartily wish their dance together well over .  molly find Lord Hollingford , the wise and learn Lord Hollingford , strangely stupid in understand the mystery of " cross hand and back again , down the middle and up again . Most girl of her age be so difficult to talk to ; but she be intelligent and full of interest in all sort of sensible thing ; well read , too she be up in Le Regne Animal and very pretty ! " 

 Mr. Gibson bow , much pleased at such a compliment from such a man , be he lord or not . it be very likely that if molly have be a stupid listener , Lord Hollingford would not have discover her beauty ; or the converse might be assert if she have not be young and pretty , he would not have exert himself to talk on scientific subject in a manner which she could understand . but in whatever way molly have win his approbation and admiration , there be no doubt that she have earn it somehow . and , when she next return to her place , Mrs. Gibson greet she with soft word and a gracious smile ; for it do not require much reasoning power to discover , that if it be a very fine thing to be mother - in - law to a very magnificent three - tail bashaw , it presuppose that the wife who make the connection between the two party be in harmony with her mother . and so far have Mrs. Gibson 's thought wander into futurity . she only wish that the happy chance have fall to Cynthia 's instead of to Molly 's lot . but Molly be a docile , sweet creature , very pretty , and remarkably intelligent , as my lord have say . it be a pity that Cynthia prefer make millinery to read ; but perhaps that could be rectify . and there be Lord Cumnor come to speak to she , and Lady Cumnor nod to she , and indicate a place by her side . 

 it be not an unsatisfactory ball upon the whole to Mrs. Gibson , although she pay the usual penalty for sit up beyond her ordinary hour in perpetual glare and movement . the next morning she awake irritable and fatigued ; and a little of the same feeling oppress both Cynthia and Molly . the former be lounge in the window - seat , hold a three - days'-old newspaper in her hand , which she be make a pretence of reading , when she be startle by her mother 's saying , 

 " Cynthia ! can not   you take up a book and improve yourself ? I be sure your conversation will never be worth listen to , unless you read something well than newspaper . why do not   you keep up your French ? there be some french book that molly be read Le Regne Animal , I think . "

 rather to Molly 's surprise , Cynthia do not reply a word ; but dutifully go and bring down from among her Boulogne school - book , Le Siecle de Louis XIV . but after a while , Molly see that this " improve reading " be just as much a mere excuse for Cynthia 's think her own thought as the newspaper have be . 

 

 

 CHAPTER XXVII . 

 FATHER and son . 
 

 illustrationhing be not go on any well at Hamley Hall . nothing have occur to change the state of dissatisfied feeling into which the Squire and his eld son have respectively fall ; and the long continuance merely of dissatisfaction be sure of itself to deepen the feeling . Roger do all in his power to bring the father and son together ; but sometimes wonder if it would not have be well to leave they alone ; for they be fall into the habit of each make he their confidant , and so define emotion and opinion which would have have less distinctness if they have be unexpresse . there be little enough relief in the daily life at the Hall to help they all to shake off the gloom ; and it even tell on the health of both the Squire and Osborne . the squire become thin , his skin as well as his clothe begin to hang loose about he , and the freshness of his colour turn to red streak , till his cheek look like Eardiston pippin , instead of resemble " a Katherine pear on the side that be next the sun . " Roger think that his father sate indoor and smoke in his study more than be good for he , but it have become difficult to get he far afield ; he be too much afraid of come across some sign of the discontinued drainage work , or be irritated afresh by the sight of his depreciate timber . Osborne be wrapt up in the idea of arrange his poem for the press , and so work out his wish for independence . what with daily writing to his wife take his letter himself to a distant post - office , and receive hers there touch up his sonnet ,   c. , with fastidious care and occasionally give himself the pleasure of a visit to the gibson , and enjoy the society of the two pleasant girl there , he find little time for be with his father . indeed , Osborne be too self - indulgent or ' sensitive , " as he term it , to bear well with the Squire 's gloomy fit , or too frequent querulousness . the consciousness of his secret , too , make osborne uncomfortable in his father 's presence . it be very well for all party that Roger be not ' sensitive , " for , if he have be , there be time when it would have be hard to bear little spurt of domestic tyranny , by which his father strive to assert his power over both his son . one of these occur very soon after the night of the Hollingford charity - ball . 

 Roger have induce his father to come out with he ; and the Squire have , on his son 's suggestion , take with he his long unused spud . the two have wander far afield ; perhaps the eld man have find the unwonted length of exercise too much for he ; for , as he approach the house , on his return , he become what nurse call in child " fractious , " and ready to turn on his companion for every remark he make . Roger understand the case by instinct , as it be , and bear it all with his usual sweetness of temper . they enter the house by the front door ; it lie straight on their line of march . on the old crack yellow - marble slab , there lie a card with Lord Hollingford 's name on it , which Robinson , evidently on the watch for their return , hasten out of his pantry to deliver to Roger . 

 " his lordship be very sorry not to see you , Mr. Roger , and his lordship leave a note for you . Mr. Osborne take it , I think , when he pass through . I ask his lordship if he would like to see Mr. Osborne , who be indoor , as I think . but his lordship say he be press for time , and tell I to make his excuse . " 

 " do not   he ask for I ? " growl the squire . 

 " no , sir ; I can not   say as his lordship do . he would never have think of Mr. Osborne , sir , if I have not   name he . it be Mr. Roger he seem so keen after . " 

 " very odd , " say the Squire . Roger say nothing , although he naturally feel some curiosity . he go into the drawing - room , not quite aware that his father be follow he . osborne sate at a table near the fire , pen in hand , look over one of his poem , and dot the I be , cross the t 's , and now and then pause over the alteration of a word . 

 " oh , Roger ! " he say , as his brother come in , " here be be Lord Hollingford want to see you . " 

 " I know , " reply Roger . 

 " and he be leave a note for you . Robinson try to persuade he it be for my father , so he be add a ' junior '   Roger Hamley , Esq . , junior   in pencil . " the squire be in the room by this time , and what he have overhear rub he up still more the wrong way . Roger take his unopened note and read it . 

 " what do he say ? " ask the Squire . 

 Roger hand he the note . it contain an invitation to dinner to meet M. Geoffroi St. H , whose view on certain subject Roger have be advocate in the article Lord Hollingford have speak about to Molly , when he dance with she at the Hollingford ball . M. Geoffroi St. H be in England now , and be expect to pay a visit at the tower in the course of the following week . he have express a wish to meet the author of the paper which have already attract the attention of the french comparative anatomist ; and Lord Hollingford add a few word as to his own desire to make the acquaintance of a neighbour whose taste be so similar to his own ; and then follow a civil message from Lord and Lady Cumnor . 

 Lord Hollingford 's hand be cramp and rather illegible . the squire could not read it all at once , and be enough put out to decline any assistance in decipher it . at last he make it out . 

 " so my lord lieutenant be take some notice of the Hamleys at last . the election be come on , be it ? but I can tell he we be not to be get so easily . I suppose this trap be set for you , Osborne ? what be this you have   be write that the french mounseer be so take with ? " 

 " it be not I , sir ! " say Osborne . " both note and call be for Roger . " 

 " I do not   understand it , " say the Squire . " these Whig fellow have never do their duty by I ; not that I want it of they . the Duke of Debenham use to pay the Hamleys a respect due to them the old landowner in the county but since he die , and this shabby Whig lord have succeed he , I have   never din at the lord lieutenant 's no , not once . " 

 " but I think , sir , I have   hear you say Lord Cumnor use to invite you , only you do not choose to go , " say Roger . 

 " yes . what d'ye mean by that ? do you suppose I be go to desert the principle of my family , and curry favour with the whig ? no ! leave that to they . they can ask the heir of the Hamleys fast enough when a county election be come on . " 

 " I tell you , sir , " say Osborne , in the irritable tone he sometimes use when his father be particularly unreasonable , " it be not I Lord Hollingford be invite ; it be Roger . Roger be make himself know for what he be , a first - rate fellow , " continue Osborne a sting of self - reproach mingling with his generous pride in his brother " and he be get himself a name ; he be be write about these new french theory and discovery , and this foreign savant very naturally want to make his acquaintance , and so Lord Hollingford ask he to dine . it be as clear as can be , " lower his tone , and address himself to Roger ; " it have nothing to do with politic , if my father would but see it . " 

 of course the Squire hear this little aside with the unlucky uncertainty of hearing which be a characteristic of the beginning of deafness ; and its effect on he be perceptible in the increase acrimony of his next speech . 

 " you young man think you know everything . I tell you it be a palpable Whig trick . and what business have Roger if it be Roger the man want to go curry favour with the French ? in my day we be content to hate them and to lick them . but it be just like your conceit , Osborne , set yourself up to say it be your young brother they be ask , and not you ; I tell you it be you . they think the eld son be sure to be call after his father , Roger Roger Hamley , junior . it be as plain as a pike - staff . they know they can not   catch I with chaff , but they have   get up this french dodge . what business have you to go write about the French , Roger ? I should have think you be too sensible to take any notice of their fancy and theory ; but if it be you they have   ask , I will not have you go and meet these foreigner at a Whig house . they ought to have ask Osborne . he be the representative of the Hamleys , if I be not ; and they can not   get I , let them try ever so . besides , Osborne have get a bit of the mounseer about he , which he catch with be so fond of go off to the continent , instead of come back to his good old English home . "

 CHAPTER XXVIII . 

 RIVALRY .

 CHAPTER XXIX . 

 BUSH - fighting . 
 

 during all the month that have elapse since Mrs. Hamley 's death , Molly have wonder many a time about the secret she have so unwittingly become possessed of that last day in the Hall library . it seem so utterly strange and unheard - of a thing to her inexperienced mind , that a man should be marry , and yet not live with his wife that a son should have enter into the holy state of matrimony without his father 's knowledge , and without be recognize as the husband of some one know or unknown by all those with whom he come in daily contact , that she feel occasionally as if that little ten minute of revelation must have be a vision in a dream .
