 Hyacinth C. K. Gibson . 
 

 there be no resist this , even if there have not be strong inclination to back up the pretty word . Roger go , and Mrs. Gibson caress and pet he in her sweetest , silky manner . Cynthia look lovely than ever to he for the slight restriction that have be lay for a time on their intercourse . she might be gay and sparkling with Osborne ; with Roger she be soft and grave . instinctively she know her man . she see that Osborne be only interested in she because of her position in a family with whom he be intimate ; that his friendship be without the least touch of sentiment ; and that his admiration be only the warm criticism of an artist for unusual beauty . but she feel how different Roger 's relation to she be . to he she be the one , alone , peerless . if his love be prohibit , it would be long year before he could sink down into tepid friendship ; and to he her personal loveliness be only one of the many charm that make he tremble into passion . Cynthia be not capable of return such feeling ; she have have too little true love in her life , and perhaps too much admiration to do so ; but she appreciate this honest ardour , this loyal worship that be new to her experience . such appreciation , and such respect for his true and affectionate nature , give a serious tenderness to her manner to Roger , which allure he with a fresh and separate grace . molly sate by , and wonder how it would all end , or , rather , how soon it would all end , for she think that no girl could resist such reverent passion ; and on Roger 's side there could be no doubt alas ! there could be no doubt . an old spectator might have look far ahead , and thought of the question of pound , shilling , and penny . where be the necessary income for a marriage to come from ? Roger have his fellowship now , it be true ; but the income of that would be lose if he marry ; he have no profession , and the life interest of the two or three thousand pound that he inherit from his mother , belong to his father . this old spectator might have be a little surprised at the empressement of Mrs. Gibson 's manner to a young son , always suppose this say spectator to have read to the depth of her worldly heart . never have she try to be more agreeable to Osborne ; and though her attempt be a great failure when practise upon Roger , and he do not know what to say in reply to the delicate flattery which he feel to be insincere , he see that she intend he to consider himself henceforward free of the house ; and he be too glad to avail himself of this privilege to examine over - closely into what might be her motif for her change of manner . he shut his eye , and choose to believe that she be now desirous of make up for her little burst of temper on his previous visit . 

 the result of Osborne 's conference with the two doctor have be certain prescription which appear to have do he much good , and which would in all probability have do he yet more , could he have be free of the recollection of the little patient wife in her solitude near Winchester . he go to she whenever he could ; and , thank to Roger , money be far more plentiful with he now than it have be . but he still shrink , and perhaps even more and more , from tell his father of his marriage . some bodily instinct make he dread all agitation inexpressibly . if he have not have this money from Roger , he might have be compel to tell his father all , and to ask for the necessary fund to provide for the wife and the come child . but with enough in hand , and a secret , though remorseful , conviction that as long as Roger have a penny his brother be sure to have half of it , make he more reluctant than ever to irritate his father by a revelation of his secret . " not just yet , not just at present , " he keep say both to Roger and to himself . " by - and - by , if we have a boy , I will call it Roger " and then vision of poetical and romantic reconciliation bring about between father and son , through the medium of a child , the offspring of a forbidden marriage , become still more vividly possible to he , and at any rate it be a staving - off of an unpleasant thing . he atone to himself for take so much of Roger 's Fellowship money by reflect that , if Roger marry , he would lose this source of revenue ; yet Osborne be throw no impediment in the way of this event , rather forward it by promote every possible mean of his brother 's see the lady of his love . Osborne end his reflection by convince himself of his own generosity . 

 

 

 CHAPTER XXX . 

 old WAYS and new WAYS .

Preston be now instal in his new house at Hollingford ; Mr. Sheepshanks have enter into dignified idleness at the house of his married daughter , who live in the county town . his successor have plunge with energy into all manner of improvement ; and among other , he fall to drain a piece of outlying waste and unreclaimed land of Lord Cumnor 's , which be close to Squire Hamley 's property that very piece for which he have have the Government grant , but which now lie neglect , and only half drain , with stack of mossy tile , and line of upturned furrow tell of abortive plan . it be not often that the Squire ride in this direction now - a - day ; but the cottage of a man who have be the squire 's gamekeeper in those more prosperous day when the Hamleys could afford to " preserve , " be close to the rush - grow ground . this old servant and tenant be ill , and have send a message up to the Hall , ask to see the squire : not to reveal any secret , or to say anything particular , but only from the feudal loyalty , which make it seem to the die man as if it would be a comfort to shake the hand , and look once more into the eye of the lord and master whom he have serve , and whose ancestor his own forbear have serve for so many generation . and the squire be as fully alive as old Silas to the claim of the tie that exist between they . though he hat the thought , and still more , should hate the sight of the piece of land , on the side of which Silas 's cottage stand , the Squire order his horse , and ride off within half - an - hour of receive the message . as he draw near the spot he think he hear the sound of tool , and the hum of many voice , just as he use to hear they a year or two before . he listen with surprise . yes ! instead of the still solitude he have expect , there be the clink of iron , the heavy gradual thud of the fall of barrow - ful of soil the cry and shout of labourer . but not on his land well worth expense and trouble by far than the reedy clay common on which the man be , in fact , employ . he know it be Lord Cumnor 's property ; and he know Lord Cumnor and his family have go up in the world   " the Whig rascal ! " , both in wealth and in station , as the Hamleys have go down . but all the same in spite of long know fact , and in spite of reason the Squire 's ready anger rise high at the sight of his neighbour do what he have be unable to do , and he a Whig , and his family only in the county since Queen Anne 's time . he go so far as to wonder whether they might not the labourer he mean avail themselves of his tile , lie so conveniently close to hand . all these thought , regret , and wonder be in his mind as he ride up to the cottage he be bind to , and give his horse in charge to a little lad , who have hitherto find his morning 's business and amusement in play at " house " with a still young sister , with some of the Squire 's neglect tile . but he be old Silas 's grandson , and he might have batter the rude red earthenware to piece a whole stack one by one , and the squire would have say little or nothing . it be only that he would not spare one to a labourer of Lord Cumnor 's . no ! not one . 

 Old Silas lie in a sort of closet , open out of the family living - room . the small window that give it light look right on to the " moor , " as it be call ; and by day the check curtain be draw aside so that he might watch the progress of the labour . everything about the old man be clean , if coarse ; and , with death , the leveller , so close at hand , it be the labourer who make the first advance , and put out his horny hand to the squire . 

 " I think you would come , Squire . your father come for to see my father as he lie a - dying . " 

 " come , come , my man ! " say the Squire , easily affect , as he always be . " do not   talk of die , we shall soon have you out , never fear . they have   send you up some soup from the Hall , as I bid them , have not   they ? " 

 " ay , ay , I have   have all as I could want for to eat and to drink . the young squire and Master Roger be here yesterday . " 

 " yes , I know . " 

 " but I be a deal near Heaven to - day , I be . I should like you to look after th ' cover in th ' West Spinney , Squire ; they gorse , you know , where th ' old fox have her hole she as give them so many a run . you will mind it , Squire , though you be but a lad . I could laugh to think on her trick yet . " and , with a weak attempt at a laugh , he get himself into a violent fit of coughing , which alarm the squire , who think he would never get his breath again . his daughter - in - law come in at the sound , and tell the squire that he have these cough - bout very frequently , and that she think he would go off in one of they before long . this opinion of hers be speak simply out before the old man , who now lay gasp and exhaust upon his pillow . poor people acknowledge the inevitableness and the approach of death in a much more straightforward manner than be customary among more educate folk . the squire be shock at her hard - heartedness , as he consider it ; but the old man himself have receive much tender kindness from his daughter - in - law ; and what she have just say be no more news to he than the fact that the sun would rise to - morrow . he be more anxious to go on with his story . 

 " they navvy I call them navvy because some on them be stranger , though some on them be th ' man as be turn off your own work , squire , when there come order to stop them last fall they be a - pull up gorse and brush to light their fire for warm up their mess . it be a long way off to their home , and they mostly dine here ; and there will be nothing of a cover leave , if you do not   see after them . I think I should like to tell ye afore I die . Parson 's be here ; but I do na tell he . he be all for the earl 's folk , and he would not ha ' heed . it be the earl as put he into his church , I reckon , for he say what a fine thing it be for to see so much employment a - give to the poor , and he never say nought o ' th ' sort when your work be agait , Squire . " 

 this long speech have be interrupt by many a cough and gasp for breath ; and have deliver himself of what be on his mind , he turn his face to the wall , and appear to be go to sleep . presently he rouse himself with a start : 

 " I know I flog he well , I do . but he be after pheasant ' egg , and I do not   know he be an orphan . Lord , forgive I ! " 

 " he be think on David Morton , the cripple , as use to go about trap vermin , " whisper the woman . 

 " why , he die long ago twenty year , I should think , " reply the squire .

 so , give sixpence to the child who have hold his horse , the squire mount . he sate still a moment , look at the busy work go on before he , and then at his own half - complete drainage . it be a bitter pill . he have object to borrow from Government , in the first instance ; and then his wife have persuade he to the step ; and after it be once take , he be as proud as could be of the only concession to the spirit of progress he ever make in his life . he have read and study the subject pretty thoroughly , if also very slowly , during the time his wife have be influence he . he be tolerably well up in agriculture , if in nothing else ; and at one time he have take the lead among the neighbouring landowner , when he first begin tile - drainage . in those day people use to speak of Squire Hamley 's hobby ; and at market ordinary , or county dinner , they rather dread set he off on long repetition of argument from the different pamphlet on the subject which he have read . and now the proprietor all around he be drain drain ; his interest to Government be run on all the same , though his work be stop , and his tile deteriorate in value . it be not a soothe consideration , and the squire be almost ready to quarrel with his shadow . he want a vent for his ill - humour ; and suddenly remember the devastation on his cover , which he have hear about not a quarter of an hour before , he ride towards the man busy at work on Lord Cumnor 's land . just before he get up to they he encounter Mr. Preston , also on horseback , come to overlook his labourer . the squire do not know he personally , but from the agent 's manner of speak , and the deference that be evidently pay to he , Mr. Hamley see that he be a responsible person . so he address the agent : " I beg your pardon , I suppose you be the manager of these work ? " 

 Mr. Preston reply , " certainly . I be that and many other thing besides , at your service . I have succeed Mr. Sheepshanks in the management of my lord 's property . Mr. Hamley of Hamley , I believe ? " 

 the Squire bow stiffly . he do not like his name to be ask or presume upon in that manner . an equal might conjecture who he be , or recognize he , but , till he announce himself , an inferior have no right to do more than address he respectfully as ' sir . " that be the Squire 's code of etiquette . 

 " I be Mr. Hamley of Hamley . I suppose you be as yet ignorant of the boundary of Lord Cumnor 's land , and so I will inform you that my property begin at the pond yonder just where you see the rise in the ground . " 

 " I be perfectly acquaint with that fact , Mr. Hamley , " say Mr. Preston , a little annoyed at the ignorance attribute to he . " but may I inquire why my attention be call to it just now ? " 

 the squire be begin to boil over ; but he try to keep his temper in . the effort be very much to be respect , for it be a great one . there be something in the handsome and well - dress agent 's tone and manner inexpressibly irritate to the squire , and it be not lessen by an involuntary comparison of the capital roadster on which Mr. Preston be mount with his own ill - groom and aged cob . 

 " I have be tell that your man out yonder do not respect these boundary , but be in the habit of pluck up gorse from my cover to light their fire . " 

 " it be possible they may ! " say Mr. Preston , lift his eyebrow , his manner be more nonchalant than his word . " I daresay they think no great harm of it . however , I will inquire . " 

 " do you doubt my word , sir ? " say the Squire , fret his mare till she begin to dance about . " I tell you I have   hear it only within this last half - hour . " 

 " I do not   mean to doubt your word , Mr. Hamley ; it be the last thing I should think of do . but you must excuse my saying that the argument which you have twice bring up for the authenticity of your statement , ' that you have hear it within the last half - hour , ' be not quite so forcible as to preclude the possibility of a mistake . " 

 " I wish you would only say in plain language that you doubt my word , " say the Squire , clenching and slightly raise his horsewhip . " I can not   make out what you mean you use so many word . " 

 " pray do not   lose your temper , sir . I say I should inquire . you have not see the man pull up gorse yourself , or you would have name it . I , surely , may doubt the correctness of your information until I have make some inquiry ; at any rate , that be the course I shall pursue , and if it give you offence , I shall be sorry , but I shall do it just the same . when I be convinced that harm have be do to your property , I shall take step to prevent it for the future , and of course , in my lord 's name , I shall pay you compensation it may probably amount to half - a - crown . " he add these last word in a low tone , as if to himself , with a slight contemptuous smile on his face . 

 " quiet , mare , quiet , " say the Squire , totally unaware that he be the cause of her impatient movement by the way he be perpetually tighten her rein ; and also , perhaps , he unconsciously address the injunction to himself . 

 neither of they see Roger Hamley , who be just then approach they with long , steady step . he have see his father from the door of old Silas 's cottage , and , as the poor fellow be still asleep , he be come to speak to his father , and be near enough now to hear the next word . 

 " I do not   know who you be , but I have   know land - agent who be gentleman , and I have   know some who be not . you belong to this last set , young man , " say the squire , " that you do . I should like to try my horsewhip on you for your insolence . " 

 " pray , Mr. Hamley , " reply Mr. Preston , coolly , " curb your temper a little , and reflect . I really feel sorry to see a man of your age in such a passion : " move a little far off , however , but really more with a desire to save the irritated man from carry his threat into execution , out of a dislike to the slander and excitement it would cause , than from any personal dread . just at this moment Roger Hamley come close up . he be pant a little , and his eye be very stern and dark ; but he speak quietly enough . 

 " Mr. Preston , I can hardly understand what you mean by your last word . but , remember , my father be a gentleman of age and position , and not accustomed to receive advice as to the management of his temper from young man like you . "

 " I desire he to keep his man off my land , " say the Squire to his son his wish to stand well in Roger 's opinion restrain his temper a little ; but though his word might be a little calm , there be all other sign of passion present the discolour complexion , the trembling hand , the fiery cloud in his eye . " he refuse , and doubt my word . " 

 Mr. Preston turn to Roger , as if appeal from Philip drunk to Philip sober , and speak in a tone of cool explanation , which , though not insolent in word , be excessively irritate in manner . 

 " your father have misunderstand I perhaps it be no wonder , " try to convey , by a look of intelligence at the son , his opinion that the father be in no state to hear reason . " I never refuse to do what be just and right . I only require further evidence as to the past wrong - doing ; your father take offence at this , " and then he shrug his shoulder , and lift his eyebrow in a manner he have formerly learn in France .

 Roger bow to his father , but take up his speech at the point it be at before the interruption .

I be go to see old Silas perhaps you do not   know that he be very ill . " so he endeavour to wile the squire away to prevent further word . he be not entirely successful . 

 Mr. Preston be enrage by Roger 's calm and dignified manner , and throw after they this parting shaft , in the shape of a loud soliloquy , 

 " Position , indeed ! what be we to think of the position of a man who begin work like these without count the cost , and come to a stand - still , and have to turn off his labourer just at the beginning of winter , leave " 

 they be too far off to hear the rest . the squire be on the point of turn back before this , but Roger take hold of the rein of the old mare , and lead she over some of the boggy ground , as if to guide she into sure footing , but , in reality , because he be determined to prevent the renewal of the quarrel . it be well that the cob know he , and be , indeed , old enough to prefer quietness to dancing ; for Mr. Hamley pluck hard at the rein , and at last break out with an oath , " Damn it , Roger ! I be not a child ; I will not   be treat as such . leave go , I say ! " 

 Roger let go ; they be now on firm ground , and he do not wish any watcher to think that he be exercise any constraint over his father ; and this quiet obedience to his impatient command do more to soothe the squire than anything else could have effect just then . 

 " I know I turn they off what could I do ? I have no more money for their weekly wage ; it be a loss to I , as you know . he do not   know , no one know , but I think your mother would , how it cut I to turn them off just before winter set in . I lie awake many a night thinking of it , and I give they what I have I do , indeed . I have not   get money to pay them , but I have three barren cow fatten , and give every scrap of meat to the man , and I let them go into the wood and gather what be fall , and I wink at their break off old branch , and now to have it cast up against I by that cur that servant . but I will go on with the work , by , I will , if only to spite he . I will show he who I be . my position , indeed ! a Hamley of Hamley take a high position than his master . I will go on with the work , see if I do not ! I be pay between one and two hundred a year interest on Government money . I will raise some more if I go to the Jews ; Osborne have show I the way , and Osborne shall pay for it he shall .  he be lash himself again into an impotent rage , painful to a son to witness ; but just then the little grandchild of old Silas , who have hold the Squire 's horse during his visit to the sick man , come run up , breathless : 

 " please , sir , please , squire , mammy have send I ; grandfather have waken up sudden , and mammy say he be die , and would you please come ; she say he would take it as a kind compliment , she be sure . " 

 so they go to the cottage , the squire speak never a word , but suddenly feel as if lift out of a whirlwind and set down in a still and awful place . 

 

 

 CHAPTER XXXI . 

 a PASSIVE COQUETTE . 
 

 it be not to be suppose that such an encounter as Mr. Preston have just have with Roger Hamley sweeten the regard in which the two young man henceforward hold each other . they have barely speak to one another before , and but seldom meet ; for the land - agent 's employment have hitherto lain at Ashcombe , some sixteen or seventeen mile from Hamley . he be old than Roger by several year ; but during the time he have be in the county Osborne and Roger have be at school and at college . Mr. Preston be prepared to dislike the Hamleys for many unreasonable reason . Cynthia and Molly have both speak of the brother with familiar regard , imply considerable intimacy ; their flower have be prefer to his on the occasion of the ball ; most people speak well of they ; and Mr. Preston have an animal 's instinctive jealousy and combativeness against all popular young man . their " position " poor as the Hamleys might be be far high than his own in the county ; and , moreover , he be agent to the great Whig lord , whose political interest be diametrically oppose to those of the old Tory squire . not that Lord Cumnor trouble himself much about his political interest . his family have obtain property and title from the whig at the time of the hanoverian succession ; and so , traditionally , he be a Whig , and have belong in his youth to Whig club , where he have lose considerable sum of money to Whig gambler . all this be satisfactory and consistent enough . and if Lord Hollingford have not be return for the county on the Whig interest as his father have be before he , until he have succeed to the title it be quite probable Lord Cumnor would have consider the british constitution in danger , and the patriotism of his ancestor ungratefully ignore . but , except at election , he have no notion of make Whig and Tory a party cry . he have live too much in London , and be of too sociable a nature , to exclude any man who jump with his humour from the hospitality he be always ready to offer , be the agreeable acquaintance Whig , Tory , or Radical . but in the county of which he be lord - lieutenant , the old party distinction be still a shibboleth by which man be test as to their fitness for social intercourse , as well as on the husting . if by any chance a Whig find himself at a Tory dinner - table or vice ver   the food be hard of digestion , and wine and viand be criticize rather than enjoy . a marriage between the young people of the separate party be almost as unheard - of and prohibit an alliance as that of Romeo and Juliet 's . and of course Mr. Preston be not a man in whose breast such prejudice would die away . they be an excitement to he for one thing , and call out all his talent for intrigue on behalf of the party to which he be ally . moreover , he consider it as loyalty to his employer to ' scatter his enemy " by any mean in his power . he have always hat and despise the Tories in general ; and after that interview on the marshy common in front of Silas 's cottage , he hat the Hamleys and Roger especially , with a very choice and particular hatred . " that prig , " as hereafter he always designate Roger " he shall pay for it yet , " he say to himself by way of consolation , after the father and son have leave he . " what a lout it be ! " watch the recede figure , " the old chap have twice as much spunk , " as the squire tug at his bridle rein . " the old mare could make her way well without be lead , my fine fellow . but I see through your dodge . you be afraid of your old father turn back and get into another rage . position indeed ! a beggarly squire a man who do turn off his man just before winter , to rot or starve , for all he care it be just like a brutal old tory . " and , under the cover of sympathy with the dismiss labourer , Mr. Preston indulge his own private pique very pleasantly . 

 Mr. Preston have many cause for rejoice : he might have forget this discomfiture , as he choose to feel it , in the remembrance of an increase of income , and in the popularity he enjoy in his new abode . all Hollingford come forward to do the earl 's new agent honour . Mr. Sheepshanks have be a crabbed , crusty old bachelor , frequent inn - parlour on market day , not unwilling to give dinner to three or four choose friend and familiar , with whom , in return , he din from time to time , and with whom , also , he keep up an amicable rivalry in the matter of wine . but he " do not appreciate female society , " as Miss Browning elegantly word his unwillingness to accept the invitation of the Hollingford lady . he be even unrefined enough to speak of these invitation to his intimate friend aforesaid as " those old woman 's worrying , " but , of course , they never hear of this . little quarter - of - sheet note , without any envelope that invention be unknown in those day but seal in the corner when fold up instead of gum as they be fasten at present occasionally pass between Mr. Sheepshanks and the Miss Brownings , Mrs. Goodenough or other . from the first - mention lady the form run as follow : " Miss Browning and her sister , Miss Phobe Browning , present their respectful compliment to Mr. Sheepshanks , and beg to inform he that a few friend have kindly consent to favour they with their company at tea on Thursday next . Miss Browning and Miss Phobe will take it very kindly if Mr. Sheepshanks will join their little circle . " 

 now for Mrs. Goodenough . 

 " Mrs. Goodenough 's respect to Mr. Sheepshanks , and hop he be in good health . she would be very glad if he would favour she with his company to tea on Monday . my daughter , in Combermere , have send I a couple of guinea - fowl , and Mrs. Goodenough hop Mr. Sheepshanks will stay and take a bit of supper . " 

 no need for the date of the day of the month . the good lady would have think that the world be come to an end if the invitation have be send out a week before the party therein name . but not even guinea - fowl for supper could tempt Mr. Sheepshanks . he remember the make - wine he have taste in former day at Hollingford party , and shudder . bread - and - cheese , with a glass of bitter - beer , or a little brandy - and - water , partake of in his old clothe   which have wear into shape of loose comfort , and smell strongly of tobacco , he like well than roast guinea - fowl and birch - wine , even without throw into the balance the stiff uneasy coat , and the tight neckcloth and tight shoe . so the ex - agent have be seldom , if ever , see at the Hollingford tea - party . he might have have his form of refusal stereotype , it be so invariably the same . 

 " Mr. Sheepshanks ' duty to Miss Browning and her sister "   to Mrs. Goodenough , or to other , as the case might be . " business of importance prevent he from avail himself of their polite invitation ; for which he beg to return his good thank . " 

 but now that Mr. Preston have succeed , and come to live in Hollingford , thing be change . 

 he accept every civility right and leave , and win golden opinion accordingly . party be make in his honour , " just as if he have be a bride , " Miss Phobe Browning say ; and to all of they he go . 

 " what be the man after ? " say Mr. Sheepshanks to himself , when he hear of his successor 's affability , and sociability , and amiability , and a variety of other agreeable " ilitie , " from the friend whom the old steward still retain at Hollingford . " Preston be not a man to put himself out for nothing . he be deep . he will be after something solider than popularity . " 

 the sagacious old bachelor be right . Mr. Preston be " after " something more than mere popularity . he go wherever he have a chance of meet Cynthia Kirkpatrick . 

 it might be that Molly 's spirit be more depressed at this time than they be in general ; or that Cynthia be exultant , unawares to herself , in the amount of attention and admiration she be receive from Roger by day , from Mr. Preston in the evening , but the two girl seem to have part company in cheerfulness . molly be always gentle , but very grave and silent . Cynthia , on the contrary , be merry , full of pretty mockery , and hardly ever silent . when first she come to Hollingford one of her great charm have be that she be such a gracious listener ; now her excitement , by whatever cause , make she too restless to hold her tongue ; yet what she say be too pretty , too witty , not to be a win and sparkle interruption , eagerly welcome by those who be under her sway . Mr. Gibson be the only one who observe this change , and reason upon it . " she be in a mental fever of some kind , " think he to himself . " she be very fascinating , but I do not   quite understand she . " 

 if Molly have not be so entirely loyal to her friend , she might have think this constant brilliancy a little tiresome when bring into every - day life ; it be not the sunshiny rest of a placid lake , it be rather the glitter of the piece of a broken mirror , which confuse and bewilder . Cynthia would not talk quietly about anything now ; subject of thought or conversation seem to have lose their relative value . there be exception to this mood of her , when she sink into deep fit of silence , that would have be gloomy have it not be for the never vary sweetness of her temper . if there be a little kindness to be do to either Mr. Gibson or Molly , Cynthia be just as ready as ever to do it ; nor do she refuse to do anything her mother wish , however fidgety might be the humour that prompt the wish . but in this latter case Cynthia 's eye be not quicken by her heart . 

 Molly be deject , she know not why .  but he be always exactly the same ; ' steady as old Time , " as Mrs. Gibson call he , with her usual originality ; " a rock of strength , under whose very shadow there be rest , " as Mrs. Hamley have once speak of he . so the cause of Mrs. Gibson 's altered manner lie not in he . yet now he be sure of a welcome , let he come at any hour he would . he be playfully reproved for have take Mrs. Gibson 's word too literally , and for never come before lunch . but he say he consider her reason for such word to be valid , and should respect they . and this be do out of his simplicity , and from no tinge of malice . then in their family conversation at home , Mrs. Gibson be constantly make project for throw Roger and Cynthia together , with so evident a betrayal of her wish to bring about an engagement , that molly chafe at the net spread so evidently , and at Roger 's blindness in come so willingly to be entrap . she forget his previous willingness , his former evidence of manly fondness for the beautiful Cynthia ; she only see plot of which he be the victim , and Cynthia the conscious if passive bait . she feel as if she could not have act as Cynthia do ; no , not even to gain Roger 's love .  it may be all very pleasant " to sport with Amaryllis in the shade , or with the tangle of Neara 's hair , " but young man at the outset of their independent life have many other care in this prosaic England to occupy their time and their thought . Roger be Fellow of Trinity , to be sure ; and from the outside it certainly appear as if his position , as long as he choose to keep unmarried , be a very easy one . his be not a nature , however , to sink down into inglorious ease , even have his fellowship income be at his disposal . he look forward to an active life ; in what direction he have not yet determine . he know what be his talent and his taste ; and do not wish the former to lie bury , nor the latter , which he regard as gift , fit he for some peculiar work , to be disregard or thwart . he rather like await an object , secure in his own energy to force his way to it , when once he see it clearly . he reserve enough of money for his own personal need , which be small , and for the ready furtherance of any project he might see fit to undertake ; the rest of his income be Osborne 's ; give and accept in the spirit which make the bond between these two brother so rarely perfect . it be only the thought of Cynthia that throw Roger off his balance . a strong man in everything else , about she he be as a child . he know that he could not marry and retain his fellowship ; his intention be to hold himself loose from any employment or profession until he have find one to his mind , so there be no immediate prospect no prospect for many year , indeed , that he would be able to marry . yet he go on seek Cynthia 's sweet company , listen to the music of her voice , bask in her sunshine , and feed his passion in every possible way , just like an unreasone child . he know that it be folly and yet he do it ; and it be perhaps this that make he so sympathetic with Osborne .  CHAPTER XXXII . 

 come event .

' the truth , the whole truth , and nothing but the truth , ' have always seem to I such a fine passage . but then I have so much that be unbend in my nature , and in our sphere of life there be so few temptation . if we be humble , we be also simple , and unshackle by etiquette . " 

 " then you blame I very much ? if somebody else will blame I , I sha'n't   be so unhappy at what I say this morning . " 

 " I be sure I never blame you , not in my innermost heart , dear Lady Harriet . blame you , indeed ! that would be presumption in I . " 

 " I think I shall set up a confessor ! and it sha'n't   be you , Clare , for you have always be only too indulgent to I . " 

 after a pause she say , " can you give I some lunch , Clare ? I do not   mean to go home till three . my ' business ' will take I till then , as the people at the tower be duly inform . " 

 " certainly . I shall be delighted ! but you know we be very simple in our habit . " 

 " oh , I only want a little bread - and - butter , and perhaps a slice of cold meat you must not give yourself any trouble , Clare perhaps you dine now ? let I sit down just like one of your family . " 

 " yes , you shall ; I will not   make any alteration ; it will be so pleasant to have you share our family meal , dear Lady Harriet . but we dine late , we only lunch now . how low the fire be get ; I really be forget everything in the pleasure of this tete - a - tete ! " 

 so she ring twice ; with great distinctness , and with a long pause between the ring . Maria bring in coal . 

 but the signal be as well understand by Cynthia as the " Hall of Apollo " be by the servant of Lucullus . the brace of partridge that be to have be for the late dinner be instantly put down to the fire ; and the prettiest china bring out , and the table deck with flower and fruit , arrange with all Cynthia 's usual dexterity and taste . so that when the meal be announce , and Lady Harriet enter the room , she could not but think her hostess 's apology have be quite unnecessary ; and be more and more convinced that Clare have do very well for herself . Cynthia now join the party , pretty and elegant as she always be ; but somehow she do not take Lady Harriet 's fancy ; she only notice she on account of she be her mother 's daughter . her presence make the conversation more general , and Lady Harriet give out several piece of news , none of they of any great importance to she , but as what have be talk about by the circle of visitor assemble at the Towers . 

 " Lord Hollingford ought to have be with we , " she say , amongst other thing ; " but he be oblige , or fancy himself oblige , which be all the same thing , to stay in town about this Crichton legacy ! " 

 " a legacy ? to Lord Hollingford ? I be so glad ! " 

 " do not   be in a hurry to be glad ! it be nothing for he but trouble . do not   you hear of that rich eccentric Mr. Crichton , who die some time ago , and fire by the example of Lord Bridgewater , I suppose leave a sum of money in the hand of trustee , of whom my brother be one , to send out a man with a thousand fine qualification , to make a scientific voyage , with a view to bring back specimen of the fauna of distant land , and so form the nucleus of a museum which be to be call the Crichton Museum , and so perpetuate the founder 's name . such various form do man 's vanity take ! sometimes it stimulate philanthropy ; sometimes a love of science ! " 

 " it seem to I a very laudable and useful object , I be sure , " say Mrs. Gibson , safely . 

 " I daresay it be , take it from the public - good view . but it be rather tiresome to we privately , for it keep Hollingford in town or between it and Cambridge and each place as dull and empty as can be , just when we want he down at the tower . the thing ought to have be decide long ago , and there be some danger of the legacy lapsing . the two other trustee have run away to the continent , feeling , as they say , the utmost confidence in he , but in reality shirk their responsibility . however , I believe he like it , so I ought not to grumble . he think he be go to be very successful in the choice of his man and he belong to this county , too , young Hamley of Hamley , if he can only get his college to let he go , for he be a Fellow of Trinity , senior wrangler or something ; and they be not so foolish as to send their crack man to be eat up by lion and tiger ! " 

 " it must be Roger Hamley ! " exclaim Cynthia , her eye brighten , and her cheek flush . 

 " he be not the eld son ; he can scarcely be call Hamley of Hamley ! " say Mrs. Gibson . 

 " Hollingford 's man be a Fellow of Trinity , as I say before . " 

 " then it be Mr. Roger Hamley , " say Cynthia ; " and he be up in London about some business ! what news for Molly when she come home ! " 

 " why , what have molly to do with it ? " ask Lady Harriet . " be ? " and she look into Mrs. Gibson 's face for an answer . Mrs. Gibson in reply give an intelligent and very expressive glance at Cynthia , who however do not perceive it . 

 " oh , no ! not at all , " and Mrs. Gibson nod a little at her daughter , as much as to say , " if any one , that . " 

 Lady Harriet begin to look at the pretty Miss Kirkpatrick with fresh interest ; her brother have speak in such a manner of this young Mr. Hamley that every one connect with the phonix be worthy of observation . then , as if the mention of Molly 's name have bring her afresh into her mind , Lady Harriet say , " and where be molly all this time ? I should like to see my little mentor . I hear she be very much grow since those day . " 

 " oh ! when she once get gossip with the Miss Brownings , she never know when to come home , " say Mrs. Gibson . 

 " the Miss Brownings ? oh ! I be so glad you name they ! I be very fond of they . Pecksy and Flapsy ; I may call they so in Molly 's absence . I will go and see they before I go home , and then perhaps I shall see my dear little molly too . do you know , Clare , I have   quite take a fancy to that girl ! " 

 so Mrs. Gibson , after all her precaution , have to submit to Lady Harriet 's leave her half - an - hour early than she otherwise would have do in order to " make herself common "   as Mrs. Gibson express it   by call on the Miss Brownings . 

 but Molly have leave before Lady Harriet arrive . 

 molly go the long walk to the Holly Farm , to order the damson , out of a kind of penitence . she have feel conscious of anger at be send out of the house by such a palpable manouvre as that which her stepmother have employ . of course she do not meet Cynthia , so she go alone along the pretty lane , with grassy side and high hedge - bank not at all in the style of modern agriculture . at first she make herself uncomfortable with question herself as to how far it be right to leave unnoticed the small domestic failing the webs , the distortion of truth which have prevail in their household ever since her father 's second marriage . she know that very often she long to protest , but do not do it , from the desire of spar her father any discord ; and she see by his face that he , too , be occasionally aware of certain thing that give he pain , as show that his wife 's standard of conduct be not as high as he would have like . it be a wonder to Molly whether this silence be right or wrong . with a girl 's want of toleration , and want of experience to teach she the force of circumstance , and of temptation , she have often be on the point of tell she stepmother some forcible home truth . but , possibly , her father 's example of silence , and often some piece of kindness on Mrs. Gibson 's part   for after her way , and when in a good temper , she be very kind to Molly , make she hold her tongue . 

 that night at dinner , Mrs. Gibson recount the conversation between herself and Lady Harriet , give it a very strong individual colouring , as be she will not , and tell nearly the whole of what have pass , although imply that there be a great deal say which be so purely confidential , that she be bind in honour not to repeat it . her three auditor listen to she without interrupt her much indeed , without bestow extreme attention on what she be say , until she come to the fact of Lord Hollingford 's absence in London , and the reason for it . 

 " Roger Hamley go off on a scientific expedition ! " exclaim Mr. Gibson , suddenly awaken into vivacity . 

 " yes . at least it be not settle finally ; but as Lord Hollingford be the only trustee who take any interest and be Lord Cumnor 's son it be next to certain . " 

 " I think I must have a voice in the matter , " say Mr. Gibson ; and he relapse into silence , keep his ear open , however , henceforward . 

 " how long will he be away ? " ask Cynthia . " we shall miss he sadly . " 

 Molly 's lip form an acquiescing " yes " to this remark , but no sound be hear . there be a buzzing in her ear as if the other be go on with the conversation , but the word they utter seem indistinct and blur ; they be merely conjecture , and do not interfere with the one great piece of news . to the rest of the party she appear to be eat her dinner as usual , and , if she be silent , there be one listener the more to Mrs. Gibson 's stream of prattle , and Mr. Gibson 's and Cynthia 's remark . 

 

 

 CHAPTER XXXIII . 

 BRIGHTENING prospect . 
 

 Illustrationt be a day or two afterwards , that Mr. Gibson make time to ride round by Hamley , desirous to learn more exact particular of this scheme for Roger than he could obtain from any extraneous source , and rather puzzle to know whether he should interfere in the project or not . the state of the case be this : Osborne 's symptom be , in Mr. Gibson 's opinion , sign of his have a fatal disease . Dr. Nicholls have differ from he on this head , and Mr. Gibson know that the old physician have have long experience , and be consider very skilful in the profession . still he believe that he himself be right , and , if so , the complaint be one which might continue for year in the same state as at present , or might end the young man 's life in an hour a minute . suppose that Mr. Gibson be right , would it be well for Roger to be away where no sudden call for his presence could reach he away for two year ? yet if the affair be conclude , the interference of a medical man might accelerate the very evil to be fear ; and after all , Dr. Nicholls might be right , and the symptom might proceed from some other cause . might ? yes . probably do ? no . Mr. Gibson could not bring himself to say " yes " to this latter form of sentence . so he ride on , meditate ; his rein slack , his head a little bent . it be one of those still and lovely autumn day when the red and yellow leaf be hang - peg to dewy , brilliant gossamer - webs ; when the hedge be full of trail bramble , load with ripe blackberry ; when the air be full of the farewell whistle and pipe of bird , clear and short not the long full - throate warble of spring ; when the whirr of the partridge 's wing be hear in the stubble - field , as the sharp hoof - blow fall on the paved lane ; when here and there a leaf float and flutter down to the ground , although there be not a single breath of wind . the country surgeon feel the beauty of the season perhaps more than most man . he see more of it by day , by night , in storm and sunshine , or in the still , soft , cloudy weather . he never speak about what he feel on the subject ; indeed , he do not put his feeling into word , even to himself . but if his mood ever approach to the sentimental , it be on such day as this . he ride into the stable - yard , give his horse to a man , and go into the house by a side entrance . in the passage he meet the Squire . 

 " that be capital , Gibson ! what good wind blow you here ? you will have some lunch ? it be on the table , I only just this minute leave the room . " and he keep shake Mr. Gibson 's hand all the time till he have place he , nothing loth , at the well - cover dining - table . 

 " what be this I hear about Roger ? " say Mr. Gibson , plunge at once into the subject . 

 " aha ! so you have   hear , have you ? it be famous , be not   it ? he be a boy to be proud of , be old Roger . Nay , you must have a glass full . it be old ale , such as we do not   brew now - a - day ; it be as old as Osborne . we brew it that autumn , and we call it the young squire 's ale . I think to have tap it on his marriage , but I do not   know when that will come to pass , so we have   tap it now in Roger 's honour . " 

 the old squire have evidently be enjoy the young squire 's ale to the verge of prudence . it be indeed as he say , " as strong as brandy , " and Mr. Gibson have to sip it very carefully as he eat his cold roast beef . 

 " well ! and what have you hear ? there be a deal to hear , and all good news , though I shall miss the lad , I know that . " 

 " I do not know it be settle ; I only hear that it be in progress . " 

 " well , it be only in progress , as you call it , till last Tuesday . he never let I know anything about it , though ; he say he think I might be fidgety with thinking of the pro and con . so I never know a word on't   till I have a letter from my Lord Hollingford where be it ? " pull out a great black leathern receptacle for all manner of paper . and put on his spectacle , he read aloud their heading . 

 " ' Measurement of timber , new railing , ' ' drench for cow , from Farmer Hayes , ' ' Dobson 's account , ' ' um ' um here it be . now read that letter , " hand it to Mr. Gibson . 

 it be a manly , feeling , sensible letter , explain to the old father in very simple language the service which be demand by the term of the will to which he and two or three other be trustee ; the liberal allowance for expense , the still more liberal reward for performance , which have tempt several man of considerable renown to offer themselves as candidate for the appointment . Lord Hollingford then go on to say that , have see a good deal of Roger lately , since the publication of his article in reply to the french osteologist , he have have reason to think that in he the trustee would find unite the various quality require in a great measure than in any of the applicant who have at that time present themselves . Roger have deep interest in the subject ; much acquire knowledge , and at the same time , great natural power of comparison , and classification of fact ; he have show himself to be an observer of a fine and accurate kind ; he be of the right age , in the very prime of health and strength , and unshackle by any family tie . here Mr. Gibson pause for consideration . he hardly care to ascertain by what step the result have be arrive at he already know what that result be ; but his mind be again arrest as his eye catch on the remuneration offer , which be indeed most liberal ; and then he read with attention the high praise bestow on the son in this letter to the father . the squire have be watch Mr. Gibson wait till he come to this part and he rub his hand together as he say , 

 " ay ! you have   come to it at last . it be the good part of the whole , be not   it ? God bless the boy ! and from a Whig , mind you , which make it the more handsome . and there be more to come still . I say , Gibson , I think my luck be turn at last , " pass he on yet another letter to read . " that only come this morning ; but I have   act on it already , I send for the foreman of the drainage work at once , I do ; and to - morrow , please God , they will be at work again . " 

 Mr. Gibson read the second letter , from Roger . to a certain degree it be a modest repetition of what Lord Hollingford have say , with an explanation of how he have come to take so decide a step in life without consult his father . he do not wish he to be in suspense for one reason . another be that he feel , as no one else could feel for he , that by accept this offer , he enter upon the kind of life for which he know himself to be most fit . and then he merge the whole into business . he say that he know well the suffer his father have go through when he have have to give up his drainage work for want of money ; that he , Roger , have be enable at once to raise money upon the remuneration he be to receive on the accomplishment of his two year ' work ; and that he have also insure his life , in order to provide for the repayment of the money he have raise , in case he do not live to return to England . he say that the sum he have borrow on this security would at once be forward to his father . 

 Mr. Gibson lay down the letter without speak a word for some time ; then he say , " he will have to pay a pretty sum for insure his life beyond sea . " 

 " he be get his Fellowship money , " say the Squire , a little depressed at Mr. Gibson 's remark . 

 " yes ; that be true . and he be a strong young fellow , as I know . " 

 " I wish I could tell his mother , " say the Squire in an under - tone 

 " it seem all settle now , " say Mr. Gibson , more in reply to his own thought than to the Squire 's remark . 

 " yes ! " say the squire ; " and they be not go to let the grass grow under his foot . he be to be off as soon as he can get his scientific trap ready . I almost wish he be not   to go . you do not   seem quite to like it , doctor ? " 

 " yes , I do , " say Mr. Gibson in a more cheerful tone than before . " it can not   be help now without do a mischief , " think he to himself . " why , Squire , I think it a great honour to have such a son . " 

 " ay , ay ; he be twice as much a son to I as Osborne , who have be all his life set up on nothing at all , as one may say . "

" 

 " Osborne might have , if he get out o ' door more , " say the Squire , moodily ; " but except when he can loaf into Hollingford he do not   care to go out at all . I do not   mean any offence , you know ; but he will have the estate , and it will not   be free , and he must marry money . I do not   think I could allow it in Roger ; but Osborne 's the eld son , you know . " 

 Mr. Gibson redden ; he be offend for a moment . then the partial truth of what the Squire say be present to his mind , and he remember their old friendship , so he speak quietly , if shortly . 

 " I do not   believe there be anything of the kind go on . I be not much at home , you know ; but I have   never hear or see anything that should make I suppose that there be . when I do , I will let you know . " 

 " now , Gibson , do not   go and be offend . I be glad for the boy to have a pleasant house to go to , and I thank you and Mrs. Gibson for make it pleasant . only keep off love ; it can come to no good . that be all . I do not   believe Osborne will ever earn a farthing to keep a wife during my life , and if I be to die to - morrow , she would have to bring some money to clear the estate . and if I do speak as I should not   have do formerly a little sharp or so why , it be because I have   be worry by many a care no one know anything of . " 

 " I be not go to take offence , " say Mr. Gibson , " but let we understand each other clearly . if you do not   want your son to come as much to my house as they do , tell they so yourself . I like the lad , and be glad to see they ; but if they do come , you must take the consequence , whatever they be , and not blame I , or they either , for what may happen from the frequent intercourse between two young man and two young woman ; and what be more , though , as I say , I see nothing whatever of the kind you fear at present , and have promise to tell you of the first symptom I do see , yet far than that I will not   go . if there be an attachment at any future time , I will not   interfere . " 

 " I should not   so much mind if Roger fall in love with your Molly . he can fight for himself , you see , and she be an uncommon nice girl . my poor wife be so fond of she , " answer the Squire . " it be Osborne and the estate I be think of ! " 

 " well , then , tell he not to come near we . I shall be sorry , but you will be safe . " 

 " I will think about it ; but he be difficult to manage . I have   always to get my blood well up before I can speak my mind to he . " 

 Mr. Gibson be leave the room , but at these word he turn and lay his hand on the squire 's arm . 

 " take my advice , squire . as I say , there be no harm do as yet , as far as I know . prevention be well than cure . speak out , but speak gently to Osborne , and do it at once . I shall understand how it be if he do not   show his face for some month in my house . if you speak gently to he , he will take the advice as from a friend . if he can assure you there be no danger , of course he will come just as usual , when he like . " 

 it be all very fine give the squire this good advice ; but as Osborne have already form the very kind of marriage his father most deprecate , it do not act quite as well as Mr. Gibson have hop . the Squire begin the conversation with unusual self - control ; but he grow irritated when Osborne deny his father 's right to interfere in any marriage he might contemplate ; deny it with a certain degree of doggedness and weariness of the subject that drive the squire into one of his passion ; and although , on after reflection , he remember that he have his son 's promise and solemn word not to think of either Cynthia or Molly for his wife , yet the father and son have pass through one of those altercation which help to estrange man for life . each have say bitter thing to the other ; and , if the brotherly affection have not be so true between Osborne and Roger , they too might have become alienate , in consequence of the Squire 's exaggerated and injudicious comparison of their character and deed . but as Roger in his boyhood have love Osborne too well to be jealous of the praise and love which the eld son , the beautiful brilliant lad , have receive , to the disparagement of his own plain awkwardness and slowness , so now osborne strove against any feeling of envy or jealousy with all his might ; but his effort be conscious , Roger 's have be the simple consequence of affection , and the end to poor Osborne be that he become moody and depressed in mind and body ; but both father and son conceal their feeling in Roger 's presence . when he come home just before sailing , busy and happy , the Squire catch his infectious energy , and Osborne look up and be cheerful . 

 there be no time to be lose . he be bind to a hot climate , and must take all advantage possible of the winter month . he be to go first to Paris , to have interview with some of the scientific man there . some of his outfit , instrument ,   c. , be to follow he to Havre , from which port he be to embark , after transact his business in Paris . the Squire learn all his arrangement and plan , and even try in after - dinner conversation to penetrate into the question involve in the research his son be about to make . but Roger 's visit home could not be prolong beyond two day . 

 the last day he ride into Hollingford early than he need to have do to catch the London coach , in order to bid the Gibsons good - by . he have be too actively busy for some time to have leisure to bestow much thought on Cynthia ; but there be no need for fresh meditation on that subject . her image as a prize to be work for , to be serve for seven year , and seven year more , be safe and sacred in his heart . it be very bad , this go away , and wish her good - by for two long year ; and he wonder much during his ride how far he should be justify in tell her mother , perhaps in tell her own sweet self , what his feeling be without expect , nay , indeed reprobate , any answer on her part . then she would know at any rate how dearly she be belove by one who be absent ; how in all difficulty or danger the thought of she would be a polar star , high up in the heaven , and so on , and so on ; for with all a lover 's quickness of imagination and triteness of fancy , he call she a star , a flower , a nymph , a witch , an angel , or a mermaid , a nightingale , a siren , as one or another of her attribute rise up before he . 

 

 

 CHAPTER XXXIV . 

 a LOVER 's mistake . 
 

 it be afternoon . molly have go out for a walk . Mrs. Gibson have be pay some call . Lazy Cynthia have decline accompany either . a daily walk be not a necessity to she as it be to Molly . on a lovely day , or with an agreeable object , or when the fancy take she , she could go as far as any one ; but these be exceptional case ; in general , she be not dispose to disturb herself from her in - door occupation . indeed , not one of the lady would have leave the house , have they be aware that Roger be in the neighbourhood ; for they be aware that he be to come down but once before his departure , and that his stay at home then would be but for a short time , and they be all anxious to wish he good - by before his long absence . but they have understand that he be not come to the Hall until the follow week , and therefore they have feel themselves at full liberty this afternoon to follow their own device . 

 molly choose a walk that have be a favourite with she ever since she be a child . something or other have happen just before she leave home that make she begin wonder how far it be right for the sake of domestic peace to pass over without comment the little deviation from right that people perceive in those whom they live with . or whether , as they be place in family for distinct purpose , not by chance merely , there be not duty involve in this aspect of their lot in life , whether by continually pass over failing , their own standard be not lower , the practical application of these thought be a dismal sort of perplexity on Molly 's part as to whether her father be quite aware of her stepmother 's perpetual lapse from truth ; and whether his blindness be wilful or not . then she feel bitterly enough that although she be sure as could be that there be no real estrangement between she and her father , yet there be perpetual obstacle throw in the way of their intercourse ; and she think with a sigh that if he would but come in with authority , he might cut his way clear to the old intimacy with his daughter , and that they might have all the former walk and talk , and quip and crank , and glimpse of real confidence once again ; thing that her stepmother do not value , yet which she , like the dog in the manger , prevent Molly 's enjoying . but after all molly be a girl , not so far remove from childhood ; and in the middle of her grave regret and perplexity , her eye be catch by the sight of some fine ripe blackberry flourish away high up on the hedge - bank among scarlet hip and green and russet leaf . she do not care much for blackberry herself ; but she have hear Cynthia say that she like they ; and besides there be the charm of scramble and gather they ; so she forget all about her trouble , and go climb up the bank , and clutch at her almost inaccessible prize , and slip down again triumphant , to carry they back to the large leaf which be to serve she as a basket . one or two of they she taste , but they be as vapid to her palate as ever . the skirt of her pretty print gown be tear out of the gather , and even with the fruit she have eat " her pretty lip with blackberry be all besmear and dye , " when have gather as many and more than she could possibly carry , she set off home , hop to escape into her room and mend she gown before it have offend Mrs. Gibson 's neat eye . the front door be easily open from the outside , and Molly be out of the clear light of the open air and in the shadow of the hall , when she see a face peep out of the dining - room before she quite recognize whose it be ; and then Mrs. Gibson come softly out , sufficiently at least to beckon she into the room . when Molly have enter Mrs. Gibson close the door . Poor Molly expect a reprimand for her tear gown and untidy appearance , but be soon relieve by the expression of Mrs. Gibson 's face mysterious and radiant . 

 " I have   be watch for you , dear . do not   go upstairs into the drawing - room , love . it might be a little interruption just now . Roger Hamley be there with Cynthia ; and I have   reason to think in fact I do open the door unawares , but I shut it again softly , and I do not   think they hear I . be not   it charming ? young love , you know , ah , how sweet it be ! " 

 " do you mean that Roger have propose to Cynthia ? " ask Molly . 

 " not exactly that . but I do not   know ; of course I know nothing . only I do hear he say that he have mean to leave England without speak of his love , but that the temptation of see she alone have be too great for he . it be symptomatic , be it not , my dear ? and all I want be to let it come to a crisis without interruption . so I have   be watch for you to prevent your go in and disturb they . " 

 " but I may go to my own room , may not   I , " plead molly . 

 " of course , " say Mrs. Gibson , a little testily . " only I have expect sympathy from you at such an interesting moment . " 

 but Molly do not hear these last word . she have escape upstairs , and shut her door . instinctively she have carry her leaf full of blackberry what would blackberry be to Cynthia now ? she feel as if she could not understand it all ; but as for that matter , what could she understand ? nothing . for a few minute her brain seem in too great a whirl to comprehend anything but that she be be carry on in earth 's diurnal course , with rock , and stone , and tree , with as little volition on her part as if she be dead . then the room grow stifle , and instinctively she go to the open casement window , and leant out , gasp for breath . gradually the consciousness of the soft peaceful landscape steal into her mind , and still the buzzing confusion . there , bath in the almost level ray of the autumn sunlight , lie the landscape she have know and love from childhood ; as quiet , as full of low hum life as it have be at this hour for many generation . the autumn flower blaze out in the garden below , the lazy cow be in the meadow beyond , chew their cud in the green aftermath ; the evening fire have just be make up in the cottage beyond , in preparation for the husband 's home - come , and be send up soft curl of blue smoke into the still air ; the child , let loose from school , be shout merrily in the distance , and she just then she hear nearer sound ; an open door , step on the low flight of stair . he could not have go without even see she . he never , never would have do so cruel a thing never would have forget poor little molly , however happy he might be ! no ! there be step and voice , and the drawing - room door be open and shut once more . she lay down her head on her arm that rest upon the window - sill , and cry , she have be so distrustful as to have let the idea enter her mind that he could go without wish her good - by she , whom his mother have so love , and call by the name of his little dead sister . and as she think of the tender love Mrs. Hamley have bear she she cry the more , for the vanishing of such love for she off the face of the earth . suddenly the drawing - room door open , and some one be hear come upstairs ; it be Cynthia 's step . molly hastily wipe her eye , and stand up and try to look unconcerned ; it be all she have time to do before Cynthia , after a little pause at the closed door , have knock ; and on an answer be give , have say , without open the door , " molly ! Mr. Roger Hamley be here , and want to wish you good - by before he go .  CHAPTER XXXV . 

 the MOTHER 's MANOUVRE . 
 

 Mr. Gibson be not at home at dinner detain by some patient , most probably . this be not an unusual occurrence ; but it be rather an unusual occurrence for Mrs. Gibson to go down into the dining - room , and sit with he as he eat his defer meal when he come in an hour or two later . in general , she prefer her easy - chair , or her corner of the sofa , upstairs in the drawing - room , though it be very rarely that she would allow Molly to avail herself of her stepmother 's neglect privilege . molly would fain have go down and keep her father company every night that he have these solitary meal ; but for peace and quietness she give up her own wish on the matter . 

 Mrs. Gibson take a seat by the fire in the dining - room , and patiently wait for the auspicious moment when Mr. Gibson , have satisfy his healthy appetite , turn from the table , and take his place by her side . she get up , and with unaccustomed attention move the wine and glass so that he could help himself without move from his chair .

 she make a faint rally back into her former playfulness .

 he give an impatient movement , and knock a wine - glass off the table . for the moment she feel grateful for the diversion , and busy herself in pick up the fragment : " bit of glass be so dangerous , " she say . but she be startle by a voice of command , such as she have never yet hear from her husband .

 CHAPTER XXXVI . 

 DOMESTIC diplomacy . 
 

 the evening of the day on which Mr. Gibson have be to see the Squire , the three woman be alone in the drawing - room , for Mr. Gibson have have a long round and be not as yet come in . they have have to wait dinner for he ; and for some time after his return there be nothing do or say but what relate to the necessary business of eat .  CHAPTER XXXVII . 

 a FLUKE , and what come of it . 
 

 illustrationhe honour and glory of have a lover of her own be soon to fall to Molly 's share ; though , to be sure , it be a little deduction from the honour that the man who come with the full intention of propose to she , end by make Cynthia an offer . it be Mr. Coxe , who come back to Hollingford to follow out the purpose he have announce to Mr. Gibson nearly two year before , of induce Molly to become his wife as soon as he should have succeed to his uncle 's estate . he be now a rich , though still a red - haired , young man . he come to the George Inn , bring his horse and his groom ; not that he be go to ride much , but that he think such outward sign of his rich might help on his suit ; and he be so justly modest in his estimation of himself that he believe that he need all extraneous aid . he pique himself on his constancy ; and indeed , consider that he have be so much restrain by his duty , his affection , and his expectation to his crab old uncle , that he have not be able to go much into society , and very rarely indeed into the company of young lady , such fidelity to Molly be very meritorious , at least in his own eye . Mr. Gibson too be touch by it , and make it a point of honour to give he a fair field , all the time sincerely hop that molly would not be such a goose as to lend a willing ear to a youth who could never remember the difference between apophysis and epiphysis . he think it as well not to tell his wife more of Mr. Coxe 's antecedent than that he have be a former pupil ; who have relinquish   " all that he know of , " understand   the medical profession because an old uncle have leave he enough of money to be idle . Mrs. Gibson , who feel that she have somehow lose her place in her husband 's favour , take it into her head that she could reinstate herself if she be successful in find a good match for his daughter Molly . she know that her husband have forbid she to try for this end , as distinctly as word could express a meaning ; but her own word so seldom express her meaning , or if they do , she hold to her opinion so loosely , that she have no idea but that it be the same with other people . accordingly she give Mr. Coxe a very sweet and gracious welcome . 

 " it be such a pleasure to I to make acquaintance with the former pupil of my husband . he have speak to I so often of you that I quite feel as if you be one of the family , as indeed I be sure that Mr. Gibson consider you . "

 " certainly , I be sure she will be so glad to see you . I send she and Cynthia you do not   know my daughter Cynthia , I think , Mr. Coxe ? she and Molly be such great friend out for a brisk walk this frosty day , but I think they will soon come back . " she go on say agreeable nothing to the young man , who receive her attention with a certain complacency , but be all the time much more engage in listen to the well - remember click at the front door , the shut it to again with household care , and the sound of the familiar bound footstep on the stair . at last they come . Cynthia enter first , bright and blooming , fresh colour in her cheek and lip , fresh brilliance in her eye . she look startled at the sight of a stranger , and for an instant she stop short at the door , as if take by surprise . then in come molly softly behind she , smile , happy , dimpled ; but not such a glowing beauty as Cynthia . 

 " oh , Mr. Coxe , be it you ? " say she , go up to he with an outstretched hand , and greet he with simple friendliness . 

 " yes ; it seem such a long time since I see you . you be so much grow so much well , I suppose I must not   say what , " he reply , speak hurriedly , and hold her hand all the time , rather to her discomfiture . then Mrs. Gibson introduce her daughter , and the two girl speak of the enjoyment of their walk . Mr. Coxe mar his cause in that very first interview , if indeed he ever could have have any chance , by his precipitancy in show his feeling , and Mrs. Gibson help he to mar it by try to assist he . molly lose she open friendliness of manner , and begin to shrink away from he in a way which he think be a very ungrateful return for all his faithfulness to she these two year past ; and after all she be not the wonderful beauty his fancy or his love have paint she . that Miss Kirkpatrick be far more beautiful and much easy of access . for Cynthia put on all her pretty air her look of intent interest in what any one be say to she , let the subject be what it would , as if it be the thing she care most about in the whole world ; her unspoken deference ; in short , all the unconscious way she possess by instinct of tickle the vanity of man . so while molly quietly repel he , Cynthia draw he to she by her soft attractive way ; and his constancy fall before her charm . he be thankful that he have not go too far with Molly , and grateful to Mr. Gibson for have prohibit all declaration two year ago ; for Cynthia , and Cynthia alone , could make he happy . after a fortnight 's time , during which he have entirely veer round in his allegiance , he think it desirable to speak to Mr. Gibson . he do so with a certain sense of exultation in his own correct behaviour in the affair , but at the same time feel rather ashamed of the confession of his own changeableness which be naturally involve . now it have so happen that Mr. Gibson have be unusually little at home during the fortnight that Mr. Coxe have ostensibly lodge at the " George , " but in reality have spend the great part of his time at Mr. Gibson 's house so that he have see very little of his former pupil , and on the whole he have think he improve , especially after Molly 's manner have make her father pretty sure that Mr. Coxe stand no chance in that quarter . but Mr. Gibson be quite ignorant of the attraction which Cynthia have have for the young man . if he have perceive it , he would have nip it in the bud pretty quickly , for he have no notion of any girl , even though only partially engage to one man , receive offer from other , if a little plain speaking could prevent it .  " Miss Kirkpatrick ? " say Mr. Gibson , a good deal surprised . 

 " yes , sir ! " continue Mr. Coxe , rush on now he have get so far . " I know it may appear inconstant and changeable , but I do assure you , I come here with a heart as faithful to your daughter as ever beat in a man 's bosom .  " while Miss Kirkpatrick ? " repeat Mr. Gibson , in such a stern voice , that Mr. Coxe , land esquire as he be now , feel as much discomfited as he use to do when he be an apprentice , and Mr. Gibson have speak to he in a similar manner .

I do not   think she could have misunderstood my meaning . perhaps she have change her mind ? it be possible that , after consideration , she have learn to prefer another , be it not ? "
