 CHAPTER XXXVIII . 

 MR . KIRKPATRICK , Q.C. 
 

 Cynthia be always the same with molly : kind , sweet - temper , ready to help , profess a great deal of love for she , and probably feel as much as she do for any one in the world . but Molly have reach to this superficial depth of affection and intimacy in the first few week of Cynthia 's residence in her father 's house ; and if she have be of a nature prone to analyse the character of one whom she love dearly , she might have perceive that , with all Cynthia 's apparent frankness , there be certain limit beyond which her confidence do not go ; where her reserve begin , and her real self be shroud in mystery . for instance , her relation with Mr. Preston be often very puzzling to Molly . she be sure that there have be a much great intimacy between they formerly at Ashcombe , and that the remembrance of this be often very galling and irritate to Cynthia , who be as evidently desirous of forget it as he be anxious to make she remember it . but why this intimacy have cease , why Cynthia dislike he so extremely now , and many other unexplained circumstance connect with these two fact , be Cynthia 's secret ; and she effectually baffle all Molly 's innocent attempt during the first glow of her friendship for Cynthia , to learn the girlish antecedent of her companion 's life . every now and then Molly come to a dead wall , beyond which she could not pass at least with the delicate instrument which be all she choose to use . perhaps Cynthia might have tell all there be to tell to a more forcible curiosity , which know how to improve every slip of the tongue and every fit of temper to its own gratification . but Molly 's be the interest of affection , not the coarser desire of know everything for a little excitement ; and as soon as she see that Cynthia do not wish to tell she anything about that period of her life , Molly leave off refer to it . but if Cynthia have preserve a sweet tranquillity of manner and an unvarying kindness for Molly during the winter of which there be question , at present she be the only person to whom the beauty 's way be unchanged . Mr. Gibson 's influence have be good for she as long as she see that he like she ; she have try to keep as high a place in his good opinion as she could , and have curb many a little sarcasm against her mother , and many a twisting of the absolute truth when he be by . now there be a constant uneasiness about she which make she more cowardly than before ; and even her partisan , Molly , could not help be aware of the distinct equivocation she occasionally use when anything in Mr. Gibson 's word or behaviour press she too hard . her repartee to her mother be less frequent than they have be , but there be often the unusual phenomenon of pettishness in her behaviour to she . these change in humour and disposition , here describe all at once , be in themselves a series of delicate alteration of relative conduct spread over many month many winter month of long evening and bad weather , which bring out discord of character , as a dash of cold water bring out the fade colour of an old fresco . 

 during much of this time Mr. Preston have be at Ashcombe ; for Lord Cumnor have not be able to find an agent whom he like to replace Mr. Preston ; and while the inferior situation remain vacant Mr. Preston have undertake to do the duty of both . Mrs. Goodenough have have a serious illness ; and the little society at Hollingford do not care to meet while one of their habitual set be scarcely out of danger . so there have be very little visit ; and though Miss Browning say that the absence of the temptation of society be very agreeable to cultivate mind , after the dissipation of the previous autumn , when there be party every week to welcome Mr. Preston , yet Miss Phobe let out in confidence that she and her sister have fall into the habit of go to bed at nine o'clock , for they find cribbage night after night , from five o'clock till ten , rather too much of a good thing . to tell the truth , that winter , if peaceful , be monotonous in Hollingford ; and the whole circle of gentility there be delighted to be stir up in March by the intelligence that Mr. Kirkpatrick , the newly - make Q.C. , be come on a visit for a couple of day to his sister - in - law , Mrs. Gibson . Mrs. Goodenough 's room be the very centre of gossip ; gossip have be her daily bread through her life , gossip be meat and wine to she now . 

 " dear - ah - I ! " say the old lady , rouse herself so as to sit upright in her easy - chair , and prop herself with her hand on the arm ; " who would ha ' think she have such grand relation ! why , Mr. Ashton tell I once that a Queen 's counsel be as like to be a judge as a kitten be like to be a cat . and to think of she be as good as a sister to a judge ! I see one oncst ; and I know I think as I should not   wish for a well winter - cloak than his old robe would make I , if I could only find out where I could get them second - hand . and I know she have her silk gown turn and dye and clean , and , for aught I know , turn again , while she live at Ashcombe . keep a school , too , and so near akin to this Queen 's counsel all the time ! well , to be sure , it be not   much of a school only ten young lady at the good o ' time ; so perhaps he never hear of it . "

 by this time Mrs. Goodenough give evident sign of be tired ; Molly 's misdemeanor and Miss Browning 's broken teacup be not as exciting subject of conversation as Mrs. Gibson 's newly - discover good luck in have a successful London lawyer for a relation . 

 Mr. Kirkpatrick have be , like many other man , struggle on in his profession , and encumber with a large family of his own ; he be ready to do a good turn for his connection , if it occasion he no loss of time , and if   which be , perhaps , a primary condition   he remember their existence . " do the woman think I have nothing to do but run about the country in search of bride and bridegroom , when this great case of Houghton v. Houghton be come on , and I have not   a moment to spare ? " he ask of his wife .

 but now the great case of Houghton v. Houghton be a thing of the past ; the hard struggle be over , the comparative table - land of Q. C.-dom gain , and Mr. Kirkpatrick have leisure for family feeling and recollection . one day in the Easter vacation he find himself near Hollingford ; he have a Sunday to spare , and he write to offer himself as a visitor to the Gibsons from Friday till Monday , express strongly   what he really feel , in a less degree ,   his wish to make Mr. Gibson 's acquaintance . Mr. Gibson , though often overwhelm with professional business , be always hospitable ; and moreover , it be always a pleasure to he to get out of the somewhat confine mental atmosphere which he have breathe over and over again , and have a whiff of fresh air : a glimpse of what be pass in the great world beyond his daily limit of thought and action . so he be ready to give a cordial welcome to his unknown relation . and be , in fact , very glad that she have do so well for herself as to marry a sensible and agreeable man , who be able to keep she in comfort , and to behave to her daughter in so liberal a manner . molly strike he as a delicate - look girl , who might be very pretty if she have a great look of health and animation : indeed , look at she critically , there be beautiful point about her face long soft grey eye , black curl eyelash , rarely - show dimple , perfect tooth ; but he do not care if he never see they again . but for Mr. Gibson he have a warm respect , a strong personal liking , which he should be glad to have ripen into a friendship , if there be time for it in this bustling world . and he fully resolve to see more of Cynthia ; his wife must know she ; they must have she up to stay with they in London , and show she something of the world . but , on return home , Mr. Kirkpatrick find so much work await he that he have to lock up embryo friendship and kindly plan in some safe closet of his mind , and give himself up , body and soul , to the immediate work of his profession . but , in May , he find time to take his wife to the Academy Exhibition , and some portrait there strike he as be like Cynthia , he tell his wife more about she and his visit to Hollingford than he have ever have leisure to do before ;  on its receipt , this letter be greet in various way by the four people who sate round the breakfast - table . Mrs. Gibson read it to herself first . then , without tell what its content be , so that her auditor be quite in the dark as to what her remark apply , she say ,

"

 " probably . " 

 " well , then , apply the same reasoning to Cynthia 's case ; and then , I ask , what have become of the money ? "

"

" to think of your be a stepfather ! and so good to my poor fatherless girl ! but , molly my dear , I think you will acknowledge that you too be very fortunate in your stepmother . be not you , love ? and what happy tete - a - tete we shall have together when Cynthia go to London ! " 

 something very like " Pshaw ! " be growl out from behind the newspaper .

 molly have leave the room before this speech be end , and Mr. Gibson be occupy with his newspaper ; but Mrs. Gibson finish it to herself very much to her own satisfaction ; for , after all , it be well to have some one of the family go on the visit , though she might not be the right person , than to refuse it altogether , and never to have the opportunity of say anything about it . as Mr. Gibson be so kind to Cynthia , she too would be kind to Molly , and dress her becomingly , and invite young man to the house ; do all the thing , in fact , which molly and her father do not want to have do , and throw the old stumbling - block in the way of their unrestrained intercourse , which be the one thing they desire to have , free and open , and without the constant dread of her jealousy . 

 

 

 CHAPTER XXXIX . 

 secret thought OOZE OUT . 
 

 molly find Cynthia in the drawing - room , stand in the bow - window , look out on the garden . she start as molly come up to she . 

 " oh , Molly , " say she , put her arm out towards she , " I be always so glad to have you with I ! " 

 it be outburst of affection such as these that always call molly back , if she have be ever so unconsciously waver in her allegiance to Cynthia . she have be wish downstairs that Cynthia would be less reserved , and not have so many secret ; but now it seem almost like treason to have want she to be anything but what she be . never have any one more than Cynthia the power speak of by Goldsmith when he write 

 he throw off his friend like a huntsman his pack , 
 for he know when he like he could whistle they back . 
 " do you know , I think you will be glad to hear what I have   get to tell you , " say Molly . " I think you would really like to go to London ; should not   you ? " 

 " yes , but it be of no use like , " say Cynthia . " do not   you begin about it , Molly , for the thing be settle ; and I can not   tell you why , but I can not   go . "

 " how kind he be ! " say Cynthia . " but I ought not to take it . I wish I have know you year ago ; I should have be different to what I be . " 

 " never mind that ! we like you as you be ; we do not   want you different . you will really hurt papa if you do not   take it . why do you hesitate ? do you think Roger will not   like it ? " 

 " Roger ! no , I be not   think about he ! why should he care ? I shall be there and back again before he even hear about it . " 

 " then you will go ? " say Molly . 

 Cynthia think for a minute or two . " yes , I will , " say she , at length . " I daresay it be not wise ; but it will be pleasant , and I will go . where be Mr. Gibson ? I want to thank he . oh , how kind he be ! molly , you be a lucky girl ! " 

 " I ? " say Molly , quite startled at be tell this ; for she have be feel as if so many thing be go wrong , almost as if they would never go right again . 

 " there he be ! " say Cynthia . " I hear he in the hall ! " and down she fly , and lay her hand on Mr. Gibson 's arm , she thank he with such warm impulsiveness , and in so pretty and caress a manner , that something of his old feeling of personal liking for she return , and he forget for a time the cause of disapproval he have against she . 

 " there , there ! " say he , " that be enough , my dear ! it be quite right you should keep up with your relation ; there be nothing more to be say about it . " 

 " I do think your father be the most charming man I know , " say Cynthia , on her return to Molly ; " and it be that which always make I so afraid of lose his good opinion , and fret so when I think he be displeased with I . and now let we think all about this London visit . it will be delightful , will not   it ? I can make ten pound go ever so far ; and in some way it will be such a comfort to get out of Hollingford . " 

 " will it ? " say Molly , rather wistfully . 

 " oh , yes ! you know I do not   mean that it will be a comfort to leave you ; that will be anything but a comfort . but , after all , a country town be a country town , and London be London . you need not smile at my truism ; I have   always have a sympathy with M. de la Palisse , 

 M. de la Palisse est mort 
 En perdant sa vie ; 
 Un quart d'heure avant sa mort 
 Il ťtait en vie , " 
 sing she , in so gay a manner that she puzzle molly , as she often do , by her change of mood from the gloomy decision with which she have refuse to accept the invitation only half an hour ago . she suddenly take molly round the waist , and begin waltz round the room with she , to the imminent danger of the various little table , load with " objet d'art "   as Mrs. Gibson delight to call they   with which the drawing - room be crowd . she avoid they , however , with her usual skill ; but they both stand still at last , surprised at Mrs. Gibson 's surprise , as she stand at the door , look at the whirl go on before she . 

 " upon my word , I only hope you be not go crazy , both of you ! what be all this about , pray ? " 

 " only because I be so glad I be go to London , mamma , " say Cynthia , demurely . 

 " I be not sure if it be quite the thing for an engaged young lady to be so much beside herself at the prospect of gaiety . in my time , our great pleasure in our lover ' absence be in think about they . " 

 " I should have think that would have give you pain , because you would have have to remember that they be away , which ought to have make you unhappy . now , to tell you the truth , just at the moment I have forget all about Roger . I hope it be not   very wrong . osborne look as if he do all my share as well as his own of the fretting after Roger . how ill he look yesterday ! " 

 " yes , " say molly ; " I do not   know if any one besides I have notice it . I be quite shocked . " 

 " ah , " say Mrs. Gibson , " I be afraid that young man will not   live long very much afraid , " and she shake her head ominously . 

 " oh , what will happen if he die ! " exclaim molly , suddenly sit down , and think of that strange , mysterious wife who never make her appearance , whose very existence be never speak about and Roger away too ! 

 " well , it would be very sad , of course , and we should all feel it very much , I have   no doubt ; for I have   always be very fond of Osborne ; in fact , before Roger become , as it be , my own flesh and blood , I like Osborne well : but we must not forget the living , dear Molly , "   for Molly 's eye be fill with tear at the dismal thought present to she . " our dear good Roger would , I be sure , do all in his power to fill Osborne 's place in every way ; and his marriage need not be so long delay . " 

 " do not   speak of that in the same breath as Osborne 's life , mamma , " say Cynthia , hastily . 

 " why , my dear , it be a very natural thought . for poor Roger 's sake , you know , one wish it not to be so very , very long an engagement ; and I be only answer Molly 's question , after all . one can not   help follow out one 's thought . People must die , you know young , as well as old . " 

 " if I ever suspect Roger of follow out his thought in a similar way , " say Cynthia , " I would never speak to he again . " 

 " as if he would ! " say Molly , warm in her turn . " you know he never would ; and you should not   suppose it of he , Cynthia no , not even for a moment ! " 

 " I can not   see the great harm of it all , for my part , " say Mrs. Gibson , plaintively . " a young man strike we all as look very ill and I be sure I be sorry for it ; but illness very often lead to death . surely you agree with I there , and what be the harm of say so ? then Molly ask what will happen if he die ; and I try to answer her question . I do not   like talk or thinking of death any more than any one else ; but I should think myself want in strength of mind if I could not look forward to the consequence of death . I really think we be command to do so , somewhere in the Bible or the Prayer - book . " 

 " do you look forward to the consequence of my death , mamma ? " ask Cynthia . 

 " you really be the most unfeeling girl I ever meet with , " say Mrs. Gibson , really hurt . " I wish I could give you a little of my own sensitiveness , for I have too much for my happiness . do not   let we speak of Osborne 's look again ; ten to one it be only some temporary over - fatigue , or some anxiety about Roger , or perhaps a little fit of indigestion . I be very foolish to attribute it to anything more serious , and dear papa might be displeased if he know I have do so . medical man do not   like other people to be make conjecture about health ; they consider it as trench on their own particular province , and very proper , I be sure . now let we consider about your dress , Cynthia ; I could not understand how you have spend your money , and make so little show with it . " 

 " Mamma ! it may sound very cross , but I must tell Molly , and you , and everybody , once for all , that as I do not   want and do not   ask for more than my allowance , I be not go to answer any question about what I do with it . " she do not say this with any want of respect ; but she say it with quiet determination , which subdue her mother for the time ; though often afterwards , when Mrs. Gibson and Molly be alone , the former would start the wonder as to what Cynthia could possibly have do with her money , and hunt each poor conjecture through wood and valley of doubt , till she be weary out ; and the exciting sport be give up for the day . at present , however , she confine herself to the practical matter in hand ; and the genius for millinery and dress , inherent in both mother and daughter , soon settle a great many knotty point of contrivance and taste , and then they all three set to work to " gar auld clae look amaist as weel be the new . " 

 Cynthia 's relation with the squire have be very stationary ever since the visit she have pay to the Hall the previous autumn . he have receive they all at that time with hospitable politeness , and he have be more charmed with Cynthia than he like to acknowledge to himself when he think the visit all over afterwards . 

 " she be a pretty lass , sure enough , " think he , " and have pretty way about she too , and like to learn from old people , which be a good sign ; but somehow I do not   like madam her mother ; but still she be her mother , and the girl 's her daughter ; yet she speak to she once or twice as I should not   ha ' like our little fanny to have speak , if it have please God for she to ha ' live . no , it be not the right way , and it may be a bit old - fashioned , but I like the right way . and then again she take possession o ' I , as I may say , and little Molly have to run after we in the garden walk that be too narrow for three , just like a little four - legged doggie ; and the other be so full of listen to I , she never turn round for to speak a word to Molly . I do not   mean to say they be not fond of each other , and that be in Roger 's sweetheart 's favour ; and it be very ungrateful in I to go and find fault with a lass who be so civil to I , and have such a pretty way with she of hang on every word that fall from my lip . well ! a deal may come and go in two year ! and the lad say nothing to I about it . I will be as deep as he , and take no more notice of the affair till he come home and tell I himself . " 

 so although the squire be always delighted to receive the little note which Cynthia send he every time she hear from Roger , and although this attention on her part be melt the heart he try to harden , he control himself into write she the brief acknowledgment . his word be strong in meaning , but formal in expression ; she herself do not think much about they , be satisfied to do the kind action that call they forth . but her mother criticise they and ponder they . she think she have hit on the truth when she decide in her own mind that it be a very old - fashioned style , and that he and his house and his furniture all want some of the brightening up and polish which they be sure to receive , when she never quite like to finish the sentence definitely , although she keep repeat to herself that " there be no harm in it . " 

 to return to the squire . occupy as he now be , he recover his former health , and something of his former cheerfulness . if Osborne have meet he half - way , it be probable that the old bond between father and son might have be renew ; but Osborne either be really an invalid , or have sink into invalid habit , and make no effort to rally . if his father urge he to go out nay , once or twice he gulp down his pride , and ask Osborne to accompany he Osborne would go to the window and find out some flaw or speck in the wind or weather , and make that an excuse for stop in - door over his book . he would saunter out on the sunny side of the house in a manner that the squire consider as both indolent and unmanly . yet if there be a prospect of his leave home , which he do pretty often about this time , he be seize with a hectic energy : the cloud in the sky , the easterly wind , the dampness of the air , be nothing to he then ; and as the squire do not know the real secret cause of this anxiety to be go , he take it into his head that it arise from Osborne 's dislike to Hamley and to the monotony of his father 's society . 

 " it be a mistake , " think the Squire . " I see it now . " 

 it be possible that Osborne might have be induce to tell his father of his marriage during their long solitary intercourse , if the Squire , in an unlucky moment , have not give he his confidence about Roger 's engagement with Cynthia . it be on one wet Sunday afternoon , when the father and son be sit together in the large empty drawing - room .  CHAPTER XL . 

 MOLLY gibson BREATHES FREELY . 
 

 that be the way in which Mrs. Gibson first broach her intention of accompany Cynthia up to London for a few day ' visit . she have a trick of produce the first sketch of any new plan before an outsider to the family circle ; so that the first emotion of other , if they disapprove of her project , have to be repress , until the idea have become familiar to they . to Molly it seem too charming a proposal ever to come to pass . she have never allow herself to recognize the restraint she be under in her stepmother 's presence ; but all at once she find it out when her heart dance at the idea of three whole day for that it would be at the least of perfect freedom of intercourse with her father ; of old time come back again ; of meal without perpetual fidgetiness after detail of ceremony and correctness of attendance . 

 " we will have bread - and - cheese for dinner , and eat it on our knee ; we will make up for have have to eat sloppy pudding with a fork instead of a spoon all this time , by put our knife in our mouth till we cut ourselves . papa shall pour his tea into his saucer if he be in a hurry ; and if I be thirsty , I will take the slop - basin . and oh , if I could but get , buy , borrow , or steal any kind of an old horse ; my grey skirt be not   new , but it will do ; that would be too delightful ! after all , I think I can be happy again ; for month and month it have seem as if I have get too old ever to feel pleasure , much less happiness again . " 

 so think molly . yet she blush , as if with guilt , when Cynthia , read her thought , say to her one day , 

 " molly , you be very glad to get rid of we , be not you ? "

 " I do not   feel you as such . you , at any rate , have be a new delight to I a sister ; and I never know how charming such a relationship could be . "

" I do not   mean to say I be not often very sorry to feel I be no long first with he ; but it be " the violent colour flush into her face till even her eye burn , and she suddenly find herself on the point of crying ; the weep ash - tree , the misery , the slow dropping comfort , and the comforter come all so vividly before she " it be Roger ! " she go on look up at Cynthia , as she overcome her slight hesitation at mention his name " Roger , who tell I how I ought to take papa 's marriage , when I be first startle and grieve at the news . oh , Cynthia , what a great thing it be to be love by he ! " 

 Cynthia blush , and look fluttered and pleased . 

 " yes , I suppose it be . at the same time , Molly , I be afraid he will expect I to be always as good as he fancy I now , and I shall have to walk on tiptoe all the rest of my life . " 

 " but you be good , Cynthia , " put in Molly . 

 " no , I be not . you be just as much mistaken as he be ; and some day I shall go down in your opinion with a run , just like the hall clock the other day when the spring break . "

 " could you ? would you be my friend if if it turn out ever that I have do very wrong thing ? would you remember how very difficult it have sometimes be to I to act rightly ? " 

 " I wish I could help you , or even understand you , " say Molly , after a moment or two of sad perplexity .

" I can trim bonnet , and make head - dress ; but somehow my hand can not   fold up gown and collar , like your deft little finger . please will you help I to pack ? that be a real , tangible piece of kindness , and not sentimental consolation for sentimental distress , which be , perhaps , imaginary after all . " 

 in general , it be the people that be leave behind stationary , who give way to low spirit at any parting ; the traveller , however bitterly they may feel the separation , find something in the change of scene to soften regret in the very first hour of separation . but as Molly walk home with her father from see Mrs. Gibson and Cynthia off to London by the " Umpire " coach , she almost dance along the street . 

 " now , papa ! " say she , " I be go to have you all to myself for a whole week . you must be very obedient . "

 " we have   just be see my wife and her daughter off to London . Mrs. Gibson have go up for a week ! "

 his eye twinkle , but the rest of his face be perfectly grave . " I be not go to be corrupt . with toil and labour I have   reach a very fair height of refinement . I will not   be pull down again . "

Sister look to sister with an approve nod as Molly name the second letter , which arrive in Hollingford the very day before Mrs. Gibson be to return . they have settle between themselves that two letter would show the right amount of good feeling and proper understanding in the Gibson family : more would have be extravagant ; only one would have be a mere matter of duty . there have be rather a question between Miss Browning and Miss Phobe as to which person the second letter   suppose it come   be to be address to . it would be very conjugal to write twice to Mr. Gibson ; and yet it would be very pretty if molly come in for her share . 

 " you have   have another letter , you say , my dear ? " ask Miss Browning . " I daresay Mrs. Gibson have write to you this time ? " 

 " it be a large sheet , and Cynthia have write on one half to I , and all the rest be to papa . "

I do call that dissipation . why , Thursday would be take up with the journey , and Friday with resting , and Sunday be Sunday all the world over ; and they must have write on Tuesday . well ! I hope Cynthia will not   find Hollingford dull , that be all , when she come back . "

" say Miss Browning , watch she out of the room . as soon as she be fairly go , Miss Browning get up and shut the door quite securely , and then sit down near her sister , she say , in a low voice , " Phobe , it be molly herself that be with Mr. Preston in Heath Lane that day when Mrs. Goodenough see they together !  CHAPTER XLII . 

 the STORM BURSTS . 
 

 the autumn drift away through all its season . the golden corn - harvest , the walk through the stubble - field , and ramble into hazel - copse in search of nut ; the stripping of the apple - orchard of their ruddy fruit , amid the joyous cry and shout of watch child ; and the gorgeous tulip - like colouring of the later time have now come on with the shortening day . there be comparative silence in the land , except for the distant shot , and the whirr of the partridge as they rise up from the field . 

 ever since Miss Browning 's unlucky conversation , thing have be ajar in the Gibsons ' house . Cynthia seem to keep every one out at   mental   arms'-length ; and particularly avoid any private talk with Molly . Mrs. Gibson , still cherish a grudge against Miss Browning for her imply accusation of not look enough after molly , choose to exercise a most wearying supervision over the poor girl . it be , " where have you be , child ? " " who do you see ? " " who be that letter from ? " " why be you so long out when you have only to go to so - and - so ? " just as if molly have really be detect in carry on some underhand intercourse . she answer every question ask of she with the simple truthfulness of perfect innocence ; but the inquiry   although she read their motive , and know that they arise from no especial suspicion of her conduct , but only that Mrs. Gibson might be able to say that she look well after her stepdaughter   chafe she inexpressibly . very often she do not go out at all , soon than have to give a plan of her intended proceeding , when perhaps she have no plan at all , only thought of wander out at her own sweet will , and of take pleasure in the bright solemn fade of the year . it be a very heavy time for Molly , zest and life have flee , and leave so many of the old delight mere shell of seem . she think it be that her youth have flee ; at nineteen ! Cynthia be no long the same , somehow : and perhaps Cynthia 's change would injure she in the distant Roger 's opinion . her stepmother seem almost kind in comparison with Cynthia 's withdrawal of her heart ; Mrs. Gibson worry she , to be sure , with all these form of watch over she ; but in all her other way , she , at any rate , be the same . yet Cynthia herself seem anxious and care - wear , though she would not speak of her anxiety to Molly . and then the poor girl in her goodness would blame herself for feel Cynthia 's change of manner ; for as Molly say to herself , " if it be hard work for I to help always fret after Roger , and wonder where he be , and how he be , what must it be for she ? " 

 one day Mr. Gibson come in , bright and swift . 

 " molly , " say he , " where be Cynthia ? " 

 " go out to do some errand " 

 " well , it be a pity but never mind . put on your bonnet and cloak as fast as you can . I have   have to borrow old Simpson 's dog - cart , there would have be room both for you and Cynthia ; but as it be , you must walk back alone . I will drive you as far on the Barford Road as I can , and then you must jump down . I can not   take you on to Broadhurst 's , I may be keep there for hour . " 

 Mrs. Gibson be out of the room ; out of the house it might be , for all molly care , now she have her father 's leave and command . her bonnet and cloak be on in two minute , and she be sit by her father 's side , the back seat shut up , and the light weight go swiftly and merrily bump over the stone - pave lane . 

 " oh , this be charming ! " say Molly , after a toss - up on her seat from a tremendous bump . 

 " for youth , but not for crab age , " say Mr. Gibson . " my bone be get rheumatic , and would rather go smoothly over macadamize street . " 

 " that be treason to this lovely view and this fine pure air , papa . only I do not   believe you . " 

 " thank you . as you be so complimentary , I think I shall put you down at the foot of this hill ; we have   pass the second mile - stone from Hollingford . " 

 " oh , let I just go up to the top ! I know we can see the blue range of the Malverns from it , and Dorrimer Hall among the wood ; the horse will want a minute 's rest , and then I will get down without a word . " 

 so she go up to the top of the hill ; and there they sate still a minute or two , enjoy the view , without much speak . the wood be golden ; the old house of purple - red brick , with its twisted chimney , rise up from among they face on to green lawn , and a placid lake ; beyond again be the Malvern Hills . 

 " now jump down , lassie , and make the good of your way home before it get dark . you will find the cut over Croston heath shorter than the road we have   come by . " 

 to get to Croston Heath , Molly have to go down a narrow lane overshadow by tree , with picturesque old cottage dot here and there on the steep sandy bank ; and then there come a small wood , and then there be a brook to be cross on a plank - bridge , and up the steeper field on the opposite side be cut step in the turfy path ; these end , she be on Croston Heath , a wide - stretch common skirt by labourer ' dwelling , past which a near road to Hollingford lay . 

 the lonely part of the road be the first the lane , the wood , the little bridge , and the clambering through the upland field . but molly care little for loneliness . she go along the lane under the over - arch elm - branch , from which , here and there , a yellow leaf come float down upon her very dress ; past the last cottage where a little child have tumble down the slop bank , and be publish the accident with frightened cry . molly stoop to pick it up , and take it in her arm in a manner which cause intense surprise to take the place of alarm in its little breast , she carry it up the rough flag step towards the cottage which she suppose to be its home . the mother come run in from the garden behind the house , still hold the late damson she have be gather in her apron ; but , on see she , the little creature hold out its arm to go to she , and she drop her damson all about as she take it , and begin to soothe it as it cry afresh , intersperse her lulling with thank to Molly . she call she by her name ; and on molly ask the woman how she come to know it , she reply that before her marriage she have be a servant of Mrs. Goodenough , and so be " bind to know Dr. Gibson 's daughter by sight . " after the exchange of two or three more word , Molly run down into the lane , and pursue her way , stop here and there to gather a nosegay of such leaf as strike she for their brilliant colouring . she enter the wood . as she turn a corner in the lonely path , she hear a passionate voice of distress ; and in an instant she recognize Cynthia 's tone . she stand still and look around . there be some thick holly - bush shin out dark green in the midst of the amber and scarlet foliage . if any one be there , it must be behind these thick bush . so Molly leave the path , and go straight , plunge through the brown tangled growth of fern and underwood , and turn the holly bush . there stand Mr. Preston and Cynthia ; he hold her hand tight , each look as if just silence in some vehement talk by the rustle of Molly 's footstep .

 for an instant no one speak . then Cynthia say ,

 " the subject of our conversation do not well admit of a third person 's presence . as Miss Gibson seem to wish for your company now , I must beg you to fix some other time and place where we can finish our discussion . " 

 " I will go if Cynthia wish I , " say Molly . 

 " no , no ; stay I want you to stay I want you to hear it all I wish I have tell you soon . " 

 " you mean that you regret that she have not be make aware of our engagement that you promise long ago to be my wife . pray remember that it be you who make I promise secrecy , not I you ! " 

 " I do not   believe he , Cynthia . do not , do not   cry if you can help it ; I do not   believe he . "

" he step forward to try and take her hand and soothe she ; but she shrink away from he , and sob the more irrepressibly . she feel Molly 's presence so much to be a protection that now she dare to let herself go , and to weaken herself by give way to her emotion .

 " I will go if Cynthia tell I , " say he at length . 

 " oh , Molly , I do not   know what to do , " say Cynthia , take down her hand from her tear - stain face , and appeal to Molly , and sob bad than ever ; in fact , she become hysterical , and though she try to speak coherently , no intelligible word would come . 

 " run to that cottage in the tree , and fetch she a cup of water , " say molly . he hesitate a little . 

 " why do not   you go ? " say Molly , impatiently . 

 " I have not do speak to she ; you will not leave before I come back ? " 

 " no . do not   you see she can not   move in this state ? " 

 he go quickly , if reluctantly . 

 Cynthia be some time before she could check her sob enough to speak . at length she say , " molly , I do hate he ! "

 " with all my heart , " say Molly . 

 he bring the water , and Cynthia drank , and be restore to calmness .

 if she hop to carry Cynthia off so easily she be mistaken . Mr. Preston be resolute on this point . he say 

 " I think since Miss Gibson have make herself acquainted with this much , we have well let she know the whole truth that you be engage to marry I as soon as you be twenty ; otherwise your be here with I , and by appointment too , may appear strange even equivocal to she . "

 Mr. Preston force a smile . " I think I have some letter that would convince Miss Gibson of the truth of what I have say ; and which will convince Mr. Osborne Hamley , if necessary I conclude it be to he she be allude . " 

 " I be quite puzzle by you both , " say Molly . " the only thing I do know be , that we ought not to be stand here at this time of evening , and that Cynthia and I shall go home directly . if you want to talk to Miss Kirkpatrick , Mr. Preston , why do not   you come to my father 's house , and ask to see she openly , and like a gentleman ? " 

 " I be perfectly willing , " say he ; " I shall only be too glad to explain to Mr. Gibson on what term I stand in relation to she . if I have not do it soon , it be because I have yield to her wish . "

I be quite well enough to walk , do let we go ; I will tell you all about it when we be at home . " she take Molly 's arm and try to hasten she away ; but Mr. Preston follow , talk as he walk by their side . 

 " I do not know what you will say at home ; but can you deny that you be my promise wife ? can you deny that it have only be at your earnest request that I have keep the engagement secret so long ? " he be unwise Cynthia stop , and turn at bay . 

 " since you will have it out , since I must speak here , I own that what you say be literally true ; that when I be a neglect girl of sixteen , you whom I believe to be a friend , lend I money at my need , and make I give you a promise of marriage . " 

 " make you ! " say he , lay an emphasis on the first word . 

 Cynthia turn scarlet . " ' make ' be not the right word , I confess . I like you then you be almost my only friend and , if it have be a question of immediate marriage , I daresay I should never have object . but I know you well now ; and you have persecute I so of late , that I tell you once for all   as I have tell you before , till I be sick of the very word , that nothing shall ever make I marry you . nothing ! I see there be no chance of escape exposure and , I daresay , lose my character , and I know lose all the few friend I have . "

 " it be hard , " say Mr. Preston . " you may believe all the bad thing you like about I , Cynthia , but I do not   think you can doubt my real , passionate , disinterested love for you . " 

 " I do doubt it , " say Cynthia , break out with fresh energy . " ah ! when I think of the self - deny affection I have see I have know affection that think of other before itself " 

 Mr. Preston break in at the pause she make . she be afraid of reveal too much to he . 

 " you do not call it love which have be willing to wait for year to be silent while silence be desire to suffer jealousy and to bear neglect , rely on the solemn promise of a girl of sixteen for solemn say flimsy , when that girl grow old . Cynthia , I have love you , and I do love you , and I will not   give you up . if you will but keep your word , and marry I , I will swear I will make you love I in return . " 

 " oh , I wish I wish I have never borrow that unlucky money , it be the beginning of it all . oh , Molly , I have save and scrimp to repay it , and he will not   take it now ; I think if I could but repay it , it would set I free . "

 he bow and turn back , vanish rapidly down the field staircase . at any rate that be a relief . yet the two girl hasten on , as if he be still pursue they . once , when molly say something to Cynthia , the latter reply 

 " Molly , if you pity I if you love I do not   say anything more just now . we shall have to look as if nothing have happen when we get home . come to my room when we go upstairs to bed , and I will tell you all . I know you will blame I terribly , but I will tell you all . " 

 so Molly do not say another word till they reach home ; and then , comparatively at ease , inasmuch as no one perceive how late be their return to the house , each of the girl go up into their separate room , to rest and calm themselves before dress for the necessary family gathering at dinner . molly feel as if she be so miserably shake that she could not have go down at all , if her own interest only have be at stake . she sate by her dressing - table , hold her head in her hand , her candle unlighte , and the room in soft darkness , try to still her beat heart , and to recall all she have hear , and what would be its bearing on the life of those whom she love . Roger . oh , Roger ! far away in mysterious darkness of distance loving as he do   ah , that be love ! that be the love to which Cynthia have refer , as worthy of the name !   and the object of his love claim by another false to one she must be ! how could it be ? what would he think and feel if ever he come to know it ? it be of no use try to imagine his pain that could do no good . what lie before Molly be , to try and extricate Cynthia , if she could help she by thought , or advice , or action ; not to weaken herself by let her fancy run into picture of possible , probable suffering . 

 when she go into the drawing - room before dinner , she find Cynthia and her mother by themselves . there be candle in the room , but they be not light , for the wood - fire blaze merrily if fitfully , and they be await Mr. Gibson 's return , which might be expect at any minute . Cynthia sate in the shade , so it be only by her sensitive ear that Molly could judge of her state of composure . Mrs. Gibson be tell some of her day 's adventure whom she have find at home in the call she have be make ; who have be out ; and the small piece of news she have hear . to Molly 's quick sympathy Cynthia 's voice sound languid and weary , but she make all the proper reply , and express the proper interest at the right place , and Molly come to the rescue , chime in , with an effort , it be true ; but Mrs. Gibson be not one to notice slight shade or difference in manner . when Mr. Gibson return , the relative position of the party be alter . it be Cynthia now who raise herself into liveliness , partly from a consciousness that he would have notice any depression , and partly because Cynthia be one of those natural coquette , who , from their cradle to their grave , instinctively bring out all their prettiest air and grace in order to stand well with any man , young or old , who may happen to be present . she listen to his remark and story with all the sweet intentness of happy day , till Molly , silent and wonder , could hardly believe that the Cynthia before she be the same girl as she who be sob and cry as if her heart would break , but two hour before . it be true she look pale and heavy - eye , but that be the only sign she give of her past trouble , which yet must be a present care , think molly . after dinner , Mr. Gibson go out to his town patient ; Mrs. Gibson subside into her arm - chair , hold a sheet of the Times before she , behind which she take a quiet and lady - like doze . Cynthia have a book in one hand , with the other she shade her eye from the light . molly alone could neither read , nor sleep , nor work . she sate in the seat in the bow - window ; the blind be not draw down , for there be no danger of their be overlook . she gaze into the soft outer darkness , and find herself strive to discern the outline of object the cottage at the end of the garden the great beech - tree with the seat round it the wire arch , up which the summer rose have clamber ; each come out faint and dim against the dusky velvet of the atmosphere . presently tea come , and there be the usual nightly bustle . the table be clear , Mrs. Gibson rouse herself , and make the same remark about dear papa that she have do at the same hour for week past . Cynthia too do not look different from usual . and yet what a hide mystery do her calmness hide ! think molly . at length come bed - time , and the customary little speech . both Molly and Cynthia go to their own room without exchange a word . when molly be in hers she have forget whether she be to go to Cynthia , or Cynthia to come to she . she take off her gown and put on her dressing - gown , and stand and wait , and even sit down for a minute or two : but Cynthia do not come , so Molly go and knock at the opposite door , which , to her surprise , she find shut . when she enter the room Cynthia sate by her dressing - table , just as she have come up from the drawing - room . she have be lean her head on her arm , and seem almost to have forget the tryst she have make with Molly , for she look up as if startle , and her face do seem full of worry and distress ; in her solitude she make no more exertion , but give way to thought of care . 

 

 

 CHAPTER XLIII . 

 CYNTHIA 's CONFESSION . 
 

 " you say I might come , " say Molly , " and that you would tell I all . " 

 " you know all , I think , " say Cynthia , heavily . " perhaps you do not   know what excuse I have , but at any rate you know what a scrape I be in . " 

 " I have   be think a great deal , " say Molly , timidly and doubtfully . " and I can not   help fancy if you tell papa " 

 before she could go on , Cynthia have stand up . 

 " no ! " say she . " that I will not . unless I be to leave here at once . and you know I have not another place to go to without warn , I mean . I daresay my uncle would take I in ; he be a relation , and would be bind to stand by I in whatever disgrace I might be ; or perhaps I might get a governess 's situation a pretty governess I should be ! " 

 " pray , please , Cynthia , do not   go off into such wild talking . I do not   believe you have   do so very wrong . you say you have not , and I believe you . that horrid man have manage to get you involve in some way ; but I be sure papa could set it to right , if you would only make a friend of he , and tell he all " 

 " no , Molly , " say Cynthia , " I can not , and there be an end of it . you may if you like , only let I leave the house first ; give I that much time . " 

 " you know I would never tell anything you wish I not to tell , Cynthia , " say Molly , deeply hurt . 

 " would you not , darling ? " say Cynthia , take her hand . " will you promise I that ? quite a sacred promise ? for it would be such a comfort to I to tell you all , now you know so much . " 

 " yes ! I will promise not to tell . you should not have doubt I , " say Molly , still a little sorrowfully . 

 " very well . I trust to you . I know I may . " 

 " but do think of tell papa , and get he to help you , " persevere molly . 

 " never , " say Cynthia , resolutely , but more quietly than before . " do you think I forget what he say at the time of that wretche Mr. Coxe ; how severe he be , and how long I be in disgrace , if indeed I be out of it now ? I be one of those people , as mamma say sometimes I can not live with person who do not   think well of I . it may be a weakness , or a sin , I be sure I do not   know , and I do not   care ; but I really can not be happy in the same house with any one who know my fault , and think that they be great than my merit . now you know your father would do that . I have often tell you that he   and you too , molly ,   have a high standard than I have ever know . oh , I could not   bear it ; if he be to know he would be so angry with I he would never get over it , and I have so like he ! I do so like he ! " 

 " well , never mind , dear ; he shall not know , " say Molly , for Cynthia be again become hysterical , " at least , we will say no more about it now . " 

 " and you will never say any more never promise I , " say Cynthia , take her hand eagerly . 

 " never till you give I leave . now do let I see if I can not help you . lie down on the bed , and I will sit by you , and let we talk it over . " 

 but Cynthia sit down again in the chair by the dressing - table . 

 " when do it all begin ? " say Molly , after a long pause of silence . 

 " long ago four or five year . I be such a child to be leave all to myself . it be the holiday , and mamma be away visit , and the Donaldsons ask I to go with they to the Worcester Festival . you can not   fancy how pleasant it all sound , especially to I . I have be shut up in that great dreary house at Ashcombe , where mamma have her school ; it belong to Lord Cumnor , and Mr. Preston as his agent have to see it all paint and paper ; but , besides that , he be very intimate with we ; I believe mamma think no , I be not sure about that , and I have enough blame to lay at her door , to prevent my tell you anything that may be only fancy " 

 then she pause and sate still for a minute or two , recall the past . molly be strike by the aged and careworn expression which have take temporary hold of the brilliant and beautiful face ; she could see from that how much Cynthia must have suffer from this hide trouble of her . 

 " well ! at any rate we be intimate with he , and he come a great deal about the house , and know as much as any one of mamma 's affair , and all the in and out of her life . I be tell you this in order that you may understand how natural it be for I to answer his question when he come one day and find I , not cry , for you know I be not much give to that , in spite of to - day 's exposure of myself ; but fret and fume because , though mamma have write word I might go with the Donaldsons , she have never say how I be to get any money for the journey , much less for anything of dress , and I have outgrow all my last year 's frock , and as for glove and boot in short , I really have hardly clothe decent enough for church " 

 " why do not   you write to she and tell she all this ? " say molly , half afraid of appear to cast blame by her very natural question . 

 " I wish I have her letter to show you ; you must have see some of mamma 's letter , though ; do not   you know how she always seem to leave out just the important point of every fact ? in this case she descant largely on the enjoyment she be have , and the kindness she be receive , and her wish that I could have be with she , and her gladness that I too be go to have some pleasure ; but the only thing that would have be of real use to I she leave out , and that be where she be go to next . she mention that she be leave the house she be stop at the day after she write , and that she should be at home by a certain date ; but I get the letter on a Saturday , and the festival begin the next Tuesday " 

 " Poor Cynthia ! " say Molly . " still , if you have write , your letter might have be forward . I do not   mean to be hard , only I do so dislike the thought of your ever have make a friend of that man . " 

 " ah ! " say Cynthia , sigh . " how easy it be to judge rightly after one see what evil come from judge wrongly ! I be only a young girl , hardly more than a child , and he be a friend to we then except mamma , the only friend I know ; the donaldson be only kind and good - natured acquaintance . " 

 " I be sorry , " say Molly , humbly , " I have be so happy with papa . I hardly can understand how different it must have be with you . " 

 " different ! I should think so . the worry about money make I sick of my life . we might not say we be poor , it would have injure the school ; but I would have stint and starve if mamma and I have get on as happily together as we might have do as you and Mr. Gibson do . it be not the poverty ; it be that she never seem to care to have I with she . as soon as the holiday come round she be off to some great house or another ; and I daresay I be at a very awkward age to have I lounge about in the drawing - room when caller come . girl at the age I be then be so terribly keen at scent out motif , and put in their disagreeable question as to the little twisting and twirling and vanishing of conversation ; they have   no distinct notion of what be the truth and falsehood of polite life . at any rate , I be very much in mamma 's way , and I feel it . Mr. Preston seem to feel it too for I ; and I be very grateful to he for kind word and sympathetic look crumb of kindness which would have drop under your table unnoticed . so this day , when he come to see how the workman be get on , he find I in the deserted schoolroom , look at my faded summer bonnet and some old ribbon I have be sponge , and half - wear - out glove a sort of rag - fair spread out on the deal table . I be in a regular passion with only look at that shabbiness . he say he be so glad to hear I be go to this festival with the donaldson ; old Betty , our servant , have tell he the news , I believe . but I be so perplexed about money , and my vanity be so put out about my shabby dress , that I be in a pet , and say I should not   go . he sate down on the table , and little by little he make I tell he all my trouble . I do sometimes think he be very nice in those day . somehow I never feel as if it be wrong or foolish or anything to accept his offer of money at the time . he have twenty pound in his pocket , he say , and really do not   know what to do with it , should not   want it for month ; I could repay it , or rather mamma could , when it suit she . she must have know I should want money , and most likely think I should apply to he . twenty pound would not   be too much , I must take it all , and so on . I know at least I think I know that I should never spend twenty pound ; but I think I could give he back what I do not   want , and so well , that be the beginning ! it do not   sound so very wrong , do it , molly ? " 

 " no , " say Molly , hesitatingly . she do not wish to make herself into a hard judge , and yet she do so dislike Mr. Preston . Cynthia go on , 

 " well , what with boot and glove , and a bonnet and a mantle , and a white muslin gown , which be make for I before I leave on Tuesday , and a silk gown that follow to the donaldson ' , and my journey , and all , there be very little left of the twenty pound , especially when I find I must get a ball - dress in Worcester , for we be all to go to the Ball . Mrs. Donaldson give I my ticket , but she rather look grave at my idea of go to the Ball in my white muslin , which I have already wear two evening at their house . oh dear ! how pleasant it must be to be rich ! you know , " continue Cynthia , smile a very little , " I can not   help be aware that I be pretty , and that people admire I very much . I find it out first at the donaldson ' . I begin to think I do look pretty in my fine new clothe , and I see that other people think so too . I be certainly the belle of the house , and it be very pleasant to feel my power . the last day or two of that gay week Mr. Preston join our party . the last time he have see I be when I be dress in shabby clothe too small for I , half - cry in my solitude , neglected and penniless . at the donaldson ' I be a little queen ; and as I say , fine feather make fine bird , and all the people be make much of I ; and at that Ball , which be the first night he come , I have more partner than I know what to do with . I suppose he really do fall in love with I then . I do not   think he have do so before . and then I begin to feel how awkward it be to be in his debt . I could not   give myself air to he as I do to other . oh ! it be so awkward and uncomfortable ! but I like he , and feel he as a friend all the time . the last day I be walk in the garden along with the other , and I think I would tell he how much I have enjoy myself , and how happy I have be , all thank to his twenty pound   I be begin to feel like Cinderella when the clock be strike twelve , and to tell he it should be repay to he as soon as possible , though I turn sick at the thought of tell mamma , and know enough of our affair to understand how very difficult it would be to muster up the money . the end of our talk come very soon ; for , almost to my terror , he begin to talk violent love to I , and to beg I to promise to marry he . I be so frightened , that I run away to the other . but that night I get a letter from he , apologize for startle I , renew his offer , his entreaty for a promise of marriage , to be fulfil at any date I would please to name in fact , a most urgent love - letter , and in it a reference to my unlucky debt , which be to be a debt no long , only an advance of the money to be hereafter mine if only you can fancy it all , Molly , well than I can remember it to tell it you . " 

 " and what do you say ? " ask Molly , breathless . 

 " I do not answer it at all until another letter come , entreat for a reply . by that time mamma have come home , and the old daily pressure and plaint of poverty have come on . Mary Donaldson write to I often , sing the praise of Mr. Preston as enthusiastically as if she have be bribe to do it . I have see he a very popular man in their set , and I like he well enough , and feel grateful to he . so I write and give he my promise to marry he when I be twenty , but it be to be a secret till then . and I try to forget I have ever borrow money of he , but somehow as soon as I feel pledge to he I begin to hate he . I could not   endure his eagerness of greet if ever he find I alone ; and mamma begin to suspect , I think . "

 CHAPTER XLV .

 CHAPTER XLVI . 

 HOLLINGFORD gossip .

 molly go upstairs to get ready to accompany Miss Phobe ; and on open one of her drawer she see Cynthia 's envelope , contain the money she owe to Mr. Preston , carefully seal up like a letter . this be what molly have so unwillingly promise to deliver the last final stroke to the affair . molly take it up , hat it . for a time she have forget it ; and now it be here , face she , and she must try and get rid of it . she put it into her pocket for the chance of the walk and the day , and fortune for once seem to befriend she ; for , on their enter Grinstead 's shop , in which two or three people be now , as always , congregate , make play of examine the book , or business of write down the title of new work in the order - book , there be Mr. Preston . he bow as they come in . he could not help that ; but , at the sight of Molly , he look as ill - temper and out of humour as a man well could do . she be connect in his mind with defeat and mortification ; and besides , the sight of she call up what he desire now , above all thing , to forget ; namely , the deep conviction , receive through Molly 's simple earnestness , of Cynthia 's dislike to he . if Miss Phobe have see the scowl upon his handsome face , she might have undeceive her sister in her supposition about he and molly . but Miss Phobe , who do not consider it quite maidenly to go and stand close to Mr. Preston , and survey the shelf of book in such close proximity to a gentleman , find herself an errand at the other end of the shop , and occupy herself in buy writing - paper . molly finger her valuable letter , as it lie in her pocket ; do she dare to cross over to Mr. Preston , and give it to he , or not ?  scandal sleep in the summer , comparatively speak . its nature be the reverse of that of the dormouse . warm ambient air , loitering abroad , gardening , flower to talk about , and preserve to make , soothe the wicked imp to slumber in the parish of Hollingford in summer - time . but when evening grow short , and people gather round the fire , and put their foot in a circle not on the fender , that be not allow then be the time for confidential conversation ! or in the pause allow for the tea - tray to circulate among the card - table when those who be peaceably incline try to stop the warm discussion about " the odd trick , " and the rather wearisome feminine way of ' shoulder the crutch , and show how field be win " small crumb and scrap of daily news come up to the surface , such as " Martindale have raise the price of his good joint a halfpenny in the pound ; " or , " it be a shame of Sir Harry to order in another book on farriery into the Book Society ; Phobe and I try to read it , but really there be no general interest in it ; " or , " I wonder what Mr. Ashton will do , now Nancy be go to be marry ! why , she be be with he these seventeen year ! it be a very foolish thing for a woman of her age to be think of matrimony ; and so I tell she , when I meet she in the market - place this morning ! " 

 so say Miss Browning on the night in question ; her hand of card lie by she on the puce baize - cover table , while she munch the rich pound - cake of a certain Mrs. Dawes , lately come to inhabit Hollingford . 

 " Matrimony be not so bad as you think for , Miss Browning , " say Mrs. Goodenough , stand up for the holy estate into which she have twice enter . " if I would ha ' see Nancy , I should ha ' give she my mind very different . it be a great thing to be able to settle what you will have for dinner , without never a one interfere with you . " 

 " if that be all ! " say Miss Browning , draw herself up , " I can do that ; and , perhaps , well than a woman who have a husband to please . " 

 " no one can say as I do not   please my husband both on them , though Jeremy be tickler in his taste than poor Harry Beaver . but as I use to say to them , ' leave the victual to I ; it be well for you than know what be to come beforehand . the stomach like to be take by surprise . ' and neither of them ever repent them of their confidence . you may take my word for it , bean and bacon will taste well   and Mr. Ashton 's Nancy in her own house   than all the sweetbread and spring chicken she be be a - do for he this seventeen year . but if I choose , I could tell you of something as would interest you all a deal more than old Nancy 's marriage to a widower with nine child only as the young folk themselves be meet in private , clandestine - like , it be perhaps not for I to tell their secret . " 

 " I be sure I do not   want to hear of clandestine meeting between young man and young woman , " say Miss Browning , throw up her head . " it be disgrace enough to the people themselves , I consider , if they enter on a love affair without the proper sanction of parent . I know public opinion have change on the subject ; but when poor Gratia be married to Mr. Byerley , he write to my father without ever have so much as pay she a compliment , or say more than the most trivial and commonplace thing to she ; and my father and mother send for she into my father 's study , and she say she be never so much frightened in her life , and they say it be a very good offer , and Mr. Byerley be a very worthy man , and they hop she would behave properly to he when he come to supper that night . and after that he be allow to come twice a week till they be marry . my mother and I sate at our work in the bow - window of the Rectory drawing - room , and Gratia and Mr. Byerley at the other end ; and my mother always call my attention to some flower or plant in the garden when it strike nine , for that be his time for go . without offence to the present company , I be rather inclined to look upon matrimony as a weakness to which some very worthy people be prone ; but if they must be marry , let they make the good of it , and go through the affair with dignity and propriety : or if there be misdoing and clandestine meeting , and such thing , at any rate , never let I hear about they ! I think it be you to play , Mrs. Dawes . you will excuse my frankness on the subject of matrimony ! Mrs. Goodenough there can tell you I be a very out - speak person . " 

 " it be not the out - speaking , it be what you say that go against I , Miss Browning , " say Mrs. Goodenough , affront , yet ready to play her card as soon as need . and as for Mrs. Dawes , she be too anxious to get into the genteelest of all   Hollingford   society to object to whatever Miss Browning   who , in right of be a deceased rector 's daughter , rather represent the select circle of the little town   advocate , whether celibacy , marriage , bigamy , or polygamy . 

 so the remainder of the evening pass over without any further reference to the secret Mrs. Goodenough be burn to disclose , unless a remark make apropos de rien by Miss Browning , during the silence of a deal , could be suppose to have connection with the previous conversation . she say suddenly and abruptly , 

 " I do not   know what I have do that any man should make I his slave . " if she be refer to any prospect of matrimonial danger she see opening before her fancy , she might have be comfort . but it be a remark of which no one take any notice , all be far too much engage in the rubber . only when Miss Browning take she early leave   for Miss Phobe have a cold , and be an invalid at home , Mrs. Goodenough burst out with 

 " well ! now I may speak out my mind , and say as how if there be a slave between we two , when Goodenough be alive , it be not   I ; and I do not   think as it be pretty in Miss Browning to give herself such air on her virginity when there be four widow in the room , who have   have six honest man among them for husband . no offence , Miss Airy ! " address an unfortunate little spinster , who find herself the sole representative of celibacy now that Miss Browning be go . " I could tell she of a girl as she be very fond on , who be on the high road to matrimony ; and in as cunne a way as ever I heerd on ; go out at dusk to meet her sweetheart , just as if she be my Sally , or your Jenny . and her name be molly too , which , as I have often think , show a low taste in they as first call she so ; she might as well be a scullery - maid at oncest . not that she be pick up anybody common ; she be look about she for a handsome fellow , and a smart young man enough ! " 

 every one around the table look curious and intent on the disclosure be make , except the hostess , Mrs. Dawes , who smile intelligence with her eye , and knowingly purse up her mouth until Mrs. Goodenough have finish her tale . then she say demurely , 

 " I suppose you mean Mr. Preston and Miss Gibson ? " 

 " why , who tell you ? " say Mrs. Goodenough , turn round upon she in surprise . " you can not   say as I do . there be many a Molly in Hollingford , besides she , though none , perhaps , in such a genteel station in life . I never name she , I be sure . " 

 " no . but I know . I could tell my tale too , " continue Mrs. Dawes . 

 " no ! could you , really ? " say Mrs. Goodenough , very curious and a little jealous . 

 " yes . my uncle sheepshank come upon they in the Park Avenue , he startle them a good deal , he say ; and when he tax Mr. Preston with be with his sweetheart , he do not   deny it . " 

 " well ! now so much have come out , I will tell you what I know . only , lady , I would not   wish to do the girl an unkind turn , so you must keep what I have   get to tell you a secret . " of course they promise ; that be easy . 

 " my Hannah , as married Tom Oakes , and live in Pearson 's Lane , be a - gathering of damson only a week ago , and Molly Gibson be a - walk fast down the lane , quite in a hurry like to meet some one , and Hannah 's little Anna - Maria fall down , and molly   who be a kind - hearted lass enough   pick she up ; so if Hannah have have her doubt before , she have none then . " 

 " but there be no one with she , be there ? " ask one of the lady , anxiously , as Mrs. Goodenough stop to finish her piece of cake , just at this crisis . 

 " no : I say she look as if she be go to meet some one , and by - and - by come Mr. Preston run out of the wood just beyond Hannah 's , and say he , ' a cup of water , please , good woman , for a lady have faint , or be ' sterical or something . ' now though he do not   know Hannah , Hannah know he . ' More folk know Tom Fool , than Tom Fool know , ' ask Mr. Preston 's pardon ; for he be no fool whatever he be . and I could tell you more , and what I have   seed with my own eye .  " it be a very suitable kind of thing , " say Miss Airy ; " why do they make such a mystery of it ? "

 " not at all bad , and I will trouble you not to use such a word as that about Molly Gibson , as I have   know all her life . it be odd if you will .
