 CHAPTER XLVII . 

 SCANDAL and its victim . 
 

 when Mr. Gibson return to Hollingford , he find an accumulation of business wait for he , and he be much inclined to complain of the consequence of the two day ' comparative holiday , which have result in over - work for the week to come . he have hardly time to speak to his family , he have so immediately to rush off to press case of illness . but Molly manage to arrest he in the hall , stand there with his great coat hold out ready for he to put on , but whispering as she do so 

 " Papa ! Mr. Osborne Hamley be here to see you yesterday . he look very ill , and he be evidently frightened about himself . " 

 Mr. Gibson face about , and look at she for a moment ; but all he say be 

 " I will go and see he ; do not   tell your mother where I be go : you have   not mention this to she , I hope ? " 

 " no , " say Molly , for she have only tell Mrs. Gibson of Osborne 's call , not of the occasion for it . 

 " do not   say anything about it ; there be no need . now I think of it , I can not   possibly go to - day , but I will go . " 

 something in her father 's manner dishearten Molly , who have persuade herself that Osborne 's evident illness be partly " nervous , " by which she mean imaginary . she have dwell upon his look of enjoyment at Miss Phobe 's perplexity , and think that no one really believe himself to be in danger could have give the merry glance which he have do ; but after see the seriousness of her father 's face , she recur to the shock she have experience on first see Osborne 's changed appearance . all this time Mrs. Gibson be busy read a letter from Cynthia which Mr. Gibson have bring from London ; for every opportunity of private conveyance be seize upon when postage be so high ; and Cynthia have forget so many thing in her hurried packing , that she now send a list of the clothe which she require . molly almost wonder that it have not come to she ; but she do not understand the sort of reserve that be spring up in Cynthia 's mind towards she . Cynthia herself struggle with the feeling , and try to fight against it by call herself " ungrateful ; " but the truth be , she believe that she no long hold her former high place in Molly 's estimation and she could not help turn away from one who know thing to her discredit . she be fully aware of Molly 's prompt decision and willing action , where action be especially disagreeable , on her behalf ; she know that Molly would never bring up the past error and difficulty ; but still the consciousness that the good , straightforward girl have learn that Cynthia have be guilty of so much underhand work cool her regard , and restrain her willingness of intercourse . reproach herself with ingratitude as she would , she could not help feel glad to be away from Molly ; it be awkward to speak to she as if nothing have happen ; it be awkward to write to she about forget ribbon and lace , when their last conversation have be on such different subject , and have call out such vehement expression of feeling . so Mrs. Gibson hold the list in her hand , and read out the small fragment of news that be intermix with notice of Cynthia 's requirement . 

 " Helen can not be so very ill , " say molly at length , " or Cynthia would not want her pink muslin and daisy wreath . " 

 " I do not   see that that follow , I be sure , " reply Mrs. Gibson rather sharply . " Helen would never be so selfish as to tie Cynthia to her side , however ill she be . indeed , I should not have feel that it be my duty to let Cynthia go to London at all , if I have think she be to be perpetually expose to the depressing atmosphere of a sick - room . besides , it must be so good for Helen to have Cynthia come in with bright pleasant account of the party she have be to even if Cynthia dislike gaiety I should desire she to sacrifice herself and go out as much as she could , for Helen 's sake . my idea of nursing be that one should not be always think of one 's own feeling and wish , but do those thing which will most serve to beguile the weary hour of an invalid . but then so few people have have to consider the subject so deeply as I have do ! " 

 Mrs. Gibson here think fit to sigh before go on with Cynthia 's letter . as far as molly could make any sense out of this rather incoherent epistle , very incoherently read aloud to she , Cynthia be really pleased , and glad to be of use and comfort to Helen , but at the same time very ready to be easily persuade into the perpetual small gaiety which abound in her uncle 's house in London , even at this dead season of the year . Mrs. Gibson come upon Mr. Henderson 's name once , and then go on with a running " um - um - um " to herself , which sound very mysterious , but which might as well have be omit , as all that Cynthia really say about he be , " Mr. Henderson 's mother have advise my aunt to consult a certain Dr. Donaldson , who be say to be very clever in such case as Helen 's , but my uncle be not sufficiently sure of the professional etiquette ,   c. " then there come a very affectionate , carefully word message to Molly , imply a good deal more than be say of love gratitude for the trouble she have take on Cynthia 's behalf . and that be all ; and Molly go away a little depressed ; she know not why . 

 the operation on Lady Cumnor have be successfully perform , and in a few day they hop to bring she down to the tower to recruit her strength in the fresh country air . the case be one which interest Mr. Gibson extremely , and in which his opinion have be prove to be right , in opposition to that of one or two great name in London . the consequence be that he be frequently consult and refer to during the progress of her recovery ; and , as he have much to do in the immediate circle of his Hollingford practice , as well as to write thoughtful letter to his medical brother in London , he find it difficult to spare the three or four hour necessary to go over to Hamley to see Osborne . he write to he , however , beg he to reply immediately and detail his symptom ; and from the answer he receive he do not imagine that the case be immediately press . Osborne , too , deprecate his come over to Hamley for the express purpose of see he . so the visit be defer to that " more convenient season " which be so often too late . 

 all these day the buzz gossip about Molly 's meeting with Mr. Preston , her clandestine correspondence , the secret interview in lonely place , have be gather strength , and assume the positive form of scandal . the simple innocent girl , who walk through the quiet street without a thought of be the object of mysterious implication , become for a time the unconscious black sheep of the town . servant hear part of what be say in their mistress ' drawing - room , and exaggerate the saying amongst themselves with the coarse strengthening of expression common with uneducated people . Mr. Preston himself become aware that her name be be couple with his , though hardly to the extent to which the love of excitement and gossip have carry people 's speech ; he chuckle over the mistake , but take no pain to correct it . " it serve she right , " say he to himself , " for meddle with other folk 's business , " and he feel himself avenge for the discomfiture which her menace of appeal to Lady Harriet have cause he , and the mortification he have experience in learn from her plain - speak lip , how he have be talk over by Cynthia and herself , with personal dislike on the one side , and evident contempt on the other . besides , if any denial of Mr. Preston 's stir up an examination as to the real truth , more might come out of his baffle endeavour to compel Cynthia to keep to her engagement to he than he care to have know . he be angry with himself for still love Cynthia ; love she in his own fashion , be it understand . he tell himself that many a woman of more position and wealth would be glad enough to have he ; some of they pretty woman too . and he ask himself why he be such a confounded fool as to go on hanker after a penniless girl , who be as fickle as the wind ? the answer be silly enough , logically ; but forcible in fact . Cynthia be Cynthia , and not Venus herself could have be her substitute . in this one thing Mr. Preston be more really true than many worthy man ; who , seek to be marry , turn with careless facility from the unattainable to the attainable , and keep their feeling and fancy tolerably loose till they find a woman who consent to be their wife . but no one would ever be to Mr. Preston what Cynthia have be , and be ; and yet he could have stab she in certain of his mood . so , Molly , who have come between he and the object of his desire , be not likely to find favour in his sight , or to obtain friendly action from he . 

 there come a time not very distant from the evening at Mrs. Dawes ' when Molly feel that people look askance at she . Mrs. Goodenough openly pull her grand - daughter away , when the young girl stop to speak to Molly in the street , and an engagement which the two have make for a long walk together be cut very short by a very trumpery excuse . Mrs. Goodenough explain her conduct in the following manner to some of her friend : 

 " you see , I do not   think the bad of a girl for meet her sweetheart here and there and everywhere , till she get talk about ; but then when she do and Molly Gibson 's name be in everybody 's mouth I think it be only fair to Bessy , who have trust I with Annabella not to let her daughter be see with a lass who have manage her matter so badly as to set folk talk about she . my maxim be this , and it be a very good work one , you may depend on't   woman should mind what they be about , and never be talk of ; and if a woman 's talk of , the less her friend have to do with she till the talk have die away , the well . so Annabella be not to have anything to do with Molly Gibson , this visit at any rate . "

 " Phobe ! either you have   some reason for puff yourself out with sigh , or you have   not . if you have a reason , it be your duty to tell it I directly ; and if you have not   a reason , you must break yourself of a bad habit that be grow upon you . " 

 " oh , sister ! do you think it be really my duty to tell you ? it would be such a comfort ; but then I think I ought not ; it will distress you so . " 

 " Nonsense . I be so well prepared for misfortune by the frequent contemplation of its possibility that I believe I can receive any ill news with apparent equanimity and real resignation . besides , when you say yesterday at breakfast - time that you mean to give up the day to make your drawer tidy , I be aware that some misfortune be impend , though of course I could not judge of its magnitude . be the Highchester Bank break ? "

 Miss Phobe begin to cry ; Miss Browning take hold of her arm , and give she a little sharp shake .

 for Miss Browning have rise without speak a word , and be leave the room in a stately and determined fashion .

I see she with my own eye run across the street to Grinstead 's , where he be , for we have just leave he there ; with a letter in her hand , too , which be not there when she come back all fluttered and blushing . but I never think anything of it at the time ; but now all the town be talk about it , and cry shame , and say they ought to be marry . " Miss Phobe sink into sob again ; but be suddenly rouse by a good box on her ear . Miss Browning be stand over she almost tremble with passion .

Mrs. Goodenough but she be a donkey ; and if I convince she , she could never convince any one else . no ; Mrs. Dawes , who tell you , shall tell I , and I will tie my hand together inside my muff , and bind myself over to keep the peace . and when I have   hear what be to be hear , I will put the matter into Mr. Gibson 's hand . that be what I will do . so it be no use your say anything against it , Phobe , for I shall not   attend to you .  " well ! " say he , come in cheerfully , and rub his cold hand as he go straight to the fire , " and what be the matter with we ? it be Phobe , I suppose ? I hope none of those old spasm ? but , after all , a dose or two will set that to right . "

 he sate down by she patiently , when he see her agitation , and take her hand in a kind , friendly manner .

 " and meet he at all sort of unseemly place and hour , out of door , in the dark , faint away in his his arm , if I must speak out . all the town be talk of it . " Mr. Gibson 's hand be over his eye again , and he make no sign ; so Miss Browning go on , add touch to touch . " Mr. Sheepshanks see they together . they have exchange note in Grinstead 's shop ; she run after he there . "

 CHAPTER XLVIII . 

 an innocent CULPRIT .

 CHAPTER XLIX . 

 MOLLY gibson find a champion . 
 

 Lady Cumnor have so far recover from the violence of her attack , and from the consequent operation , as to be able to be remove to the tower for change of air ; and accordingly she be bring thither by her whole family with all the pomp and state become an invalid peeress . there be every probability that " the family " would make a long residence at the Towers than they have do for several year , during which time they have be wanderer hither and thither in search of health . somehow , after all , it be very pleasant and restful to come to the old ancestral home , and every member of the family enjoy it in his or her own way ; Lord Cumnor most especially . his talent for gossip and his love of small detail have scarcely fair play in the hurry of a London life , and be much nip in the bud during his Continental sojourning , as he neither speak French fluently , nor understand it easily when speak . besides , he be a great proprietor , and like to know how his land be go on ; how his tenant be fare in the world . he like to hear of their birth , marriage , and death , and have something of a royal memory for face . in short , if ever a peer be an old woman , Lord Cumnor be that peer ; but he be a very good - natured old woman , and ride about on his stout old cob with his pocket full of halfpence for the child , and little packet of snuff for the old people . like an old woman , too , he enjoy an afternoon cup of tea in his wife 's sitting - room , and over his gossip 's beverage he would repeat all that he have learn in the day .  " Conversation ! do I call it conversation ? I do not   think I say much . I listen . he really have always a great deal to say . More than Preston , for instance . and , by the way , he be tell I something about Preston ; old sheepshank think he will be marry before long , he say there be a great deal of gossip go on about he and Gibson 's daughter . they have   be catch meet in the park , and corresponding , and all that kind of thing that be likely to end in a marriage . " 

 " I shall be very sorry , " say Lady Harriet . " I always like that girl ; and I can not   bear papa 's model land - agent . " 

 " I daresay it be not true , " say Lady Cumnor , in a very audible aside to Lady Harriet . " papa pick up story one day to contradict they the next . "

 " I do not   think it do sound quite a nice story . I wonder what Clare could be do to allow such going on , " say Lady Cuxhaven . 

 " I think it be much more likely that Clare 's own daughter that pretty pawky Miss Kirkpatrick be the real heroine of this story , " say Lady Harriet . " she always look like a heroine of genteel comedy ; and those young lady be capable of a good deal of innocent intriguing , if I remember rightly . now little Molly Gibson have a certain gaucherie about she which would disqualify she at once from any clandestine proceeding . besides , ' clandestine ! I be sorry I say anything about it , if it annoy you or my lady there . sheepshank do say Miss Gibson , though , and he go on to say it be a pity the girl have get herself so talk about ; for it be the way they have carry on that give rise to all the chatter .  " I warn she against he the day of her father 's wedding . and what a straightforward , out - speak topic it be then ! I do not   believe it ; it be only one of old sheepshank ' story , half invention and half deafness . "

 " certainly ; I shall only be too happy to give you any information in my power .  he could not help the touch of insolence that accompany these last word . it be not in the word themselves , nor in the tone in which they be speak , nor in the look which accompany they , it be in all ; it imply a doubt of Lady Harriet 's right to question he as she do ; and there be something of defiance in it as well . but this touch of insolence put Lady Harriet 's mettle up ; and she be not one to check herself , in any course , for the opinion of an inferior .

 CHAPTER L. 

 CYNTHIA AT BAY . 
 

 Mrs. Gibson be slow in recover her strength after the influenza , and before she be well enough to accept Lady Harriet 's invitation to the Towers , Cynthia come home from London . if Molly have think her manner of departure be scarcely as affectionate and considerate as it might have be , if such a thought have cross Molly 's fancy for an instant , she be repentant for it as soon as ever Cynthia return , and the girl meet together face to face , with all the old familiar affection , go upstairs to the drawing - room , with their arm round each other 's waist , and sit there together hand in hand . Cynthia 's whole manner be more quiet than it have be , when the weight of her unpleasant secret rest on her mind , and make she alternately despondent or flighty . 

 " after all , " say Cynthia , " there be a look of home about these room which be very pleasant . but I wish I could see you look strong , mamma ! that be the only unpleasant thing . molly , why do not   you send for I ? " 

 " I want to do , " begin molly 

 " but I would not   let she , " say Mrs. Gibson . " you be much well in London than here , for you could have do I no good ; and your letter be very agreeable to read ; and now Helen be well , and I be nearly well , and you have   come home just at the right time , for everybody be full of the Charity Ball . "

 " you really seem determined to make I out bad than I be , child , " say Mrs. Gibson , rather querulously , she be one of those who , when their malady be only trifle , exaggerate it , but when it be really of some consequence , be unwilling to sacrifice any pleasure by acknowledge it . it be well for she in this instance that her husband have wisdom and authority enough to forbid she go to this ball , on which she have set her heart ; but the consequence of his prohibition be an increase of domestic plaintiveness and low spirit , which seem to tell on Cynthia the bright gay Cynthia herself and it be often hard work for Molly to keep up the spirit of two other people as well as her own . Ill - health might account for Mrs. Gibson 's despondency , but why be Cynthia so silent , not to say so sighing ? molly be puzzle to account for it ; and all the more perplexed because from time to time Cynthia keep call upon she for praise for some unknown and mysterious virtue that she have practise ; and Molly be young enough to believe that , after any exercise of virtue , the spirit rise , cheer up by an approve conscience . such be not the case with Cynthia , however . she sometimes say such thing as these , when she have be particularly inert and desponding : 

 " ah , Molly , you must let my goodness lie fallow for a while ! it have bear such a wonderful crop this year . I have be so pretty - behaved if you know all ! " or , " really , Molly , my virtue must come down from the cloud ! it be strained to the utmost in London and I find it be like a kite after soar aloft for some time , it suddenly come down , and get tangle in all sort of briar and bramble ; which thing be an allegory , unless you can bring yourself to believe in my extraordinary goodness while I be away give I a sort of right to fall foul of all mamma 's briar and bramble now . " 

 but Molly have have some experience of Cynthia 's whim of perpetually hint at a mystery which she do not mean to reveal in the Mr. Preston day , and , although she be occasionally pique into curiosity , Cynthia 's allusion at something more in the background fall in general on rather deaf ear . one day the mystery burst its shell , and come out in the shape of an offer make to Cynthia by Mr. Henderson and refuse . under all the circumstance , Molly could not appreciate the heroic goodness so often allude to . the revelation of the secret at last take place in this way . Mrs. Gibson breakfast in bed : she have do so ever since she have have the influenza ; and , consequently , her own private letter always go up on her breakfast - tray . one morning she come into the drawing - room early than usual , with an open letter in her hand . 

 " I have   have a letter from aunt Kirkpatrick , Cynthia . she send I my dividend , your uncle be so busy . but what do she mean by this , Cynthia ? "   hold out the letter to she , with a certain paragraph indicate by her finger . Cynthia put she net on one side , and look at the writing . suddenly her face turn scarlet , and then become of a deadly white . she look at Molly , as if to gain courage from the strong serene countenance . 

 " it mean mamma , I may as well tell you at once Mr. Henderson offer to I while I be in London , and I refuse he . " 

 " refuse he and you never tell I , but let I hear it by chance ! really , Cynthia , I think you be very unkind . and pray what make you refuse Mr. Henderson ? such a fine young man , and such a gentleman ! your uncle tell I he have a very good private fortune beside . " 

 " Mamma , do you forget that I have promise to marry Roger Hamley ? " say Cynthia quietly . 

 " no ! of course I do not   how can I , with molly always din the word ' engagement ' into my ear ? but really , when one consider all the uncertainty , and after all it be not a distinct promise , he seem almost as if he might have look forward to something of this sort . "

 in a little while Mrs. Gibson be able to accept the often - repeat invitation from the tower to go and stay there for a day or two .  " well , mamma , how be you ? we do not   expect you so early ! let I take off your bonnet and shawl ! " she reply dolefully ,

I do not   like it , at any rate . it be not pleasant to I to hear first of my daughter 's misdoing from Lady Cumnor , and then to be lecture about she , and her flirt , and her jilting , as if I have have anything to do with it .  " troubles never come alone . "

 CHAPTER LII . 

 SQUIRE HAMLEY 's SORROW . 
 

 it seem very long before Mr. Gibson come down . he go and stand with his back to the empty fireplace , and do not speak for a minute or two . 

 " he be go to bed , " say he at length . " Robinson and I have get he there . but just as I be leave he he call I back and ask I to let you stop . I be sure I do not   know but one do not   like to refuse at such a time . " 

 " I wish to stay , " say Molly . 

 " do you ? there be a good girl . but how will you manage ? " 

 " oh , never mind that . I can manage . Papa , " she pause " what do Osborne die of ? " she ask the question in a low , awe - stricken voice . 

 " something wrong about the heart . you would not   understand if I tell you . I apprehend it for some time ; but it be well not to talk of such thing at home . when I see he on Thursday week , he seem well than I have   see he for a long time . I tell Dr. Nicholls so . but one never can calculate in these complaint . " 

 " you see he on Thursday week ? why , you never mention it ! " say Molly . 

 " no . I do not   talk of my patient at home . besides , I do not   want he to consider I as his doctor , but as a friend . any alarm about his own health would only have hasten the catastrophe . " 

 " then do not   he know that he be ill ill of a dangerous complaint , I mean : one that might end as it have do ? " 

 " no ; certainly not . he would only have be watch his symptom accelerate matter , in fact . " 

 " oh , papa ! " say Molly , shock . 

 " I have   no time to go into the question , " Mr. Gibson continue . " and until you know what have to be say on both side and in every instance , you be not qualified to judge . we must keep our attention on the duty in hand now . you sleep here for the remainder of the night , which be more than half - go already ? " 

 " yes . " 

 " promise I to go to bed just as usual . you may not think it , but most likely you will go to sleep at once . People do at your age . " 

 " Papa , I think I ought to tell you something . I know a great secret of Osborne 's , which I promise solemnly not to tell ; but the last time I see he I think he must have be afraid of something like this . " a fit of sobbing come upon she , which her father be afraid would end in hysteric . but suddenly she master herself , and look up into his anxious face , and smile to reassure he .

 " married ! nonsense . what make you think so ? "

 CHAPTER LIII . 

 unlooked - for arrival . 
 

 Robinson open the door for Molly almost before the carriage have fairly draw up at the Hall , and tell she that the squire have be very anxious for her return , and have more than once send he to an upstairs window , from which a glimpse of the hill - road between Hollingford and Hamley could be catch , to know if the carriage be not yet in sight . molly go into the drawing - room . the squire be stand in the middle of the floor await she in fact , long to go out and meet she , but restrain by a feeling of solemn etiquette , which prevent his move about as usual in that house of mourning . he hold a paper in his hand , which be tremble with excitement and emotion ; and four or five open letter be strew on a table near he . 

 " it be all true , " he begin ; ' she be his wife , and he be her husband be her husband that be the word for it be ! poor lad ! poor lad ! it be cost he a deal . pray God , it be not   my fault . read this , my dear . it be a certificate . it be all regular Osborne Hamley to Marie - Aimťe Scherer , parish - church and all , and witness . oh , dear !  Molly 's knowledge of write French of the present day be not so great as her knowledge of the French of the Mťmoires de Sully , and neither the spelling nor the writing of the letter be of the good ; but she manage to translate into good enough colloquial English some innocent sentence of love , and submission to Osborne 's will as if his judgment be infallible , and of faith in his purpose , little sentence in " little language " that go home to the squire 's heart . perhaps if molly have read French more easily she might not have translate they into such touching , homely , broken word . here and there , there be expression in English ; these the hungry - hearted Squire have read while wait for Molly 's return . every time she stop , he say , " go on . " have you see this , sir ? this certificate of baptism "   read aloud   " of Roger Stephen Osborne Hamley , bear June 21 , 183 , child of Osborne Hamley and Marie - Aimťe his wife "

" ' Roger , ' that be I , ' Stephen , ' that be my poor old father : he die when he be not so old as I be ; but I have   always think on he as very old . he be main and fond of Osborne , when he be quite a little one . it be good of the lad to have think on my father Stephen . ay ! that be his name . and Osborne Osborne Hamley ! one Osborne Hamley lie dead on his bed and t'other t'other I have   never see , and never hear on till to - day . he must be call Osborne , Molly . there be a Roger there be two for that matter ; but one be a good - for - nothing old man ; and there be never an Osborne any more , unless this little thing be call Osborne ; we will have he here , and get a nurse for he ; and make his mother comfortable for life in her own country . Osborne Hamley ! and if God will give I grace , he shall never hear a cross word from I never ! he shall not   be afeard of I . oh , my Osborne , my Osborne "   he burst out , " do you know now how bitter and sore be my heart for every hard word as I ever speak to you ? do you know now how I love you my boy my boy ? " 

 from the general tone of the letter , molly doubt if the mother would consent , so easily as the Squire seem to expect , to be part from her child ; the letter be not very wise , perhaps   though of this molly never think , but a heart full of love speak tender word in every line . still , it be not for Molly to talk of this doubt of hers just then ; but rather to dwell on the probable grace and charm of the little Roger Stephen Osborne Hamley . she let the Squire exhaust himself in wonder as to the particular of every event , help he out in conjecture ; and both of they , from their imperfect knowledge of possibility , make the most curious , fantastic , and improbable guess at the truth . and so that day pass over , and the night come . 

 there be not many people who have any claim to be invite to the funeral , and of these Mr. Gibson and the Squire 's hereditary man of business have take charge . but when Mr. Gibson come , early on the follow morning , Molly refer the question to he , which have suggest itself to her mind , though apparently not to the Squire 's , what intimation of her loss should be send to the widow , live solitary near Winchester , watch and wait , if not for his come who lie dead in his distant home , at least for his letter . one from she have already come , in her foreign handwriting , to the post - office to which all her communication be usually send , but of course they at the Hall know nothing of this . 

 " she must be tell , " say Mr. Gibson , musing . 

 " yes , she must , " reply his daughter . " but how ? " 

 " a day or two of waiting will do no harm , " say he , almost as if he be anxious to delay the solution of the problem . " it will make her anxious , poor thing , and all sort of gloomy possibility will suggest themselves to her mind amongst they the truth ; it will be a kind of preparation . " 

 " for what ? something must be do at last , " say Molly . 

 " yes ; true . suppose you write , and say he be very ill ; write to - morrow . I daresay they have   indulge themselves in daily postage , and then she will have have three day ' silence . you say how you come to know all you do about it ; I think she ought to know he be very ill in great danger , if you like : and you can follow it up next day with the full truth . I would not   worry the squire about it . after the funeral we will have a talk about the child . " 

 " she will never part with it , " say Molly .

" 

 the illness which he apprehend come upon molly ; not violently or acutely , so that there be any immediate danger to be dread ; but make a long pull upon her strength , which seem to lessen day by day , until at last her father fear that she might become a permanent invalid . there be nothing very decide or alarming to tell Cynthia , and Mrs. Gibson keep the dark side from she in her letter . "

" 

 so Molly 's health and spirit improve rapidly after Cynthia 's return : and although she be likely to retain many of her invalid habit during the summer , she be able to take drive , and enjoy the fine weather ; it be only she as yet tender spirit that require a little management . all the Hollingford people forget that they have ever think of she except as a darling of the town ; and each in his or her way show kind interest in her father 's child . "

 CHAPTER LV . 

 an ABSENT LOVER return . 
 

 Illustrationnd now it be late June ; and to Molly 's and her father 's extreme urgency in pushing , and Mr. and Mrs. Kirkpatrick 's affectionate persistency in pull , Cynthia have yield , and have go back to finish her interrupted visit in London , but not before the bruit of her previous sudden return to nurse Molly have tell strongly in her favour in the fluctuate opinion of the little town . her affair with Mr. Preston be thrust into the shade ; while every one be speak of her warm heart . under the gleam of Molly 's recovery everything assume a rosy hue , as indeed become the time when actual rose be fully in bloom .

 CHAPTER LVI . 

 " OFF with the OLD love , and on with the NEW . " 
 

 the next morning see Mrs. Gibson in a much more contented frame of mind . she have write and post her letter , and the next thing be to keep Cynthia in what she call a reasonable state , or , in other word , to try and cajole she into docility . but it be so much labour lose . Cynthia have already receive a letter from Mr. Henderson before she come down to breakfast , a declaration of love , a proposal of marriage as clear as word could make it ; together with an intimation that , unable to wait for the slow delay of the post , he be go to follow she down to Hollingford , and would arrive at the same time that she have do herself on the previous day . Cynthia say nothing about this letter to any one . she come late into the breakfast - room , after Mr. and Mrs. Gibson have finish the actual business of the meal ; but her unpunctuality be quite account for by the fact that she have be travel all the night before . molly be not as yet strong enough to get up so early . Cynthia hardly speak , and do not touch her food . Mr. Gibson go about his daily business , and Cynthia and her mother be leave alone . 

 " my dear , " say Mrs. Gibson , " you be not eat your breakfast as you should do . I be afraid our meal seem very plain and homely to you after those in Hyde Park Street ? " 

 " no , " say Cynthia ; " I be not hungry , that be all . " 

 " if we be as rich as your uncle , I should feel it to be both a duty and a pleasure to keep an elegant table ; but limited mean be a sad clog to one 's wish . I do not   suppose that , work as he will , Mr. Gibson can earn more than he do at present ; while the capability of the law be boundless . Lord Chancellor ! Titles as well as fortune ! "

 CHAPTER LVII . 

 BRIDAL visit and ADIEUX .

"

 CHAPTER LIX . 

 MOLLY gibson AT HAMLEY HALL . 
 

 the conversation end there for the time . wedding - cake and wine be bring in , and it be Molly 's duty to serve they out . but those last word of Mrs. Goodenough 's tingle in her ear , and she try to interpret they to her own satisfaction in any way but the obvious one . and that , too , be destine to be confirm ; for directly after Mrs. Goodenough take she leave , Mrs. Gibson desire Molly to carry away the tray to a table close to an open corner window , where the thing might be place in readiness for any future caller ; and underneath this open window go the path from the house - door to the road . molly hear Mrs. Goodenough say to her grand - daughter ,

 CHAPTER LX . 

 ROGER HAMLEY 'S CONFESSION . 
 

 Roger have a great deal to think of as he turn away from look after the carriage as long as it could be see . the day before , he have believe that Molly have come to view all the symptom of his grow love for she , symptom which he think have be so patent , as disgusting inconstancy to the inconstant Cynthia ; that she have feel that an attachment which could be so soon transfer to another be not worth have ; and that she have desire to mark all this by her change treatment of he , and so to nip it in the bud . but this morning her old sweet , frank manner have return in their last interview , at any rate . he puzzle himself hard to find out what could have distress she at breakfast - time . he even go so far as to ask Robinson whether Miss Gibson have receive any letter that morning ; and when he hear that she have have one , he try to believe that the letter be in some way the cause of her sorrow . so far so good . they be friend again after their unspoken difference ; but that be not enough for Roger . he feel every day more and more certain that she , and she alone , could make he happy . he have feel this , and have partly give up all hope , while his father have be urge upon he the very course he most desire to take . no need for " try " to love she , he say to himself , that be already do . and yet he be very jealous on her behalf . be that love worthy of she which have once be give to Cynthia ? be not this affair too much a mocking mimicry of the last again just on the point of leave England for a considerable time if he follow she now to her own home , in the very drawing - room where he have once offer to Cynthia ? and then by a strong resolve he determine on this course . they be friend now , and he kiss the rose that be her pledge of friendship . if he go to Africa , he run some deadly chance ; he know well what they be now than he have do when he go before . until his return he would not even attempt to win more of her love than he already have . but once safe home again , no weak fancy as to what might or might not be her answer should prevent his run all chance to gain the woman who be to he the one who excel all . his be not the poor vanity that think more of the possible mortification of a refusal than of the precious jewel of a bride that may be win . somehow or another , please God to send he back safe , he would put his fate to the touch . and till then he would be patient . he be no long a boy to rush at the coveted object ; he be a man capable of judging and abide . 

 molly send her father , as soon as she could find he , to the Hall ; and then sate down to the old life in the home drawing - room , where she miss Cynthia 's bright presence at every turn . "

 " and go into lodging at Jennings ' farm ; not four hundred yard from the Park - field gate , " continue Mr. Gibson . " the Squire and his daughter - in - law have get to be much well friend over the little fellow 's sick - bed ; and I think he see now how impossible it would be for the mother to leave her child , and go and be happy in France , which have be the notion run in his head all this time . to buy she off , in fact . but that one night , when I be very uncertain whether I could bring he through , they take to cry together , and condole with each other ; and it be just like tear down a curtain that have be between they ; they have be rather friend than otherwise ever since . still Roger "   Molly 's cheek grow warm and her eye soft and bright ; it be such a pleasure to hear his name   " and I both agree that his mother know much well how to manage the boy than his grandfather do . I suppose that be the one good thing she get from that hard - hearted mistress of her . she certainly have be well train in the management of child . and it make her impatient , and annoyed , and unhappy , when she see the squire give the child nut and ale , and all sort of silly indulgence , and spoil he in every possible way . yet she be a coward , and do not   speak out her mind . now by be in lodging , and have her own servant nice pretty room they be , too ; we go to see they , and Mrs. Jennings promise to attend well to Mrs. Osborne Hamley , and be very much honour , and all that sort of thing not ten minute ' walk from the Hall , too , so that she and the little chap may easily go backwards and forwards as often as they like , and yet she may keep the control over the child 's discipline and diet . in short , I think I have   do a good day 's work , " he continue , stretch himself a little ; and then with a shake rouse himself , and make ready to go out again , to see a patient who have send for he in his absence . 

 " a good day 's work ! " he repeat to himself as he run downstairs . " I do not   know when I have be so happy ! " for he have not tell Molly all that have pass between he and Roger . Roger have begin a fresh subject of conversation just as Mr. Gibson be hasten away from the Hall , after complete the new arrangement for Aimťe and her child . 

 " you know that I set off next Tuesday , Mr. Gibson , do not   you ? " say Roger , a little abruptly . 

 " to be sure . I hope you will be as successful in all your scientific object as you be the last time , and have no sorrow await you when you come back . " 

 " thank you . yes . I hope so . you do not   think there be any danger of infection now , do you ? " 

 " no ! if the disease be to spread through the household , I think we should have have some sign of it before now . one be never sure , remember , with scarlet fever . " 

 Roger be silent for a minute or two . " should you be afraid , " he say at length , " of see I at your house ? " 

 " thank you ; but I think I would rather decline the pleasure of your society there at present . it be only three week or a month since the child begin . besides , I shall be over here again before you go . I be always on my guard against symptom of dropsy . I have know it supervene . " 

 " then I shall not see Molly again ! " say Roger , in a tone and with a look of great disappointment . 

 Mr. Gibson turn his keen , observant eye upon the young man , and look at he in as penetrate a manner as if he have be begin with an unknown illness . then the doctor and the father compress his lip and give vent to a long intelligent whistle . " whew ! " say he . 

 Roger 's bronzed cheek take a deep shade . 

 " you will take a message to she from I , will not   you ? a message of farewell ? " he plead . 

 " not i. I be not go to be a message - carrier between any young man and young woman . I will tell my womenkind I forbid you to come near the house , and that you be sorry to go away without bid good - by . that be all I shall say . " 

 " but you do not disapprove ? I see you guess why . oh ! Mr. Gibson , just speak to I one word of what must be in your heart , though you be pretend not to understand why I would give world to see Molly again before I go . " 

 " my dear boy ! " say Mr. Gibson , more affected than he like to show , and lay his hand on Roger 's shoulder . then he pull himself up , and say gravely enough , 

 " Mind , Molly be not Cynthia . if she be to care for you , she be not one who could transfer her love to the next comer . " 

 " you mean not as readily as I have do , " reply Roger . " I only wish you could know what a different feeling this be to my boyish love for Cynthia . " 

 " I be not   think of you when I speak ; but , however , as I might have remember afterwards that you be not a model of constancy , let we hear what you have to say for yourself . " 

 " not much . I do love Cynthia very much . her manner and her beauty bewitch I ; but her letter , short , hurried letter , sometimes show that she really have not   take the trouble to read mine through , I can not tell you the pain they give I ! twelve month ' solitude , in frequent danger of one 's life face to face with death sometimes age a man like many year ' experience . still I long for the time when I should see her sweet face again , and hear her speak . then the letter at the Cape ! and still I hop . but you know how I find she , when I go to have the interview which I trust might end in the renewal of our relation , engage to Mr. Henderson . I see she walk with he in your garden , coquet with he about a flower , just as she use to do with I . I can see the pitying look in Molly 's eye as she watch I ; I can see it now . and I could beat myself for be such a blind fool as to what must she think of I ? how she must despise I , choose the false Duessa . " 

 " come , come ! Cynthia be not   so bad as that . she be a very fascinating , faulty creature . " 

 " I know ! I know ! I will never allow any one to say a word against she . if I call she the false Duessa it be because I want to express my sense of the difference between she and molly as strongly as I could . you must allow for a lover 's exaggeration . besides , all I want to say be , do you think that Molly , after see and know that I have love a person so inferior to herself , could ever be bring to listen to I ? " 

 " I do not   know . I can not   tell . and even if I could , I would not . only if it be any comfort to you , I may say what my experience have teach I . woman be queer , unreasone creature , and be just as likely as not to love a man who have be throw away his affection . " 

 " thank you , sir ! " say Roger , interrupt he . " I see you mean to give I encouragement . and I have resolve never to give molly a hint of what I feel till I return , and then to try and win she by every mean in my power . I determine not to repeat the former scene in the former place , in your drawing - room , however I might be tempt . and perhaps , after all , she avoid I when she be here last . " 

 " now , Roger , I have   listen to you long enough . if you have   nothing well to do with your time than to talk about my daughter , I have . when you come back it will be time enough to inquire how far your father would approve of such an engagement . " 

 " he himself urge it upon I the other day but then I be in despair I think it be too late . " 

 " and what mean you be likely to have of maintain a wife ? I always think that point be pass too lightly over when you form your hurried engagement to Cynthia . I be not mercenary , molly have some money independently of I , that she by the way know nothing of , not much ; and I can allow her something . but all these thing must be leave till your return . " 

 " then you sanction my attachment ? " 

 " I do not   know what you mean by sanction it . I can not   help it . I suppose lose one 's daughter be a necessary evil . still " see the disappointed expression on Roger 's face " it be but fair to you to say , I would rather give my child , my only child , remember ! to you , than to any man in the world ! " 

 " thank you ! " say Roger , shake hand with Mr. Gibson , almost against the will of the latter . " and I may see she , just once , before I go ? " 

 " decidedly not . there I come in as doctor as well as father . no ! " 

 " but you will take a message , at any rate ? " 

 " to my wife and to she conjointly . I will not separate they . I will not in the slight way be a go - between . " 

 " very well , " say Roger . " tell they both as strongly as you can how I regret your prohibition . I see I must submit . but if I do not   come back , I will haunt you for have be so cruel . " 

 " come , I like that . give I a wise man of science in love ! no one beat he in folly . good - by . " 

 " good - by . you will see molly this afternoon ! " 

 " to be sure . and you will see your father . but I do not   heave such portentous sigh at the thought . " 

 Mr. Gibson give Roger 's message to his wife and to Molly that evening at dinner . it be but what the latter have expect , after all her father have say of the very great danger of infection ; but now that her expectation come in the shape of a final decision , it take away her appetite . she submit in silence ; but her observant father notice that after this speech of his , she only play with the food on her plate , and conceal a good deal of it under her knife and fork . 

 " Lover versus father ! " think he , half sadly . " Lover win . " and he , too , become indifferent to all that remain of his dinner . Mrs. Gibson patter on ; and nobody listen . 

 the day of Roger 's departure come . molly try hard to forget it in work away at a cushion she be prepare as a present to Cynthia ; people do worst - work in those day . one , two , three . one , two , three , four , five , six , seven ; all wrong : she be think of something else , and have to unpick it . it be a rainy day , too ; and Mrs. Gibson , who have plan to go out and pay some call , have to stay indoor . this make her restless and fidgety . she keep go backwards and forwards to different window in the drawing - room to look at the weather , as if she imagine that while it rain at one window , it might be fine weather at another . 

 " molly come here ! who be that man wrap up in a cloak , there , near the Park wall , under the beech - tree he have be there this half - hour and more , never stir , and look at this house all the time ! I think it be very suspicious . " 

 molly look , and in an instant recognize Roger under all his wrap . her first instinct be to draw back . the next to come forwards , and say " why , mamma , it be Roger Hamley ! look now he be kiss his hand ; he be wish we good - by in the only way he can ! " and she respond to his sign ; but she be not sure if he perceive her modest quiet movement , for Mrs. Gibson become immediately so demonstrative that molly fancy that her eager foolish pantomimic motion must absorb all his attention . 

 " I call this so attentive of he , " say Mrs. Gibson , in the midst of a volley of kiss of her hand . " really , it be quite romantic . it remind I of former day but he will be too late ! I must send he away ; it be half - past twelve ! " and she take out her watch and hold it up , tap it with her forefinger , and occupy the very centre of the window . molly could only peep here and there , dodge now up , now down , now on this side , now on that , of the perpetually - move arm . she fancy she see something of a correspond movement on Roger 's part . at length he go away slowly , slowly , and often look back , in spite of the tap watch . Mrs. Gibson at last retreat , and Molly quietly move into her place to see his figure once more before the turn of the road hide it from her view . he , too , know where the last glimpse of Mr. Gibson 's house be to be obtain , and once more he turn , and his white handkerchief float in the air . molly wave her high up , with eager longing that it should be see . and then , he be go ! and Molly return to her worst - work , happy , glowing , sad , content , and think to herself how sweet be friendship ! 

 when she come to a sense of the present , Mrs. Gibson be say , 

 " upon my word , though Roger Hamley have never be a great favourite of mine , this little attention of his have remind I very forcibly of a very charming young man a soupirant , as the French would call he Lieutenant Harper you must have hear I speak of he , molly ? " 

 " I think I have ! " say Molly , absently . 

 " well , you remember how devoted he be to I when I be at Mrs. Duncombe 's , my first situation , and I only seventeen . and when the recruiting party be order to another town , poor Mr. Harper come and stand opposite the schoolroom window for nearly an hour , and I know it be his do that the band play ' the girl I leave behind I , ' when they march out the next day . poor Mr. Harper ! it be before I know dear Mr. Kirkpatrick ! dear I . how often my poor heart have have to bleed in this life of mine ! not but what dear papa be a very worthy man , and make I very happy . he would spoil I , indeed , if I would let he . still he be not as rich as Mr. Henderson . " 

 that last sentence contain the germ of Mrs. Gibson 's present grievance . have marry Cynthia , as her mother put it take credit to herself as if she have have the principal part in the achievement she now become a little envious of her daughter 's good fortune in be the wife of a young , handsome , rich , and moderately fashionable man , who live in London . she naively express her feeling on this subject to her husband one day when she be really not feel quite well , and when consequently her annoyance be much more present to her mind than her source of happiness . 

 " it be such a pity ! " say she , " that I be bear when I be . I should so have like to belong to this generation . " 

 " that be sometimes my own feeling , " say he . " so many new view seem to be open in science , that I should like , if it be possible , to live till their reality be ascertain , and one see what they lead to . but I do not   suppose that be your reason , my dear , for wish to be twenty or thirty year young . " 

 " no , indeed . and I do not put it in that hard unpleasant way ; I only say I should like to belong to this generation . to tell the truth , I be think of Cynthia . without vanity , I believe I be as pretty as she be when I be a girl , I mean ; I have not her dark eyelash , but then my nose be straight . and now look at the difference ! I have to live in a little country town with three servant , and no carriage ; and she with her inferior good look will live in Sussex Place , and keep a man and a brougham , and I do not   know what . but the fact be , in this generation there be so many more rich young man than there be when I be a girl . " 

 " oh , ho ! so that be your reason , be it , my dear ? if you have be young now you might have marry somebody as well off as Walter ? " 

 " yes ! " say she . " I think that be my idea . of course I should have like he to be you . I always think if you have go to the bar you might have succeed well , and live in London , too . I do not   think Cynthia care much where she live , yet you see it have come to she . " 

 " what have London ? " 

 " oh , you dear , facetious man . now that be just the thing to have captivate a jury . I do not   believe Walter will ever be so clever as you be . yet he can take Cynthia to Paris , and abroad , and everywhere . I only hope all this indulgence will not   develope the fault in Cynthia 's character . it be a week since we hear from she , and I do write so particularly to ask she for the autumn fashion before I buy my new bonnet . but rich be a great snare . " 

 " be thankful you be spar temptation , my dear . " 

 " no , I be not . everybody like to be tempt . and , after all , it be very easy to resist temptation , if one wish . " 

 " I do not   find it so easy , " say her husband . 

 " here 's medicine for you , mamma , " say Molly , enter with a letter hold up in her hand . " a letter from Cynthia . " 

 " oh , you dear little messenger of good news ! there be one of the heathen deity in Mangnall 's Questions whose office it be to bring news . the letter be date from Calais . they be come home ! she be buy I a shawl and a bonnet ! the dear creature ! always think of other before herself : good fortune can not spoil she . they have   a fortnight left of their holiday ! their house be not quite ready ; they be come here . oh , now , Mr. Gibson , we must have the new dinner - service at Watts 's I have   set my heart on so long ! ' home ' Cynthia call this house . I be sure it have be a home to she , poor darling ! I doubt if there be another man in the world who would have treat his step - daughter like dear papa ! and , Molly , you must have a new gown . " 

 " come , come ! remember I belong to the last generation , " say Mr. Gibson . 

 " and Cynthia will not notice what I wear , " say Molly , bright with pleasure at the thought of see she again . 

 " no ! but Walter will . he have such a quick eye for dress , and I think I rival papa ; if he be a good stepfather , I be a good stepmother , and I could not bear to see my molly shabby , and not look her good . I must have a new gown too . it will not   do to look as if we have nothing but the dress which we wear at the wedding ! " 

 but Molly stand out against the new gown for herself , and urge that if Cynthia and Walter be to come to visit they often , they have well see they as they really be , in dress , habit , and appointment . when Mr. Gibson have leave the room , Mrs. Gibson softly reproach molly for her obstinacy . 

 " you might have allow I to beg for a new gown for you , Molly , when you know how much I have admire that figure silk at Brown 's the other day . and now , of course , I can not   be so selfish as to get it for myself , and you to have nothing . you should learn to understand the wish of other people . still , on the whole , you be a dear , sweet girl , and I only wish well , I know what I wish ; only dear papa do not like it to be talk about . and now cover I up close , and let I go to sleep , and dream about my dear Cynthia and my new shawl ! "
